documents.ai_summary: MISO updated its proposal regarding charging limits for storage units in generation interconnection studies, replacing the Summer Shoulder charging case with a Light Load charging case to permit storage to charge across multiple dispatch phases while remaining subject to violation-based limits from DPP analysis. The revised BPM-015 updates are targeted for effectiveness prior to DPP 2026 kick-off (status: pending July Planning Advisory Committee approval). For pumped-storage operators, this expands the hours and conditions under which they can charge without contractual restrictions, improving their ability to arbitrage between low and high prices and optimize state-of-charge management—though charging remains subject to reliability-driven limits. Separately, MISO invited feedback (due June 26, 2026) on replacing the 2-Year Light Load, 0% wind MTEP scenario with a 2-Year Winter Peak, Low wind case; stakeholders raised concerns about how batteries are modeled under light load sensitivities, indicating sensitivity assumptions may affect resource adequacy accreditation.

documents.ai_summary: MISO's Interconnection Process Working Group met June 9, 2026, to review generator-queue procedures under FERC Order 2023 compliance. Key developments: (1) DPP 2026 cycle application deadline is September 10, 2026, with study cycle kick-off December 9, 2026; application fee (D1) is $7,600, with all projects in the cycle required to recalculate and 'true-up' fees every three years per GIP Section 3.6. (2) East (ITC) Regional Queue Cap has been met; the Queue Cap Tracker includes all submitted projects. (3) Expedited Resource Addition Study (ERAS) Cycle 4 is underway for six LSE projects (launched June 1, 2026); IPP requests are pending but proceeding as designed. (4) MISO reorganized its Resource Planning Team and is aligning DPP schedule with annual MTEP cycle, with Order 2023 requiring annual cluster studies. These procedural and fee changes affect cost, timing, and queue position for hydro and PSH projects seeking interconnection or uprates in MISO, especially those filing in DPP 2026 or competing within the regional cap; no comment deadline is stated in the minutes.

documents.ai_summary: MISO has launched its 2026 Long-Range Transmission Planning (LRTP) process for the Midwest and South regions, with issue identification and solutions development following a seven-step process; solutions proposals are anticipated in 2027. The LRTP will assess reliability and load-serving needs across a 20-year horizon (CAGR ~1.5% annually) using Future 2 and 3+ scenarios that show resource and energy adequacy under MISO's 1-in-10-years Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) criterion. Hydro owners' revenue and dispatch flexibility are affected because transmission bottlenecks identified in LRTP feed into the solutions phase — constraints on interregional transfers or load pockets in the South region (Louisiana/Texas) directly impact where MISO can call on existing hydro for scarcity pricing and ramping services, and load growth shapes the baseline scarcity against which hydro's flexibility premium is valued. Feedback on Future scenarios is due June 17, 2026; solutions will be developed in 2027, making this the foundational study for MISO transmission expansion over the next decade.

documents.ai_summary: The SPP Markets+ Seams Working Group met July 10, 2026, to discuss interchange transaction system scheduling, eTag coordination, high-priority export calculations, and direct dialogue with CAISO on seams governance. Key agenda items include MIR60-R (MITSPS scheduling), MIR83 (eTag implementation), and MIR36.1-R (high-priority export system sales) — all mechanisms governing how power transfers across the SPP-CAISO seam and how surplus capacity is allocated. These rules directly determine whether hydro operators in the SPP footprint can reliably export energy and reserve products to California and whether competing export priorities (load-serving vs. merchant) favor or penalize hydro flexibility and peaking capability. The group's work on SPP–CAISO dialogue addresses governance alignment and the broader question of how Markets+ integrates with CAISO operations — a strategic choice for NHA members evaluating multi-RTO participation. Future meetings scheduled through December 2026.

documents.title: Draft Minutes – OC – 6426
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Storage as a Transmission Asset (SATA) Phase II proposal directly affects pumped-storage hydro revenue and strategic options by establishing how storage resources are valued and compensated for transmission-service provision in PJM; regulation market redesign and synchronous reserve performance updates touch ancillary-service products hydro supplies.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Operating Committee endorsed the PJM/Constellation Storage as a Transmission Asset (SATA) Phase II proposal (Package A) on June 4, 2026, with 72% support versus the Monitoring Analytics alternative (Package B), which received only 13.5% support. SATA Phase II restructures how energy-storage resources — including pumped-storage hydro — earn revenue for deferring or avoiding transmission infrastructure investment, establishing whether storage dispatch can be optimized jointly with transmission needs and what compensation mechanisms apply. The endorsement moves the proposal toward implementation; details on how pumped-storage facilities will be accredited, when charging/discharging is compensated, and whether PSH gains preferential treatment versus battery storage remain in design phases. Owners with existing or planned PSH in PJM should monitor the Phase II rulebook for charging-energy treatment, state-of-charge operational rules, and whether SATA compensation stacks with or replaces energy and ancillary-service revenues. Regulation market bifurcation simulations and synchronous-reserve performance reviews (Items 8, 9, 12) indicate PJM is stress-testing the reserve products on which hydro dispatch depends; results will be presented in follow-up meetings.

Manual 14D (Generator Operational Requirements) and Manual 12 (Balancing Operations) directly govern hydro dispatch obligations, reserve performance standards, and emergency procedures; Manual 13 (Emergency Operations) and Manual 36 (System Restoration) touch black-start and frequency-response accreditation criteria. The plan also includes recurring items on Frequency Response Review, Regulation Performance Updates, and Synchronous Reserve Updates—all mechanisms on which hydro revenue and compliance depend. documents.title: Item 02 – 1 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM is mandating stricter reactive power/voltage support performance standards for all synchronous generators, including hydroelectric units, with new enforcement mechanisms and D-curve requirements that directly affect hydro's compliance obligations and the economic parameters of voltage-support revenue.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 14d Revision 71 tightens reactive power (VAR) control requirements for all generators in response to NERC's May 2026 lowering of IBR registration thresholds and the 2025 Spain blackout. The revision mandates that generators operate in 'automatic controlling voltage' mode (AVR in constant-voltage control) unless documented exceptions are approved by PJM and the Transmission Owner; defines volt-var control boundaries (reactive output limits of ±32.87% of maximum MW across a narrow voltage deadband); specifies minimum and maximum MW thresholds for reactive capability; and introduces a three-party (PJM–Transmission Owner–Generator Owner) investigative process for reactive-control performance issues. Hydroelectric facilities must comply with these new D-curve and reactive-capability specifications. The revision is a PJM Operating Committee presentation (status: approval sought), with no explicit comment deadline stated in this excerpt, but the urgency language ('urgent need to proceed efficiently') suggests near-term enforcement.

documents.title: Item 02 – 2 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: OBLIGATION: Updates to PJM Manual 14d for generator operational requirements, including reactive power (VAR) control and AVR mode specifications, impose new compliance requirements on all synchronous generators including conventional hydro and pumped-storage units that must adhere to reactive-power and voltage-support standards.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14d sections 7.1.2 and Attachment D (Generator Operational Requirements), effective July 23, 2026, to incorporate NERC Category 2 GO standards and clarify reactive-power (VAR) and automatic voltage regulator (AVR) requirements for generators. The revision addresses PJM's stated operational challenges: rising penetration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) and load growth outpacing generation, both increasing voltage risk. The manual update adds new references to NERC Category GO, specifies allowable AVR modes, provides graphs of acceptable volt-var and watt-var control ranges, and codifies PJM's monitoring and engagement protocols with generator operators. Conventional hydro and pumped-storage units with synchronous generation capability will become subject to these tightened reactive-power performance standards and monitoring obligations. MRC voting is scheduled for July 23rd, 2026.

documents.title: Item 02 – 3 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 14D specifies operational requirements, dispatch procedures, metering/data exchange, and ancillary-services participation rules affecting all generators, including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities in PJM.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 14D Revision 71 (effective June 30, 2026) defines generator operational requirements across interconnection, control-center standards, data exchange, market participation, and dispatch procedures. Relevant to hydro: Section 2 covers generation-owner responsibilities and transfers; Section 5 governs ancillary-services participation and market tools; Section 7 specifies dispatch procedures, switching, and synchronization rules; Section 10 details black-start procurement (a key hydro revenue stream); and Sections 12–13 address mixed-technology and solar forecasting (relevant as precedent for storage-hybrid treatment). Section 4 establishes metering and synchrophasor requirements affecting settlement accuracy. This is an operational-compliance baseline document for all PJM generators; the redline revision likely reflects recent tariff or reliability-standard changes. No specific comment deadline or vote date is evident in the excerpt provided.

documents.title: Item 05 – Security Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a cybersecurity awareness briefing with no connection to market design, pricing, participation rules, operational standards, or strategic options affecting hydro/PSH revenue, compliance, or dispatch.

documents.title: Item 06 – Reliability Compliance Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NERC reliability standards for inverter-based resources and computational loads directly affect large-load-collocated hydro and pumped-storage hybrid operations, and Essential Action #1 modifies interconnection modeling requirements that touch both traditional hydro and storage facilities co-located with new loads.
documents.ai_summary: PJM reports on multiple NERC standards and alerts relevant to hydro operations. Project 2025-05 revises PRC-029-1 ride-through requirements for VSC-HVDC-connected inverter-based resources, carving out a narrow technical exception that recognizes thermal limits on DC chopper energy absorption—relevant to synchronous hydro units required to meet cumulative voltage ride-through mandates and to any hydro owner considering VSC-HVDC export interfaces. More broadly, NERC's Level 3 Computational Load Alert (due August 3, 2026) and Essential Action #1 require Transmission Planners and Planning Coordinators to develop detailed modeling data and interconnection requirements for computational loads, which directly affect hydro facilities in footprints where large loads (especially data centers) are co-locating with or near hydro generation; this reshapes facility interconnection requirements and facility use modeling. NERC is also hosting Small Group Advisory Sessions (August 10–14, 2026) on IBR standards (PRC-028-1, PRC-029-1, PRC-030-1, MOD-026-2) with mandatory compliance in 2026–2027, and has opened comment on draft commissioning best practices and EV task force white papers. Hydro owners with synchronous machines at large-load sites or planning exports via HVDC should monitor the ride-through revisions; all hydro facility owners in planning footprints subject to computational load modeling should assess how Essential Action #1 data requirements may alter their interconnection or operating parameters.

documents.title: Item 08 – Regulation Redesign Phase 2 Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's Regulation Redesign Phase 2 restructures how the regulation ancillary service is offered, cleared, and priced — splitting the bidirectional product into separate RegUp and RegDn products with independent requirements, offers, clearing prices, and settlement, which directly affects how hydro and pumped-storage resources value and bid their fast-response capability.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is implementing Regulation Redesign Phase 2 on December 1, 2026, replacing its one-signal bidirectional regulation product with two separate products: RegUp (operates above zero crossing) and RegDn (operates below zero crossing). Each product has its own market requirement MW (seasonal and hourly, possibly asymmetric), clearing price, performance metrics (score, mileage, bias factor), and settlement billing; resources will bid separately for each and receive independent awards, though only one product deploys in any given interval. Regulation market participants (including hydro operators) must submit RegUp/RegDn offers and parameters to the Markets Gateway sandbox immediately (deadline overdue), complete signal conversion testing (overdue), and prepare for cutover by December 1, 2026; a virtual open house is scheduled July 30, 2026, and special education sessions occur October–November 2026. For hydro and pumped-storage owners, this restructuring requires new offer engineering for each product and affects how flexibility revenue is recognized — operators will need to optimize bids separately for up and down regulation rather than offering a single bidirectional capability, potentially exposing separate value in each direction depending on system conditions and seasonal asymmetries.

documents.title: Item 09 – Winter Generator Site Preparedness Visit Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Winter generator site-preparedness visits and winterization best practices directly address PJM's obligation to maintain unit readiness and ride-through capability during cold weather, a synchronous-unit operating standard that affects both conventional and hydro generators' compliance and outage scheduling.
documents.ai_summary: PJM and Reliability First conduct voluntary winter generator site-preparedness visits to inspect cold-weather readiness at generating facilities, including hydro units. The program has expanded from 3 PJM-partnered visits in 2023–2024 to 24 in 2025–2026, with a target of 30 sites in 2026–2027; SERC is launching a parallel program. Statistical analysis of participants in the ReliabilityFirst region between November 2023 and March 2025 shows significant improvements in Equivalent Forced Outage Rate (EFOR), Weighted EFOR, and Unavailability Factor (UF) among visited units compared to non-participating generators. Best practices shared include heat tracing, insulation, windbreaks, temperature monitoring, pre-staged supplies, and dual-fuel testing. Hydro operators selected for visits are identified by NERC GADS data, PJM eDART data, recent ownership changes, units deemed critical by the ISO, and black-start-capable units; visits are non-binding and not part of compliance enforcement, but recommendations and findings are documented in confidential reports. The expansion and formalization of this program signal PJM's prioritization of winter preparedness as a reliability obligation touching all generator types, and the demonstrated performance improvements may raise expected standards for forced-outage and unavailability metrics across the fleet.

documents.title: Item 10 – Project 2026 02 Computational Load – PJM Outreach
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Project 2026-02 addresses NERC reliability standards for computational load (data center) integration onto the bulk power system; while it touches interconnection and operations compliance, it creates obligations and standards specifically for large loads and load operators, not for generation or storage resources, and imposes no direct revenue, accreditation, participation, or strategic consequences for hydropower or pumped-storage owners.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Fuel inventory tracking for coal and oil units has no bearing on hydropower revenue mechanisms, compliance obligations, or strategic options; PJM has minimal hydropower, and this data does not inform hydro accreditation, pricing, dispatch, or reliability constructs.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document reports poll results on PJM Day-Ahead market timing; while natural-gas coordination touches fuel-constrained thermal generators, it does not address hydropower or pumped-storage revenue, obligations, or strategic options, which are not gas-constrained resources.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Document is meeting minutes recording administrative roll call, action-item status, and committee management; it contains no substantive proposals, decisions, or technical content touching hydro revenue streams, compliance obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: 20260709 MSC Item 13b DAMAP Reduction Total - Presentation (MSC-2026-7).pdf
documents.filename: 20260709 MSC Item 13b DAMAP Reduction Total - Presentation (MSC-2026-7).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: DAMAP Reduction Total calculation directly affects how derated and manually re-dispatched hydro and storage units are compensated for lost ancillary-service revenue, and the change alters the baseline against which their schedule adjustments are measured.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes to revise the Day-Ahead Margin Assurance Payment (DAMAP) Reduction Total calculation for derated and manually re-dispatched units, replacing a simple sum-of-cleared-products approach with constraint-based clearing values that reflect physical MW-sharing limits between regulation, contingency reserves, ramp capability, and short-term reserves. Currently, the sum method can overstate total cleared MWs (since ancillary-service MWs can be shared with energy), leading to larger schedule reductions than physical capability warrants; the proposed method uses the maximum of two DART constraint combinations to more accurately cap the Reduction Total. MISO also proposes a parallel change to one Real-Time Revenue Sufficiency Guarantee distribution component (DDC_NDL_CAP_VOL) using the same constraint logic. Stakeholder feedback is due July 24, 2026, with a target Q3 2026 filing and 60-day effective date. The change most directly affects dispatchable hydro, pumped storage, and other use-limited resources that routinely clear multiple ancillary services alongside energy.

documents.title: 20260709 MSC Item 13a DAMAP Reduction Total - New Issue Introduction (MSC-2026-7).pdf
documents.filename: 20260709 MSC Item 13a DAMAP Reduction Total - New Issue Introduction (MSC-2026-7).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: DAMAP (Day-Ahead Margin Assurance Payment) and RSG Distribution calculations directly affect revenue sufficiency and make-whole payments for dispatchable units including hydro; overstated Reduction Totals suppress adjusted DA schedules and thus unit revenue when derated or manually redispatched.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes correcting the Day-Ahead Margin Assurance Payment (DAMAP) Reduction Total calculation, which currently overstates the sum of cleared day-ahead products by double-counting MWs shared across multiple products, resulting in artificially suppressed adjusted DA schedules for derated or manually redispatched units. The fix would base Reduction Totals on physical unit limitations rather than product sums, and would also correct a similar defect in Revenue Sufficiency Guarantee (RSG) Distribution. Both changes require tariff filings; MSC-2026-7 has been introduced for adoption at the July 9, 2026 Market Subcommittee meeting. Any hydro or pumped-storage unit that experiences deratings, manual redispatch, or forced outages would receive more accurate (and typically higher) make-whole compensation under the corrected formulas.

documents.title: 20260709 MSC Item 08 2026 Biennial Review of MSC Inactive Issues on MISO Dashboard.pdf
documents.filename: 20260709 MSC Item 08 2026 Biennial Review of MSC Inactive Issues on MISO Dashboard.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Storage-as-transmission asset (MSC-2020-1) and hybrid resource participation rules (MSC-2020-3) are among nine inactive issues under biennial review; both directly affect PSH participation models and revenue-recognition pathways, though both remain deprioritized for 2026.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's 2026 biennial review recommends keeping all nine inactive Market Subcommittee issues in inactive status, including MSC-2020-1 (Using Storage as a Transmission Asset to Provide Market Services) and MSC-2020-3 (Enhanced Hybrid Resource Participation Model). Both issues were placed inactive in December 2022 and remain deprioritized due to competing initiatives—MSC-2020-3 is contingent on separate hybrid-resource work (MSC-2020-2) for which MISO has requested a delayed implementation date beyond June 2026. This preserves the current treatment of PSH and co-located hydro-storage hybrids under baseline participation rules rather than advancing alternative frameworks that could unlock additional revenue streams or operational flexibility. The next biennial review is scheduled for 2028, meaning no near-term market-design changes are anticipated for storage or hybrid resources in MISO.

documents.title: 20260709 MSC Item 09 MSC Issue Status Change Recommendations.pdf
documents.filename: 20260709 MSC Item 09 MSC Issue Status Change Recommendations.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses the Uninstructed Deviation Enhancement (UDE) Flag, which touches settlement rules and dispatch compliance affecting how hydro and other dispatchable resources are charged or credited for deviations from dispatch instructions—a direct obligation and settlement mechanism for hydro operators.
documents.ai_summary: MISO implemented the Uninstructed Deviation Enhancement (UDE) Flag in November 2025, with settlement enhancements becoming effective June 1, 2026. The MSC recommends closing this issue (MSC-2024-7) as Completed. The UDE flag modifies how deviations from dispatch instructions are metered and settled; hydro and other dispatchable resources must now comply with revised settlement rules that took effect June 1, 2026, affecting how they are charged or credited for off-dispatch performance. The mechanism is live; no further stakeholder action is requested, but operators should verify their settlement systems align with the June 1, 2026 rules.

documents.title: 20260709 MSC Item 04c ARR FTR TCM Quarterly Results.pdf
documents.filename: 20260709 MSC Item 04c ARR FTR TCM Quarterly Results.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ARR/FTR transmission revenue allocation and congestion-rent distribution directly affects hydro's transmission-dependent revenue stream and capacity value in MISO.
documents.ai_summary: MISO reports quarterly Transmission Customer Metric (TCM) results measuring what portion of day-ahead congestion revenue is returned to transmission customers via ARR/FTR credits versus retained by non-transmission customers. The 2025–2026 planning year shows 60% TCM, with 2026–2027 at 95% (partial-year data through June 29, 2026). The metric tracks total DA congestion revenue ($1.845B in 2025–2026), splits between transmission-customer returns ($1.102B) and non-transmission-customer revenue ($743M). Seasonal variation is substantial: winter 2025 TCM was 50%, while summer 2024 peaked at 92%. This directly impacts reservoir and pumped-storage hydro located in congested zones—a higher TCM means more congestion rent flows back to hydro owners with ARRs, improving revenue from transmission-constrained dispatch; lower TCM diverts that rent elsewhere. The metric has trended upward since 2020 lows (55% in 2020–2021), suggesting improving transmission-customer treatment, but shows high volatility by season and year.

documents.title: 20260709 MSC Item 04b MSE Quarterly Update.pdf

documents.title: 20260709 MSC Item 04a FERC Status Report.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's demand curve updates for regulation, spinning reserves, and up-ramp capability directly affect the ancillary-services revenue hydro operators earn; the co-located resource dispatch rule affects strategic dispatch options for hybrid and co-located hydro; and uninstructed deviation settlement changes alter operational compliance obligations and economic incentives for all dispatchable units including hydro.
documents.ai_summary: MISO filed three tariff changes: (1) Demand Curve update (ER26-2705-000, effective August 4, 2026) revising the demand curves for Regulation and Spinning Reserves and the Up Ramp Capability product in Day-Ahead and Real-Time Markets — hydro's core ancillary-service revenue streams. (2) Interconnection Service Limit Constraint for Co-located Resources (ER26-1915-000, filed 3/25/26, amended 6/11/26 for later implementation) allowing co-located resources to dispatch more efficiently within their interconnection limits — relevant to owners of hybrid hydro-solar or hydro-wind plants seeking optimized dispatch. (3) Uninstructed Deviation Settlement Charges (ER26-1521-000, accepted by FERC 5/29/26, effective 6/1/26) modifying settlement rules to incentivize reduction of uninstructed deviations — affects all dispatchable units including hydro by changing the economic penalty structure for unscheduled output. Ancillary-service demand curves are particularly material to large, responsive generators; co-located rules widen strategic options for mixed-renewable portfolios; uninstructed-deviation incentives reshape operational discipline across all hydro plants.

documents.title: 20260709 MSC Item 02c DERWG Status Report.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Order 2222 status update and DER aggregation framework directly affect small hydro and pumped-storage participation options as dispatchable, use-limited resources competing in retail-DR aggregation and VPP models.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Distributed Energy Resource Working Group reported on operational coordination among MISO, distribution companies, transmission owners, aggregators, and load-balancing authorities for DER deployment; the Local Enrollment System (LES) is in multi-phase rollout beginning with emergency DR and expanding to demand-response aggregators (DERAs). FERC issued a Notice of Withdrawal of Inquiry and Termination of Rulemaking on Order 2222 (Docket RM21-14, April 16, 2026), ending the rulemaking on aggregator participation in RTO markets. For small hydro and pumped-storage owners, the termination of RM21-14 closes the federal rule-making pathway for aggregator participation but leaves MISO's tariff-level Order 2222 compliance in place; the LES framework and DERA enrollment phase are the next operational gate determining whether small hydro/PSH can participate at scale through aggregators or remain subject to full-resource-model registration. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this status report.

documents.title: 20260709 MSC Item 01b Agenda.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Agenda includes multiple market-design items directly affecting hydro revenue and compliance: reserve deliverability (regulation and operating reserve), FTR-ARR enhancements, ancillary-services pricing mechanics (TCDC evaluation), and a new margin-assurance payment mechanism (DAMAP) that modifies energy/capacity price formation.
documents.ai_summary: The July 9, 2026 MISO Market Subcommittee meeting agenda covers four items of direct interest to hydro operators: (1) Enhance Reserve Deliverability (MSC-2026-2) — MISO will discuss regulation reserve deliverability and performance, directly affecting how hydro's primary AS product is procured and compensated; (2) Evaluation of Updates to Congestion Management and Transmission Constraint Demand Curves (MSC-2026-6) — initial findings on TCDC changes, which shape both energy scarcity pricing and operating-reserve demand curves where hydro earns flexibility revenue; (3) Day Ahead Margin Assurance Payment Reduction Total (MSC-2026-7) — new issue introduction on DAMAP, a capacity/margin-assurance construct that may alter how day-ahead scarcity and adequacy are priced; and (4) FTR-ARR Market Enhancements (MSC-2025-5) — problem statement on transmission-rights market redesign, with feedback requested. The meeting also includes IMM seasonal outlook and market recommendations. No comment deadlines or vote dates are stated in the agenda itself.

documents.title: 20260709 MSC Item 14b DAMAP Reduction Total - Presentation (MSC-2026-7).pdf
documents.filename: 20260709 MSC Item 14b DAMAP Reduction Total - Presentation (MSC-2026-7).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: DAMAP Reduction Total calculation affects how derated or manually re-dispatched hydro units have their day-ahead schedules scaled, directly impacting revenue recognition and ancillary-services stacking for dispatchable resources including pumped storage and reservoir hydro.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes to change how the Day-Ahead Margin Assurance Payment (DAMAP) Reduction Total is calculated for derated and manually re-dispatched units, replacing a summation of all cleared day-ahead products (Energy + Regulation + Contingency Reserves + Ramp + Short-Term Reserve) with the maximum of two DART constraint-clearing values that reflect which products can physically share megawatts. The current method potentially overstates the Reduction Total by double-counting shared MWs across products, resulting in lower adjusted day-ahead schedules for affected units; the proposed method aligns the reduction calculation with actual physical constraints that limit product combinations during day-ahead clearing. MISO also proposes a conforming change to one Revenue Sufficiency Guarantee Distribution component (DDC_NDL_CAP_VOL). Stakeholder feedback is due July 24, 2026, with a target FERC filing in Q3 2026 and effective date 60 days thereafter. This principally affects dispatchable thermal and hydroelectric units that regularly clear ancillary services alongside energy, and pumped-storage facilities that rely on accurate product-stacking calculations for profit optimization.

documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes to correct the Day-Ahead Margin Assurance Payment (DAMAP) Reduction Total calculation, which currently overstates reductions by summing MWs across shared products when units are derated or manually re-dispatched. The correction would base the reduction on physical unit limitations instead, yielding more accurate adjusted day-ahead schedules and affecting the Revenue Sufficiency Guarantee (RSG) Distribution calculation as well. Both changes require a tariff filing; the Market Subcommittee is adopting this as a new issue (MSC-2026-7) for proceeding to tariff modification.

documents.title: 20260709 MSC Item 09 2026 Biennial Review of MSC Inactive Issues on MISO Dashboard.pdf
documents.filename: 20260709 MSC Item 09 2026 Biennial Review of MSC Inactive Issues on MISO Dashboard.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's biennial review of inactive market-design issues includes three proposals directly touching hydro and PSH revenue and participation: storage-as-transmission (MSC-2020-1), hybrid-resource participation (MSC-2020-3), and uncertainty products (MSC-2021-9), all held inactive pending other work — their eventual reactivation will reshape how PSH and hybrid hydro participate in MISO's markets.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's 2026 biennial review recommends keeping nine market-design issues inactive, including three with direct hydro impact: MSC-2020-1 (Using Storage as a Transmission Asset to Provide Market Services), MSC-2020-3 (Enhanced Hybrid Resource Participation Model), and MSC-2021-9 (Uncertainty Product in DA, RT, AS). All three remain inactive due to other competing priorities and contingencies—MSC-2020-3 explicitly waits on completion of a separate hybrid-resource initiative (MSC-2020-2) for which MISO requested a delayed implementation beyond June 2026. MSC-2020-1 has been inactive since December 2022; MSC-2021-9 was reactivated in February 2024 but returned to inactive status in November 2024. These issues, once active, would expand PSH's ability to offer grid services beyond energy and ancillary services, unlock hybrid participation in MISO's markets, and introduce flexibility products that could shift PSH's revenue sources—but no work is scheduled before the next biennial review in 2028.

documents.title: PDCWG-Report-to-ROS_070926
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's 2027 ancillary-services methodology revision directly informs frequency-response and inertia-procurement design elsewhere; the declining synchronous inertia trend and FFR award uptake set precedent for storage and synthetic inertia treatment that hydro operators should monitor for cross-market design migration.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT is revising its 2027 ancillary-services (AS) methodology with board approval targeted for September 15, 2026; stakeholder feedback is being solicited through July 2026 ROS and WMS subcommittee meetings. The report shows ERCOT's minimum synchronous inertia has declined from 143 GW·s (2016) to 114.7 GW·s (2026) despite rising non-synchronous generation penetration (now 81% of system load), while fast-frequency response (FFR) awards have ramped from zero in prior years to 430 MW in 2026—indicating a structural shift toward synthetic/storage-based frequency support to replace lost synchronous inertia from retiring thermal units. For hydro operators in other RTOs considering their own inertia and frequency-response portfolios, this documents ERCOT's precedent-setting approach to pricing and accrediting FFR as a substitute for synchronous reserve margins, a model that may inform future NERC standards or multiregional frequency-adequacy assessments.

documents.title: 03. DRAFT Minutes Special ROS 20260414
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large load interconnection standards (Batch Zero) and co-located generation constructs directly affect hydro's strategic option to serve large loads behind-the-meter and influence transmission planning constraints on hydro export capability.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's ROS discussed PGRR 145 and NPRR 1325, which establish a Batch Zero process for large load interconnections (≥25 MW) with eligibility pathways, financial commitment requirements, and site-control standards aligned with PUCT Project No. 58481. The framework includes treatment of 'bring your own generation' constructs and Provisional Controllable Load Resource (PCLR) implementation; approximately 100 GW of potential study load could enter Batch Zero. Key open issues include Year 6 MW allocation, load prioritization, and handling of post-refinement load changes. Matt Mereness indicated clarifying language on CLR and co-generation will be filed ahead of the April 23, 2026 Special ROS meeting, with ROS consideration expected May 7, 2026 (no votes taken April 14). For hydro owners, the co-location of generation with large loads creates both a strategic opportunity (behind-the-meter supply to anchor tenants) and a transmission-planning risk — the 100 GW study queue and PUCT-mandated site control and financial security requirements signal a competitive environment for large-load siting that may constrain hydro's ability to locate or relocate export infrastructure. The Batch Zero framework's interaction with RPG-approved transmission projects and TSP coordination will influence which transmission corridors remain available for conventional hydro.

documents.title: 04. May 2026 ERCOT Operations Report Public
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT operations data on frequency response, ramping capability, renewable penetration extremes, and congestion patterns provides cross-market comparison for how a non-hydro RTO manages the flexibility and balancing challenges that hydro-rich systems address differently, informing NHA's monitoring of market-design alternatives.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's May 2026 operations report documents system conditions during a month with record-high renewable penetration (86.77% at peak) and extreme net-load ramps (16,825 MW over 60 minutes on 5/2/2026), managed through 76 reliability unit commitments for capacity and two frequency events met by synchronous unit trips. The report shows how a low-hydro, predominantly thermal/renewable system accommodates multi-GW ramp rates and high wind/solar variability—a contrast to hydro-rich RTOs where flexible ramping and frequency response from conventional hydro avoid equivalent RUC commitments. Most relevant to NHA: ERCOT's reliance on capacity-driven RUC deployments and synthetic inertia rather than dispatchable hydro reserves highlights the revenue opportunity hydro's real-time flexibility commands in systems with high variable renewable penetration, and underscores why storage and ramping accreditation rules matter to NHA members competing against ERCOT-style procurement.

documents.title: 04. SystemPlanningROS_May2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT transmission planning includes major load-interconnection projects (CPS Energy, STEC, LCRA) that affect grid scarcity and the strategic value of existing dispatchable resources; large-load projects also signal demand growth patterns that influence seasonal energy adequacy and transmission bottlenecks — relevant as a cross-market comparison point for how ERCOT's non-hydro transmission-planning regime creates or relieves constraints that affect interregional trade and hydro-plant deliverability into high-value load centers.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's May 2026 System Planning report shows 42 Regional Planning Group (RPG) transmission projects under review or completed, totaling approximately $32.6 billion in all-project costs. Key large-load interconnection projects include CPS Energy's Large Load Additions ($333.7M), STEC's Medina County additions ($492M), and Nueces Green Ammonia ($161.1M), plus major grid-strengthening projects in North/Central Texas (Oncor sets 1 & 2, $2.4–2.4B each). This signals sustained load growth in Texas demand centers, which raises the scarcity value of firm generation and imports. The transmission expansion pipeline — with major projects endorsed through mid-2026 — removes potential binding constraints but does not relieve seasonal energy-adequacy pressure. For hydro owners considering ERCOT export exposure or market-design benchmarking, this demonstrates how ERCOT's RPG-driven, utility-led transmission planning differs from RTO-coordinated interregional transmission in hydro-rich regions, and how ERCOT's lack of interregional flexible capacity products (e.g., WEIM's ancillary-services integration) leaves seasonal peaking and ramping risks within ERCOT's footprint — a structural advantage for hydro exports from the Pacific Northwest if Southwest transmission expands.

documents.title: 04. 2027_AS_Methodology_ROS_070926
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT ancillary-services methodology review directly affects how frequency response, regulation, and reserve products are procured and priced — a cross-market comparison point for hydro's role in reserve markets and frequency services that other RTOs benchmark.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT is reviewing its 2027 ancillary-services (AS) methodology for Regulation, Responsive Reserve Service (RRS), and reserve procurement (ECRS/Non-Spin), with votes scheduled August 5–6, 2026, and PUCT approval targeted November–December 2026. The 2026 methodology introduced a probabilistic framework for reserve procurement; 2027 will refine that engine by testing sensitivities in risk inputs (forecast error, forced outages), risk credits (headroom discounts for already-committed capacity), and convergence criteria, while separately monitoring whether large-load ramping (up to 400+ MW spikes) requires a new Regulation adjustment factor. ERCOT noted that inertia trends have shifted lowest-inertia hours from night to midday since 2025, affecting RRS requirements keyed to inertia and frequency-response obligations (NERC BAL-003 IFRO); 2027 RRS quantities remain pending NERC's 2027 IFRO assessment. The sensitivity analysis will compare risk levels (Watch, EEA1, EEA2 vs. Load Shed Probability targets of 1-in-10/5/1-year), look-ahead windows (1–6 hours), risk credits (current 60% night / 25% day vs. higher levels), and solar forecast-error logic. This is a reference point for how non-hydro-dominant RTOs structure flexibility and reserve procurement under high renewable penetration and intra-hour load volatility.

documents.title: 06. DWG_Report_to_ROS_July_2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PGRR 122 establishes new reliability criteria for large load loss that may constrain interconnection of load-serving resources (including co-located load at hydro sites) and could trigger operational mitigation mechanisms affecting hydro dispatch and revenue.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT DWG proposes PGRR 122, setting a 3,200 MW stability threshold for total load loss across P1/P2/P4/P5/P7 single contingencies and certain multi-event scenarios. When this threshold is exceeded, ERCOT will first evaluate transmission solutions (capital cost cap: $200M), then may limit Large Load interconnection allocation, and finally apply operational mitigation (IROLs, real-time curtailment) as backstop. This creates a new transmission constraint and potential dispatch curtailment mechanism that could affect hydro owners with co-located large loads or those operating in constrained areas where operational mitigation is invoked; the proposal is advancing to ROS (Reliability and Operations Subcommittee) for action, with implementation language refinement ongoing as of the July 9, 2026 meeting.

documents.title: 09. PDCWG-Report-to-ROS_070926
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's 2027 ancillary-services (AS) methodology review and frequency-control/inertia analysis directly parallels hydro-rich RTOs' ancillary-services accreditation and pricing — this report documents ERCOT's approach to frequency response, regulation, and inertia procurement as a cross-market comparison point for how non-hydro systems procure flexibility and balance services that hydro dominates elsewhere.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT is refreshing its 2027 ancillary-services methodology with board review and approval targeted for September 15, 2026; stakeholder feedback is being solicited at July 2026 ROS and WMS subcommittee meetings. The report documents ERCOT's frequency-control performance (CPS1 at 181.08%), minimum synchronous inertia declining from 143 GW·s in 2016 to 114.7 GW·s in 2026 despite load growth to 48,013 MW, and fast-frequency-response (FFR) awards growing from zero in 2023 to 430 MW in 2026 as non-synchronous generation penetration reached 81% of system load. This inertia-decline trajectory and FFR expansion illustrate how a non-hydro system responds to high wind/solar penetration — ERCOT relies on synthetic inertia procurement and FFR products rather than synchronous reserve capacity, providing contrast to hydro-rich markets' reliance on governor response and synchronous units for frequency control.

documents.title: 04-MIR9.2-R SPP Comments-20260702.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Dynamic Schedule Resources directly affect how dispatchable generation (including hydropower and pumped-storage resources) can be scheduled, registered, and settled when exporting across balancing-authority boundaries in Markets+, materially altering offer/dispatch obligations and revenue settlement for multi-state hydro operations.
documents.ai_summary: SPP submits comment on MIR9.2-R (Dynamic Schedule Resources) clarifying how Markets+ will process and manage Dynamic Schedules within and outside the Markets+ footprint, including registration, e-Tagging, ICCP expectations, and dispatchability rules for resources across native and attaining balancing authorities. The proposal requires 100% e-Tagging of Dynamic Schedule Resource output and establishes separate treatment for self-scheduled Dynamic Export Interchange Transactions in day-ahead versus real-time markets: day-ahead use fixed e-Tag energy profile; real-time uses Dynamic Schedule Resource Interface. Pumped-storage and other multi-state reservoir hydro scheduling exports across BA boundaries under Dynamic Schedules are directly affected, particularly the distinction between what can remain dispatchable for market optimization versus what becomes non-dispatchable export, and the settlement basis (LMP at external interface) for self-scheduled exports. Status: comment submitted July 2, 2026 to Markets+ Design Working Group; next step is working group review and potential tariff/protocol adoption.

documents.title: 04-MIR9.2-R SRP Comments-20260622.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Dynamic Schedule Resources (DSRs) rule clarification for pumped-storage and co-located hybrid resources — affects how PSH charging/discharging is tagged, settled, and dispatched across BA boundaries, with direct implications for PSH revenue streams (export/import pricing), registration obligations, and co-location strategies in Markets+ footprint.
documents.ai_summary: Salt River Project (SRP) comments on Markets+ Initiative Request 9.2-R, which defines how Markets+ will register, tag, and dispatch Dynamic Schedule Resources (energy transfers between BAs using real-time dynamic adjustments). SRP specifically requests clarity on how multi-state resources (MSRs), particularly co-located battery and solar systems, should be handled under dynamic tags — proposing that a single POI with combined generation (e.g., solar + battery discharge) use one export dynamic tag and one separate tag for battery charging imports. The proposed tariff and protocol revisions (April 2025 and 1.0a versions) address registration, e-Tagging, ICCP coordination, and power-balance accounting for DSRs. This clarification directly affects PSH and hybrid owners' ability to stack charging and discharging schedules across BA lines without pseudo-tie-out overhead or regulation burden shift, and affects cost basis for MSR registration and settlement.

documents.title: 05-MIR9.3-R SPP Comments-20260702.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: External resource participation framework affects how hydro and other resources external to Markets+ can register and participate in SPP's markets, establishing regulatory clarity on pseudo-tie modeling that shapes revenue recognition, settlement, and balancing-authority coordination.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ Design Working Group is adopting a new registration and modeling framework for 'dynamically scheduled quasi-pseudo-ties'—external resources that are modeled as pseudo-tied into a participating BAA for Markets+ purposes while remaining dynamically scheduled (not formally tied) between Native BA and Attaining BA per NAESB standards. The framework adds a new resource designation to Section 9.1.1 and creates two pathways: formal electronic pseudo-ties (Section 9.1.9.1) and dynamically scheduled quasi-pseudo-ties (9.1.9.2–9.1.9.3). Under the dynamic pathway, the Market Participant must register the resource, designate a Meter Agent to submit settlement meter data, and obtain an attestation letter from the Attaining BA confirming it understands the resource will be modeled as a pseudo-tie in Markets+; the resource continues to be e-Tagged as a Dynamic Schedule between BAs. This clarification benefits external hydro plants and other resources in adjacent balancing authorities that wish to participate in Markets+ while preserving existing dynamic-schedule operations, reducing administrative ambiguity and enabling more consistent participation pathways. The framework is documented in Markets+ Protocols version 12.0a; this is SPP's comment form on the MIR with proposed edits, indicating the proposal is in design and comment phase.

documents.title: 05-MIR9.3-R SRP Comments-20260623.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Establishes registration and operational framework for dynamically scheduled pseudo-ties in Markets+, directly affecting how external hydropower and storage resources (particularly those in neighboring balancing authorities) can participate in SPP's market, participate in settlements, and access revenue streams through pseudo-tie modeling pathways.
documents.ai_summary: Salt River Project (SRP) comments on Markets+ Initiative Request 9.3-R, which clarifies how dynamically scheduled pseudo-ties (external resources represented as pseudo-tied into Markets+ while energy schedules as a Dynamic Schedule under NAESB/WECC standards) should be registered and modeled in Markets+ protocols. SRP requests that SPP expand the protocols to explicitly address multi-party ownership scenarios: jointly owned pseudo-ties between multiple Markets+ Participants, or between Markets+ Participants and non-Markets+ entities, across External Resource Pseudo-Ties, Internal Resource Pseudo-Ties, and Dynamically Scheduled Pseudo-Ties. SRP also requests that SPP provide a template attestation letter (required by the attaining BA under Section 9.1.9.2) within the Protocols rather than requiring ad hoc handling. The proposal affects primarily external hydro and storage owners in the Southwest seeking to participate in Markets+ through dynamic scheduling arrangements; this comment addresses governance gaps in multi-party co-ownership that could delay or complicate registration of jointly held assets.

documents.title: 06-MIR60-R Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) submission.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.filename: 06-MIR60-R Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) submission.docx
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Establishes Markets+ System Points (MPSP) for managing interchange transactions at the market boundary and internal market transfers; affects how hydropower and storage resources schedule and tag export/import transactions across Markets+ balancing authority areas, touching transaction scheduling, settlement location mechanics, and operational compliance for resources participating in Markets+ footprint transfers.
documents.ai_summary: SPP staff (Kelli Schermerhorn) submitted MIR 60-R on May 29, 2026 to implement Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS), a framework enabling market participants to schedule and e-Tag interchange transactions that result from market clearing rather than pre-bilateral arrangements. MITSPS uses Markets+ System Points (MPSP)—generic scheduling points within Markets+ BAAs that serve as sources for exports and sinks for imports—allowing transactions to move in and out of the Markets+ footprint and internally between BAAs. The proposal revises Markets+ Protocols (glossary, definitions, offer submittal, day-ahead and real-time inputs) to define MPSP registration, e-Tag requirements, and optional hourly interchange limits per BAA, with MPSP Interchange Limits enforced in day-ahead, RUC, and real-time market runs. Approval is targeted for Q3 2026 and implementation for Q1 2027. For hydro and storage owners in the Markets+ footprint (SPP's multistate region), this establishes operational and commercial rules for how their export and import transactions are scheduled, tagged, and settled at market interfaces, affecting transaction flexibility and compliance obligations.

documents.title: 06-MIR60-R SPP Comments-20260702.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: defines how Markets+ will handle cross-boundary interchange transactions through generic system points (MPSP), affecting hydro and storage operators' ability to schedule and export energy across balancing authority boundaries, and potentially altering how their interchange transactions are treated in unit commitment and real-time market clearing.
documents.ai_summary: SPP staff (Kelli Schermerhorn) submits proposed protocol revisions to implement Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS), a framework for uncommitted import/export transactions using generic system points rather than bilateral pre-schedules. The revisions define MPSP as a non-resource-specific scheduling point within each Markets+ BAA, establish how e-Tags flow through these points, and integrate MPSP interchange limits into day-ahead and real-time market inputs (day-ahead, RUC, RTBM, and intra-day RUC). For hydro and storage operators, this creates a new pathway to participate in cross-footprint energy interchange without pre-scheduled bilateral agreements, potentially improving market access and revenue opportunities for exports during high-price periods — but also subjects those transactions to BA-imposed hourly and net-position limits that constrain flexibility. The document is a protocol revision submission to the Markets+ Design Working Group; next step and vote timeline are not stated in the excerpt.

documents.title: 07-MIR77-R Dispatchable Variable Energy Resource Deployment in RTBM submission.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.filename: 07-MIR77-R Dispatchable Variable Energy Resource Deployment in RTBM submission.docx
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Clarifies Markets+ protocol treatment of Dispatchable Variable Energy Resources (DVERs) in real-time energy dispatch, removing legacy Real-Time Capability language that does not apply to Markets+ operations — directly affects how variable hydro and pumped-storage resources are instructed and settled in the RTBM.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ staff proposes MIR 77-R to remove the defined term "Real-Time Capability" from the Markets+ Tariff and clarify RTBM energy deployment for DVERs that clear Short-Term Flex Down. The MIR addresses legacy language carried over from the Integrated Marketplace that does not apply to Markets+, since Markets+ does not co-optimize for Operating Reserves (that function is left to Participating Balancing Authorities). The proposal is a low-complexity correction and clarification; implementation is targeted Q1 2027. This affects any hydro or pumped-storage resource registered as a DVER in SPP Markets+, ensuring their dispatch instructions and settlement reflect Markets+ actual operating model rather than inherited concepts from an earlier market design. Desired MPEC approval is Q4 2026.

documents.title: 07-MIR77-R SPP Comments-20260702.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This SPP Markets+ tariff change removes Real-Time Capability language from DVER (Dispatchable Variable Energy Resource) dispatch rules, which affects how variable generation—including run-of-river hydro and solar/wind resources that may co-locate with hydro—are dispatched and priced in the real-time balancing market, and specifies data-reporting requirements (ICCP) for wind and solar that establish a precedent for how variable resources are metered and forecast.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ (the successor RTO design for the SPP region) proposes to remove outdated Real-Time Capability language carried over from the legacy Integrated Marketplace that does not apply to Markets+ dispatch of DVERs in the Real-Time Balancing Market (RTBM). The change removes Real-Time Capability as a dispatch constraint for DVERs—which would otherwise cap Desired Dispatch output—but retains it as an ICCP (Inter-Control Center Communications Protocol) data point communicated to the Market Operator for use in variable-energy-resource forecasting. The document includes detailed registration and real-time data requirements for wind and solar resources (wind-speed, irradiance, turbine availability, Real-Time Capability in MW), establishing the data infrastructure for variable-resource integration. This is part of the ongoing Markets+ Implementation Request (MIR77-R) process; the summary does not state a comment deadline or vote date. The change most directly affects owners of run-of-river hydro or variable resources participating in Markets+; the removal of Real-Time Capability as a dispatch hard limit could allow higher output from variable generators when marginal costs warrant it, shifting the price signal available to inflexible or peaking hydro.

documents.title: 08-MIR79-R Addendum_Markets+ Initiative Request (MIR) Process v2.0_Draft.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document is a procedural manual for Markets+ request submission and governance workflows — it establishes HOW proposed changes are routed, reviewed, and approved, but does not itself propose or decide any revenue-affecting mechanisms, obligations, or strategic options for hydropower or pumped storage.

documents.title: 08-MIR79-R Markets+ Initiative Request (MIR) Process document updates submission.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.filename: 08-MIR79-R Markets+ Initiative Request (MIR) Process document updates submission.docx
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document is an administrative process update to the Markets+ Initiative Request (MIR) submission and routing procedures — it revises terminology, clarifies intake and approval workflows, and aligns supporting documents, but contains no changes to market design, tariff, protocols, or operational rules that would affect hydro revenue, compliance obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: 08-MIR79-R MIR Process document-SRP Comments-20260526.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This document establishes the Markets+ Revision Initiative Request (MIR) process — the governance procedure by which hydro and storage owners can submit proposals affecting Markets+ Tariff, Protocols, and systems. Understanding who can submit MIRs, what qualifies as a complete submission, routing to working groups, and approval pathways directly determines hydro's strategic option to initiate rule changes affecting revenue, obligations, and operational flexibility in the SPP Markets+ footprint.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ has adopted and clarified a Revision Initiative Request (MIR) Process (effective November 13, 2025; current version 2.0 as of May 12, 2026) governing how stakeholders submit and advance proposals to change the Markets+ Tariff, Protocols, and related documents. Qualified Entities—including any Markets+ Participant, SPP Staff, the Market Monitoring Unit, governmental authorities, and SPP Members—may submit MIRs via the RMS using a standardized Submission Form; incomplete submissions are rejected and resubmitted by deadline. Submissions are routed by Primary Policy Coordination staff to the appropriate Primary Working Group (MDWG for Tariff/Protocols/MIR Process itself) and Secondary Working Groups; comments must be filed within five business days of Primary WG scheduling and are posted within three days. The Primary Working Group (e.g., MDWG) approves or rejects changes, which then route through Secondary WGs, the Markets+ Participant Executive Committee (MPEC), and the Markets+ Independent Panel (MIP) for final approval; system-change MIRs receive impact analysis (scope, budget, timeline, staffing implications, alternatives) within 30 calendar days of Primary WG approval. This process is the formal gateway by which hydro and PSH owners can propose market-design changes—faster than new tariff proceedings but still multi-stage—and affects what rule changes are feasible to pursue in Markets+.

documents.title: 08-MIR79-R SRP Comments-20260526.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a procedural comment on the Markets+ Initiative Request (MIR) process document itself — governance of how SPP handles market-design proposals — with no substantive content touching hydro revenue, obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: 09-MIR82-R MTSP OASIS Queue Pause presentation_MTWG.pptx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Interconnection queue management and transmission service request processing timelines directly affect hydro's ability to submit and advance generation or storage interconnection requests, and alter the practical timing constraints on transmission-service applications.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ proposes revising its OASIS Queue Pause rules (MIR82-R) to allow individual Transmission Service Providers (MTSPs) flexibility in managing transmission service requests during the Day-Ahead Market window, rather than imposing a uniform mandatory pause. The revision shifts from a hard pause to a managed-pause model, requiring each MTSP to document its own queue-management procedures and coordinate with Market Operator timelines while preserving request prioritization. The change addresses NAESB WEQ 001-4.13 timing-compliance concerns SPP Legal identified as preventing a global waiver; it moves the choice of queue-management approach to individual MTSPs. The MDWG is scheduled to approve MIR82-R on July 8, 2026, with MPEC approval targeted for August 25, 2026. Hydro and storage interconnection applicants relying on queue advancement in the day-ahead timeframe should monitor which MTSP service territories are affected and what queue-pause conditions each MTSP implements under the new flexibility provision.

documents.title: 09-MIR82-R Puget Sound Energy Comments-20260609.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: INTERCONNECTION/TRANSMISSION: PSE's comments on Markets+ MTSP queue-pause rules affect transmission service request (TSR) processing timing and how interconnection queues coordinate with day-ahead market timelines — a core strategic issue for hydro and storage seeking new capacity or deliverability upgrades in the Markets+ footprint.
documents.ai_summary: Puget Sound Energy proposes revising Markets+ tariff and protocol language governing when Transmission Service Providers must pause or manage processing of transmission service requests relative to Day-Ahead Market operations. PSE seeks clarification on queue-processing definitions, pause triggers, and the duration of TSRs subject to pause, proposing language that allows each MTSP to determine queue-management conditions based on its own operational requirements rather than adhering to a universal pause obligation. The revision aims to reduce administrative burden, enhance market–transmission coordination, and improve transparency by requiring MTSPs to document and publish their queue procedures. This affects hydro and storage developers' ability to move TSRs through interconnection queues smoothly and predictably; interconnection queue timing and coordination with market operations directly shape the cost and speed of new capacity coming online and existing-plant uprates, both strategic levers for fleet adequacy decisions.

documents.title: 11-WRAP Program Split _ Market impacts.pptx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses governance and market-system integration affecting how WRAP binding-season participants (including hydropower owners) interact with Markets+ operations, which directly impacts must-offer rules, holding requirements, resource-sharing calculations, and operational-program settlements affecting hydro revenue and compliance obligations.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ and the Western Renewable Power Pool (WPP) are proposing to split operational responsibilities: starting October 2028 (Wave Two go-live), Markets+ will assume WRAP's operations-program functions (including sharing calculations, holdback optimization, and reserve-zone coordination), while WPP retains the forward-showing program. WRAP binding-season participants (including Bonneville Power Administration, Avista, Idaho Power, Puget Sound Energy, and others) who are not full Markets+ members would participate under a limited-tariff model and must sign participation agreements; governance changes and a new Markets+ WRAP-focused working group (MRAWG, standing up Q3 2026) will address tariff bifurcation, settlements, and coordination between the two programs. For non-Markets+ WRAP participants and full Markets+ members alike, the move integrates holdback inputs, must-offer ceilings, and sharing calculations into the M+ market-clearing engine, potentially refining uncertainty inputs and expanding the resource pool eligible to meet load via reserve zones. MPEC feedback/decision is scheduled for August 2026.

documents.title: 12-MIR8.1-R MSR SOC Management draft language.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Modifies state-of-charge management rules for Market Storage Resources (MSRs)—pumped storage's core participation model in Markets+. Shifts from participant-forecast to market-operator calculation of SOC within Day-Ahead and RUC studies, directly affecting PSH dispatch feasibility, financial penalties for forecast mismatches, and capacity commitment reliability—a use-limited-resource obligation that hydro owners must track.
documents.ai_summary: SPP and the MSR Subgroup propose enhanced MSR state-of-charge management by introducing time-coupled intervals in Day-Ahead Market and RUC studies, allowing the market operator to calculate SOC rather than relying solely on participant forecasts. Currently, market participants bear full responsibility for SOC prediction in their Resource Offers, creating risk that Day-Ahead commitments become infeasible in real-time and expose operators to financial penalties and capacity shortfalls. The proposal—submitted as draft tariff and protocol language—reduces the burden on MSR participants by enabling more accurate SOC tracking across study horizons. Pumped-storage operators in Markets+ should review whether this shifts SOC management burden away from them and how it interacts with their obligation to register loss-factor parameters and directional commitment limitations.

documents.title: 13-MIR8.2-R Co-Located Resource Constraints draft language.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Co-located resource constraint mechanism directly affects how pumped-storage hydro and paired-resource portfolios are dispatched, offered into must-offer obligations, and accounted for at injection-constrained points — a novel market design rule that creates or removes revenue optionality for PSH and hybrid hydro installations.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ proposes a new Co-Located Resources Constraint mechanism (MIR8.2-R) that formalizes 'clipping' — transferring MWs between co-located resources behind a single interconnection point when their combined capacity exceeds the injection limit. The rule applies to Day-Ahead Market, RUC, and Real-Time Balancing Market, with the market-clearing engine respecting whichever is lower: the sum of individual resource capacities or the declared co-located constraint. Critically, must-offer obligations for co-located resources use the same lesser-of logic, meaning a PSH facility paired with solar (for example) could see its must-offer requirement capped by the injection limit rather than its full capacity. This changes the dispatch flexibility and revenue recognition for PSH in portfolios with injection constraints, and improves market visibility into actual MW availability at the point of interconnection. The draft language touches tariff definitions, participation rules, must-offer requirements, and settlement guidance; status and comment deadline not stated in this excerpt.

documents.title: 14-Default Hubs for SHOC.pdf  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: SHOC (Seasonal Hydroelectric Offer Curve) is a hydro-specific offer-formation mechanism in Markets+; the choice of default pricing hubs directly affects the short-term energy component of hydro revenue and bid development.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ is proposing to define default electric pricing hubs for the Seasonal Hydroelectric Offer Curve (SHOC), which currently contains a 'TBD' placeholder in Appendix D, Section D.7.f of the Markets+ Protocols. SHOC's short-term component is indexed to peak day-ahead prices and on-peak monthly futures at a default hub; the hub choice determines the price signal hydro generators see when structuring seasonal offers. Candidate hubs include Mid-Columbia (Northwest) and Palo Verde (Arizona), selected by liquidity (≥2,000 MWh average daily volume or ≥5 transactions/counterparties) and location (footprint representation, regional fit). The MMU will submit an MIR for MDWG approval in August 2026, with MPEC approval targeted for August or November; hydro operators in Northwest and Southwest regions should monitor hub selection, as basis between their resource location and the default hub will shape the financial foundation of SHOC bids.

documents.title: 15-MIR36.1-R High Priority Export System Sales-Design submission.docx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.filename: 15-MIR36.1-R High Priority Export System Sales-Design submission.docx
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This MIR clarifies Markets+ Protocol rules governing how Load Responsible Entities (LREs) validate and effectuate High Priority Export Interchange Transactions during WRAP Binding Seasons, directly affecting the revenue recognition and compliance pathway for hydropower exports from SPP-member utilities with seasonal surplus capacity.
documents.ai_summary: SPP staff (Dan Kheloussi) submitted MIR 36.1-R to clarify Markets+ Protocol sections 4.2.3.3 and 9.1.14, refining the validation criteria and operational mechanics for High Priority Export System Sales tied to LRE slice-of-system resources during WRAP Binding Seasons. The proposal designates exports as "High Priority" when they originate from an LRE's WRAP-reserved seasonal surplus (monthly positive difference between QCC capacity, contract adjustments, and forward-showing obligation) or are backed by WRAP forward showings or holdbacks; pro-rata reduction rules apply when aggregate High Priority e-tags exceed available seasonal surplus. This streamlines administrative handling of export bids and aligns LRE seasonal-surplus accounting with export authorization, reducing financial risk for market participants maximizing seasonal flexibility sales. Implementation is targeted for Q1 2027, with MPEC approval sought in Q3 2026.

documents.title: 16-MIR90-R MMU Dispute Resolution.pptx  [in MDWG Meeting Materials 20260708.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Dispute resolution with the Market Monitor Unit (MMU) affects hydro operators' ability to challenge market-power mitigation decisions, offer validations, and cost-recovery determinations — all of which directly shape revenue and compliance obligations for dispatchable resources in SPP Markets+.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ proposes MIR90-R, which codifies the first-ever dispute resolution process between Market Participants and the MMU, covering three categories: reconsideration of market-monitor decisions, verification of actual costs over $1,000, and validation of mitigated offers. The process includes 30-day deadlines for both MP submissions and MMU decisions, mirrors language from SPP IM's RR 733 (approved by FERC on April 10, 2026), and proposes tariff/protocol amendments in Attachment B Section 4.1 and related sections. For hydropower and especially pumped storage—chronically subject to mitigation of offers and opportunity-cost challenges—this formalizes a previously unavailable recourse mechanism. The MDWG will vote on MIR approval August 11, 2026, with MPEC consideration targeted for either August 25 or November 17, 2026.

documents.title: Informational Only – Interregional Coordination Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Interregional coordination and congestion-management mechanics affect hydro's transmission deliverability and interregional export capability; Energy Storage Resource considerations in market-flow calculation directly touch pumped-storage participation rules; Parallel Flow Visualization and Firm Flow Limits shape how hydro's use-constrained generation is modeled in multi-RTO settlements.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Congestion Management Process (CMP) working group is advancing Freeze Date mechanics for interregional congestion allocation, with next CMPWG meeting July 30, 2026 and CMP Council meeting August 12, 2026. Key workstreams include Energy Storage Resource considerations in market-flow calculation, Firm Flow Limit updates, and evaluation of Parallel Flow Visualization (PFV)—an Eastern Interconnect standard using Generator-to-Load flow concepts and GPS-based prioritization to replace prior Network/Native Load constructs. PFV is being considered as an alternative to Freeze Date for establishing firm market-flow entitlements in M2M settlements. These changes affect how PJM's (and partner RTOs') hydro and pumped-storage resources are modeled for transmission-constraint relief obligations and interregional credit calculations; storage owners in particular should track ESR treatment in market-flow calculation and PFV adoption status, as they determine whether use-constrained generation receives firm-flow priority in congestion-relief protocols.

documents.title: Item 01 1 – Manual 18 Periodic Review And Conforming Changes – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 18 periodic review addresses capacity-market accreditation rules, performance assessments, and supply-resource participation mechanics that directly govern how generation resources—including hydropower and pumped-storage hydro—qualify for and are compensated in PJM's capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 18 Revision 63 (effective September 1, 2026) is a periodic update to PJM's Capacity Market operating procedures covering resource-adequacy frameworks, RPM auction design, demand curves, supply-resource qualification, performance assessments, and settlement. This redline document shows conforming changes to the manual's sections on generation resources (4.2), seasonal capacity performance resources (4.10), non-performance assessment (8.4A), and summer/winter capability testing (8.5–8.6), among others. For hydropower and pumped-storage operators, the most material sections are 4.2 (how generation resources—including hydro—register and submit offers), 4.10 (seasonal capacity performance, which affects run-of-river and weather-dependent hydro accreditation), 8.5 (capability testing rules that apply to synchronous hydro units), and 9.1–9.2 (performance penalties and locational charges). The redline marks technical conforming updates; no vote date or comment deadline is stated in the extracted text.

documents.title: Item 01 2 – Manual 18 Periodic Review And Conforming Changes – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 18 periodic review touches capacity market accreditation rules (AUCAP factors, resource deficiency charges), must-offer exceptions for exports, DER aggregation resource participation, and billing/settlement — all operational obligations for PJM hydro and storage assets.
documents.ai_summary: PJM staff is updating Manual 18 (Capacity Market Operations) to incorporate conforming changes from recent tariff revisions (ER26-980, ER26-455, ER25-783, ER25-2002, ER24-2447) and IMM suggestions. Key changes affecting hydro/storage: correction of PAI rates to use RTO Net CONE rather than LDA Net CONE; removal of environmentally limited resources from winter period capacity resource definitions; clarification of must-offer exception administration for exported generation; new penalty for derate caused by decreases in final Accredited AUCAP factors; and refinements to DER aggregation resource test-failure charges. The periodic review appears to be in final-read phase at the Market Implementation Committee (July 8, 2026 meeting). Most exposed are existing hydro and pumped-storage operators subject to accreditation adjustments and export-transaction approvals; DER-aggregated small hydro faces clarified test-failure billing.

documents.title: Item 02 1 – Economic Load Response Regulation Only – Manual 11 – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This manual revision updates demand-resource regulation participation rules (Economic Load Response Regulation Only) and includes sections on hydropower operation and pumped-storage modeling, both of which affect how hydro and storage assets participate in PJM's ancillary-services markets and how their dispatch is constrained by operational rules.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 11 Revision 137 (effective July 28, 2026) incorporates three FERC-ordered updates: Advance Scheduled Resources Phase 1 (affecting self-scheduling), Generation Self-Scheduling Must-Offer requirements in reserve markets, and Economic Load Response Regulation Only participation rules. The document includes standing sections on Hydropower Operation (9.3) and Pumped Storage Modeling (Attachment B), which define how hydro and PSH operators must schedule, offer, and represent their dispatch constraints in PJM's day-ahead and real-time markets. Pumped-storage owners and hydropower operators must comply with the updated must-offer and self-scheduling requirements when submitting bids; the regulation-market changes may alter the revenue pathway available to regulation-qualified demand resources that compete with hydro for regulation supply. Most exposed are PSH operators managing state-of-charge constraints and hydro operators with variable output; implementation is effective as of July 28, 2026.
