documents.title: Item 04 1 – Offer Verification Exception Request Timeline Definition – Problem Statement
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Defines submission timelines for offer-verification exception requests supporting high-cost offers in PJM, directly affecting how dispatchable resources including hydropower can justify offers above $1,000/MWh and recover make-whole payments when offers are rejected.
documents.ai_summary: PJM proposes to clarify and revise the deadline for submitting exception requests and supporting documentation when offers exceeding $1,000/MWh fail Markets Gateway validation. Currently, Manual 11 Attachment D requires submission by 11:00 AM EPT the next business day—a deadline from 2017 that PJM considers burdensome during extended winter Offer Verification events and outdated given enhanced Markets Gateway functionality. PJM seeks input on a revised timeline that would reduce the administrative load on Market Sellers while maintaining the integrity of the exception process. This directly affects any generator (including hydro and PSH) that must justify high-cost operating or opportunity-cost offers to recover make-whole uplift; a more relaxed timeline lowers operational burden during winter resource-scarcity events when such offers are most likely.

documents.title: Item 04 2 – Offer Verification Exception Request Timeline Definition – Issue Charge
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: After-the-fact make-whole payments are PJM's uplift/make-whole mechanism, a revenue pathway for dispatchable resources (including hydropower) when Offer Verification events occur; this issue charge adjusts the timeline deadline for invoking that recovery, directly affecting the revenue-protection procedures hydro operators rely on.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is revising the deadline timeline in Manual 11, Attachment D for Market Sellers to request after-the-fact make-whole payments following Offer Verification events—a procedure triggered when equipment limitations or extraordinary market conditions prevent full energy output. The timeline, written in 2017, is being modernized to align with PJM's enhanced Markets Gateway functionality, which now allows faster upload and documentation of offer data and supporting materials. Work is assigned to the Market Implementation Committee under Quick Fix (Tier 1) process with a two-month duration and solution target before winter 2026 operations; no tariff changes are in scope. Hydro operators relying on make-whole recovery after unplanned or operational constraints during tight conditions should monitor the revised deadline to ensure timely claims.

documents.title: Item 04 3 – Offer Verification Exception Request Timeline Definition – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Clarifies the timeline and procedural requirements for offer-verification exception requests in PJM's make-whole settlement process — a critical compliance obligation for generation resource owners, including hydro and storage, whose cost-based offers fail automated screens.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is documenting the offer-verification exception-request process timeline in Manual 11, Section 2.3.6.2: generation owners whose resource offers fail the offer screen have 60 calendar days following the operating day to submit an exception request with cost documentation via Markets Gateway, supported by the Market Monitoring Unit. If Markets Gateway is unavailable, owners must email EnergyOfferVerification@pjm.com and MMU_Energy_Offers_Review@monitoringanalytics.com with unit name, operating date/time, cost calculation per Manual 15 (Cost Development Guidelines), and PJM dispatch contact details. This deadline and procedural specification is foundational to how hydro and storage operators defend cost-based offers against failed screens and maintain eligibility for dispatch, LMP setting, and operating-reserve credits — particularly relevant for storage operators and pumped-hydro plants offering complex cost structures that may not fit automated validation logic.

documents.title: Item 04 4 – Offer Verification Exception Request Timeline Definition – PJM Proposal
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Offer verification and make-whole procedures directly affect the revenue compensation mechanism for generation resources (including hydropower) committed by PJM when cost-based offers exceed $1,000/MWh; changes to the submission timeline and process documentation alter compliance obligations and settlement deadlines.
documents.ai_summary: PJM proposes updating Manual 11, Section 2.3.6.2 and Attachment D to clarify and extend the deadline for submitting offer-verification exception requests in support of incremental offers exceeding $1,000/MWh from 11:00 EPT on the business day following the operating day to 60 calendar days following the operating day. The change aligns the exception-request deadline with other PJM settlement deadlines and reflects enhancements to the Markets Gateway fuel-price exceptions functionality, deferring to email backup if tool outages occur. Generation owners (including any committed hydroelectric or pumped-storage units) will gain substantially more time to review bills, gather supporting documentation, and submit requests before the 60-day window closes, reducing the risk of forfeiting make-whole credits; the 60-day deadline also provides PJM, the MMU, and markets with certainty on settlement closure. Most affected are owners of committed units with cost-based offers that fail the automated screening and require after-the-fact verification to set LMP or receive operating-reserve credits.

documents.title: Item 04 5 – Offer Verification Exception Request Timeline Definition – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Modifies PJM's offer verification exception request timeline for high-cost offers (>$1000/MWh), directly affecting when hydropower and other Market Sellers must submit documentation supporting make-whole payment claims — a key revenue-recovery mechanism for use-limited and dispatchable resources.
documents.ai_summary: PJM proposes updating PJM Manual 11 sections 2.3.6.2 and Attachment D to establish a uniform 60-calendar-day deadline for exception requests supporting offers exceeding $1000/MWh, replacing the current fragmented timeline. The change aligns administrative procedures with Markets Gateway's enhanced functionality and aims to provide Market Sellers time to recover from extended winter events while establishing certainty for closure. MIC approval path is July/August 2026; MRC first read is July 28, 2026, and voting is August 19, 2026 — the effective date. Hydropower operators and other dispatchable resources relying on make-whole uplift to cover scarcity-event costs will need to meet the new 60-day filing window to substantiate high-cost offers, affecting their revenue-recovery process during stress periods.

documents.title: Item 05 2 – Manual 6 Conforming Changes – Presentation

documents.title: Item 06 – Regulation Redesign Phase 2 Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Regulation market redesign splits PJM's bidirectional regulation product into RegUp and RegDn products with separate offers, clearing prices, and settlement — directly affecting how hydropower and other dispatchable resources bid and capture revenue in the regulation ancillary-services market.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is implementing Regulation Redesign Phase 2, effective December 1, 2026, which bifurcates the single bidirectional regulation product into two separate products: RegUp (above zero crossing) and RegDn (below zero crossing), each with its own requirement MW, clearing price, offer parameters, performance metrics, and settlement. Hydropower and pumped-storage resources must submit RegUp and RegDn offers and parameters in the Markets Gateway sandbox by overdue deadline and complete signal-conversion testing to participate; PJM is holding a virtual open house on July 30, 2026, and market open is scheduled for November 25, 2026. This redesign increases the complexity and granularity (from hourly to 30-minute) of regulation participation, requiring hydro owners to develop separate bidding strategies for each directional product and validate system integrations before go-live.

documents.title: Item 07 1 – Periodic Review Of Default CONE And Acr Values
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CONE and ACR values directly determine the Minimum Offer Price Rule (MOPR) floor bids and Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC) that apply to all capacity-market resources in PJM, including hydropower and pumped storage; changes to these values alter the competitiveness and profitability of hydro's participation in the capacity auction.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is updating its default Gross CONE (Cost of New Entry) and Gross ACR (Avoidable Cost Rate) values for delivery years 2030/2031, which feed directly into MOPR floors for new resources and MSOC offer caps for existing resources under Attachment DD §§ 5.14(h-2) and 6.4. Brattle Group and Sargent & Lundy are conducting the analysis; PJM will present draft values to the MIC in July 2026, with final values going to the MRC and MC for an advisory vote in August, and tariff filing at FERC by October 1, 2026. The update includes tax-law adjustments (100% bonus depreciation reinstatement) and revised overnight-capital and fixed-O&M assumptions from EIA 2026 and the 2025 Quadrennial Review. For hydropower and pumped storage operators, the revised Gross ACR directly impacts the offer cap applied to their existing units; a lower ACR narrows the band within which they can bid and may compress margins, while higher ACR provides headroom. The preliminary Gross CONE values span from $298–$4,403/MW-day depending on technology and delivery year, and Battery Energy Storage is preliminarily set at $502–$696/MW-day, which establishes a competitive benchmark for long-duration storage against which PSH will be evaluated for capacity qualification. Deadline for FERC filing is October 1, 2026.

documents.title: Item 09 2 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Work Plan
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: PJM is designing pre-day-ahead resource scheduling rules that will affect how hydro and storage resources submit offers and manage dispatch flexibility before energy-market clearing — a foundational change to offer/dispatch timing that touches dispatch obligations and potentially the revenue capture window for flexible resources.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Market Implementation Committee is developing a Phase 2 work plan for 'Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market,' a multi-stage design initiative running from July 2025 through August 2026. The work plan charts progression from first read and approval through interest identification, design-component development, option and package narrowing, and final vote. The initiative addresses how resources (including hydro and pumped storage) will schedule and offer into PJM markets before day-ahead clearing — a fundamental change to the offer window and dispatch-instruction timing. Pumped-storage operators especially will need to track whether pre-DA scheduling rules affect charging-energy treatment, state-of-charge management, and the ability to lock in flexibility commitments ahead of real-time; the phases through early 2026 will determine whether storage faces tighter scheduling constraints or gains more visibility for day-ahead dispatch planning.

documents.title: Item 10 1 – Energy Storage Resource Model Enhancements – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's ESR model design directly determines how pumped-storage hydro and battery storage compete for capacity accreditation, ancillary-services revenue, and energy-market dispatch—the core mechanisms by which storage owners recover fixed costs and optimize multi-hour operating decisions.
documents.ai_summary: PJM presented its current Energy Storage Resource (ESR) participation model, covering opt-in market participation, state-of-charge tracking, ancillary-services eligibility, cost-offer development, and capacity accreditation. The ESR model is a self-schedule framework (no PJM commitment); resources offer as one continuous resource with charge/discharge modes and optional SOC parameters, but PJM does not track SOC in dispatch and offers very limited opportunity-cost recovery for forward energy-limited constraints—a structural gap that disadvantages use-limited resources like pumped storage competing against batteries. Capacity accreditation uses class-based ELCC (4, 6, 8, 10-hour durations), and PSH with shorter physical duration can derate to match a longer ELCC class, but there is no explicit mechanism for PSH to reflect seasonal or hydrological variation in available energy. This presentation supports an active stakeholder issue charge on ESR model enhancements; no vote or comment deadline is stated in the slides, but the mention of a published position paper and ongoing stakeholder process signals that PJM is revisiting the model as the battery storage pipeline grows (8.4 GW under construction or in Cycle 2, 66.5 GW proposed).

documents.ai_summary: PJM's Market Implementation Committee work plan (as of July 2026) charts active and periodic reviews affecting hydro and storage operators. Active issues include PJM Energy Storage Resource Model Enhancements (status unclear, ongoing), Flexible Resource Lost Opportunity Cost Eligibility (touching how storage's energy opportunity costs are credited in markets), and Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day-Ahead Energy Market Phase 2 (begun September 2025). The document is an internal scheduling roadmap; no specific proposals, deadlines for stakeholder comment, or vote dates are stated, but it flags the subjects and timelines under active development.

title: Resource Adequacy Subcommittee (RASC) - July 8, 2026

documents.title: 20260708 RASC Item 01c Minutes 20260520.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO is updating resource accreditation frameworks for solar (affecting capacity-RA and accreditation precedent for variable resources), discussing CONE reference-technology retention for capacity markets, and developing PRMR allocation and LOLE adjustment methodologies — all mechanisms that establish accreditation and capacity-valuation precedents applicable to hydro and pumped-storage accreditation, offer development, and adequacy-study participation.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Resource Adequacy Subcommittee (May 20, 2026) finalized Module E-2 tariff revisions for new LSE resource-adequacy requirements and advanced two material accreditation proposals: (1) a shift in solar Non-GADS Template accreditation hours (PY27-28) to align with observed reliability-risk windows rather than fixed windows, maintaining existing accreditation percentages (Summer/Fall/Spring 50%, Winter 5%) but adjusting timing, with no tariff change required and stakeholder feedback closing by stated dates; and (2) continued development of PRMR allocation design and LOLE capacity-adjustment methodology with results to be shared at the LSE/LRZ level. The solar accreditation shift is a stepping stone toward a longer-term Dynamic Loss-of-Load (DLOL) design. These accreditation mechanics—particularly the use of observed risk-hour timing and the movement away from fixed templates—directly affect how variable resources and seasonal/peaking-capable resources like pumped storage are credited in adequacy showings; hydro owners should monitor whether similar risk-timing adjustments are proposed for hydro and whether DLOL eventually treats all dispatchable and storage resources under a unified framework that could disadvantage long-duration assets. Action items on PRMR results and volatility-analysis methodology remain open.

documents.title: 20260708 RASC Item 03b BPM-011-r35 Resource Adequacy_Redline.docx
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's Resource Adequacy Business Practices Manual (BPM-011-r35) governs accreditation methodology, seasonal capacity credit, must-offer requirements, and RA compliance processes that directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are credited and obligated in MISO's resource-adequacy regime.
documents.ai_summary: This is MISO's annual-review revision (r35, effective September 30, 2026) of BPM-011 (Resource Adequacy manual), the governing document for MISO's seasonal RA accreditation, UCAP/ISAC processes, demand-response and storage qualification, zonal deliverability, and compliance settlement. The document title indicates it is in redline (showing changes), and the revision summary notes edits are pending final summarization. Key prior revisions (r27–r34) addressed seasonal accreditation implementation (FERC ER22-495), electric storage resource accreditation (r26), intermittent-resource UCAP qualification, demand-resource testing and registration, and GVTC (Guaranteed Vendor Test Conditions) language for hydro and run-of-river resources (r10). Hydro owners participating in MISO must track this manual for changes to how seasonal capacity credit, zonal deliverability benefit, run-of-river accreditation, and storage (including pumped-storage) qualification are calculated and verified; the 2026 annual review may contain material changes to these mechanisms, but the summary of edits is incomplete in this excerpt.

documents.title: 20260708 RASC Item 01b Agenda.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This agenda covers three capacity/resource-adequacy items directly affecting hydro and storage accreditation — solar accreditation for PY27-28, external resource accreditation revision, and a 24-hour lead-time accreditation proposal — plus PRMR allocation methodology that shapes how reserves (a hydro strength) are procured and compensated.
documents.ai_summary: MISO Resource Adequacy Subcommittee meeting scheduled for July 8, 2026, with five substantive items: (1) Solar accreditation for planning years 2027-28, (2) External resource accreditation revision (tied to RASC-2019-2 and RASC-2024-5), (3) a 24-hour lead-time accreditation proposal (RASC-2026-1), (4) PRMR (Planning Reserve Margin Requirement) allocation (RASC-2020-4 and 2019-2), and (5) BPM-011 annual review. Items 3, 5, and 6 request feedback; items 6 and 8 are potential voting items. The 24-hour lead-time rule and external resource accreditation overhaul will reshape how hydro and pumped storage compete for RA value alongside solar and batteries; PRMR reallocation directly affects the reserve procurements that hydro supplies. No decision dates are listed, but the feedback-request flag on items 3 and 6 signals imminent drafting or voting.

documents.ai_summary: MISO has five active FERC dockets affecting resource-adequacy rules and hydro participation: (1) EL26-70-000 (large load accommodation) — FERC directed MISO to file an informational report on generation adequacy for large loads and interconnection speed by July 20, 2026; (2) ER26-2974-000 (deliverability accreditation) — MISO proposed tariff revisions to account for deliverability in SAC capacity value when implementing Schedules 53A and 53B in Planning Year 2028/2029, with Module A effective September 1, 2026; (3) ER26-2706-000 (LOLE capacity adjustment) — MISO proposed methodology changes to the LOLE study with effective date August 1, 2026; (4) ER26-1882-000 (load-modifying resource penalties) — MISO amended proposed penalty rules for LMRs that fail to respond to Scheduling Instructions, requesting effective date of June 1, 2027, after FERC deficiency letter on May 22, 2026; (5) ER25-1886-000 (demand response and emergency resources) — FERC accepted MISO's reform tariff on June 18, 2026 with compliance filing due July 20, 2026, effective September 1, 2027. Hydro owners are affected by deliverability rules that determine their capacity accreditation value, LOLE study changes that drive RA requirements, and demand-response/LMR rules that define competitive adjacencies and performance penalties.

documents.title: 20260708 RASC Item 01d Management Plan.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a RASC management and work-plan document that governs which resource-adequacy topics MISO will address; it directly impacts hydro accreditation rules, external-resource participation (relevant to run-of-river and PSH), and capacity accreditation reform—all core to hydro revenue and compliance.
documents.ai_summary: The RASC Management Plan schedules MISO's resource-adequacy work through Q4 2027, with three items directly material to hydro owners: (1) External Resource Accreditation (RASC-2024-5)—covering accreditation of hydro and storage resources that connect via HVDC or other transmission—appears on the July 8, August 19, and November 11, 2026 agendas; (2) Accreditation Reform BPM Language (RASC-2019-9) is scheduled for July and September 2026, addressing how accreditation calculations apply to use-limited resources like hydro; (3) 24-Hour Lead Time in Accreditation (RASC-2026-1, marked as requesting feedback) appears July 8, 2026 and may alter how hydro must pre-commit to RA showings. The plan also flags CONE Reference Technology updates and PRMR Allocation (RASC-2020-4, RASC-2019-2) as recurring items. Most exposed are pumped-storage operators and external hydro resources (particularly those connected via HVDC) seeking RA accreditation under new rules; next engagement points are July 8, August 19, and September 30, 2026 meetings.

documents.title: 20260708 RASC Item 02b LOLEWG Status Report.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) modeling directly affects hydro and storage resource accreditation under both current ELCC and future DLOL frameworks, which determines capacity value and RA contributions; wind profile enhancements and cross-class accreditation impacts alter the competitive accreditation landscape for storage and thermal resources.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation Working Group reported advances in wind profile modeling (transitioning to weather-conditioned synthetic profiles) and is conducting PY 2027-2028 LOLE and Capacity/Energy Limited (CIL/CEL) studies. Wind accreditation under the enhanced profiles shows increases in Summer, Winter, and Spring ELCC values; critically, shifts in risk-hour distribution under the future Direct Loss of Load (DLOL) framework will cross-impact accreditation for storage and thermal resources. MISO requested stakeholder feedback on 2027-2028 CIL/CEL study input files by July 7, 2026, and preliminary results will be presented by September 2026. Load growth is unprecedented and calling into question load-forecast uncertainty assumptions in the LOLE process. Pumped storage operators face material changes to seasonal capacity accreditation as MISO refines its RA modeling; the shift from ELCC to DLOL fundamentally redefines how storage is credited during critical risk hours, most likely reducing seasonal/winter accreditation benefits for use-limited resources like PSH.

documents.title: 20260708 RASC Item 08 PRMR Allocation (RASC-2020-4 and 2019-2).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: MISO's shift from forecasted load (CPDf) to three-year-lookback settled load for Planning Reserve Margin Requirement (PRMR) allocation changes how reserve obligations are assigned to Load Serving Entities, directly affecting the revenue adequacy and resource-procurement obligations of hydro and PSH owners whose output and strategic flexibility are valued in MISO's resource-adequacy and Planning Resource Auction frameworks.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes replacing Coincident Peak Demand forecast (CPDf)-based PRMR allocation with a Resource Adequacy Hour Demand (RAHD) metric using three-year-lookback settled load during Tier 2 RA hours, weighted by effective margin. The proposal aligns PRMR allocation with MISO's updated Resource Accreditation reform and the DLOL framework, tying reserve requirements to actual historical load behavior during highest-risk hours rather than peak forecasts. Hydro and PSH operators benefit when this mechanism improves accuracy of LSE reserve obligations: precise allocation signals encourage load-serving entities to procure capacity-constrained products (like the ramping and storage flexibility hydro and pumped storage supply) at times of actual scarcity rather than all-hours forecasts. MISO requests stakeholder feedback by July 24, 2026, with initial tariff redlines due in August 2026, final redlines in September 2026, and FERC filing targeted for end of 2026.

documents.title: 20260708 RASC Item 05 Solar Accreditation for PY27-28 (RASC-2019-2).pdf
documents.filename: 20260708 RASC Item 05 Solar Accreditation for PY27-28 (RASC-2019-2).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's decision to maintain fixed solar accreditation windows for PY27-28 and defer to a Direct Loss of Load (DLOL) based accreditation model in PY28-29 affects how non-dispatchable resources are credited in capacity/resource-adequacy calculations, setting a methodological precedent that influences how hydro's own accreditation and capacity value are benchmarked and may be reformed across MISO.
documents.ai_summary: MISO decided to maintain current fixed solar accreditation hours for planning years 2027-28 despite stakeholder concern that observed peak reliability-risk hours for solar have shifted later in the day as installed capacity has grown. The decision defers proposed accreditation updates pending full implementation of Direct Loss of Load (DLOL) based accreditation in PY28-29, when solar class accreditation will be based on critical hours in the loss-of-load-expectation (LOLE) model rather than fixed hour windows. This matters to hydro owners because DLOL methodologies (which use observed loss-of-load events to define accreditation windows) are being adopted to address the divergence between fixed-hour accreditation and actual system scarcity; hydro's own accreditation framework may face similar pressure to move from traditional ELCC windows toward performance-driven models, and precedents set for solar will inform how flexibility and run-of-river resources are credited in MISO resource adequacy.

documents.title: 20260708 RASC Item 04 Liaison Report.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Large-load interconnection and generation-adequacy planning (strategic option affecting hydro's value through load growth), plus MISO's resource-adequacy compliance calendar and demand-response accreditation rules that directly affect hydro and storage operators' seasonal obligations and capacity claims.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's RASC liaison report covers four core items: (1) a PRA calendar reminder for Planning Year 2026–2027 with ICAP Deferral deadlines Aug 31, Nov 30, and Feb 26, 2027 for LSEs and resource owners; (2) a FERC Order to Show Cause (EL26-70-000, June 18, 2026) directing MISO to improve large-load interconnection and report by July 20, 2026 on generation adequacy and queue acceleration; (3) a June 18, 2026 FERC acceptance of MISO's DR/ER tariff reforms with compliance filing due July 20, 2026 on emergency-resource switching, baseline backfilling, and demand-resource capability calculations; and (4) announcements on market-data-exchange transitions and workshops (including a Large Load Working Group July 16, Locational Marginal Emissions workshop July 17, and RI initiatives workshop July 29). The large-load FERC order and MISO's generation-adequacy reporting obligation create a material strategic window: hydro owners need awareness that MISO is under commission mandate to accelerate interconnection of large loads (data centers, industrial) and that generation-adequacy studies will now explicitly inform queue timing and resource-procurement urgency — scarcity signals that can raise hydro's energy and capacity value. The seasonal ICAP deadlines directly govern reserve accreditation; the DR/ER reforms affect how demand-resource competition for capacity-market share is structured. Most exposure for pumped-storage and conventional hydro in resource-adequacy portfolios relying on seasonal capacity credits.

documents.title: 20260708 RASC Item 07 24 Hour Lead Time in Accreditation (RASC-2026-1).pdf
documents.filename: 20260708 RASC Item 07 24 Hour Lead Time in Accreditation (RASC-2026-1).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO proposes three alternative rules for how offline resources' capacity accreditation should be calculated based on lead time, which directly affects how thermal and hydro peaking units can be credited toward resource-adequacy showings during emergencies — the key revenue mechanism for dispatchable generation.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes three solutions to close a perceived gap in its 24-hour lead-time accreditation rule for offline resources. The problem: thermal generators increased their start-time lead times approaching Winter Storms Fern, Enzo, and Heather, rendering 3.1 GW unavailable for commitment in the Day-Ahead Market and Forward Reliability Assessment Commitment (FRAC) processes while still receiving full accreditation value, despite contributing to emergency deployment of LMRs. Solution 1 (12-hour max lead time) aligns offline-resource accreditation with the Operating Margin calculation but risks uneconomic commitments. Solution 2 (zero availability for lead-time increases within 48 hours of the operating day) would penalize changes near emergencies but allow longer-lead resources to participate in multi-day FRAC. Solution 3 (sliding scale: full availability at 4 hours, declining 5% per hour to zero at 24+ hours) offers graduated incentives. MISO will select a recommended solution in August 2026, file revised tariff at FERC in October 2026. Stakeholders should comment by July 22, 2026. Pumped-storage and conventional-hydro operators should monitor because the same accreditation rules govern their RA value; any tightening of lead-time rules that penalizes start-time flexibility, or that creates asymmetries between thermal and hydro accreditation, could compress the capacity credit PSH and peaking hydro claim during peak-scarcity periods.

documents.title: 20260708 RASC Item 06 External Resources Accreditation (RASC-2024-5 and 2019-2).pdf
documents.filename: 20260708 RASC Item 06 External Resources Accreditation (RASC-2024-5 and 2019-2).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO is reforming accreditation methodology for external resources (pseudo-tied and non-pseudo-tied), which directly affects how external hydropower (including pumped storage participating across seams) receives capacity-value accreditation and participates in the Planning Resource Auction—a core revenue and participation mechanism for hydro owners exporting into MISO or participating via external-resource schedules.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes to accredit pseudo-tied external resources using Schedule 53A (real-time offer data, 3-year lookback, 80/20 weighting between Tier 2 RA and Tier 1 non-RA hours) and non-pseudo-tied external resources using day-ahead e-Tag availability (same weighting, no outage exemptions, 3-year lookback). MISO is also eliminating the Use-Limited Resources category, redirecting accreditation of all use-limited assets (including run-of-river and storage hydro) into the Schedule 53A and Schedule 53B frameworks. The proposal shifts external hydro accreditation away from ad-hoc techniques toward seasonal-availability-aligned methods, but non-pseudo-tied resources (which may include many Northwest hydro exports) lose outage exemptions and rely on day-ahead e-Tag offers rather than real-time operational data—potentially reducing accreditation for resources whose actual availability exceeds their day-ahead self-commitments. Stakeholder comments are due July 26, 2026; MISO targets FERC filing in Q4 2026 or Q1 2027 with effective date Planning Year 2029/30 or beyond. Most exposed: pumped storage and run-of-river hydro exporting from adjacent balancing authorities via external-resource schedules; large reservoir hydro with seasonal energy constraints that may be miscaptured by day-ahead e-Tag methodology.

title: WMS Meeting
meeting_hydro_reason: ERCOT's Wholesale Market Subcommittee addresses energy price formation, ancillary services, capacity mechanisms, and storage participation rules that set cross-market precedent relevant to how hydro and PSH compete in other RTOs.

documents.title: WMS Meeting Agenda 2026-07-08
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Agenda previews ERCOT's July 2026 treatment of ancillary services methodology (2027 AS framework) and multiple protocol revisions affecting energy-storage participation (ERS Enhancements, Advanced Grid Support Service, Generation Firming Program) — market-design precedents NHA monitors across RTOs for implications on storage revenue and dispatch rules.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Wholesale Market Subcommittee agenda for July 8, 2026 includes review of 2027 Ancillary Services Methodology (presenter Luis Hinojosa, 6/17/26) and votes on three storage-adjacent NPRRs: NPRR1337 (ERS Enhancements), NPRR1278 (Advanced Grid Support Service as ancillary service), and NPRR1328 (Generation Firming Program). Four other tabled items touch dispatch and demand-response design. No vote dates or comment deadlines are stated in the agenda itself; the substantive work occurs in working-group meetings (WMWG, CMWG, SAWG) listed as concurrent sessions. This agenda is a planning document with no immediate action deadlines for external stakeholders, but the three storage-related NPRRs signal ERCOT's evolution on how storage—including pumped hydro—participates in ancillary service procurement and grid-support products, a design question NHA tracks across all RTOs. Most relevant to energy-storage operators (batteries and PSH) considering ERCOT participation.

documents.title: 02. WMS Agenda 202606708
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT agenda covers ancillary services methodology, advanced grid support service design, energy storage resource (ESR) participation rules, and price-formation mechanisms (System Lambda capping, reliability deployment pricing) — cross-market design elements that hydro-industry analysts track for comparison to hydro-rich RTOs and for precedent on storage and flexible-resource participation.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Wholesale Market Subcommittee meeting on July 8, 2026, agendizes several protocol revisions affecting energy storage and ancillary services participation. NPRR1278 would establish Advanced Grid Support Service as a formal ancillary service; NPRR1337 addresses Energy Resource Supplement (ERS) enhancements; and multiple open action items track ancillary service duration parameters, proxy offer floors, and System Lambda capping (NPRR1290, deferred to 2027 unless urgency arises). The subcommittee is also reviewing post-RTC reliability deployment pricing, transparency in DOE 202(c) emergency authority use, and performance metrics for Ancillary Service Demand Curves. These design choices—particularly ERS availability requirements, ancillary service duration rules, and price-adder mechanisms—set precedent for how other RTOs (including those with pumped storage and hydropower) structure storage and flexible-resource compensation; ERCOT's approach to ESR participation and grid-support service crediting is increasingly referenced in cross-market discussions of storage-market design.

documents.title: 04. 2027_AS_Methodology_WMS_070826
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT is refining its 2027 ancillary-services (Regulation, Responsive Reserve, and probabilistic ECRS/Non-Spin) procurement methodology, which directly affects how reserves are required and priced — a cross-market comparison point for hydro-rich RTOs on frequency-response obligation design, reserve-procurement risk criteria, and handling of variable load and renewable ramping.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT is reviewing its 2027 Ancillary Services methodology for Regulation, Responsive Reserve Service (RRS), Contingency Reserve Service (ECRS), and Non-Spinning Reserve. Key changes under evaluation: (1) potential new adjustment factors for Regulation to address large-load ramping extremes (up ramps 41–51 MW average, down ramps −44 to −61 MW, with outliers exceeding ±400 MW); (2) refinement of the 2026 probabilistic framework for ECRS and Non-Spin to test sensitivity to risk inputs, risk credits (currently 60% night, 25% day), convergence criteria (1-in-X violation thresholds), and net-load forecast error distributions; (3) evaluation of look-ahead period (up to 6 hours), solar adjustment logic including potential shift to Gross Real-Time Power Potential (GRPP), and adjacent-hour smoothing. RRS methodology is unchanged pending NERC's 2027 BAL-003 IFRO assessment (not yet published; analysis uses 2026 minimum of 1,377 MW). WMS vote scheduled August 5, 2026; PUC approval November–December 2026. This document exemplifies how RTOs are adapting reserve procurement to accommodate large-load volatility and renewable variability — methodologies that increasingly influence bidding strategies and revenue expectations for fast-response and flexible resources including hydropower.

documents.title: Item 05 – Ugi Supplemental Projects
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large customer interconnection request (500 MW load) triggers transmission planning and interconnection deliverability work that affects hydro resource dispatch capability and interregional transfer pathways in PJM.
documents.ai_summary: UGI Transmission proposes a 230 kV supply solution for a 500 MW customer load in Newport Township, Pennsylvania (250 MW in Q3 2029, 250 MW in Q1 2030), comprising a 4-mile tap line, new 230 kV switchyard, 0.5-mile customer tap, and Mountain Substation upgrades. Total estimated cost is $107.2 M with in-service date of March 29, 2030. The project is in engineering stage; stakeholders may submit comments on this solution within 10 days, and a "Do No Harm" analysis must be completed before the solution is selected for integration into PJM's RTEP. Load growth of this magnitude and configuration affects available transmission capacity for existing resources including hydropower, and the supplemental interconnection project establishes precedent for how PJM accommodates large new loads in the transmission footprint.

documents.title: Item 06 – Atsi Supplemental Projects
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Document addresses a routine FirstEnergy transmission zone operational constraint (breaker outage on a 345 kV line in the ATSI zone) within PJM's M-3 supplemental planning process; contains no hydro-relevant revenue mechanisms, operating obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: Item 07 – Aps Supplemental Projects
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Two large new customer connection requests (1000 MW and 368 MW loads) and interconnection-driven transmission expansion projects affect hydro deliverability, interregional transfer capability, and the value of transmission-connected generation in PJM.
documents.ai_summary: FirstEnergy (APS transmission zone) is advancing two supplemental transmission needs for new customer connections — a 1000 MW retail load (in-service 12/31/2032, need APS-2026-041) and a 368 MW wholesale load (in-service 4/30/2032, need APS-2026-039) — both near existing 230 kV substations in the Doubs/Sage/Ballenger area. The document also presents solutions for the Rhodes Lane–Yukon 500 kV line (terminal equipment upgrade, $5.45M, in-service 5/12/2028) and the Shingletown 230 kV substation reconfiguration (bus conversion, $19.11M, in-service 6/25/2029) to address operational constraints. Stakeholders must comment within 10 days of the 7/7/2026 meeting to influence the next phase of FirstEnergy's M-3 planning process. For hydro owners, load growth of this scale (1.3+ GW) expands the value of existing generation and storage connected to PJM's backbone, and transmission uprates improve export capability from the region; conversely, concentrated large-load interconnection can create local transmission bottlenecks that advantage nearby generation or storage. All projects are in early conceptual phase (models, costs, and in-service dates subject to change).

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large-load interconnection (1000 MW customer service request in Kentucky) directly affects transmission deliverability and represents a strategic option that increases scarcity value of existing hydro resources capable of serving the region.

This triggers PJM's M-3 transmission-planning process, moving through needs and solutions meetings. The supplemental project is in the Hanging Rock, OH/Baker, KY transmission zone (Need AEP-2026-AP007). Stakeholders may comment within 10 days after this 07/07/2026 meeting; comments feed into the subsequent solutions and local-plan phases before submission to PJM's Regional Transmission Expansion Plan (RTEP). Large-load interconnection at this scale and location affects transmission congestion patterns and reserve margins in the Mid-Atlantic region; hydro owners with interconnection points or deliverability in the AEP zone should monitor the selected solution and its impact on locational price signals and transmission-congestion costs.

documents.title: Item 12 – Dayton Supplemental Projects
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large customer load growth (up to 1.3 GW) requiring transmission expansion directly affects hydro deliverability, interregional transfer capability, and the strategic value of flexible capacity at existing hydro plants to serve incremental load.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Transmission Expansion Advisory Committee approved supplemental transmission solutions for three large customer-load requests in the Dayton transmission zone, totaling approximately 2.2 GW of new load by 2032 (phased from 35 MW in 2026 through 1.3 GW by 2032). The projects include new 345 kV transmission lines, substation expansions, and reactive-support equipment (capacitors and STATCOMs) with total estimated cost of approximately $590M and in-service dates ranging from October 2026 through January 2031. Load growth of this magnitude in a single transmission zone increases scarcity-driven energy prices during peak periods and raises the marginal value of every existing hydro MW's ability to ramp quickly and serve demand at times of system stress; it also affects the economic case for interregional transmission and storage-as-transmission solutions. Stakeholders must submit comments within 10 days of the July 7, 2026 meeting.

documents.title: Item 14 – Deok Supplemental Projects
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Large customer load interconnection (500 MW + 300 MW expansion near Trenton, OH) triggers transmission planning under PJM's M-3 process; load growth at specific nodes can alter locational scarcity signals and the economic value of dispatchable hydro serving that zone, and transmission solutions selected may create or foreclose storage-as-transmission opportunities.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's DEOK Transmission Zone is evaluating supplemental transmission projects driven by a customer service request: a 500 MW load near Trenton in Butler County, Ohio (to be online around 2026–2027) with a planned 300 MW expansion in 2028 at the same location. This is at the Needs Meeting stage of the M-3 process (held July 7, 2026); stakeholders must submit comments within 10 days. The transmission solution(s) to be proposed at the Solutions Meeting will determine whether new or upgraded transmission capacity, demand-side alternatives, or storage-as-transmission options are evaluated. Load growth in an existing transmission constraint can raise locational energy and reserve prices for suppliers in that zone — potentially benefiting hydro operators with capacity to serve the constraint — but the selected solution may also create new transmission corridors that displace locational premiums or enable new competitive resources.

documents.title: Item 15 – DOM Supplemental Projects
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Transmission planning directly affects hydro deliverability and revenue; load-growth assumptions (especially data-center load in Prince William County) alter the value of existing hydro capacity and flexibility, and reactive-power/voltage-support needs in constrained load pockets signal opportunities for hydro to supply ancillary services.
documents.ai_summary: PJM/Dominion presented supplemental transmission projects to the Transmission Expansion Advisory Committee on July 7, 2026, addressing operational bottlenecks and load growth in Dominion's Virginia transmission zone. Key drivers include: (1) chronic low-voltage conditions and reactive-power scarcity in the Prince William County load pocket during outages—currently mitigated by a temporary mobile capacitor bank—exacerbated by continued data-center load growth (DOM-2026-0026); (2) multiple do-no-harm violations triggered by new customer interconnections requiring transformer upgrades and line rebuilds in the Meadowville, James Hill, Tropical, and Orrock load areas (projects budgeted $17.5M–$212M, mostly in-service Q4 2031); and (3) two smaller distribution upgrades in Herndon and Prince William County serving non-data-center load by end-2027/2028. The Meadowville project (DOM-2019-0021, DOM-2024-0022/23/24) represents the most significant constraint, with a 300+ MW N-1-1 contingency violation by 2029 requiring new substations and dual 230 kV lines. Hydro owners should monitor whether these transmission investments will increase the scarcity value of voltage support and reactive-power reserves that reservoir hydro can supply during shoulder months and peak periods in a load-constrained region; conversely, transmission relief may reduce ancillary-service revenue in the zone.

documents.title: Informational Only – 2026 RTEP Assumptions Feedback

documents.ai_summary: Dominion Energy Virginia and FirstEnergy submit joint feedback to PJM on 2026 RTEP assumptions, urging (1) execution-focused generation modeling that prioritizes dispatchable resources with site control, permitting progress, and fuel contracts over speculative capacity, and (2) maintenance of a 7–10-year transmission planning horizon aligned with generation development timelines. Both companies emphasize that large, clustered load growth—especially data centers and electrification—must be directly incorporated into planning assumptions with scenario-based approaches and early state/local stakeholder engagement. The letters stress that planning must account for real-world siting, permitting, outage, and supply-chain constraints. These recommendations are informational feedback to PJM's planning process; no vote or deadline is stated in the document.

documents.title: Item 02 – Generation Deactivation Notification Update

documents.ai_summary: PJM's Generation Deactivation Notification Update reports Q2 2026 deactivation notices: two units at West Lorain (50 MW and 51 MW, ATSI zone) with notification received June 19, 2026 and requested deactivation date of June 1, 2030. PJM states it does not approve deactivations but identifies reliability concerns; when adverse impacts are found, it requests the owner extend operations, with the option to file a Cost of Service Recovery Rate (CSRR) at FERC or accept a Deactivation Avoidable Cost Credit (DACC) under the tariff. Reliability analysis is in progress for both units. For hydro owners, deactivations of fossil generation in transmission-constrained zones reduce competing supply and can enhance locational pricing and scarcity signals; this report is part of PJM's standing deactivation-tracking process with no near-term deadline, but hydro operators monitor these announcements to assess congestion relief and RA adequacy outlook.

documents.ai_summary: PJM UGI transmission zone is processing a supplemental project (Need UGI-2026-0003) to serve a 500 MW customer requesting service at 230 kV in Newport Township, with a phased in-service schedule: 250 MW by Q3 2029 and an additional 250 MW by Q1 2030. The proposed solution comprises approximately $107.2 M in transmission investment, including a 4-mile 230 kV line tap, Newport Switching Station, Mountain Substation upgrades, with projected full in-service date of March 29, 2030 and current status in engineering phase. Stakeholders have until 10 days after this TEAC meeting (by July 17, 2026) to submit comments; the project then enters the "Do No Harm" (DNH) analysis phase before being submitted to PJM's RTEP process. PJM-interconnected hydro owners and pumped-storage operators relying on UGI-zone transmission access should monitor this supplemental project's progress and environmental/operational constraints during the DNH and Local Plan phases.

documents.title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A01.a.ii - Draft June 9-11, 2026 Joint Markets and Reliability Committees Meeting Minutes
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Capacity Auction Reforms—Seasonal Accreditation (CAR-SA) directly reshapes how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are accredited and valued in ISO-NE's capacity market, affecting RA showings, auction timing, seasonal MRIC-based accreditation, and revenue recognition under a proposed transition glide path (2-3 years); also introduces winter gas-constraint impacts on capacity procurement.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE introduced a potential two-year transition mechanism for its Capacity Auction Reforms—Seasonal Accreditation (CAR-SA) proposal, which shifts resource accreditation from current Qualified Capacity (QC) values to seasonal Marginal Reliability Impact Capacity (MRIC) values. During transition, resources would sell a weighted average of current and MRIC-based accreditation; the full CAR-SA design includes auction seasonality, auction-timing changes, and a winter gas constraint. The Committee raised concerns about equity across resource types (some resources face larger derate than others), lack of concrete year-by-year formulas, and treatment of resources with seasonal asymmetry, higher MRIC than QC, or changing MRIC year to year. The ISO clarified it does not plan to delay the core seasonal framework or deactivation changes, and offered to provide impact analysis and cost information; stakeholders broadly supported a transition for planning certainty but demanded transparent mechanics before final CAR-SA rules are adopted. No comment deadline or vote date was stated in these minutes.

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE finalized post-implementation adjustments to the Day-Ahead Ancillary Services market, including a tariff floor for the DA A/S Strike Price based on distillate-fired combustion turbine cost characteristics (with regional SOx, NOx, CO2 emissions included), reduction of the Forecast Energy Requirement Demand Quantity by expected uncleared wind and solar, a $3/MWh floor on the DA A/S Impact Test Threshold, and updates to NCPC credit calculations for imports. The Non-Performance Factor is set to 115% as of May 1, 2026. The Committee voted to approve these tariff revisions without stated opposition, and implementation follows from this June 9–11, 2026 meeting. For hydro operators offering regulation and reserves, the strike-price floor and FER DQ reduction change both the demand curve and the valuation framework for their ancillary service bids; the NPF update affects the penalty structure for non-performance, relevant to any hydro unit with AS obligations.

documents.title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03d - CAR-SA: Storage and Energy-Limited Resource Modeling and Accreditation Design Updates Cont. - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAPACITY ACCREDITATION: ISO-NE is updating how pumped-storage and energy-limited resources are credited toward resource adequacy; the rMRI adjustment mechanism directly affects the capacity value (MRIC) PSH owners receive in the ISO-NE capacity auction.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes an upward rMRI adjustment for energy-limited and storage resources in its Capacity Auction Reforms: Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) initiative, effective Q2 2027. The adjustment corrects a MARS modeling limitation that prevents storage from charging other storage during constrained hours, which currently underestimates storage reliability value. The formula scales adjusted rMRI so that very long-duration storage with zero forced outage rate would receive an rMRI of 1 (fully available). For a 10-hour battery, the winter adjustment increases rMRI from 0.414 to 0.492; for a 12-hour energy-limited resource, summer rMRI increases from 0.839 to 0.851. Class-level impacts show winter MRIC gains of 149.9 MW for energy storage and 140.0 MW for oil/dual-fuel, with larger adjustments favoring longer-duration resources. Next step: committee vote and FERC filing date not specified in excerpt; written questions due to Jasleen Singh (jsingh@iso-ne.com) by July 29, 2026.

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE Manual RPA (Registration and Performance Auditing) Revision 22, effective November 1, 2026, incorporates Order 2222 conforming changes governing registration of Distributed Energy Resource Aggregations (DERAs), DERs, and alternative technologies. The manual establishes that a DERA must have a single Lead Market Participant (the DER Aggregator) responsible for registering aggregations under one or more participation models, assigning Demand Designated Entities, mapping resources to dispatch locations, and ensuring compliance with all ISO asset requirements. DERs within a DERA cannot transfer between aggregations and must be retired and re-registered to move; the DDE is responsible for RTU installation and mapping DRRs/DRDERAs to remote terminal units. This framework creates new participation pathways for small hydropower and distributed storage but also imposes strict aggregation governance, single-point-of-control dispatch requirements, and metering/telemetry standards that could increase compliance costs for small-to-mid-size hydro operators entering aggregation structures.

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE Manual M-RPA Revision 21 incorporates conforming changes to implement Storage as Transmission-Only Asset (SATOA) registration procedures, effective September 1, 2026. SATOA rules allow storage facilities (including pumped-storage hydro) to register as transmission assets that provide grid services without energy arbitrage, requiring Lead Market Participant, asset owner, Host Participant, and Assigned Meter Reader authorization, plus designation of a single Dispatch Location and Designated Entity (DE) to receive ISO dispatch instructions. This creates a new compliance and operational pathway for PSH owners seeking to monetize grid-support services (frequency response, voltage support, flexibility) separately from energy sales, but operators must navigate new registration workflows, dispatch-location requirements, and dispatch-instruction protocols—a shift in how PSH interfaces with ISO-NE's operational and financial systems.

documents.title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03k - CAR-SA: Tariff Redlines - Auditing - Section III.1.5-7 Redlines

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is revising its claimed capability audit rules (Section III.1.5–7 tariff redlines), with Claimed Capability Audits and Seasonal DR Audits ending August 31, 2027. The tariff specifies audit duration requirements for hydro by type: hydraulic turbine reversible (2 hours), conventional daily pondage (2 hours), conventional run-of-river (2 hours), and conventional weekly (2 hours); it exempts non-intermittent daily hydro from mandatory Establish Claimed Capability Audits and Seasonal Claimed Capability Audits. Seasonal audits must be conducted at least once per capability year (summer ≥80°F or during ISO-announced window; winter ≤32°F or window, once per three years), with failure to meet requirements resulting in zero seasonal values. This directly determines the claimed capabilities that underpin ISO-NE's capacity accreditation and RA obligations; hydro operators must ensure audits are scheduled and completed within deadlines to avoid penalty (zero seasonal value), making compliance on the August 31, 2027 sunset critical for any asset still accruing seasonal claims.

documents.title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03k - CAR-SA: Tariff Redlines - Auditing - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE is redesigning seasonal capacity accreditation rules and resource-auditing frameworks that directly affect how hydro assets (dispatchable and non-dispatchable) are credited in the capacity market and priced by seasonal dependability.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes to sunset existing Claimed Capability and Seasonal DR audits after August 31, 2027, replacing them with three new metrics: Global Maximum Capability (36-month rolling max hourly performance, updated weekly), Seasonal Maximum Capability (same but for seasonally-specific temperature conditions), and Seasonal Dependable Capability (median availability during the 100 highest load hours per season, with a performance factor applied to dispatchable resources). The framework treats dispatchable resources (those receiving electronic dispatch instructions) differently from non-dispatchable resources, applying a performance factor to dispatchable median offers. This is part of ISO-NE's broader Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA) initiative scheduled for Q2 2027 implementation. Most exposed are conventional hydropower operators, especially dispatchable hydro in ISO-NE's system, whose seasonal dependable credits will now hinge on demonstrated performance during peak-load hours rather than on-demand auditing — reducing discretion to claim seasonal uplifts and tying accreditation to actual dispatch compliance and offer discipline.

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE presents the final Resource Accreditation Modeling Impact Analysis for CAR-SA, effective Q2 2027, which determines capacity accreditation (rMRI) values for energy-limited and storage resources under different weather weighting and system-reliability scenarios. documents.title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03b - CAR-SA: Transition Mechanism, cont. - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAR-SA's seasonal capacity accreditation and marginal reliability impact (MRIC) methodology directly reshapes how hydro (especially pumped storage and seasonal resources) is credited in ISO-NE's capacity market, altering revenue recognition across seasons and affecting reserve margins that determine must-offer and procurement rules.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing Capacity Auction Reforms – Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) with a three-year transition beginning in CCP 2028–29 (Q2 2027 proposed effective date). The reform shifts accreditation from a long-standing annual Qualified Capacity (QC) methodology to seasonal Marginal Reliability Impact Capacity (MRIC) values that reflect resource contributions to each season's adequacy. During transition, each resource's accredited capacity will be a weighted average: Year 1 (2/3 QC + 1/3 MRIC), Year 2 (1/3 QC + 2/3 MRIC), Year 3 (full MRIC). Hydro owners—particularly seasonal resources like winter-peaking or pumped-storage operators—will experience year-by-year accreditation shifts; some resources (e.g. passive demand response and winter solar) will see accreditation rise under CAR-SA, while others (especially summer-peaking hydro) will experience lower recognized capacity. The demand curve for procurement will also shift leftward across the three-year glide path, mirroring supply-side accreditation changes to maintain unit consistency and cost equivalence. Next steps include stakeholder review of transition mechanics and expected board/Markets Committee approval in coming meetings.

issues.canonical_name: Capacity Auction Reforms

documents.title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03l - CAR-SA: Winter Gas Constraint, cont. - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The proposal establishes a non-firm gas capacity demand curve and bilateral-transaction rules affecting how gas-backed resources—and by extension, competing dispatchable and storage resources—are accredited and compensated in ISO-NE's capacity market, directly impacting hydro's competitive position and RA value.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA) introduce a non-firm gas capacity demand curve effective Q2 2027 to reflect winter regional gas-supply constraints in capacity-market pricing. The proposal specifies: (1) gas-contract requirements (16-hour daily firmness for LNG; affidavit requirements dropped); (2) bilateral CSO (Capacity Supply Obligation) transaction limits tied to truncation points on the demand curve, preventing overtrading of constrained non-firm capacity across zones; and (3) restrictions on gas-only resource-backed imports, which cannot sell non-firm CSO in winter. The mechanism directly affects hydro's accreditation and capacity revenue: by capping non-firm gas capacity and pricing it separately, ISO-NE reduces the volume of low-cost gas capacity competing in winter auctions, tightening supply and raising clearing prices for all resources—especially valuable for pumped-storage hydro and reservoir hydro with winter peaking capability. The filing to stakeholders occurred in July 2026; the ISO notes it will "assess potential enhancements... in the months to come and after the CAR-SA filing." Most exposed are PSH and winter-peaking conventional hydro in zonal export-constrained areas, which benefit from reduced gas competition in winter RA auctions.

documents.title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03a - CAR-SA: Proposal Overview - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA) restructure capacity accreditation and seasonal procurement to reflect resource-specific reliability contributions, directly affecting how hydro and pumped-storage accreditation values are calculated and the seasonal demand curves that set their capacity revenue.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes moving from annual to seasonal (summer/winter) capacity auctions with accreditation reforms tied to Expected Unserved Energy (EUE) reduction, effective Q2 2027. The design improves resource operational-characteristic modeling—including energy-limited and storage resources—and calculates accreditation values (rMRI) based on margro reliability impact at system-adequacy criteria, rather than uniform assumptions. This revalues hydro and pumped-storage capacity by season and by performance characteristics (outage rates, generation profiles, energy limits); a transition mechanism will phase in the new rules. Key implications for storage and energy-limited hydro: accreditation will reflect their seasonal and state-dependent contributions more precisely, affecting revenue in capacity auctions and long-term investment signals. FERC accepted the prior CAR-PD filing in March 2026; CAR-SA filing anticipated by end of 2026, with key auction activities in first half of 2027.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms—Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) redesigns the capacity market demand curve and accreditation framework that directly affects capacity revenue and seasonal participation incentives for all resources, including hydro and pumped storage.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE's CAR-SA proposal restructures capacity-market demand curves to run separately for summer and winter seasons, with Seasonal Payment Rates replacing the single annual Net CONE to ensure cost recovery and constant scaling factors. The proposal derives summer SPR of $8.50/kW-month and winter SPR of $17.88/kW-month from a reference CONE resource's seasonal MRIC values and modeled capacity capabilities. This creates seasonal revenue differentiation: a resource's capacity accreditation and payment will now depend on its seasonal capability profile—critical for hydropower and pumped storage, whose seasonal energy constraints and discharge capabilities vary significantly. The ISO presented updated baseline and sensitivity results; with a proposed effective date of Q2 2027, the design is still in stakeholder feedback and modeling refinement.

documents.title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03k - CAR-SA: Tariff Redlines - Qualification - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms—Seasonal Accreditation (CAR-SA) directly modifies how hydropower and other resources qualify for and are accredited in seasonal capacity markets, affecting capacity revenue streams and qualification/accreditation obligations for New England hydro operators.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing Seasonal Capacity Auctions and Relative Marginal Reliability Impact (MRI)–based accreditation in place of annual auctions and Qualified Capacity values, with tariff revisions targeting Q2 2027 effective date. The qualification process will occur twice per year (summer and winter), and resources must requalify seasonally with updated Qualification Data Submission Packages including Global Maximum Capability estimates. Hybrid Capacity Resources and Distributed Energy Resources face new submission requirements; material-change procedures are tightened with pre-consultation deadlines, and forced-outage treatment now reduces seasonal MRI Capacity Values to zero for the affected period. Hydro operators—especially those with seasonal peaking patterns or variable output—will need to adapt qualification and capability-demonstration cycles to the new biannual cadence and MRI-based accreditation methodology; tariff amendment votes are expected Q3 2026 with FERC filing to follow.

documents.title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03k - CAR-SA: Tariff Redlines - Qualification- Section III.15.2 Redlines
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Tariff redlines to ISO-NE's capacity qualification rules (Section III.15.2) directly affect how hydro and hybrid resources qualify for the Annual Seasonal Capacity Auction, including seasonal MRI capacity value thresholds, qualification data submission requirements, and hybrid resource configuration rules—all material to hydro's capacity accreditation and revenue.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE's Markets Committee is advancing tariff redlines to Section III.15.2 (Capacity Resource Qualification) governing participation in the Annual Seasonal Capacity Auction. Key changes include: redefining summer period as June–September and winter period as October–May (except Demand Capacity Resources); clarifying qualification data submission requirements for resources never previously counted as capacity; and adding detailed requirements for Hybrid Capacity Resources (including facility limits, seasonal MRI allocation percentages, storage capability, grid charging, round-trip efficiency, and configuration-type classification). The document also specifies that Import Capacity Resources must be backed by external resources or control areas, and that firm fuel capacity can be qualified in advance of Winter auctions. These redlines materially affect how hydro (especially pumped-storage hybrids and imports) document capability and qualify for seasonal accreditation; next step is Markets Committee decision and board/stakeholder feedback.

documents.title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03g - CAR-SA: Flatiron Feedback, cont. - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAPACITY/RA accreditation methodology — proposal to reshape how ISO-NE models marginal resource adequacy contributions (rMRI) for all resources including hydro, affecting accreditation values, capacity-market clearing prices, and revenue recovery for dispatchable and storage units under ISO-NE's Competitive Auctions with Reserves (CAR) framework.
documents.ai_summary: Flatiron Energy presents a critique of ISO-NE's proposed Capacity Accreditation and Reserves (CAR-SA) modeling assumptions, arguing that the 'accreditation base case' (which adds 5 GW of load and uses historical 2002–03 extreme-weather years) creates artificially long marginal resource index (MRI) events that over-accredit resources designed for conditions that won't materialize. Flatiron proposes an 'Expected System Conditions' pathway: modeling expected supply and forecasted load without artificial load scaling, which would reduce or eliminate simulated MRI hours in a system with excess capacity. When MRI hours are minimal, Flatiron proposes accreditation shift to MRIC (marginal resource index credit) based on EFORd (equivalent forced outage rate) for most resource types, reflecting contingency-driven rather than energy-shortage-driven reliability events. ISO-NE's capacity demand curve and Net ICR would adjust accordingly, but the price-formation mechanism (reference-unit recovery at Net CONE × rMRI factor) would be preserved. This proposal, if adopted, would fundamentally change how all accredited resources including hydro, pumped storage, and thermal units recover capacity value, with most exposure to resource types whose value depends heavily on modeled energy availability in GE MARS software.

issues.canonical_name: Capacity Auction Reforms

documents.title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03f - CAR-SA: FirstLight Power - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE's CAR-SA capacity accreditation design—particularly seasonal payment-rate allocation, MRI-based CONE splits, and at-criteria accreditation—directly affects how pumped-storage hydro and seasonal hydro resources are compensated and incentivized to enter, especially when supply is tight in only one season.
documents.ai_summary: FirstLight Power presents technical analysis of interconnected design flaws in ISO-NE's Capacity Accreditation and Reserves-Seasonal Attributes (CAR-SA) proposal to the Markets Committee. The core issue: the current design allocates seasonal capacity payment rates proportional to Marginal Reliability Index (MRI) at Net ICR for each season, driven by an administrative 50/50 seasonal risk split, weather-year weighting that overstates winter event duration, and accreditation at 'at-criteria' conditions (demand-curve location inconsistent with actual clearing risk). FirstLight demonstrates that under this design, a reference resource may not recover Net CONE annually even when the system is tight in one season, suppressing entry signals for seasonal capacity. Potential solutions offered include dynamic seasonal risk splits aligned to actual system conditions, accreditation within a feasible clearing-point range, revised weather-year weighting, and payment-rate approaches that ensure annual Net CONE recovery. No deadline or vote date specified; this is positioned as urgent discussion to prevent compounding design errors before CAR-SA implementation. Pumped-storage operators—particularly those operating in winter-constrained regions or managing seasonal energy constraints—face material revenue exposure: if winter accreditation and payment rates are structurally overstated relative to summer, then summer-constrained capacity and flexibility are undervalued, and winter-heavy PSH assets may receive artificially elevated seasonal payments that don't reflect true entry incentives.

documents.title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03c - CAR-SA: Updated Activity Schedule - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: CAR-SA introduces seasonal capacity auction design and accreditation reforms affecting how hydro's capacity value is calculated and procured; this directly impacts capacity revenue and eligibility rules for both conventional hydro and pumped-storage resources.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing the Capacity, Accreditation and Resource – Seasonal and Accreditation (CAR-SA) reform, proposed effective Q2 2027, which shifts from a single annual capacity auction to seasonal (winter/summer) auctions and incorporates accreditation reforms and a winter gas constraint. The activity schedule governs the timing of resource qualification, market-power mitigation reviews, demand-curve development, and auction windows — all of which directly affect how hydro resources are accredited, how their capacity offers are mitigated, and when they must submit data. Most relevant to pumped-storage and seasonal-constraint hydro: the seasonal framework, the integration of accreditation into RA modeling, the Installed Capacity Requirement process, and the qualification deadlines (resource-qualification data submission one month pre-auction; unexpected full forced outage window tied to seller-side market power close). The presentation is an activity-schedule roadmap with no votes or comment deadlines stated; follow-up questions may be submitted in writing to Jasleen Singh by July 27, 2026, and this is Phase 2 of the CAR workstream, with Final Design and tariff amendments scheduled for Q4 2026.

documents.title: 2026-07-07-09 MC A03j - CAR-SA: Qualification - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAR-SA resource qualification process directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage resources demonstrate capacity accreditation and qualify for ISO-NE's seasonal capacity market, with material-change rules determining when resource modifications trigger re-qualification and affect Seasonal Claimed Capability and accreditation outcomes.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE presented the CAR-SA (Capacity Accreditation Reform – Seasonal/Accreditation) resource qualification process, effective Q2 2027, which introduces conforming changes to the material-change framework to align with seasonal accreditation concepts. The material-change definition now references Seasonal Maximum Capability (SMCap) instead of Annual Qualified Capacity, and triggers historical-data restart for Maximum Capability and Dependable Capability when deemed material; resource qualification deadlines shift from the original to at least five business days prior to Monthly Capacity Demonstration Deadlines. Hydro and PSH operators—especially those modifying interconnection facilities, changing technology type, or adjusting capacity by >5 MW or 10%—must report material changes, update Qualification Data Submission Packages, and demonstrate updated Commercial Capacity; failure to meet deadlines can defer participation to the next seasonal or monthly auction event. Questions may be submitted to ISO-NE MC Secretary Jasleen Singh (jsingh@iso-ne.com) by July 27, 2026.

documents.title: 20260707 RSC Item 02d Top 10 Congestion Constraints.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Congestion cost and flowgate performance directly affects hydro's transmission-constrained revenue from energy and ramping; M2M coordinated flowgates and outage scheduling influence when hydro can deliver value to export/load-pocket markets.
documents.ai_summary: MISO Reliability Subcommittee reports on the top 10 cost-generating congested constraints in its footprint as of July 2026. Seven of the ten constraints are M2M (Midwest-to-Market) coordinated flowgates, and six of the top ten stem from planned outages. The document presents a baseline congestion topology but does not indicate proposed mitigations or next steps. The relevance to hydro lies in flowgate binding and outage scheduling: tight M2M flowgates limit transmission capability for exporting generators (including hydro from the upper Midwest or neighboring regions) into constrained zones, and planned-outage timing can either relieve or exacerbate scarcity pricing windows when hydro's ramping and energy offer highest margin. Operators in hydro-rich neighboring RTOs (e.g., PJM members with hydro capacity, or DSOs considering MISO seams) should track whether MISO's congestion profile shifts—persistent binding on M2M corridors may signal interregional transmission expansion opportunity or pressure for storage-as-transmission alternatives.

documents.title: 20260707 RSC Item 04 Regulatory Compliance Update.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NERC ride-through standard (PRC-029-2) currently under ballot affects synchronous unit operating standards that hydro must comply with; large-load interconnection Show Cause Order touches hydro's strategic position in resource adequacy.
documents.ai_summary: MISO reports on regulatory compliance status across FERC and NERC activities. A FERC Show Cause Order on large-load integration (Docket EL26-70-000) requires MISO to address generation adequacy and interconnection speed by August 17, 2026, with an abeyance request possible August 2 to allow further stakeholder input on future tariff filings—directly relevant to how quickly new resources (and by extension, existing hydro's scarcity value) are interconnected. Separately, NERC's PRC-029-2 (Ride Through Revisions) is in draft ballot (through 7/13/2026), and PRC-024-4 (Frequency and Voltage Protection for Synchronous Generators) has an October 1, 2026 enforcement date; these impose operating standards on synchronous machines including conventional hydro. Additionally, Project 2025-05 on ride-through revisions and Project 2025-06 on supply-chain management are progressing with comment windows closing 7/13/2026 and 7/17/2026 respectively.

documents.title: 20260707 RSC Item 01d Management Plan.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document establishes MISO's Reliability Subcommittee governance and work program, which directly oversees issues affecting hydro operational compliance, winterization requirements, ramping/uncertainty products, and frequency response standards — core obligations and revenue mechanisms for hydro operators in the MISO footprint.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Reliability Subcommittee (RSC) management plan outlines the 2026–2027 governance and meeting calendar for the committee responsible for advising on reliability policies, NERC Reliability Standards, tariff administration, and market processes within MISO. Key topics scheduled include Primary Frequency Response and GIA Limits (RSC 2025-3), Dynamic Ramp Capability and Dynamic Regulation Reserve requirements (RSC-2024-1 and RSC-2024-2), Uncertainty Management (quarterly), annual Generator Winterization Survey, FERC Order 881 compliance, and Reconfiguration for Congestion Cost updates. The document's significance to hydro operators lies in its roadmap for setting and revising rules governing winterization compliance, ramping-product design, regulation reserves, and frequency response accreditation — revenue streams and operational obligations that directly affect how MISO-region hydro (especially run-of-river and storage-dependent generators) are dispatched and compensated. The RSC also reviews event analyses and NERC lessons learned relevant to hydro-dependent grid stability. No specific comment deadlines or votes are quoted beyond the committee's routine Q1–Q2 2027 schedule; the document is procedural rather than action-forcing.
