documents.title: 20260707 EPR-TSTF Item 01d Agenda_UPDATED.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Large-load interconnection (data center additions) in MISO's West Region directly affects hydro deliverability and scarcity value in that region; interconnection queue and transmission-constrained area studies shape the strategic value of existing hydro resources and their position in regional adequacy.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Expedited Project Review (EPR) task force is advancing technical studies for multiple infrastructure projects across four regions, including two data-center load additions in the West Region (Hannover facility projects 51293 and 51404). The EPR process streamlines interconnection review for projects meeting MISO criteria, reducing study timelines. Data-center loads are drivers of regional scarcity and transmission constraint—the addition of these loads in the West Region increases locational price signals and raises the economic value of existing hydro resources that can deliver into that area; simultaneously, transmission upgrades bundled with large-load projects (e.g., the Hannover upgrades) may relieve or shift constraints that affect hydro's ability to move power to constrained nodes. Next meeting is August 4, 2026; this session (July 7, 2026) reviews results and technical findings for Central, East, West, and South region projects.

documents.title: 20260707 EPR-TSTF Item 03 MISO EPR Statistics.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Large-load interconnection (data centers and industrial facilities) in MISO creates scarcity and demand growth that raises the value and dispatch frequency of existing hydro resources, while also establishing transmission planning priorities that affect deliverability and strategic co-location opportunities for hydro owners.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Expedited Project Review process has approved 67 projects through July 2026 representing ~14,000+ MW of new load additions across February, April, and June cycles. Prominent new loads include multiple large data centers (Hannover 430 MW, Project Skyway 780 MW, Project Atom 300 MW, Peninsula DC 430 MW) and industrial facilities concentrated in Central and West regions. These projects trigger transmission build-outs and distribution upgrades that reshape MISO's interconnection queue and establish new load pockets. For hydro and pumped-storage owners, this load concentration raises scarcity rents during peak periods, increases the value of ramping flexibility to serve demand ramps at large loads, and may create co-location or load-serving-entity pathways if MISO permits behind-meter arrangements; it also signals which transmission corridors MISO is prioritizing, which affects where existing hydro deliverability constraints may bind or be relieved.

documents.title: 20260707 SC Item 01b Agenda
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO is reviewing co-located load and generation participation rules and large-load definitions, both of which directly affect strategic options for hydro owners seeking to serve load behind the meter or participate in emerging co-location markets.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Steering Committee is convening a special meeting to charter a Large Load Working Group and review two active policy issues: (1) Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation at the same Point of Interconnection (ZGIA, PAC-2024-4a), and (2) Large Load Definition (PAC-2024-1). Both items are flagged as potential voting matters and candidates for reassignment to the new LLWG. These rules determine whether hydro owners can co-locate generation with load for behind-the-meter service and how large-load interconnections are treated, directly opening or constraining pathways for hydro to participate in new market structures as load growth and data-center demand accelerate in MISO's footprint. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this agenda.

documents.title: 20260707 SC Item 01c Management Plan
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO Steering Committee is reviewing charters and management plans for entities that will govern large-load and co-located-generation participation (ZGIA PAC-2024-4A and Large Load Definition PAC-2024-1), which directly affect hydro's strategic options to serve co-located load and influence market-design rules that touch revenue and dispatch.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Steering Committee is reviewing two proposals on its July 7, 2026 special meeting agenda: (1) Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation behind the same Point of Interconnection (ZGIA PAC-2024-4A), and (2) Large Load Definition (PAC-2024-1), with the Large Load Working Group charter and management plan also up for review. These proposals will establish how MISO treats behind-the-meter load and generation co-location and defines large loads going forward. For hydropower owners, especially those with run-of-river or reservoir operations, co-location rules determine whether they can serve load behind the meter or co-locate with new load, altering revenue streams and operational flexibility; large-load definitions affect how resource-adequacy and capacity-market rules apply to hydro-serving-large-load arrangements. The Steering Committee will vote on these items on July 7, 2026 (marked as potential voting items), making that date the actionable deadline for stakeholder input if comments are still open.

documents.title: 20260707 SC Item 02 DRAFT 2026 LLWG Charter
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Large-load interconnection and co-location can materially alter hydro's strategic positioning and revenue — loads with synchronized timing can drive scarcity pricing, absorb hydro output, and create co-location participation opportunities; the LLWG's charter establishes the forum through which MISO will develop policies on large-load integration affecting hydro's market access and locational value.
documents.ai_summary: MISO is establishing a Large Load Working Group (LLWG) reporting to its Advisory Committee to provide policy guidance on processes and market structures for integrating large loads in the MISO footprint, addressing issues spanning markets, operations, planning, and resource adequacy. The LLWG will convene stakeholders on an as-needed basis to coordinate with other MISO stakeholder groups. For hydro owners, this signals formalization of large-load integration as a strategic market-design priority — policies emerging from the LLWG will directly shape how big loads (data centers, industrial, co-located generation) interact with hydro's locational dispatch value, ancillary-service opportunities, and potential hybrid/co-location participation pathways. The group's scope encompasses resource adequacy and operations, where hydro's role as a flexible, location-sensitive asset will be relevant to load-integration decisions.

documents.title: 20260707 SC Item 02 LLWG Management Plan
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's Large Load Working Group is actively developing market-participation rules for co-located load and generation (including zero-injection GIAs), reserve-product enhancements, and interconnection requirements that directly affect hybrid/co-located hydropower and storage participation options and revenue models.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group (LLWG) is developing several rule packages in 2026–27 that will shape how large loads and co-located generation participate in MISO markets. Key active issues include: (1) Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation with zero-injection GIA consideration (PAC-2024-4, feedback requested May 14 and June 10); (2) Large Load Firm Service Step Up (LLWG-2026-1, tariff redlines due August 27); (3) Large Load Reserve Product Enhancements (LLWG-2026-2, evaluation and cost-allocation recommendations due Q4 2026); and (4) Interconnection Reliability Requirements including stability assessments (LLWG-2026-3). The co-location rule package directly opens a pathway for hydropower and pumped-storage facilities to register and dispatch co-located load behind the meter, potentially unlocking new revenue streams from load service and reducing network-cost exposure. Reserve-product enhancements and firm-service mechanics will change the mix of flexibility and capacity products available to large, controllable loads—affecting how hydro-connected or hybrid-sited PSH competes for reserve and flexibility revenue. LLWG meetings are scheduled through Q4 2026, with tariff redlines and final recommendations due by December 2026.

documents.title: 2. Null Power redlines 20260701.docx  [in MGHGTF Meeting Materials 20260701.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Null Power designation rules directly affect how renewable energy attributes (RECs) and non-power attributes are separated from hydro-generated energy and accounted for in Markets+ GHG tracking, which determines both the revenue treatment of attribute sales and the GHG attribution of hydro exports into the footprint—a critical issue for NHA members exporting renewable energy across the SPP Markets+ seam.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ GHG Task Force is redlining protocol language on 'Null Power'—energy from which a Reporting Entity (generator/owner) has separated and retained or sold RECs and non-power attributes. The redline clarifies timing (prior to Day-Ahead Market, or up to 90 calendar days post-operating-day via METra Portal) and the Market Operator's authority to adjust Null Power designations. This mechanism determines how hydropower owners account for attribute sales in the regional GHG accounting protocol and affects both the energy-market treatment of attribute-separated power and the GHG footprint attribution of hydro exports—especially material for pumped-storage and conventional hydro owners in the West who commonly separate and sell RECs. Status and next steps are not stated in this excerpt; this is a redline document circulated to the MGHGTF for comment/approval.

documents.title: 3. GHG Tracking and Reporting Backstop Emissions Report 20260701.pptx  [in MGHGTF Meeting Materials 20260701.zip]
documents.filename: 3. GHG Tracking and Reporting Backstop Emissions Report 20260701.pptx
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Markets+ GHG attribution and accounting rules directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage exports are valued and marketed in clean-energy programs and cross-RTO trade, particularly for Northwest hydro entering WEIM/EDAM footprints.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ GHG Task Force proposes replacing EPA eGRID and EIA fuel-type emissions factors with two superior backstop sources: GRETA (a real-time heat-rate curve model already used by MISO, SPP IM, and APS) and Open Grid Emissions (OGE, a peer-reviewed, generator-specific annual dataset). The proposal would establish a four-tier pecking order—Market Participant submission first, GRETA second, OGE third, and Market Operator calculation as fallback—shifting away from outdated annual averages to dynamic and sub-plant-specific emissions accounting. This matters to hydro owners because zero-carbon hydro and pumped-storage assets will receive more accurate (and favorable) emissions-factor treatment in clean-energy programs and multiregional GHG accounting; the use of real-time heat-rate modeling and open-source peer-reviewed data strengthens the defensibility of hydro's zero-carbon claim in export markets and capacity-procurement contexts where GHG attribution determines eligibility and value. No vote date or comment deadline is stated in the document.

documents.title: 5. 2026-06-17_Revised Unspecified Emissions Factor Conclusions & Recommendation_DRAFT 20260701.docx  [in MGHGTF Meeting Materials 20260701.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Unspecified emissions factor design and revenue treatment for GHG-zone imports affects hydro's energy-price formation and revenue capture under Washington's GHG pricing program, particularly for exporters and multi-zone participants in Markets+ footprint.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ GHG Task Force proposes adopting Washington Department of Ecology's unspecified emissions factor for pricing imports into GHG zones, rather than attempting to calculate a real-time marginal-emissions rate. The proposal addresses a foundational issue: if the unspecified EF is set too high, specified/internal resources are economically undercut; too low, it underrepresents emissions costs and can raise marginal energy prices. The task force recommends proceeding with an adopted static factor and establishing post-implementation monitoring, with open questions on how SPP distributes unspecified EF revenue to market participants and how unspecified imports are tracked and reported. This directly affects hydro exporters' energy revenue in the GHG zone and their ability to compete against unspecified imports when dispatch incorporates the emissions adder.

documents.title: 6.b. MGHGTF Action Items 20260701.docx  [in MGHGTF Meeting Materials 20260701.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Markets+ GHG pricing framework design directly affects energy price formation and revenue streams for hydropower and storage resources; GHG-tracking protocols and emissions accounting will alter dispatch economics and opportunity-cost calculations for hydro participation in SPP.
documents.ai_summary: SPP's Markets+ GHG Task Force is developing a greenhouse-gas pricing and tracking framework for the SPP footprint, with ongoing work on tariff language, protocols for emissions reporting, treatment of specified-source (imported) energy, and accounting for storage and hybrid resources. Recent action items (January–June 2026) focus on defining hybrid storage market resources (HSMR), designing mechanisms for partially contracted internal GHG resources, allowing independent power producers to designate energy as 'Null Power,' and providing sample input forms and GHG reporting tools. No completion date is named in the action-items list, but the framework is post-go-live implementation. For hydro owners, the mechanism is two-fold: (1) GHG-based pricing will create new scarcity signals and marginal-cost adders that alter dispatch merit order, affecting when hydro generation is economically selected; (2) the tracking and reporting requirements (particularly for imports, exports, and storage state-of-charge) will impose operational and compliance obligations on hydro operators. Pumped-storage resources are explicitly targeted by the HSMR definition revisions and multi-configuration resource (MCR) merit-order stacking proposals (Action Item 180), which will reshape how PSH offers energy and frequency support. Most exposed are PSH operators and large run-of-river exports into SPP; smaller reservoir hydro in SPP territory will face reduced dispatch value in high-emissions periods.

title: SOS Meeting
meeting_hydro_reason: PJM's SOS (Strategic Options Study) committee addresses long-term planning, resource adequacy, capacity markets, and transmission constraints that directly affect hydropower and pumped-storage revenue, accreditation, and participation opportunities in PJM's footprint and neighboring regions.

documents.title: Item 06 – EDART Auto Ticket Creation – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Affects how hydro and PSH owners must report generator availability and forced outages to PJM, touching both dispatch obligations and the accuracy of outage data that feeds forced-outage rates used in capacity accreditation and forecasting.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is implementing automatic eDART (Outage Database) ticket creation in September 2026 to capture all unavailable MW from committed capacity resources in real time. The system will auto-generate forced-outage tickets when a unit shows unavailable in Markets Gateway but lacks an active outage ticket in eDART, closing a gap exposed by Winter Storm Elliott and reinforced by Winter Storm Fern where unplanned outages went unreported. This directly affects hydro and PSH operators' obligation to report unavailable capacity timely and accurately under Schedule 1 Section 1.10.1A(d) (must-offer rules); the auto-creation logic will flag operators whose availability data in Markets Gateway diverges from eDART submissions, and forced-outage rates derived from eDART feed into capacity accreditation, reserve calculations, and PJM's forecast accuracy to stakeholders. Hydro and PSH owners dependent on accurate outage reporting for capacity value and performance assessment should monitor the Tech Change and eDART forums as details are formalized before the September 2026 implementation.

title: Cost Development Subcommittee Meeting

title: NEPOOL Power Supply Planning Committee Meeting
meeting_hydro_reason: Power Supply Planning Committee meetings typically address resource adequacy, seasonal energy assessments, capacity accreditation, transmission planning, and reliability mechanisms—all of which directly affect hydro revenue and operational strategy in ISO-NE's footprint and the broader Northeast market.

documents.title: 2026-07-01 PSPC A00 Agenda
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: ISO-NE's annual capacity auction and ICR/capacity accreditation methodology development directly affects how New England's hydropower and pumped-storage resources are accredited for resource adequacy and capacity revenue; demand-resource performance audits and load forecasting also inform the adequacy construct that drives RA value.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE's Power Supply Planning Committee will meet July 1, 2026, to establish the 2026 schedule for developing ICR-Related Values (Installed Capacity Requirements, Locational Seasonal Reserves, Minimum Capacity Levels, Hydro-Québec Interconnection Capability Constraints, and MRI Demand Curves) that feed the annual reconfiguration auction, plus review Summer 2025 and Winter 2025–2026 demand-resource performance and update the 2026 CELT load forecast. The ISO's capacity accreditation methodology and demand-resource audit results directly influence the reliability margin and seasonal reserve requirements against which hydro RA value is calculated; changes to the load forecast or demand-resource performance assumptions ripple into the adequacy baseline and thus hydro's capacity accreditation share. The agenda indicates this is a schedule-setting meeting with no proposals tabled yet, so substantive changes are likely to emerge in subsequent PSPC meetings through the 2026 cycle.

documents.title: 2026-07-01 PSPC A02 2026 Schedule for Development of New England's ICR-Related Values
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Installed Capacity Requirement (ICR) and accreditation values determine how much capacity New England hydro and other resources can claim toward resource adequacy; this schedule governs the development cycle for ARA 3 (2027–2028), which directly affects hydro's capacity accreditation and the revenue hydro receives from New England's capacity auction.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE has released the development schedule for Installed Capacity Requirement (ICR) and related values—including Hydro-Québec Interconnection Capability Credit (HQICC), Maximum Capacity Limit (MCL), and Marginal Reliability Impact (MRI) demand curves—to be used in the third Annual Reconfiguration Auction (ARA 3) for the 2027–2028 Capacity Commitment Period, scheduled to occur in March 2027. The PSPC will review assumptions and proposed values across meetings in August, September, and October 2026; the RC and PC will vote on October 20 and November 12, 2026, respectively; FERC filing is due by November 30, 2026. For New England hydro owners, this proceeding determines the accreditation rules and demand-curve parameters that govern how much capacity they can claim in the auction and the clearing prices they face—particularly relevant for owners supplying reserve margin and for understanding how interconnection credits and seasonal constraints shape their RA revenues in ISO-NE's forward capacity market.

documents.title: 2026-07-01 PSPC A03 Review Summer 2025 & Winter 2025-2026 Demand Resource Performance
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Document reports ISO-NE demand-response resource (DRR) audit performance against capacity obligations; DRRs are retail/behind-the-meter load reduction programs unrelated to hydro or PSH revenue streams, obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: 2026-07-01 PSPC A04 Review Active Demand Capacity Resource (ADCR) Availabilities
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document reports historical forced-outage rates (FOR) for Active Demand Capacity Resources (demand response) in ISO-NE's Forward Capacity Market—a purely demand-side resource class with no direct revenue, obligation, or strategic relevance to hydropower or pumped-storage operators.

documents.title: 2026-07-01 PSPC A05 Review Of 2026 CELT Load Forecast
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: ISO-NE's 2026 gross load forecast directly drives the Installed Capacity Requirement (ICR) for the Forward Capacity Market auctions and the Annual Reconfiguration Auction, which sets the demand curve and accreditation targets that determine hydro and PSH capacity revenue and clearing in New England.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE presents the 2026 vintage gross load forecast using its pre-existing methodology (pre-CELT 2025) to support calculation of the Installed Capacity Requirement (ICR) for upcoming Annual Reconfiguration Auctions (ARA). The forecast incorporates load data through end of 2025, reconstitutes for behind-the-meter PV and Passive Demand Resources (PDRs) using FCA 18 CSO values, and applies separate models for energy and seasonal peaks. For the 2027–2028 capacity commitment period, the 2026 forecast yields summer 50/50 and 90/10 peaks of 27,835 MW and 29,778 MW, and winter peaks of 22,461 MW and 23,337 MW, with an annual energy forecast of 136,064 GWh; ARA-specific adjustments are reported separately. This forecast directly determines the capacity-market demand curve and resource-adequacy baseline against which hydro and PSH plants are accredited and paid for capacity in New England's FCM.

documents.title: 20260701 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 04 State of the Market Presentation.pdf
documents.filename: 20260701 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 04 State of the Market Presentation.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's 2025 State of the Market report addresses capacity accreditation reform (marginal DLOL methodology), reserve and frequency response incentives, congestion pricing and transmission management, virtual market liquidity effects on energy pricing, large load interconnection impacts, and renewable resource integration challenges—each directly affecting hydro and PSH revenue, strategic positioning, and system value in a 26.6 GW load-growth scenario.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Independent Market Monitor presented findings from the 2025 State of the Market report showing all-in prices rose 67% to $53/MWh, driven by natural gas cost increases and capacity-market reforms. Key findings for hydro owners: (1) MISO transitioned to a reliability-based demand curve in capacity markets and reformed resource accreditation to reflect resources' marginal contribution to reliability in riskiest hours—shifting value from solar/wind with declining accreditation to dispatchable resources, including hydro; (2) shortage-pricing reforms in Fall 2025 improved energy and ancillary-service price signals, and MISO filed recommended penalties to improve incentive for units to follow dispatch setpoints; (3) real-time congestion costs rose 23% to $2.2B, driven partly by conservative transmission ratings and MISO's manual re-dispatch—highlighting transmission constraints that affect hydro deliverability; (4) MISO projects 26.6 GW load additions by 2030 (3.1–5.1% CAGR), with guidance that co-located generation and load should not avoid capacity or transmission costs, and siting at weak network locations should be non-firm pending upgrades—affecting co-location economics and interconnection strategy; (5) renewable penetration peaked at 45.7% in 2025, creating ramp-demand volatility and voltage-support constraints that favor dispatchable resources and storage; (6) marginal accreditation for new renewables is falling sharply, making continued investment in dispatchable generation essential—directly elevating the system value of existing hydro and PSH in MISO's reliability planning.

documents.title: 20260701 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 04 State of the Market Report.pdf
documents.filename: 20260701 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 04 State of the Market Report.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: State of the market report covering MISO energy and ancillary services pricing, resource adequacy trends, transmission congestion, virtual market activity, and recommended market reforms that affect hydro revenue, capacity accreditation, and dispatch performance.
documents.ai_summary: Potomac Economics' 2025 State of the Market Report for MISO examines energy price formation, ancillary services markets, capacity adequacy, transmission congestion, and resource operations across the year, with a forward-looking section on future market needs as wind/solar penetration rises and net-load ramps accelerate. Key findings include uplift costs in real-time markets, changes in MISO's generating capacity mix, congestion dynamics on key transfer constraints, and seasonal adequacy challenges highlighted by 2025 load-shed events and transmission emergencies in MISO South. The report's recommendations (Section X) propose reforms to energy pricing, operating reserves, ramping products, transmission congestion management, and capacity market design — with particular relevance to resource adequacy rules and scarcity-pricing mechanisms that directly influence dispatchable and storage resource revenues and obligations. Most exposed: all MISO hydro and pumped-storage owners subject to MISO's capacity and ancillary services accreditation, and those exposed to real-time pricing and make-whole payment volatility during tight-supply periods.

documents.title: 03b IPPNY Comments on NYISO Demand Curve Reset Methodology Improvement Project
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: IPPNY comments on NYISO's Demand Curve Reset (DCR) methodology directly touch capacity-market accreditation and proxy-unit design, which set the reference prices for NYISO capacity revenues — the 'missing money' mechanism on which dispatchable hydro and pumped storage depend for cost recovery and investment signals.
documents.ai_summary: IPPNY, representing 80% of New York generation, submits comments on NYISO's preliminary DCR improvements, urging four changes: (1) require proxy units to meet firm-fuel winter-availability requirements (minimum 8 hours daily operation December–February, or 56 hours on-site fuel storage); (2) adjust the Level of Excess to close the gap between market and planning assumptions; (3) shift the zero-crossing point (ZCP) of capacity demand curves rightward to reduce price volatility with small supply changes; and (4) improve annual escalation factors for financial parameters while retaining the quadrennial reset cycle, using New York-specific data for cost-of-debt and market-return indices. The core grievance is that NYISO's 2022 switch to a 2-hour battery as proxy unit excluded firm-fuel costs needed for winter reliability, causing capacity prices to fall below levels needed to retain or invest in generation — a signal deficiency that IPPNY argues has induced retirements and underinvests in reliability. Status: IPPNY feedback on preliminary recommendations; no vote or implementation date stated. Most exposed are any hydro/PSH operators relying on NYISO capacity-market revenues for cost recovery, particularly if they have winter firm-fuel capability that the battery-proxy ignores.

stakeholders.name: Richard Bratton

documents.title: 1 Agenda
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ICAP Demand Curve Reset (DCR) process directly affects capacity-market accreditation and RA pricing for all resources including hydro and pumped storage; NYISO's capacity market is a major revenue stream for NHA members.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Installed Capacity Working Group (ICAP/MIWG/PRLWG) is meeting July 1, 2026 to discuss improvements to the ICAP Demand Curve Reset (DCR) process and methodology — a core mechanism governing how NYISO's capacity accreditation and pricing are calibrated. The DCR controls the demand-curve parameters (cone, pivots, slopes) that determine what hydro and pumped-storage resources receive in RA revenue and how much capacity the market clears. No specific proposals or deadlines are stated in this agenda excerpt, but the working group is actively revisiting the methodology, which could reshape accreditation for all resources. This is an active process to monitor for substantive proposals in follow-up materials.

documents.title: 2 DADRP/DSASP Cleanup Tariff Revisions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO tariff cleanup affecting market participation pathways for aggregations (including DER/small hydro) and energy storage resources; removes deprecated demand-reduction program language and integrates aggregations into cost-based reference-level framework, which bears on offer-development obligations and mitigation-offer rules for non-traditional participants.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is cleaning up Market Services Tariff (MST) and Open Access Transmission Tariff (OATT) sections to remove obsolete language from the sunsunset Demand Aggregation/Demand Reduction Provider (DADRP) and Demand-Side Ancillary Service Provider (DSASP) programs. Key substantive change: aggregations (including small hydro and DER) are now folded into MST 23.7.3 cost-based reference-level framework, replacing a prior special exemption (MST 23.7.4) that had carved them out because NYISO previously did not calculate reference levels for demand-side resources. The removal of MST 23.7.4 means aggregations must now develop cost-based reference levels for mitigation-offer compliance, aligning them with generator standards. Other revisions correct grammatical errors, restore erroneously deleted BPCG recovery language in OATT 6.1.12, and clarify minimum-generation-bid and supplier definitions. Filing timeline: BIC discussion 7/15/2026; Market Committee vote 7/29/2026. Small hydro and distributed hydro resources participating via aggregation must monitor this closely, as it resets the compliance baseline for offer development.

documents.title: 3 ICAP Demand Curve Reset (DCR) Process and Methodology Improvements
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's ICAP demand curve reset methodology directly affects capacity accreditation and pricing for all resources including hydro and pumped storage, which depend on accurate CONE (Cost of New Entry) calculations and proxy-unit definitions to determine their capacity value and revenue.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is redesigning its Installed Capacity (ICAP) demand curve reset (DCR) process and cost-to-build methodology, with initial recommendations evaluated against reliability-based valuation, transparency, investment efficiency, and ease of administration. Key proposals include: using EIA overnight capital cost data to streamline cost-to-build estimates (rather than consultant-driven bottom-up approaches), codifying proxy-unit screening criteria to increase predictability, implementing more frequent indexed updates to financial parameters, and adjusting demand curve structures (kinked curves, zero-crossing informed by Minimum Reliability Index). The document is an interim presentation (July 1, 2026) building to an initial concept proposal in August and a complete market design with tariff updates by year-end. For hydro and pumped-storage owners, changes to CONE inputs and proxy-unit definitions directly affect capacity accreditation values and the price signal that drives the DCR clearing price—hydro's primary revenue mechanism in NYISO; any shift toward faster reset cycles or non-consultant methodologies could increase or decrease the accuracy of cost-to-build estimates that anchor their capacity payments, and technology-prescriptive proxy choices (e.g., mandating a gas turbine) would exclude alternative dispatch profiles that might better reflect hydro's actual value.

title: MORWG Net Conference
committee: Markets+ Operations and Reliability Working Group
meeting_hydro_reason: The Markets and Operations and Reliability Working Group typically addresses market rules, resource dispatch, ancillary services, and reliability requirements that directly affect hydropower and energy storage participation in SPP's markets.

documents.title: 09-10-MIR8.1 and MIR8.2 Update to MORWG June 2026.pptx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
documents.filename: 09-10-MIR8.1 and MIR8.2 Update to MORWG June 2026.pptx
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MIR8.1 restructures how pumped-storage and battery resources manage state-of-charge forecasting across Markets+ unit-commitment studies, shifting from market-participant prediction to market-operator calculation of SOC trajectories — a material change to how storage revenue exposure and dispatch predictability are handled under Markets+ rules.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ MIR8.1 proposes to introduce time-coupled intervals in all unit-commitment studies (Day-Ahead Market, Day-Ahead RUC, Intra-Day RUC, and Short-Term RUC), allowing market operators to calculate state-of-charge for all intervals after the first, rather than requiring market participants to submit a SOC forecast for every interval. The change is optional: participants can elect either the current design (submitting SOC forecast for each interval) or the new coupled design (submitting SOC forecast only for the first interval, letting the market operator time-couple and calculate subsequent SOC values). The proposal targets a FERC filing timeline, with MPEC approval targeted for August 2026. For pumped-storage owners, this addresses a key exposure: misforecasting dispatch leads to infeasible SOC trajectories in real-time, triggering capacity shortfalls and financial penalties; time-coupling within studies mitigates that risk by ensuring the market solver respects physical charging/discharging constraints. The optionality preserves flexibility for hybrid resources and those charging/discharging outside the market (where the operator lacks full visibility). MORWG convened June 30, 2026; MPEC approval is scheduled August 25, 2026.

documents.title: 01 - June MORWG Agenda.docx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This agenda covers multiple Markets+ Market Design initiatives directly affecting hydro and storage revenue and operational rules: state-of-charge management for multi-hour storage (MIR8.1), co-located resource constraints (MIR8.2), dynamic scheduling and pseudo-ties for resource participation (MIR9.2-R, MIR9.3-R), and real-time market settlement mechanics (MIR81-R, MIR60-R).
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ Markets and Operations working groups will discuss and vote on six Market Implementation Requests in June 2026, with August 2026 MPEC approval targets for most items. MIR8.1 (State of Charge Management) and MIR8.2 (Co-Located Resource Constraints) introduce new participation and operational frameworks for multi-hour storage resources—both drawn from MSR (multi-service resource) subgroup whitepapers and directly affecting how pumped storage and hybrid generators (including co-located hydro + solar) can operate and earn revenue. MIR9.2-R and MIR9.3-R expand dynamic-scheduling and pseudo-tie pathways, opening participation for neighboring resources and potentially enabling smaller or out-of-footprint hydro to access Markets+ balancing. MIR60-R and MIR81-R address real-time transaction settlement and dynamic multi-transaction-interchange pricing, altering how interchange flows (critical for Northwest-to-SPP hydro exports) settle and are valued. Comment deadlines range from June 18 to June 25, 2026; August MPEC vote is the approval milestone for most items.

documents.title: 02 - Antitrust Statement.pptx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]

documents.title: 03a - May MORWG Meeting Minutes.docx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MIR9.1-R establishes rules for non-dispatchable physical resources (likely including run-of-river hydro and variable generation), and MIR78-R adjusts market-clearing rules for MCR transition states in Markets+—both touch hydro participation mechanics and obligations in SPP's evolving regional market.
documents.ai_summary: The Markets+ Operations and Reliability Working Group approved two market-initiative requests at its May 27, 2026 meeting. MIR9.1-R (Non-Dispatchable Physical Resources) creates a framework for resources whose owners are legally or contractually unable to submit financial offer information, allowing them to be modeled in the market without unavailable offer data—directly relevant to run-of-river hydro and other variable resources that cannot control output. MIR78-R (MCR Transition) removes a 30-minute transition-state eligibility constraint on reserve clearing that was ported from the Integrated Marketplace but does not apply in Markets+, simplifying multi-commitment-request behavior. Both motions passed unanimously; no comment deadlines or further action items were assigned in these minutes. The group also received education on MIR1 (Market System Outage BA Overrides) and MIR79-R (MIR Process terminology revision), with updates scheduled for June and July MORWG meetings.

documents.title: 04-MIR75-R SPP Comments-20260520.docx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This MIR75-R proposal directly affects high-priority export scheduling and adjustment procedures for hydro and other dispatchable resources in SPP Markets+, touching revenue realization for exports and operational constraints on resource commitment and discharge.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ MIR75-R proposes clarified mechanics for adjusting high-priority export e-Tags when a resource's maximum economic limit or Variable Energy Resource forecast falls below committed export volumes. The proposal specifies that adjustments will be made pro-rata when multiple e-Tags share a single resource source, with a 1 MW minimum threshold and whole-MW rounding. Hydro and pumped-storage operators exporting power into or through SPP Markets+ will face automatic downward adjustment of export schedules when their available capacity (Max ECOL for conventional hydro, Maximum Discharge Limit for MSRs, VER forecast for wind/solar) cannot support the export commitment — a constraint that directly reduces realized export revenue and requires operators to manage export bids against real-time resource availability, especially during low-water or variable-generation conditions. The language resulted from an April 2026 MSWG/MDWG policy decision; this MIR specifies the operational algorithm. SRP supports the threshold addition.

documents.title: 05-MIR9.2-R Dynamic Schedule Resources June 2026.pptx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Markets+ Dynamic Schedule Resource rules establish how dispatchable hydro and other resources physically located in one balancing authority but serving load in another are accounted for in dispatch, power-balance settlement, and interbalancing-authority transfer accounting — directly affecting how hydro output is cleared, metered, and compensated across BA boundaries, particularly for multi-state/multi-BA hydro operators.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ proposes a new 'Dynamic Schedule Resource' participation type (MIR 9.2-R, June 2026) to solve a double-counting problem: when a dispatchable resource in one Balancing Authority (Native BA) is dynamically scheduled to serve load in another (Attaining BA), current rules treat its dispatch change in both the dynamic net scheduled interchange (NSI) and the market-to-interchange (MTI), forcing both BAs to regulate redundantly. The solution links a Dynamic Schedule Resource to a Dynamic Schedule Resource Interface; the resource remains dispatchable by Markets+ in real-time (RTBM), but only the interface value (not the resource's full output) is counted in the Attaining BA's power balance, preventing double-counting. The proposal defines five use cases: participating-to-participating BAs (resource-coupled or not), participating-to-non-participating (resource-coupled or not), and non-participating to participating. Most exposed are multi-BA hydro operators and pumped-storage facilities dynamically scheduled across BA lines; use-case 1a (participating-to-participating, resource-coupled) preserves dispatchability and shifts regulation responsibility via the interface, altering how dynamic hydro transfers compete in dispatch and how their locational and marginal-loss signals propagate across seams. Tariff and protocol sections are newly drafted; implementation timeline and stakeholder comment opportunity are not detailed in this presentation.

documents.title: 05-MIR9.2-R SPP Comments-20260602.docx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Dynamic Schedule Resources clarifies how Markets+ will treat dispatchability and real-time ICCP management of hydro and other resources subject to physical energy transfers across BA boundaries—a revenue and compliance issue for hydro sellers using dynamic dispatch to serve load or other BAs, and for multi-state resource compliance with e-Tag and regulation-burden rules.
documents.ai_summary: SPP staff proposes tariff and protocol revisions (April 2025/1.0a) to clarify registration, e-Tagging, and operational treatment of Dynamic Schedule Resources and Interfaces across Markets+ timeframes (DA, RUC, pre-RTBM, RTBM). The proposal distinguishes resource-coupled versus non-resource-coupled dynamic schedules and specifies when a resource may remain dispatchable under a dynamic schedule (internal transfers) versus when transactions must be non-dispatchable (certain exports). Key change: MSRs (multi-state resources, of which hydro is the canonical example) registered as Dynamic Schedule Resources can represent import or export depending on dispatch, but must coordinate separate e-Tags with the Native and Attaining BAs and accept ICCP-reported real-time quantities as fixed; Dynamic e-Tags referencing these resources are ignored by settlements, shifting compliance burden to the Market Participant. This reduces regulation burden on receiving BAs by eliminating pseudo-ties but locks the Dynamic Schedule Resource's output into the native BA's power balance and removes it from real-time economic dispatch in the attaining BA—a material constraint for hydro operators managing multi-BA export strategies. Status: tariff/protocol revisions have been proposed; next step and comment deadline are not stated in this excerpt.

documents.title: 06-MIR9.3-R Dynamic Schedule Resource Pseudo-Ties June 2026.pptx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Markets+ is refining pseudo-tie registration rules for external resources, which directly affects how hydropower and other dispatchable generation located outside the Markets+ footprint can participate, be dispatched, and settle — a strategic option for NHA members operating in non-participating balancing areas seeking access to SPP Markets+.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ (SPP's combined market and reliability coordinator) is adopting a new registration pathway (MIR 9.3-R) that allows external resources physically located in non-participating balancing authorities to be modeled as pseudo-tied within Markets+ using dynamic schedules instead of formal NAESB pseudo-tie agreements. External resources registered under this new option will be dispatchable by Markets+, settled at their physical-location LMP, and participate identically to formally pseudo-tied resources, but the underlying dynamic schedule between native and attaining balancing authorities will remain in place (and ignored by the market system and settlements). The change preserves all formal pseudo-tie requirements and adds two new attributes: the market participant must designate the resource as a "dynamically scheduled pseudo-tie" in the registration workbook, and the participating attaining BA must provide an attestation letter confirming its understanding that the resource will be modeled as pseudo-tied. This creates operational flexibility for BA arrangements that cannot or prefer not to establish formal pseudo-ties, potentially broadening external-resource participation. The document shows protocol language updates to sections 9.1.1, 9.1.9, 9.1.9.1, and 9.1.9.2 but states no vote date or comment deadline.

documents.title: 06-MIR9.3-R Dynamic Schedule Resource Pseudo-Ties submission.docx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: External resource pseudo-tie registration and modeling rules directly affect how hydropower resources outside the Markets+ footprint (including those in adjacent balancing authorities) can participate in the day-ahead and real-time energy market and settle, and clarifies the framework for pseudo-tied hydro to remain on dynamic schedules while competing in Markets+—a critical pathway for cross-BA hydro participation and revenue capture.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ (SPP's organized market launching 2027) is codifying registration and operational requirements for two forms of external resource pseudo-ties: formal pseudo-ties with binding BA agreements, and dynamically scheduled pseudo-ties where the resource is modeled as pseudo-tied into the attaining BAA in Markets+ commercial and network models while energy continues to flow as a dynamic schedule under NAESB/WECC standards between native and attaining BAs. The MIR (Markets+ Initiative Request 9.3-R) adds a new resource designation ('dynamically scheduled pseudo-tie') and defines meter-agent, attestation, and settlement responsibilities in Protocols Sections 9.1.1, 9.1.9, 9.1.9.1 (new), and 9.1.9.2 (new). SPP staff rates this as medium design, implementation, and operational complexity; MPEC approval is targeted Q3 2026 with Q1 2027 go-live. This standardizes a workaround already in Markets+ base design, reducing ad hoc handling and participation ambiguity for external hydro resources seeking to serve load within the footprint—directly relevant to northwest hydro exports and cross-border hydro from Mexican utilities or other external basins.

documents.title: 07-MIR60-R Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) submission.docx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
documents.filename: 07-MIR60-R Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) submission.docx
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) establish a new framework for import/export transactions across the Markets+ boundary and internal transfers within the footprint, affecting how hydro and other resources can participate in interregional energy trading and settlement — a direct revenue vector for hydro owners seeking to export power or manage interchange scheduling within the expanding Markets+ footprint.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ is implementing the Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) framework, which creates generic system points (MPSP) within each Markets+ Balancing Authority Area to facilitate scheduling and tagging of import/export transactions across the Markets+ boundary and internal market transfers. The protocol revisions establish registration, operational, and settlement requirements for MPSPs as sources and sinks for interchange transactions, with optional hourly limits managed by each BA in the Day-Ahead and Real-Time Balancing Markets. For hydro owners exporting from or importing into Markets+ BAAs, MITSPS enables participation in interchange transactions that are not pre-scheduled bilateral trades but are cleared by the market — expanding the scheduling and revenue-participation pathways available to hydroelectric resources. The MIR seeks MPEC approval in Q3 2026 with implementation targeted for Q1 2027, affecting how existing hydro plants can access interregional markets and how new capacity (especially in growing Markets+ footprint) will schedule transactions.

documents.title: 08-MIR81-R Dynamic MTI-MOTI in RTBM submission.docx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: MTI/MOTI tagging and BAA Scheduling Constraints directly affect how SPP Markets+ dispatches interregional power flows in real-time; distinguishing Normal (DA-locked) from Dynamic (RTBM-only) transaction types changes the sequencing and flexibility of resource commitment and redispatch — a structural change to market dispatch that hydro operators use to optimize revenue from flexible ramping and interregional sales.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ (MIR 81-R) proposes to distinguish Normal and Dynamic MTI/MOTI transaction types, with Normal types locked after Pre-RTBM SCED and Dynamic types eligible only in RTBM. This allows BAAs to create separate BAA Scheduling Constraints on Dynamic paths without retroactively constraining the day-ahead and RUC commitment plan. The change is ranked High priority, with MPEC approval targeted Q4 2026 and implementation Q1 2027. Hydro operators in the SPP footprint benefit by avoiding artificial redispatch and binding constraints when BAs adjust transmission limits for reliability mid-day; it preserves the optimization value of DA and RUC schedules and lets BAs manage incremental transfer changes without forcing costly RTBM repricing and potential VRL violations. Most exposed are pumped-storage and flexible reservoir operators using MTI/MOTI paths for interregional arbitrage and capacity sales.

documents.title: 08-MIR81-R Dynamic MTI-MOTI in RTBM.pptx  [in MORWG Meeting Materials 20260630.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Modifies balancing-area scheduling constraints and market-transfer-interface (MTI) rules that affect real-time dispatch flexibility and locational price signals — hydro's revenue from ramping/flexibility procurement and locational scarcity pricing depends on how transmission constraints are enforced across market intervals.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ proposes MIR81-R, which distinguishes Normal (day-ahead/RUC/pre-RTBM) and Dynamic (real-time only) MTI/MOTI transaction types to allow balancing authorities to set different scheduling constraints in RTBM than were used in DA commitment, preventing large NSI swings without undermining DA/RUC optimization. Normal MTIs become fixed quantities in real-time; Dynamic MTIs operate only in RTBM with nonbinding limits (defaulting to 99,999 MW unless constrained by BA-to-BA scheduling restrictions). The proposal updates tariff sections 4.2.7, 8.2, 8.2.1.1, 8.2.2.1, 8.2.6, and 9.4.1 to implement transaction-type registration and constraint-submission requirements via the MUI. This affects real-time dispatch optimization and price formation: by allowing separate constraints for incremental transfers, the scheme constrains BAA scheduling-constraint binding in RTBM while preserving DA-cleared transfers, potentially reducing real-time price separation and limiting the incremental scarcity premiums hydro can capture on flexible ramping in congested intervals. All market participants must submit Normal MTIs/MOTIs prior to DA market close and register at least one Dynamic MTI at resource registration.

title: Department of Market Monitoring 2025 Annual Report on Market Issues and Performance Public Call
committee: Department of Market Monitoring Market Issues and Performance Report
meeting_hydro_reason: CAISO's annual market-monitoring report typically analyzes price formation, ancillary services, energy-storage performance, resource-adequacy outcomes, and market-power mitigation—all core revenue and strategic-option axes for hydro and PSH operators in the Western markets.

documents.title: Presentation - 2025 Fourth Quarter Market Issues and Performance - Apr 02, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO market-monitoring report on Q4 2025 real-time pricing, regional generation mix by fuel (including hydro), transmission congestion, and interregional transfer flows — directly bearing on hydro revenue (price formation and regional scarcity signals) and strategic positioning in the West.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Department of Market Monitoring reports Q4 2025 market outcomes: electricity prices in the West fell 7% year-over-year; less frequent high-price intervals occurred; Pacific Northwest shifted to net exporter across all hours; and California prices remain elevated by greenhouse-gas costs rather than congestion. The report shows generation-mix shifts by region (Slides 8–11) but does not propose tariff changes. For hydro owners, the data reveals that interregional transmission capacity and fuel-mix changes—especially lower prices and reduced Northwest scarcity—signal weakened export value from the PNW into California, while California's GHG-driven premium persists. Analysts using this as a reference point would identify market conditions affecting hydro's locational revenue; no comment deadline or action item is stated.

documents.title: Presentation - 2025 Third Quarter Market Issues and Performance - Jan 08, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO market-monitoring report analyzing Q3 2025 price formation, congestion patterns, WEIM transfers, and generation mix changes across multiple Western regions — directly informs hydro revenue via locational pricing signals, interregional trade dynamics, and operational conditions that shape dispatch and scarcity.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Department of Market Monitoring presents Q3 2025 market performance: Western region prices down 4% year-over-year; California dominated by south-to-north congestion and GHG costs; Northwest saw higher solar-hour prices while California peaked in non-solar hours; WEIM transfers flowed from CAISO mid-day and from Intermountain/Desert Southwest in non-solar hours, with assistance-energy opt-ins increasing transfers for 8 of 10 entities. Real-time three-pivotal-supplier test found 4.2% of WEIM 15-minute intervals uncompetitive; RTD mitigation errors added ~37 MW incremental dispatch. Hydro owners should track the pronounced shift in congestion direction (north-to-south peak in Q3 2024 vs. current south-to-north pattern) and the interregional transfer behavior, which directly affects locational scarcity pricing and arbitrage opportunities for reservoir and pumped-storage resources positioned to move energy across the Western interconnect.

documents.title: Presentation - 2025 Second Quarter Market Issues and Performance - Oct 09, 2025
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO market-monitoring report on Q2 2025 energy price formation, congestion, interregional transfers (WEIM), and resource dispatch patterns—directly informative for hydro owners assessing revenue drivers, transmission constraints affecting their exports, and strategic positioning across the West.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Department of Market Monitoring reports Q2 2025 real-time market conditions: electricity prices in the West rose 12% versus Q2 2024, driven by south-to-north congestion and California greenhouse-gas costs; Northwest prices were higher during solar hours, California during non-solar hours—indicating hydro's value shifts with solar generation patterns. WEIM transfer congestion into or out of Pacific Northwest areas occurred over 20% of 5-minute intervals, and Mid-Columbia and Palo Verde bilateral prices converged with ISO market prices, suggesting arbitrage opportunities narrowed. Pacific Northwest coal output rose despite more outages, and transfers from the Intermountain West during non-solar hours declined Q-o-Q, signaling reduced availability or changed dispatch incentives. For hydro owners, these trends show tightening price signals in hydro-export corridors, persistent congestion reducing transmission access to California, and seasonal shifts in the regional generation stack that affect dispatch positioning and revenue timing.

documents.title: Presentation - 2024 Annual Report on Market Issues and Performance
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO's 2024 market-performance report provides comprehensive data on hydro generation patterns, regional price formation, congestion outcomes, and ancillary-services procurement mix — all drivers of hydro revenue and dispatch value — plus recommendations affecting storage bid-cost recovery, default energy bids, and local market-power mitigation that extend to hybrid resources including hydropower.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Department of Market Monitoring presents 2024 annual performance data and forward recommendations. Pacific Northwest hydro generation increased in 2024 relative to 2023; Northwest prices rose mid-day during solar hours and followed net-load patterns, with transfer-congestion elevation noted (page 7, 11–12). Battery participation in ancillary services grew substantially (page 43), while overall flexible ramping product prices remained infrequent at system/BA level (page 42). Recommendations include extending local market-power mitigation to hybrid resources, enhancing default energy bids for batteries to reflect intraday opportunity costs (with standardized bids for WEIM batteries proposed), and revising bid-cost recovery rules — changes that would apply similarly to hydro and PSH if codified (pages 46–47). No specific vote or comment deadline stated in this presentation; this is a published annual report. Most exposure: pumped-storage operators subject to bid-cost recovery and default-energy-bid rules, and larger reservoir hydro in the Pacific Northwest tier where congestion pricing and seasonal price patterns drive revenue.

documents.title: Presentation - 2025 First Quarter Market Issues and Performance - Jun 26, 2025
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO Q1 2025 market-monitoring report documents flexible ramping product procurement split by fuel type (hydro vs. solar/wind/batteries), WEIM interregional transfer patterns involving hydro-exporting regions (Pacific Northwest, Intermountain West), and price formation across WEIM footprint affecting hydro export value.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Department of Market Monitoring Q1 2025 performance report shows hydro and batteries supplying the bulk of upward flexible ramping product (FRP) procurement, while solar and wind dominate downward FRP demand in the 15-minute market. Intermountain West and Pacific Northwest became dominant WEIM exporters during non-solar hours in Q1 2025, with PacEast and Idaho Power identified as main peak-hour exporters; transfer congestion into/out of those hydro-rich regions occurred in ~20% of 5-minute intervals, creating locational scarcity signals. Electricity prices in the West dropped 39% year-over-year but remain driven by south-to-north congestion and greenhouse-gas costs within California. The report is observational and carries no proposals or compliance deadlines, but provides a baseline on how hydro's FRP-market participation, export positioning, and regional pricing have shifted in the WEIM footprint — useful for understanding Q1 2025 hydro revenue dynamics and transmission-constrained export opportunity.

title: Tentative:FERC Order 881 Refresher Training: WebLineR Application Overview and Updates
meeting_hydro_reason: A refresher training on WebLineR application mechanics and IT procedures is operational administration with no bearing on market design, pricing, settlement, or dispatch rules affecting hydropower revenue, compliance, or strategy.

title: Budget & Priorities Working Group
meeting_hydro_reason: Budget & Priorities Working Group meetings typically address NYISO operational budgets, staffing, and administrative priorities rather than market-design rules affecting hydropower revenue, compliance, or strategic options.

documents.title: 1 Agenda
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a bare meeting agenda with no substantive content on market design, pricing, accreditation, participation rules, or operational standards that would affect hydropower or PSH revenue, obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: 3b 2027 Enterprise Project Descriptions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ADVANCED STORAGE TOOL and BLACK START AUTOMATION PORTAL are enterprise projects that directly support hydro/PSH participation infrastructure and compliance obligations; RENEWABLE ENERGY FORECAST INTEGRATION affects variable hydro scheduling; SYSTEM RESOURCE PLANNING enhancements with AI capability touch interconnection process for generation (including hydro uprates); and several projects support grid operations and reliability mechanisms hydro must operate within.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is planning 2027 enterprise projects (internal technology and back-office improvements, no market rule changes) that include three items material to hydro/PSH: (1) Advanced Storage Tool—development of a software capability to model and manage storage operations, directly benefiting pumped-storage operators' dispatch and planning; (2) Black Start Automation Portal—automating black-start capability tracking and compliance for synchronous resources including hydro units; (3) Renewable Energy Forecast Integration and Reporting Enhancements (REFIRE)—improving NYISO's forecasting and reporting of variable renewable generation, which affects the reserve and ramping requirements hydro must meet and the scarcity signals hydro responds to. Additionally, System Resource Planning enhancements with AI capability are designed to improve the interconnection process, potentially reducing queue time for hydro uprates and new storage interconnections. These are "Prioritize" category projects expected to be included in the 2027 budget pending feasibility assessment; no formal decision or comment deadline is stated in this draft.

documents.title: 2b 2027 Project Prioritization Survey
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Project 1 (5-Minute Transaction Scheduling with Hydro-Québec) directly affects NYISO hydro export revenue and market-efficiency mechanisms; Projects 3–5 touch DER aggregation and ICAP participation rules that extend to small hydro and storage under Order 2222, and Project 2 (transparency) affects price-formation confidence critical to all resources.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Budget & Priorities Working Group is surveying stakeholders on 2027 project prioritization. Project 1 proposes five-minute transaction scheduling with Hydro-Québec to improve price convergence between RTC and RTD and increase real-time flexibility; market-design work is due in 2027. Project 3 (Aggregation System Integration) seeks API-driven automation of DER enrollment and safety review to streamline FERC 2222 compliance for Member System utilities and small DER operators. Project 4 (Demand Response Baseline Alignment) will investigate rules preventing DSRs from becoming net ICAP suppliers, addressing a MMU recommendation. Project 5 (Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers) implements tariff changes to auto-resell UCAP surplus and reduce clearing prices. Survey responses due July 15, 2026; all responses will be made public.

documents.title: 3 2027 Proposed Enterprise Projects Updates and Costs
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is an internal NYISO budget and enterprise-project planning document covering IT infrastructure, facility upgrades, and administrative automation — with no bearing on hydropower revenue mechanisms, participation rules, settlement, or strategic options.

documents.title: 2c 2027 Proposed Market Project Descriptions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's 2027 market project list includes multiple initiatives affecting hydro and PSH revenue mechanisms, strategic options, and compliance obligations: capacity accreditation and RA modeling, storage-as-transmission, dynamic reserves, ancillary-service pricing, operating-reserve design, demand-curve resets, and outage scheduling.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2027 Market Project Candidates document lists 62 proposed initiatives across four prioritization tiers (Prioritize, Mandatory, Continuing, Future). Hydro and PSH owners should track: (1) Balancing Intermittency Phase 2: Evaluating Needs for Duration in Reserves (ENDURE) — examining whether longer-duration reserve products are needed, directly relevant to PSH scheduling and RA accreditation; (2) Capacity zone redesign and locational capacity pricing enhancements — affecting where hydro gets recognized for RA; (3) Storage as Transmission — potentially enabling PSH to serve transmission functions and unlock new revenue; (4) Dynamic Reserves Phase 1 (Continuing) and Phase 2 (Prioritize) — redesigning how reserves are scheduled and cost-recovered, bearing on hydro's ancillary-service margins; (5) ICAP Demand Curve Reset methodology improvements and seasonal ICAP market improvements — updating capacity-auction parameters that determine bid acceptance and clearing prices for hydro's capacity offerings; (6) Outage scheduling enhancements, operating-reserve performance review, and fast-start pricing logic — touching dispatch and settlement rules affecting hydro's operational flexibility value. The document is a prioritization framework for budget and resource allocation; next steps and timelines are not specified within this excerpt, but member engagement through stakeholder survey is noted as ongoing.

documents.title: 2 2027 Proposed Market Projects Updates and Costs
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO is prioritizing 61 market projects for 2027 including capacity-zone redesign, DER enhancements, storage/intermittency-duration reserves, demand-response baselining, and large-load integration — all affecting hydro and storage revenue, accreditation, and participation frameworks; the document establishes the project pipeline and stakeholder-scoring process that will shape NYISO market design through 2027.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2027 budget and market-project prioritization process is now open for stakeholder input through a July 15, 2027 survey. The project slate includes 61 candidates (6 mandatory, 2 continuing, 42 prioritize, 11 future) spanning energy markets, capacity markets, new-resource participation, and reliability planning. Key projects directly touching hydro and storage include: Capacity Zone Redesign (SOM) [#7], Balancing Intermittency Phase 2: Evaluating Needs for Duration in Reserves (ENDURE) [#6], DER Market Enhancements [#8] (which will enable small hydro aggregation under Order 2222), and a newly mandatory FERC Order on Large Loads project that eliminates and absorbs three related projects (Reliability Planning & Large Load Integration [#32], DER Market Enhancements [#8], and Flexible Load Models—Large Loads [#16]). The FERC order's treatment of large-load interconnection, frequency support, and flexible supply will reshape the incentive for hydro participation. Weighted stakeholder scores (voting members only, governance-weighted by sector, with Generation Owner and Other Supplier each at 21.5%) will drive prioritization at the July 30 BPWG meeting, leading to Board approval in December 2026. Comments are due July 15, 2027.
