title: Large Load Working Group (LLWG) - June 29, 2026
meeting_hydro_reason: Large load interconnection and co-located load developments directly affect hydro's strategic positioning and market participation options, particularly regarding the value of flexible generation and the potential for behind-the-meter resource arrangements.

documents.title: 20260716 LLWG Item 01c Minutes 20260610.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's Zero-Injection GIA (ZGIA) proposal directly affects how co-located generation and load can participate in markets and be registered—a strategic option for hydro owners seeking to site generation with load, and a novel participation pathway that may extend to small hydro and hybrid resources under Order 2222 principles.
documents.ai_summary: MISO is developing a Zero-Injection Generator Interconnection Agreement (ZGIA) framework to enable market participation and registration of co-located load and generation. The proposal reflects six months of stakeholder input and is nearing a tariff filing at end of June 2026, with draft language and Planning Advisory Committee feedback still being incorporated. The framework adapts existing interconnection processes (surplus, replacement, ERAS) to zero-injection scenarios. For hydro owners, ZGIAs represent a new pathway to co-locate generation with large industrial load—potentially raising MW value by shifting pricing exposure, enabling behind-the-meter hydro dispatch, and avoiding transmission constraints; most relevant to pumped-storage operators and small hydro seeking high-load-density sites. Comment deadlines and detailed tariff language appear in related filings (PAC-2024-2B, LLWG-2026-3, LLWG-2026-2) due May 26–June 4, 2026.

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 01b Agenda.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large Load Working Group agenda addresses co-located load-and-generation registration, interconnection reliability, and operational requirements that directly affect hydro's strategic options for serving large loads and co-location arrangements, plus hydro's obligations for operational reliability and dispatch coordination.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group (LLWG) is meeting June 29, 2026, to address market participation rules for co-located load and generation (item 6: Zero-Injection GIA under PAC-2024-2B), interconnection reliability requirements (LLWG-2026-3), large-load telemetry/PMU requirements (LLWG-2026-4C), operational forecasting requirements (LLWG-2026-4A), and reserve product enhancements (LLWG-2026-2). The co-location and zero-injection items directly enable hydro owners to serve large loads behind the meter or as bundled resources, expanding revenue and strategic flexibility; the interconnection and operational requirements impose new compliance obligations on dispatchable generation (including hydropower) serving large loads. Next LLWG meeting is July 16, 2026. No voting or comment deadlines are stated in this agenda.

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 01d Management Plan.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This MISO working group management plan addresses large-load integration, co-located generation/load, and operational reliability requirements that directly affect hydro and pumped-storage participation options, interconnection pathways, and reserve-product design.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group (LLWG) is tracking six active workstreams through Q4 2026 that reshape how large loads and co-located resources (including hydro-paired load) participate in MISO markets. Key items include: (1) Large Load Firm Service Step Up (LLWG-2026-1) and reserve-product enhancements (LLWG-2026-2), which will define capacity/reserve accreditation and service-level requirements for flexible resources attached to large loads; (2) Interconnection reliability requirements (LLWG-2026-3) and operational-reliability workstreams (LLWG-2026-4A/B/C covering forecasting, network models, and PMU/telemetry) that establish must-offer and real-time operational standards for co-located assets; and (3) Market participation rules for zero-injection GIAs (PAC-2024-4B), affecting how hydro or storage can register co-located load without serving the MISO grid. The June 29, 2026 meeting will surface a tentative FERC action and initial reserve-enhancement evaluation results; final tariff redlines and recommendations are scheduled for August and November 2026. Pumped-storage operators and conventional hydro owners considering co-location or large-load service opportunities need to monitor reserve-product design and the zero-injection GIA interpretation, as these will determine whether co-located capacity can stack ancillary-service revenues or will be constrained by MISO operational requirements.

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 01c Action Items.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large-load interconnection and co-located load affect hydro's strategic positioning in energy markets and scarcity value; this action-items document flags active MISO work on large-load firm service that could shape transmission access and revenue opportunities for hydro.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group issued feedback requests on June 10, 2026 (closed) for the Large Load Firm Service Step Up proposal (LLWG-2026-1), with comments originally due June 24. The proposal addresses how MISO accommodates large-load customers seeking firm service, a mechanism that intersects hydro's role in providing dispatchable energy and flexibility to serve new demand. Large-load firm-service design can shift the value of hydro by changing transmission constraints, scarcity pricing, and the premium for flexible supply — particularly for pumped-storage operators positioned to serve or compete with large loads. The document provides no detail on the proposal's substance, status, or next steps.

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 07 Draft MISO Large Load Interconnection Requirement Technical Compendium
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large load interconnection and operational requirements directly affect hydro's strategic options for co-location, load-following capability valuation, and transmission/grid-stability service revenue through emerging demand for ramp, ride-through, and dynamic-performance capabilities that hydro uniquely supplies.
documents.ai_summary: MISO has published a Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements technical compendium establishing interconnection standards for rapid-growth transmission-connected loads (data centers, AI computing, etc.), with four requirement categories: data/telemetry/forecasting; ramp management; voltage/frequency ride-through; and stability assessment. The framework defines minimum disturbance-performance expectations and dynamic-response capabilities that large loads must meet. This expands MISO's valuation of ramping, frequency support, and voltage stability — services hydro suppliers have historically dominated — and opens strategic pathways for hydro co-location with large loads and behind-the-meter service delivery. The document establishes technical requirements that may inform future MISO ancillary-services procurement and reserve-adequacy modeling as load volatility and grid-stability demands increase; hydro owners should monitor whether MISO translates these reliability needs into new product definitions or accreditation adjustments that reward fast-response, high-ramp-capability resources.

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 07 Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements (LLWG 2026 3)
documents.filename: 20260629 LLWG Item 07 Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements (LLWG-2026-3).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large load interconnection requirements and their operational reliability mandates (remote disconnect, ramp-rate controls, ride-through, telemetry/PMU) directly constrain how co-located loads (data centers, AI/HPC) can interact with the transmission system and may affect hydro's ability to serve or coordinate with these loads, plus set a precedent for how RTOs will require dispatchable resources to respond to fast-ramping demand.
documents.ai_summary: MISO is filing revised Tariff language by end of July 2026 to impose interconnection reliability requirements on large loads (≥50 MW new, ≥25 MW cumulative increase) and computational loads (≥1 MW of data-center-type digital demand), implementing FERC's show-cause order. Requirements include basic facility data, modeling, hourly forecasts, telemetry, and PMU data; computational loads face additional phasor measurement, ride-through (voltage/frequency), and ramp-rate controls; MISO will install and charge customers for remote disconnect equipment and control systems. A transitional provision exempts loads with service commencement before 11/1/2027 from ramp and ride-through requirements for their lifetime unless they upgrade by ≥25 MW after 10/1/2026. This affects hydro owners because (1) it establishes a co-location precedent — large loads are now defined as discrete withdrawal points, creating opportunities for hydro to site behind-the-meter generation and enter direct load-service arrangements; (2) the ramp-rate and ride-through enforcement on computational loads (AI, crypto, HPC) creates a demand-side flexibility requirement that could be partially satisfied by hybrid hydro-storage systems and (3) the remote-disconnect and PMU mandates on loads set operational-control expectations that may extend to dispatchable generation. Stakeholders expressed concerns about applicability, transition, and specific language; feedback has influenced the revised Tariff language.

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 08b Large Load Operational Forecast Requirements (LLWG 2026 4A)
documents.filename: 20260629 LLWG Item 08b Large Load Operational Forecast Requirements (LLWG-2026-4A).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large load forecasting requirements directly affect hydro's operational flexibility value and dispatch obligations, and the rule design (monitoring, incentives, settlement potential) may establish precedent for how variable/dispatchable resources' forecast accuracy is treated in MISO's commitment and real-time dispatch processes.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes to require large loads (data centers, industrial facilities, campuses) to submit short-term operational forecasts at hourly (next 1–168 hours) and 5-minute (next 6 hours) granularities, feeding into MISO's Multi-Day/Next-Day/Intraday reliability commitment and look-ahead commitment/real-time dispatch processes. Forecasts are submitted at the Commercial Pricing (CP) node level by the Tariff Customer; MISO will monitor timeliness and accuracy against telemetered actual load and is considering incentive mechanisms (ranging from escalation to settlement-based penalties). Status: requirements finalized for LLWG feedback by July 13, 2026; accuracy incentives and BPM implementation to follow. For hydro operators, the mechanism is load-side variability management: large loads add volatile demand that MISO's standard weather/calendar forecasts cannot capture; if these forecasts are inaccurate, MISO's commitment stack and reserve procurement tighten or loosen unpredictably, affecting the scarcity signals and ramping/flexibility value hydro receives in real-time and look-ahead markets. The settlement-based penalty option (SPP precedent cited) could create a parallel market-discipline framework applied to demand-side forecast accuracy, potentially informing future treatment of hydro forecast errors and opportunity-cost offers.

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 09 Large Load Reserve Product Enhancements (LLWG 2026 2)
documents.filename: 20260629 LLWG Item 09 Large Load Reserve Product Enhancements (LLWG-2026-2).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's revised methodology for quantifying reserve requirements (Regulation Reserve, Ramp Capability, Short-Term Reserve) directly affects how uncertainty and variability from large loads are valued in reserve-product pricing and procurement — a core revenue stream for hydro and pumped storage, which are traditional suppliers of all three products.
documents.ai_summary: MISO has established a dynamic reserve requirement methodology that quantifies net uncertainty and derives seasonal reserve requirements for Regulation Reserve (4 sec–5 min timeframe), Ramp Capability Product (10 min), and Short-Term Reserve (30 min–3 hr), with requirements varying by season, region, and time of day. Large loads exhibiting non-conforming behavior (volatile industrial or storage-adjacent profiles) were evaluated as a source of net-load and tie-line uncertainty; however, MISO's correlation analysis (sampling February–November 2025) found weak or negligible relationships between non-conforming load profiles and reserve-product uncertainty requirements across all three products, suggesting existing large loads have limited impact on reserve sizing to date. MISO will continue to monitor uncertainty patterns periodically and revise reserve methodologies if non-conforming load impact reaches criticality. Stakeholder feedback (six respondents, May 2026) was mostly supportive, and further engagement will occur at the Large Load Working Group.

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 09 Reserve Product Enhancement (LLWG 2026 2) Feedback Response (20260514)
documents.filename: 20260629 LLWG Item 09 Reserve Product Enhancement (LLWG-2026-2) Feedback Response_(20260514).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO is evaluating reserve product enhancements and regulation requirements to manage load variability from large data-center and computational loads, which directly affects how much regulation and ramping reserve MISO procures and prices — the demand curve for these products that conventional hydro and pumped storage depend on for revenue.
documents.ai_summary: MISO is developing reserve product enhancements to accommodate fast ramp rates and variability from large loads (data centers, computational facilities), with focus on regulation (4-second dispatch cycle, 5-minute deployment) and contingency-reserve upward and downward directions. Stakeholder feedback from six participants—mostly supportive of MISO's approach—pushed MISO to evaluate whether existing initiatives (RSC-2024-1/2, MSC-2026-1) already suffice before adding new products, and to allocate reserve costs to the loads driving demand rather than socializing them. MISO commits to reserve-enhancement evaluation updates and cost-allocation recommendations at July or August 2026 and later in 2026 LLWG meetings. The mechanism: larger, more variable loads will increase MISO's procurement of regulation and ramping reserves; if MISO adds new reserve products or expands regulation requirements, traditional hydro and PSH owners will face higher competition for those product revenues and greater uncertainty in which resources are called upon to provide regulation—particularly affecting resources with ramp-rate and regulation-eligibility constraints.

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 06 Market Participation and Registration of Co Located Load and Generation (PAC 2024 4B)
documents.filename: 20260629 LLWG Item 06 Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation (PAC-2024-4B).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's Zero-Injection GIA (ZGIA) framework directly affects co-located generation and load registration, which creates new revenue and settlement rules for hydropower and storage resources operating at or near large industrial loads, and establishes precedent for how RTOs treat behind-the-meter or tightly co-located hybrid resources.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes to require Zero-Injection GIAs (generation interconnection agreements where generation is co-located with large industrial loads) whose associated load exceeds the Large Load threshold to register as market resources. Grandfathered ZGIAs already accepted by FERC are exempt. MISO proposes no time limit on ZGIAs and will clarify that loads are firm NITS loads paying gross transmission charges. Market registration will ensure both load and generation volumes are reported separately for accurate ancillary-service charge allocation, affecting settlement and LMP exposure. This proposal responds to FERC's recent Show Cause Order. Stakeholder feedback is due July 15, 2026, with tariff filing expected July 2026. The mechanism matters most to pumped-storage operators and conventional hydro owners considering co-location with or service to large loads (data centers, manufacturing, etc.); the registration and metering rules will determine whether co-located generation can capture locational price signals or is treated as internal to the load node.

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 08a Large Load Telemetry and PMU Requirements (LLWG 2026 4C)
documents.filename: 20260629 LLWG Item 08a Large Load Telemetry and PMU Requirements (LLWG-2026-4C).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's telemetry and PMU requirements for large loads will impose new operational compliance obligations on hydropower and storage facilities co-located with or behind the same point of interconnection as computational loads, and may affect dispatch visibility and real-time market participation for generation resources at large-load sites.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes mandatory real-time SCADA telemetry (MW, MVAR, breaker status via ICCP) for all large loads and generation resources ≥20 MVA sharing the same point of interconnection, plus PMU installation (sampling at 60 Hz minimum in IEEE C37.118 format) at computational large loads before service commencement. Generation behind the large load's POI cannot be netted with load and must stream individual telemetry including state-of-charge data for battery or storage devices, and control-mode telemetry for market-participating generators. MISO requests feedback by July 13, 2026, with detailed requirements to be drafted in Business Practice Manuals. Pumped-storage facilities and conventional hydro units co-located at large-load sites (especially data-center campuses) will face new capital costs (PMU installation and ICCP comms) and operational constraints (non-netting, continuous dispatch visibility, reduced operational flexibility).

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 02a Steering Committee Status Update
documents.filename: 20260629 LLWG Item 02a Steering Committee Status Update.pdf

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 05 Legal Review of Show Cause Order Presentation.pdf
documents.filename: 20260629 LLWG Item 05 Legal Review of Show Cause Order Presentation.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: FERC Show Cause Order directs MISO to redesign interconnection processes and transmission service offerings for large co-located generation and load, which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage facilities can interconnect, participate in co-location arrangements, and be studied for network impacts—a strategic option under interconnection and deliverability rules.
documents.ai_summary: FERC issued a Show Cause Order (June 18, 2026, Docket EL26-70-000) directing MISO to revise its tariff to accommodate large loads through tailored interconnection processes for electrically proximate and co-located generation-load pairs, transmission service studies that reflect limited network scope, and transparency mechanisms for upgrade costs. MISO proposes a Zone Generation Interconnection Agreement (ZGIA) service type and expedited study process, but must reconcile its approach with FERC's specific requirements around interim/permanent service options, remote disconnection capability, and escalating readiness milestones. MISO's informational report is due July 18, 2026, with abeyance request August 2, 2026, and compliance filing August 17, 2026 (absent abeyance)—this timeline affects whether pumped-storage and other hydro facilities seeking co-location or electrically proximate generation arrangements will face revised study procedures and may open faster interconnection pathways for hydro behind large-load nodes.

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 01c Minutes 20260610.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO is developing Zero-Injection Generator Interconnection Agreements (ZGIAs) to enable co-located load-and-generation market participation, which directly affects hydro and pumped-storage strategic options for hybrid/co-located resource models and load-following services.
documents.ai_summary: MISO is advancing a Zero-Injection GIA (ZGIA) framework to permit market participation and registration of co-located load and generation resources, with draft tariff language posted and a planned FERC filing at end of June 2026. The proposal follows six months of collaborative stakeholder input and includes interconnection process, market participation, and registration mechanics. For hydro and pumped-storage owners, ZGIAs create a participation pathway for co-located load (e.g., data centers, industrial hydro-adjacent facilities) to be served behind-the-meter or via dedicated interconnection without exporting net energy to the grid, potentially increasing asset utilization and revenue optionality for hybrid facilities and enabling PSH charging load registration. The framework is near completion; the June filing will trigger FERC review and any final stakeholder feedback window.

documents.title: 20260629 LLWG Item 04 Liaison Report.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's Large Load Working Group is developing interconnection frameworks, co-located resource rules (ZGIA), and reserve product enhancements that directly affect how pumped-storage and conventional hydropower can participate in markets and operate at existing or new facility locations; operational requirements for large loads including ride-through and stability control establish precedent for dispatchable resource standards.
documents.ai_summary: MISO is pursuing a three-phase framework for rapid large-load integration: Connect (120-day GIA completion), Transition (flexible service options including firm step-up), and Ensure Long-Term Reliability (real-time coordination and reserve product enhancements). The ZGIA (zero-injection GIA) tariff language covers market participation, resource adequacy treatment, ride-through, and stability requirements for co-located generation and load; initial proposal was filed with FERC in June 2026, with a non-firm service option and firm service step-up option timeline influenced by FERC show-cause order review (target July 2026 filing). Co-located resource rules and the firm service step-up option create new pathways for PSH and hydro to participate in MISO markets with flexible injection/withdrawal capability, while operational reliability requirements (SCADA telemetry, PMU standards, dynamic reserve preparedness) establish obligations that will apply to dispatchable and storage-like resources. The Steering Committee special meeting on July 7, 2026, will determine the forum for continued ZGIA discussion and review LLWG charter.

title: WMWG Meeting
meeting_hydro_reason: ERCOT's Wholesale Market Working Group addresses energy price formation, ancillary services, storage participation, and flexibility products that serve as cross-market design benchmarks for hydro-rich RTOs tracking alternative market structures.

documents.title: Wind Power Forecast Monthly May 2026 WMWG
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Wind forecasting performance is a variable-generation monitoring metric with no direct bearing on hydro or PSH revenue, obligations, or strategic options—ERCOT has no hydropower fleet, and wind-forecast accuracy does not affect energy price formation, ancillary-service pricing, capacity accreditation, storage participation rules, or transmission/seams governance.

documents.title: Solar Power Forecast Monthly May 2026 WMWG
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Solar forecasting methodology and accuracy metrics have no bearing on hydropower revenue, compliance, or strategic options — this is fuel-specific reporting for a non-hydro resource with no cross-resource market-design implications.

documents.title: Intra-Hour IRR Forecast Accuracy May 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's intra-hour wind and solar ramp-rate forecasting accuracy directly affects how much ramping and flexibility capability must be procured to cover variable-resource uncertainty — a cross-market comparison point for how RTOs handle forecast-driven flexibility procurement, relevant to hydro's role as the primary ramp supplier in markets where it exists.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT Operations Analysis reports May 2026 performance of its intra-hour ramp-rate forecasting (PWRR for wind, PSRR for solar) used in 5-minute SCED dispatch. Wind ramp forecasts showed 88 MW/5-min mean absolute error (vs. 119 MW for a zero-ramp baseline); solar ramp forecasts showed 222 MW/5-min error (vs. 400 MW baseline). When actual ramps exceed 100 MW, forecast errors widen significantly (wind: 131 MW, solar: 256–257 MW). This is a reference document for how ERCOT's demand for flexibility products derives from variable-resource forecasting — in hydro-rich markets, superior forecast accuracy could reduce the volume and cost of ramping procurements that hydro resources supply. No action items or next steps are stated in the excerpt.

documents.title: Monthly Review of RUC Market Impacts - May 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's Reliability Unit Commitment (RUC) market triggers price adders for energy and ancillary services during scarcity; this cross-market comparison shows how ERCOT's commitment and uplift mechanics differ from hydro-centric RTOs, informing NHA's understanding of alternative market-design approaches to reliability procurement and cost recovery.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's May 2026 RUC market review reports 386.9 effective resource-hours committed, with 78% addressing capacity needs and most commitments (71%) issued with lead-time margins of 2 hours or less. RUC triggered 181.2 Reliability Deployment Price Adder (RDPA) hours at a time-weighted average of $0.75/MWh for energy, plus ancillary-service RDPAs averaging $0.13–$0.18/MWh across regulation, responsive reserve, and contingency reserve. Make-whole payments totaled $1.14M in May; 77% of committed resource-hours came from generators at least 50 years old, predominantly non-opt-out resources. For hydro-centric markets, this illustrates ERCOT's reliance on must-offer thermal capacity and uplift cost-recovery rather than dynamic AS procurement or storage participation—a design that contrasts with how CAISO, NYISO, and PJM price flexibility and commitment from dispatchable and storage resources.

documents.title: Market Update May 2026 WMWG
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document is a routine market operations report with no substantive content on pricing mechanisms, ancillary services, capacity accreditation, storage participation, interconnection, transmission, or resource-adequacy rules affecting hydropower or pumped-storage revenue, obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: WMWG Meeting Agenda 2026-06-29
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT agenda includes multiple ancillary-service and storage-related proposals (Advanced Grid Support Service, Generation Firming Program, ERS enhancements) that set precedent for how flexible and dispatchable resources participate in markets — relevant as cross-RTO comparison for NHA's analytical corpus even though Texas has no hydropower fleet.
documents.ai_summary: The ERCOT WMWG agenda for June 29, 2026 includes discussion of the 2027 ancillary-services methodology (Luis Hinojosa, ERCOT presenter); NPRR 1278 proposing a new Advanced Grid Support Service as an ancillary service (Bob Helton, Engie); NPRR 1328 on a Generation Firming Program (Anupam Thatte, ERCOT); and ERS Enhancements under NPRR 1337 (Mark Patterson, ERCOT). The agenda also covers monthly renewable-forecast accuracy, intra-hour IRR forecasts, and discussion of FPA 202(c) orders. No vote dates or comment deadlines are specified in this agenda document. These proposals — particularly the Advanced Grid Support Service and Generation Firming Program — illustrate how ERCOT is structuring new products for flexible/dispatchable resources; the mechanisms differ from hydro-rich RTOs and provide a comparative baseline for how fast-response and storage-like capabilities are accredited and priced in a largely thermal/renewable market.

documents.title: DRAFT 1328NPRR-12 ERCOT comments 06XX26
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's Generation Firming Program is a Texas-specific reserve obligation targeting thermal and wind resources during low-reserve hours; it does not establish mechanisms affecting hydropower revenue streams, storage participation rules, or strategic options that would transfer to hydro-rich RTOs, and Texas has no material hydropower fleet.

documents.title: 2027_AS_Methodology_WMWG_062926
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's 2027 ancillary-services methodology revisions—particularly changes to Regulation Service, RRS, and probabilistic reserve procurement—set a cross-market design precedent for how system operators address wind/solar ramps, large-load variability, and frequency response adequacy; NHA tracks ERCOT's solutions to these problems because similar frameworks are adopted or debated in hydro-rich RTOs.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT is considering methodology changes for 2027 ancillary-services procurement, with focus on Regulation Service (adding a potential Large Load adjustment factor for ramps exceeding 400 MW), Responsive Reserve Service (pending NERC's 2027 BAL-003 IFRO assessment), and refinement of the 2026 probabilistic framework for ECRS and Non-Spinning Reserve. The presentation notes that current Regulation methodology accounts for Large Load ramping only through the NLFE term but acknowledges extreme downward ramps (to –61 MW in some hours) with greater outliers than upward ramps, suggesting a dedicated adjustment may be needed. ERCOT is also exploring whether to move solar/wind calculations from Historical Scheduled Load (HSL) to Gross Real-Time Power Potential (GRPP) to better capture intra-hour renewable ramps. The probabilistic reserve methodology is undergoing sensitivity analysis on risk thresholds (Watch/EEA1/EEA2/Load Shed vs. frequency of violations), look-ahead periods (1–6 hours), risk credits, and tail-event treatment of net load forecast errors. Stakeholder meetings continue through August 2026, with final board vote scheduled for September 15, 2026.

documents.title: 1337NPRR-05 ERCOT Comments 062626 (003)
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT Emergency Response Service (ERS) is a demand-response / load-shedding procurement mechanism with no hydro analog; the technical revisions to baseline calculations, availability factors, and payment formulas apply only to load-serving entities and QSEs managing interruptible load, not to generation or storage resources.

documents.title: ERS Enhancements_6292026 meeting (002)
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's ERS (Energy Reserve Service) enhancements and baseline-qualification mechanics address demand-response and load-curtailment products unique to ERCOT's retail-adjacent market; no hydro or pumped-storage revenue stream, obligation, or strategic option is affected, and the price-chasing detection logic applies only to price-responsive loads (crypto mining, data centers, manufacturing), not to dispatchable generation or storage.

documents.title: ERS_Alternate Baseline Test_Concept_Paper
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document addresses demand-response baseline methodology for ERCOT's Emergency Response Service (ERS), which is a load-reduction program with no direct hydropower or pumped-storage participation pathways, revenue streams, or obligations.

documents.title: Suspension and Reinstatement Procedures_draft_6252026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT Emergency Response Service (ERS) suspension and reinstatement procedures apply to demand-response and distributed load/generation resources in Texas; hydro and pumped-storage assets do not participate in ERS, and the document contains no cross-market design precedent or comparative analysis relevant to hydro revenue, obligations, or strategic options elsewhere.

documents.title: 2027_AS_Methodology_WMWG_062926_v2
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's 2027 ancillary-services methodology governs procurement of Regulation, Responsive Reserve Service (frequency response), and Contingency/Non-Spinning reserves — products hydro supplies nationwide and that set market-design precedent; while Texas has no hydro fleet, NHA tracks ERCOT's reserve-procurement framework, ramp-response logic, and treatment of large-load variability as comparative reference for hydro-rich RTOs.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT is refining its 2027 ancillary-services methodology with one material change: addition of a Large Load Adjustment factor to Regulation Service requirements to account for extreme 5-minute ramps from data-center and non-conforming loads that can exceed 400 MW and are currently underrepresented in the net-load-forecast-error (NLFE) term. ERCOT is also tuning its probabilistic framework for Contingency and Non-Spinning reserves by testing sensitivity to risk thresholds (Watch / EEA1 / EEA2 / Load Shed), look-ahead periods (1–6 hours), risk-credit levels (current 60% night / 25% day), and tail-event distribution methods; it is investigating migration from HSL (historical scheduled level) to GRPP (Gross Real-Time Power Potential) for solar and wind adjustments. Comparative interest: ERCOT's modeling of large-load variability and its probabilistic criteria tuning (probability of violation targets of 1-in-10-year to 1-in-1-year) show how an inflexible-load-heavy system approaches reserve adequacy differently from hydro-rich grids where demand variability is less extreme. Timeline: WMWG discussion today (June 29, 2026); WMS and ROS votes August 5–6; TAC vote August 26; Board approval September 15; PUC filing November–December 2026.

documents.title: Agenda - Extended Day-Ahead Market - Virtual Town Hall - Feb 04, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: EDAM launch and governance directly shape Western hydro's market participation, revenue recognition, and interregional trading optionality—a core strategic choice for NHA members.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO is hosting a virtual town hall on February 4, 2026 to present the Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) ahead of its planned May 2026 launch. The agenda covers EDAM governance evolution, transmission connectivity and coordination across market seams (WEIM/EDAM), subscriber participation models, and implementation status. For Western hydropower owners, EDAM is the successor architecture to the WEIM, reshaping how hydro-rich jurisdictions participate in interregional energy markets, access scarcity pricing, and coordinate dispatch across state lines. The meeting will address governance independence at FERC and stakeholder roles—key institutional questions for NHA members deciding market membership and participation strategy in the West.

documents.ai_summary: PJM's Interconnection Process Subcommittee is evaluating options packages for expanding provisional service — a queue-reform mechanism that allows generation and storage to begin operations before full interconnection studies conclude, accelerating revenue realization. This document presents a matrix of alternative implementations affecting study timelines, cost allocation, and deliverability risk for both conventional and storage resources. The specific package designs (scope, triggering conditions, cost-sharing rules, and performance guarantees) will determine how quickly new hydro capacity or PSH uprates can reach commercial operation and begin earning, and how much study cost and operational risk falls on the resource owner versus the network. Most exposed are owners pursuing interconnection for new-build PSH or conventional hydro uprates in queued positions; next steps and decision dates are not stated in this excerpt.

documents.title: Item 03 – Cycle Schedule Update

documents.ai_summary: PJM Interconnection has posted Transition Cycle 2 Phase II Studying & Information (SIS) reports covering 270 projects (June 5, 2026) and opened Decision Point II (DP2) from June 8–July 7, 2026, during which project developers must confirm participation and modifications via NextGen to proceed to Phase III SIS (starting July 8). Cycle 01 applications total 829 requests (811 generator interconnections at 220,227 MWE cumulative), with deficiency reviews underway and scoping meetings anticipated to start end-Q2; Cycle 01 model posting and Phase I study commencement are targeted for Q3 2026. PJM also offers an Interim Deliverability Study for the 2027/28 in-service window (application deadline June 30, 2026; results October 2026), which allows projects to demonstrate reliable delivery before required network upgrades are completed—a pathway valuable to hydro developers needing early commercial operation. Hydro and pumped-storage owners in PJM should resolve deficiency notices in NextGen and monitor DP2 elections (closing July 7) and interim-study deadlines (June 30) to preserve queue position and deliverability timing.

documents.title: Item 04 – Market Participation Options For Mixed Technology Facilities
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Defines participation pathways (co-located vs. hybrid models) and capacity/must-offer rules affecting how mixed-technology resources—including pumped-storage hydro paired with other resources—can be classified, accredited, and dispatched in PJM markets, with direct implications for hybrid PSH revenue and operational flexibility.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Interconnection Process Subcommittee presented market participation options for Mixed Technology Facilities (MTF)—facilities with multiple generation or storage types behind a single interconnection point. Resources can participate as independent Co-Located Resources (each a separate market unit) or as a Hybrid Resource (single market unit with unit-specific accreditation). A closed-loop hybrid with storage can charge from grid; an open-loop hybrid cannot. Facilities with independent components can elect either model; those with significant interdependency (e.g., storage unable to charge from grid) must use the hybrid model. Classification must be declared 6 months before in-service date; capacity resources must choose the same model for both capacity and energy markets and must re-elect within RAA Schedule 9.2 deadlines; energy-only resources can change election once per calendar year with notice by May 30. For any MTF, the site Maximum Facility Output (MFO) cannot be exceeded in real time and capacity-must-offer obligations apply to the original Certified Interconnection Rights. The four use cases illustrate that inverter-gen + storage (like solar + battery or wind + battery) and non-inverter gen + storage (gas + battery) can form hybrids, while non-inverter gen + inverter gen (gas + solar) must participate as co-located resources. This framework directly affects pumped-storage hydro owners considering co-location with other generation or considering participation in paired-resource models, constraining how PSH state-of-charge management interacts with capacity accreditation and must-offer rules.

documents.title: Item 05 – Nextgen Updates Dp2 Reminders
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This document describes updates to PJM's NextGen interconnection application system, which directly affects the operational and compliance obligations for all interconnection applicants including hydropower and pumped-storage projects seeking queue entry, site-control documentation, and progression through decision points.
documents.ai_summary: PJM has released updates to its NextGen interconnection platform affecting how applicants submit and manage interconnection requests. Key changes include: (1) change-history tracking by page with user attribution; (2) PJM's ability to edit certain application fields (company profile, applicant information, and banking data excepted) with email notification to applicants; (3) kickoff-call notes now visible to all parties (PJM, transmission owner, project developer); (4) restricted editing until Decision Point 2 election is made; (5) limited document uploads during DP2 site-control and supporting-documents phases; and (6) stricter submission validation requiring all sections saved and deficiency comments resolved before the submit button enables. A known bug affecting nearly all TC2 projects (insufficient-funds-deposit warning for TC2 study deposits) does not invalidate DP2 submissions. Timeline: Replacement Generation went live April 2026; Expedited Interconnection Track is July 2026; Surplus goes live August/September 2026; Upgrade track rollout TBD. Hydro and PSH applicants must follow these new workflows to advance projects through the interconnection queue; any deficiencies in the submission process or misunderstanding of the new validation requirements could delay or jeopardize project advancement.

documents.ai_summary: PJM's Resource Adequacy Analysis Subcommittee will discuss inputs to an upcoming FPR (Forced Performance Rate) and ELCC (Effective Load-Carrying Capability) run on June 26, 2026. The ELCC accreditation process is how PJM values hydro and pumped-storage capacity contribution to resource adequacy — changes to FPR/ELCC methodology or inputs can shift the capacity credit assigned to conventional hydro and PSH, altering their RA revenue and bid curves. The meeting is for planning purposes (no decision or vote is scheduled in this agenda), but the FPR/ELCC run itself will reset hydro accreditation factors used in PJM's 2026–2027 capacity auction.

documents.title: Item 3 – Update On Inputs For Upcoming Fpr ELCC Run

documents.ai_summary: PJM is running the Effective Load Carrying Capability (ELCC) and Forced Penetration Ratio (FPR) analysis for the 2029/30 Delivery Year BRA (Capacity Auction) with updated load forecasts. The 2029/30 load shapes show lower summer and higher winter extreme loads compared to 2028/29, with winter peak risk 8.3 percentage points higher in the model — putting upward pressure on overall system risk and the Installed Reserve Margin (IRM). No procedural changes to the ELCC methodology are planned relative to the prior run. For hydro and PSH owners, the winter-heavy load shape directly influences accreditation of winter-peaking and seasonal-flexible resources; PJM expects to release the IRM and FPR first read and endorsement at the MRC/MC in July/August 2026. Deadlines for resource-portfolio lock-in include Notice of Intent to Offer (June 12, 2026), Dual Fuel Attestation (June 15, 2026), and Transitional Deliverability Study and Announced Deactivation Review (both June 2026).

documents.title: 2__SAWG_2026_LTLF_Update_6-26-2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT load forecast directly informs regional resource-adequacy and scarcity conditions that drive energy and ancillary-services prices and reliability decisions affecting comparable hydro-rich markets' strategic dispatch and reserve margins.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's 2026 Long-Term Load Forecast (LTLF) update revises demand projections across major components: summer base load ranges from 86.2–89.3 GW (2026) depending on 4CP demand-response assumptions; rooftop solar contribution is lowered due to reduced federal/state incentives; EV load rises modestly despite charging constraints; crypto load grows to ~6–10 GW with price-responsive behavior peaking on high-temperature days. Combined effect: by 2031, the crypto-plus-base net outlook reaches 95+ GW summer peak. Large-load values will be finalized via Batch Zero process after June 18 Commission guidance. This forecast sets the framework for ERCOT resource-adequacy requirements and scarcity pricing conditions; cross-market analysts tracking how demand growth (especially price-responsive crypto load) reshapes peaker economics and reserve-margin tightness will find the refined crypto and 4CP modeling instructive as a comparison point to hydro-centric RTOs' treatment of flexible, temperature-correlated, and demand-response-sensitive load.

documents.title: 3__SAWG_2025_DG-Report_NPRR1265_6-26-2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's distributed-generator reporting rule (NPRR1265) and unregistered DG tracking framework sets a cross-market precedent for how small, distributed storage and variable resources integrate into system planning; the battery and renewable data and reporting timelines are benchmarks for hydro-rich markets considering similar DER participation frameworks.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Supply Analysis Working Group presented annual distributed-generator estimates (2015–2025) and the implementation timeline for NPRR1265, which establishes reporting requirements for unregistered distributed generators (≤1 MW) beginning in 2027. NPRR1265 requires distribution service providers to report aggregated UDG data to transmission service providers by February 1, 2027, with TSP submission to ERCOT by March 30, 2027; UDG data will be included in ERCOT planning cases by October 2027. The rule data show rapid growth in small distributed battery (from 0 MW in 2015 to 590 MW by 2025 in units <1 MW) and solar, revealing how small storage and variable resources are being modeled in a capacity-constrained, non-hydro RTO. For hydro-rich RTOs (WEIM, NYISO, PJM) considering Order 2222 DER aggregation or small-hydro participation pathways, this ERCOT case demonstrates how a high-penetration DG environment drives system-modeling requirements and reporting cadence; the battery growth trajectory is directionally relevant to understanding storage participation pressure across markets.

documents.title: 4__SAWG_2026_Activity_Update_6-26-2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's resource-adequacy methodology, load-forecasting approach, and CONE study directly parallel NHA members' exposure to capacity/RA mechanisms and scarcity pricing in other RTOs; the Batch Zero large-load interconnection process and load-growth forecasting are strategic context for how demand growth shapes the value of existing hydro assets across RTOs.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Supply Analysis Working Group updated its 2026 Triennial Reliability Assessment and NERC Long-Term Reliability Assessment methodology. Key decisions: adoption of Batch Zero large-load forecast (with initial data by August 7, 2026) and 'studied load' bookend scenarios for reliability modeling; reference-margin-level targets of 22% summer and 20% winter for the 2027–2036 assessment period; selection of a large-frame combustion turbine (GE 7HA.03) as the reference technology for a revised CONE study (results expected September 25, 2026); and implementation of an Emergency Pricing Plan post-processing mechanism and optimized reserve-program dispatch (including DRRS) during scarcity. The load-growth methodology and CONE updates will affect how reserve margins and capacity valuations are set in ERCOT's reliability planning—a comparison point for NHA members evaluating how peer RTOs handle seasonal RA, scarcity pricing, and the interaction of large-load growth with resource-adequacy frameworks that may assign lower accreditation to hydro and storage.

documents.title: Item 02 – Pjms Rcstf Proposal Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's proposal restructures reserve products and requirements, introducing new ramping and uncertainty reserves (10-min and 30-min RUR) with locational procurement and 10-min response times that will directly affect hydro and PSH revenue and dispatch obligations.
documents.ai_summary: PJM proposes restructuring its reserve construct to align scheduling with operational needs and address growing ramping and uncertainty requirements. The proposal introduces three new reserve products—10-minute Ramp and Uncertainty (RUR), 10-minute Ramp and Uncertainty Down, and 30-minute RUR—each with specified offer caps ($10/MWh day-ahead, $0 real-time), balancing settlement, and locational procurement. These products carry fast response requirements (10–30 min) that hydro and PSH are positioned to supply, and are priced at 1.25× the clearing price or cost-to-provide, whichever is higher. The proposal also shifts Day-Ahead Scheduling Reserve from a fixed 4.51%-of-peak formula to a dynamic, risk-based framework classified by operating-day conditions. Hydro and pumped-storage operators will directly participate in procurement of these ramping/flexibility products, which represent new revenue streams for fast-responding resources; exposure depends on portfolio flexibility and locational advantage. The document does not state a vote date or comment deadline.

issues.canonical_name: Reserve Certainty and Resource Flexibility Incentives

documents.title: Item 03a – Imm Rcstf Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's reserve product redesign proposal directly affects hydro revenue through reserve-clearing incentives, online/offline reserve mix, and reserve performance accountability — reserve-eligible hydro and PSH earn revenue from reserve provision and market-efficiency changes that alter clearing prices and call-out frequency.
documents.ai_summary: The Independent Market Monitor (IMM) presents its interim reserve-redesign proposal for PJM's Reliability Committee, opposing PJM's parallel proposal. The IMM recommends three sequential implementation packages: (1) incorporating net load forecast uncertainty into reserve calculations (near-term priority); (2) reserve performance measurement and settlement changes; (3) reserve eligibility and market-clearing engine changes. The critical difference is the online/offline reserve mix: PJM proposes all 10-minute reserves be online-only, significantly raising production costs and clearing prices; the IMM proposes maintaining offline reserve capability (primary reserves) and synchronized (online) reserves separately. The IMM contends PJM's approach reduces market efficiency and jeopardizes reliability if current fast-start generators drop fast-start capability to avoid the online-reserve requirement. Solar generation in PJM has grown dramatically (14.3 GW maximum hourly output in 2026, 2.4% of annual generation), driving net-load uncertainty that both proposals address but with different reserve adequacy methods — the IMM's approach captures generator performance uncertainty in 30-minute reserves and includes demand response as eligible supply. No vote date or next-step deadline is stated; the presentation positions the IMM proposal as the operational-necessity baseline against PJM's preferred-design alternative.

documents.title: Updated Information Only – Rt Market Pricing Impact Multi Step Ordcs With Extended Requirements 06092026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Operating Reserve Demand Curve (ORDC) restructuring changes ancillary-services revenue for reserve suppliers; multi-step ORDCs and unnesting of synchronized reserves affects how hydro's reserve products are priced and when they clear, with particular impact on 10-minute and 30-minute reserve pricing and frequency of scarcity events.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Advanced Analytics team analyzed 2025 real-time market pricing impacts from proposed multi-step Operating Reserve Demand Curves (ORDCs) unnesting 10-minute Synchronized Reserve from 30-minute reserves and creating explicit 30-minute and 10-minute Online Reserve products. The Full Case scenario (updated ORDCs with Primary Reserve not clearing) showed 10-minute Online reserve prices rising from $10.25/MWh to $13.04/MWh (+$2.79), energy LMPs rising $0.80/MWh, uplift declining 3.8%, and load reserve payments rising $216M for 10-minute Online reserves—with greatest price pressure during peak hours when reserves are most needed. The simulation found that prepositioning units with ramping products in the multi-step ORDC case reduced scarcity-event frequency compared to the base case, particularly during two modeled shortage events (3/3/2025 and 8/12/2025), suggesting improved reserve-positioning efficiency, though the analysis carries limitations (PROBE PD simulation tool, combustion-turbine-only commitment, 30-minute Online requirement added to SR requirement rather than separately modeled). Document presented to RCSTF with no stated vote or deadline; further results posted post-presentation.

documents.title: 2026-06-25 RC A05.1 Planning Procedures 5-1 and 5-3
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE is creating a new requirement for large load interconnections (≥20 MW) to submit Proposed Plan Applications, which affects hydro owners' strategic options by changing transmission planning visibility for major loads that can significantly impact regional scarcity and dispatch value of existing generation.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes updates to Planning Procedures 5-1 and 5-3 to enhance the Proposed Plan Application (PPA) process, including a new form and requirements for large load facilities (≥20 MW peak demand). The changes formalize how new or materially changed large loads must be evaluated for their impact on system stability and reliability before interconnection, with material changes including capacity increases ≥1 MW and changes to load function (e.g., cryptocurrency mining, AI training). This makes large-load interconnections visible in the planning framework for the first time, allowing hydro owners to anticipate transmission congestion and scarcity conditions that affect energy pricing and dispatch opportunity. The proposal targets September 2026 implementation, with redlines and draft PPA forms planned for the July 2026 Reliability Committee meeting.

documents.title: 2026-06-25 RC A01.1 MC/RC May 12-14, 2026 Meeting Minutes
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms—Seasonal Accreditation (CAR-SA) restructures how hydro and other dispatchable resources qualify for and clear seasonal capacity obligations, directly affecting accreditation thresholds, outage-treatment rules, and capacity-market revenue for smaller hydro assets and pumped storage.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE staff presented the resource-qualification framework for CAR-SA, which eliminates the existing Estimated Qualified Capacity (QC) process, imposes a 100 kW Marginal Reliability Impact Capacity (MRIC) minimum for standalone participation, and removes grandfathering for legacy sub-100 kW resources. Committee members, including representatives of generation owners and municipal utilities, raised material concerns about the disqualification of 60–80 smaller legacy resources unless they aggregate into distributed capacity structures, treatment of planned and partial outages (especially long-term equipment outages), and the risk of seasonal capacity-clearing obligations during known extended unavailability. ISO-NE committed to stakeholder consultation on planned-outage and EFORD (forced-outage-rate) rules with the Independent Market Monitor but did not commit to changes; preliminary activity schedule for CAR-SA implementation is now under development with Summer 2028 as a truncated transition season. Small hydro and PSH operators below 100 kW threshold are most directly affected; aggregation and distributed-resource pathways remain open but require reconfiguration.

documents.title: 2026-06-25 RC A01.1 MC/RC May 21, 2026 Meeting Minutes
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE's seasonal tie-benefits and capacity-zone formation methodologies directly affect the accreditation and RA value of hydropower and pumped storage, particularly seasonal capacity imports from neighboring regions and zonal pricing signals that influence dispatch and revenue.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE's Reliability Committee discussed three capacity-market design topics with votes scheduled at future meetings. On seasonal tie benefits, the ISO confirmed that Capacity Import Capability (CIC) will be updated during intervening years using an annual sensitivity-study threshold, and that summer and winter tie benefits are calculated together in a single study aligned triennially with NYISO's model via the Long-Range Adequacy Overview (LRAO). On capacity-zone formation, the ISO proposed removing gas-only resources from objective-criteria testing to trigger price separation signals, but members from the Alternative Resources, Supplier, and End User sectors raised concerns: Alternative Resources opposed removing all gas-only resources; Suppliers requested application of the methodology to FCA 18 for transparency; and one Supplier advocated that all zones be modeled at all times. The removal of gas-only resources could reduce seasonal import capability assumptions and tighten capacity zones in winter months—raising accreditation and RA value for dispatchable resources including hydropower and pumped storage during peak-scarcity periods, particularly in constrained zones. Votes on both seasonal tie benefits and capacity-zone formation are scheduled for a future meeting (no specific date stated).

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE's Reliability Committee voted on three interconnection Proposed Plan Application (PPA) revisions: a battery project (QP 1320, Flatiron Energy and New England Power), a solar project with related interconnection updates (QP 1100, Central Maine Power), and an automatic voltage regulator (AVR) upgrade at Milford Power Plant (a synchronous generator). Each motion recommends ISO-NE find no significant adverse effect on transmission stability or reliability. These are routine reliability-screen passes in the interconnection queue, but the battery revision (FLA-24-G03-Rev1, FLA-24-T03-Rev1, NEP-24-T04-Rev1) is substantive: it signals ISO-NE's acceptance of battery participation models and charging-energy treatment precedents that shape how storage competes against pumped hydro for energy and ancillary-services revenue. The AVR upgrade at a conventional plant illustrates ISO-NE's reinforcement of synchronous-machine ride-through and voltage-support standards, which create implicit compliance costs for traditional hydro units. All three items have completed their June 2026 review cycle with approval.

documents.title: 2026-06-25 RC A06 Power Supply Planning Committee (PSPC) Kick-Off
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This document establishes the development schedule and parameters for ISO-NE's 2027 Annual Reconfiguration Auction (ARA 3), which determines the Installed Capacity Requirement (ICR), Hydro-Québec Interconnection Capability Credit (HQICC), Maximum Capacity Limit (MCL), and demand curves for the 2027–2028 capacity commitment period — directly affecting capacity accreditation, reserve requirements, and revenue for hydro and pumped-storage resources participating in ISO-NE's capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is launching development of capacity parameters for the 2027 Annual Reconfiguration Auction (ARA 3), with scheduled PSPC reviews from July through October 2026, RC and PC votes in October–November 2026, and FERC filing by November 30, 2026. The values under development include the Installed Capacity Requirement (ICR), Hydro-Québec Interconnection Capability Credit (HQICC), Maximum Capacity Limit (MCL), and System-Wide and Capacity Zone Marginal Reliability Impact demand curves — all determinants of how much capacity must be procured and how hydro and pumped-storage units are credited and compensated in the 2027–2028 auction. Hydro and PSH owners should monitor the July 1 PSPC meeting (load forecast and ADCR performance review), August 27 assumptions discussion, and October 15 proposed values review to understand accreditation adjustments and to submit input; the November 30 FERC filing date is the hard deadline for compliance.

documents.title: 2026-06-25 RC A07 2026 Long-Term Energy Assessments Sensitivity Process
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Long-term energy-adequacy assessment methodology and sensitivity analyses directly inform resource-adequacy constructs and the supply-adequacy signals that hydro operators depend on for scarcity-driven revenue and investment decisions; the sensitivity scenarios (load growth, resource retirements, import constraints) define the conditions under which hydro's flexibility and dispatchability are valued.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE has established a Probabilistic Energy Adequacy Tool (PEAT) and Regional Energy Shortfall Threshold (REST) framework to conduct 5- and 10-year energy assessments (study years 2031 and 2036). For 2026, the ISO is running 19 selected sensitivities spanning load variations (+10% net load, +100% large load), resource-mix changes (offshore-wind deletions up to 3,650 MW in 2036; RFO and NG retirements of 2,000–4,000 MW), and fuel/import constraints (-50% imports, ±10% oil/LNG inventory). The replacement strategy for retired resources uses interconnection-queue composition (70% batteries, 15% PV, 15% onshore wind) scaled by nameplate-to-capacity factors, not direct assumption of hydro replacement. For hydro and pumped-storage operators, the most material scenarios are the large-load and import-constrained sensitivities, which will reveal energy-shortfall risk periods and durations under high-scarcity conditions — data that affects seasonal accreditation exposure and the likelihood of sustained high-price regimes. The methodology explicitly excludes offshore wind from the replacement portfolio due to buildout uncertainty, leaving a net-generation gap in retirement scenarios that hydro supply could fill. No formal comment deadline or next action date is stated in this overview.

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE presented refinements to its Capacity Auction Reforms Seasonal Accreditation (CAR SA) proposal, addressing resource qualification under the continuous process: resources under 100 kW of Marginal Reliability Impact Capacity may aggregate to meet the 100 kW minimum threshold; prior FCM minimum-size exceptions will not carry forward; the Estimated Qualified Capacity process (which previously addressed limited audit data) will be eliminated; and composite offers will be discontinued due to separate seasonal clearing. These changes reshape qualification burden and aggregation rules for small hydro, run-of-river, and variable hydro operators, most of whom have historically relied on exception pathways or composite bidding. The document does not state a comment deadline or next vote date.

documents.title: 12-MIR9.3-R Dynamic Schedule Resource Pseudo-Ties submission.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Establishes registration and modeling framework for pseudo-tied external resources in Markets+, affecting how hydropower and other dispatchable resources outside the SPP footprint participate in the market and settle — a key strategic option for hydro owners considering multi-market participation.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ is codifying registration and tariff language for two forms of external-resource pseudo-ties: formal pseudo-tie agreements (where a Native BA and Attaining BA electronically tie a resource per NAESB standards) and dynamically scheduled pseudo-ties (where energy continues to be scheduled as a Dynamic Schedule between BAs but the resource is modeled as pseudo-tied into the Attaining BAA for Markets+ network and commercial models). The MIR adds 'dynamically scheduled pseudo-tie' as a resource designation and defines requirements: resource must be registered with the designation attribute, the Attaining BA must provide attestation, and the Meter Agent must submit export pseudo-tie quantity equal to resource output. This standardizes ad-hoc handling and reduces operational ambiguity ahead of Markets+ go-live. The proposal is ranked High priority; MPEC approval is sought for Q3 2026 with implementation target Q1 2027. Hydropower owners with resources outside the Markets+ footprint seeking to participate (e.g., Northwest hydro exporting into SPP territory, or PSH facilities straddling footprint boundaries) gain clarity on registration and settlement mechanics, and avoid manual workarounds that could create participation or liquidity friction.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Markets+ transmission and interconnection queue governance directly affects hydro deliverability, strategic options for resource participation, and transmission planning that allocates interregional transfer capability — core to NHA members' market-seams choices and transmission-dependent revenue.
documents.ai_summary: The Markets+ Transmission Working Group (MTWG) agenda for June 25, 2026 covers four substantive items with direct relevance to hydro and storage participation in the emerging Markets+ construct: (1) MMU Transmission Market Power Terminology (MIR32-R), establishing how market-power mitigation applies to use-limited resources; (2) OASIS Queue Pause (MIR82-R), addressing interconnection queue management and deliverability gating; (3) MTI/MOTI Update (MIR81-R, Option 1A), on market-to-interchange coordination protocol affecting how hydro exports across seams; and (4) Dynamic Schedule Resources and Pseudo-Ties (MIR9.2-R and MIR9.3-R), governing how variable and storage resources integrate operationally into the interchange and day-ahead market. These topics touch transmission planning, seams governance, interconnection queue administration, and the operational rules under which hydro must participate — all foundational to NHA members' decisions on Markets+ membership versus EDAM/WEIM participation. No comment deadlines or votes are stated in this agenda.

documents.title: 05-MIR82-R MTSP OASIS Queue Pause presentation_MTWG.pptx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Interconnection and transmission-service request processing during Markets+ Day-Ahead Market operations — changes to queue-pause design affect the timing and feasibility of transmission-service submissions for hydro generators seeking operational flexibility and transmission rights, and indirectly touch deliverability during scarcity events.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ is revising the OASIS Queue Pause design (MIR82-R) to allow individual Transmission Service Providers (MTSPs) to pause, limit, or manage transmission-service requests flexibly — rather than mandate a uniform pause — during the window between Day-Ahead Market close and Energy Schedule updates. The revision addresses NAESB WEQ 001-4.13 timing-compliance concerns raised by individual MTSPs and gives each MTSP discretion to document and implement queue-management procedures consistent with its operational requirements. MIR82-R was posted June 1, 2026, with comment deadline June 19, 2026; MTWG approval is scheduled June 25, 2026, followed by MDWG (July 8), MORWG (July 29), and MPEC (August 25, 2026). Hydro operators in SPP Markets+ should assess whether the proposed flexibility in queue management creates new opportunities or risks for intra-day transmission-service requests (e.g., for storage recharge or ramping adjustments) or whether MTSP-specific procedures may disadvantage certain resource types.

documents.title: 05-MIR82-R MTSP OASIS Queue Pause submission.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Interconnection queue management and transmission service provider operational procedures directly affect hydro project development timelines, queue priority, and market participation eligibility under Markets+ governance.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ MIR 82-R proposes replacing the mandatory MTSP queue-pause requirement with flexible queue-management discretion, allowing each Transmission Service Provider to pause, limit, or otherwise manage transmission service request processing between Day-Ahead Market close and schedule updates based on its own operational needs. The change enhances MTSP operational flexibility while requiring documented procedures and coordination with Market Operator timelines; it is ranked low priority with MPEC approval targeted Q3 2026 and implementation Q1 2027. For hydro developers and operators seeking transmission service interconnection in the Markets+ footprint, this shifts queue predictability from a uniform pause rule to provider-specific management protocols — potentially affecting interconnection timelines and the transparency of queue advancement between developers.

documents.title: 05-MIR82-R Puget Sound Energy Comments-20260609.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Queue-processing rules directly affect transmission interconnection timelines and deliverability coordination for new or upgraded hydro resources, and the flexibility granted to individual transmission service providers may alter how hydro interconnection requests are evaluated and sequenced relative to day-ahead market operations.
documents.ai_summary: Puget Sound Energy (Vincent Ching) comments on Markets+ Initiative Request MIR82-R, which proposes revising the MTSP OASIS Queue Pause language in the Markets+ Tariff and Protocols to permit individual transmission service providers (MTSPs) to flexibly manage transmission service request (TSR) processing rather than applying a universal pause requirement. Currently MTSPs must pause TSR processing between day-ahead market close and schedule updates; MIR82-R allows each MTSP to set its own queue-management conditions aligned with operational requirements, provided they document and publish procedures and coordinate with Market Operator timelines. PSE seeks clarification on protocol language defining 'queue processing,' pause conditions, and which TSR durations must be paused. The revision trades a predictable, universal pause schedule for operator-specific flexibility, which could affect hydro interconnection request timing and prioritization in a capacity-constrained Markets+ region.

documents.title: 06-Stakeholder Training Update.pptx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: SPP Markets+ is a strategic governance option for NHA members considering RTO membership and market participation in the Southwest Power Pool region; this training-readiness document illuminates which market-design concepts (must-offer obligations, GHG attribution, storage resource registration, transmission congestion, settlements) Markets+ participants must master, affecting entry barriers and operational complexity for hydro and PSH owners evaluating Markets+ membership.
documents.ai_summary: SPP's Stakeholder Training team is rolling out a multi-level training curriculum to onboard Markets+ participants ahead of go-live. Level 1 covers market fundamentals (energy markets, roles, DAM/RTBM/RUC components, settlements, congestion rent); Level 1.5 includes podcasts and registration deep-dives (Market Storage Resources, pseudo-tied assets, JOU/CIR configurations, DER); Level 2 offers virtual instructor-led topical courses (Transmission, GHG, Must-Offer, Day-Ahead/RUC/RTBM, Market Monitoring, BA Considerations, Settlements, Congestion Rent) with three-time scheduling starting late 2026; Level 3 will be task-based "day-in-the-life" modules (Analyze, Design, Develop, Implement, Evaluate). RC Services training on outage management, congestion, and communications begins October 2026. For PSH and hydro operators evaluating Markets+ membership, the curriculum's emphasis on storage-resource registration, must-offer rules, transmission-constraint management, and GHG tracking signals the operational and compliance obligations they will assume; the Level 2 and 3 course design (and timing through late 2026) indicates Markets+ launch readiness is imminent and market participation will require significant trainer engagement.

documents.title: 07-MIR81-R Dynamic MTI-MOTI in RTBM submission.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Dynamic vs. Normal transaction-type rules for market-transfer interfaces (MTIs/MOTIs) affect interregional scheduling and dispatch flexibility that hydro and storage use to manage net-load volatility and capture arbitrage; BAA scheduling constraints on dynamic transfers create new operational and revenue risks for resources optimized by day-ahead and RUC runs.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ MIR 81-R proposes to distinguish Normal (eligible DAM through pre-RTBM, then fixed for RTBM) and Dynamic (RTBM-only) transaction types on MTIs/MOTIs, permitting separate BAA scheduling constraints on each type to prevent post-DA constraint changes from forcing redispatch and artificial binding constraints. The MIR identifies a financial risk: absent this change, dynamic BAA constraint tightening could override the DA/RUC commitment plan, triggering costly real-time redispatch and potential VRL exceedances. Status: submitted for normal-track review; MPEC approval targeted Q4 2026, implementation Q1 2027. SPP staff identified this as High-rank, Low complexity; no impact analysis deemed necessary because design is already accounted for in current work.

documents.title: 07-MIR81-R Dynamic MTI-MOTI in RTBM.pptx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MIR81-R establishes a dual-transaction-type framework for Market Transfer Interfaces (MTIs) that affects how BAA scheduling constraints and transmission paths are managed across day-ahead, pre-real-time, and real-time markets—directly impacting transmission-constrained hydro's dispatch, transfer capability accreditation, and real-time pricing exposure.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ proposes MIR81-R, which distinguishes 'Normal' MTIs (active in day-ahead, RUC, and pre-RTBM; then fixed in RTBM) from 'Dynamic' MTIs (active only in RTBM). A BA may register separate scheduling constraints for the same path using different transaction types, allowing it to restrict incremental real-time transfers independently of day-ahead commitment without disrupting the prior optimized dispatch plan. The mechanism allows BAs to limit swing in net scheduled interchange (NSI) between RTBM dispatch intervals while preserving the DA/RUC commitment. This affects transmission-constrained hydro by potentially reducing real-time flexibility access on congested paths and creating dynamic constraint shadow prices in RTBM that were previously zero or absorbed in DA/Pre-RTBM; hydro with limited upstream flexibility or PSH relying on real-time inter-BA transfers for arbitrage or reserve charging face higher exposure to binding Dynamic constraints. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in the presentation.

documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ has issued draft version 0.1 of the Interchange Scheduling Reference Manual (April 16, 2026), a technical guidance document for market participants on e-tagging, scheduling roles, and transaction types (import, export, through, internal) for energy transfers crossing Markets+ balancing authority boundaries. The manual establishes roles for the Market Operator (SPP/MPLS), participating BAs, transmission service providers, and purchasing/selling entities, and specifies validation rules and settlement-location registration requirements. This is a reference document, not a binding tariff, but hydro owners evaluating or active in Markets+ must understand these scheduling mechanics to ensure compliance with interchange-tagging requirements and to optimize transaction settlement and revenue recognition across BA boundaries. documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Interchange scheduling rules, settlement location definitions, and transmission service treatment for Markets+ directly affect how hydro and PSH owners schedule energy exports/imports, settle transactions across balancing authority boundaries, and interface with transmission service providers—core revenue and operational mechanics.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ releases Version 0.2 of its Interchange Scheduling Reference Manual (June 8, 2026), a procedural guide for energy transfers crossing Markets+ participating balancing authority areas. The manual specifies e-tagging requirements, roles of market operators, transmission service providers (MTSPs), participating balancing authorities (M+ BAs), and purchasing/selling entities (PSEs) in scheduling interchange transactions (imports, exports, through-flows, and internal transfers). The Markets+ Market Operator (designated MPLS in NAESB registry) is SPP, not a transmission service provider; each MTSP and M+ BA has distinct approval authority over schedules on their facilities or within their balancing area. Hydro and PSH owners participating in Markets+ must register as market participants and PSEs, understand transmission-loss treatment and settlement-location mapping on tagged schedules, and comply with validation requirements imposed by both the MTSP and M+ BA on each path segment. The manual covers day-ahead and real-time market interchange, self-scheduled vs. economic schedules, and dynamic schedules; it is a reference document only and subordinate to the Markets+ Tariff and Protocols in case of conflict.
