documents.ai_summary: SPP has published a draft Interchange Scheduling Reference Manual (v0.1, dated April 16, 2026) establishing business rules for scheduling energy transfers that cross Markets+ Participating Balancing Authority boundaries. The manual defines roles for the Market Operator (SPP, designated as MPLS in NAESB), participating BAs, transmission service providers (MTSPs), and purchase/selling entities (PSEs), and specifies the e-tagging process, transmission service mechanics, validation checkpoints, and loss accounting for day-ahead and real-time market schedules under NAESB standards. This is a reference document (not official governing policy) intended to help market participants and tagging entities understand what system changes they must make to comply with Markets+ interchange scheduling. The document reflects Markets+ Tariff and Protocols but the formal next step (board adoption, tariff filing, comment deadline) is not stated in this excerpt. Hydro owners exporting from or transacting within the Markets+ footprint will need to understand these tagging, settlement-location, and transmission-service rules to participate; the treatment of transmission losses and interchange transaction types directly affects revenue settlement.

documents.title: 10-MIR60-R Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) submission.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
documents.filename: 10-MIR60-R Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) submission.docx
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Establishes the Markets+ System Points (MPSP) and Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) framework, which defines how hydropower and other resources can schedule interchange transactions (imports and exports) across the Markets+ boundary and within the footprint. This affects hydro operators' ability to execute bilateral and market-cleared energy transfers, their interchange transaction offer requirements, must-offer obligations treatment, and their access to generic scheduling points—central to how hydro monetizes peaking and load-following services across balancing authority areas in the SPP/Markets+ footprint.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ is implementing a new Markets+ System Points (MPSP) and Interchange Transaction System Point Schedules (MITSPS) protocol framework, targeting Q1 2027 implementation with Q3 2026 MPEC approval. MPSPs are generic (not resource- or load-specific) scheduling points within Markets+ BAAs that facilitate exports from and imports to the Markets+ footprint, and also enable internal market transfers. MITSPS transactions (uncommitted import/export interchanges using an MPSP as source or sink) are excluded from net-position calculations and do not satisfy must-offer obligations—a key difference from bilateral transactions. The framework establishes hourly MPSP interchange limits (import, export, and net) set by each BA and enforced in Day-Ahead and Real-Time markets, plus e-Tag confirmation requirements between the MPSP and the Markets+ boundary. Hydro operators relying on interchange flexibility to manage run-of-river or PSH output will be directly affected by these new scheduling rules, limit-setting authority, and the clarification that MITSPS transactions are not must-offer-eligible. The revision is primarily a Markets+ Protocols change (multiple sections), classified as an Enhancement with low design and implementation complexity.

documents.title: 11-MIR9.2-R Dynamic Schedule Resources June 2026.pptx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Dynamic scheduling rules directly affect how hydro and other dispatchable resources physically sited in one balancing authority can be marketed and dispatched to serve load in another BA — a fundamental strategic option for multi-BA hydro portfolios and interregional export optimization, especially relevant to Northwest hydro supplying load across SPP Markets+ participant boundaries.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ is establishing tariff and protocol language (MIR 9.2-R) to create a new "Dynamic Schedule Resource" participation type for dispatchable resources physically located in one participating BA but dynamically scheduled to serve load in another BA. The proposal addresses a double-counting problem in current power-balance accounting: when a dynamically scheduled resource is dispatched to a new output level, both the MTI (market transmission interface) and the dynamic NSI (net scheduled interchange) reflect the change, forcing both BAs to regulate separately. MIR 9.2-R introduces a Dynamic Schedule Resource Interface tied to each such resource, so only the interface — not both the output and the NSI — enters the attaining BA's power balance, eliminating double regulation. The framework allows resource-coupled dynamic schedules (Use Case 1a: participating BA to participating BA with a specific dispatchable resource) to remain under Markets+ dispatch control while transferring regulation responsibility to the attaining BA, with e-tagging requirements and registration mandates for market participants. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in the document; it appears to be a technical briefing of tariff and protocol changes already adopted or in final preparation.

documents.title: 11-MIR9.2-R SPP Comments-20260602.docx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Dynamic Schedule Resources rules directly affect how pumped storage and other use-limited hydro can participate in interregional energy transfers and remain dispatchable for Markets+ optimization; clarifies registration, e-Tagging, and real-time dispatch treatment of resources exporting across BA boundaries.
documents.ai_summary: SPP staff propose tariff and protocol amendments (submitted June 2, 2026) to clarify how Markets+ will register, tag, and operationally treat Dynamic Schedule Resources—resources within one BA whose output is transferred to another BA via Dynamic Transfer. The revisions define Dynamic Schedule Resource and Dynamic Schedule Resource Interface constructs and specify when such resources remain dispatchable for Markets+ optimization versus when they must be non-dispatchable (e.g., certain exports). For multi-state resources (MSRs), separate e-Tags are required to represent import/export dispatch; the Market Participant, Native BA, and Attaining BA must coordinate tagging to avoid double-counting in settlements. Most exposed: PSH and other dispatchable resources located in one BA but contracting to export across interconnection seams; owners should track whether the final rule preserves dispatch optionality for resource-coupled Dynamic Schedules (currently proposed to remain dispatchable) and whether the e-Tagging framework reduces settlement ambiguity that has historically burdened multi-state operations. Status and comment deadline not stated in this excerpt.

documents.title: 12-MIR9.3-R Dynamic Schedule Resource Pseudo-Ties June 2026.pptx  [in MTWG Meeting Materials 20260625.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MIR9.3-R expands the participation pathways for external resources (including hydro and storage) into Markets+ by allowing dynamic-schedule pseudo-ties, which affects how hydro owners can structure market participation, settlements, and dispatch control across balancing-authority boundaries.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ proposes MIR9.3-R to allow external resources physically located outside the Markets+ footprint to participate as pseudo-tied resources using dynamic schedules instead of formal NAESB pseudo-tie agreements. Resources designated as 'dynamically scheduled pseudo-ties' would be dispatchable by Markets+, modeled within the attaining participating BA for power-balance and settlement purposes (LMP at physical location), and governed by the same obligations as formal pseudo-ties—but the underlying dynamic schedule between native and attaining BAs would be submitted but ignored by Markets+ systems. Key requirements include: resource-market-participant designation in the registration workbook, an attestation letter from the attaining BA confirming awareness of the pseudo-tie modeling, and a meter-agent export pseudo-tie submission matching resource output. This lowers the barrier to entry for external resources (including small and mid-sized hydro plants and pumped storage in adjacent non-participating BAAs) that operate under dynamic-schedule arrangements rather than formal transmission agreements, expanding the pool of dispatchable, settlable resources in Markets+ without requiring new transmission arrangements.

documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Dynamics Working Group agenda addresses four compliance and operational topics: NERC MOD-026-2 (R1) compliance oversight; 2026 Flat Start process and kickoff; Reliability Performance Criteria for Loss of Load (PGRR122); and Load Model Submission and Review Requirements (PGRR144), plus UFLS/UVLS and large-load survey coordination. documents.title: Presentation - On-peak Deliverability Assessment Methodology Refinements - Jun 11, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO is refining its on-peak deliverability assessment methodology—a transmission planning process that determines whether new generation (including hydro and storage) can physically deliver power to load, directly affecting the deliverability accreditation and interconnection pathway for proposed hydro and PSH projects.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO proposes three refinements to its on-peak deliverability assessment methodology: (1) modeling proposed resources identified in transmission planning as needed for local reliability in the base case dispatch (rather than only when they relieve constraints), conditional on reasonable certainty of completion before deliverability is needed; (2) including existing demand response in the base case to avoid over-allocating deliverability; and (3) reducing the dispatch value of generators in the base case from 80% to 70% of maximum study amount to reflect the increased planning reserve margin (from ~15% to 25%). The refinements affect how new hydro and PSH projects are studied during interconnection—projects that would need to compete with modeled proposed resources to secure a deliverable position in queue. Comments are due June 25, 2026. Most exposed are developers of new hydro or PSH seeking interconnection in California who will compete for deliverability allocation against projects CAISO assumes will be built to meet identified local needs.

documents.title: Draft - On-Peak Deliverability Study Methodology - Jun 03, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO refines its on-peak deliverability assessment methodology — the mechanism by which generators (including hydro and storage) qualify for full or partial capacity credit toward resource adequacy; changes to Pmax assumptions, scenario hours, and dispatch modeling directly affect how much RA capacity hydro and pumped storage can claim and at what cost.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO has refined its generator on-peak deliverability study methodology, effective January 2024 and documented here as of June 2026, to address bottleneck risk as the generation fleet diversifies and solar penetration shifts peak net load. The methodology now assesses two distinct scenarios: (1) highest system need (hours 19–22 in summer, peak sale with low solar), where non-intermittent generators' Pmax is set to highest summer Qualifying Capacity in the prior three years, and intermittent generators at 20% exceedance level; and (2) secondary system need (hours 15–18, peak consumption with high solar output), where Pmax for intermittent resources is 50% exceedance level and energy storage Pmax is 50% of 4-hour discharge capacity. For hybrid and energy-storage projects, Pmax is limited by requested maximum output. Hydro and pumped-storage owners face a direct revenue impact: the Pmax values used in deliverability studies directly determine how much capacity credit they can claim in RA markets and interconnection queues; storage capacity credit in secondary scenarios is capped at half the 4-hour discharge rating, potentially disadvantaging long-duration or seasonal storage relative to short-duration batteries.

documents.title: Consent Agenda B – 1 Manual 11 Revisions Advance Scheduled Resources Phase 1 – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Advance scheduling of resources with >24-hour start-up times affects how dispatchable generation (including hydroelectric units and pumped-storage plants with slow start times) are committed, offered, and compensated in PJM's day-ahead and real-time energy markets, directly impacting revenue realization and offer-management obligations for slow-start hydro and PSH.
documents.ai_summary: PJM has formalized Manual 11 revisions implementing advance pre-scheduling of generation resources with start-up or notification times exceeding 24 hours, committing them to cost-based offers prior to the day-ahead market close to ensure resource availability during forecasted system emergencies (lessons from Winter Storm Elliott). Resources pre-scheduled in advance must be committed on cost-based schedules subject to Fuel Cost Policy rules; those with >24-hour start times cannot update cost-based offers after advance scheduling, while shorter start-time resources may update cost-based offers only through the day-ahead market. The revision locks the 'Switch to Cost Schedule' flag automatically for advance-scheduled units, preventing market sellers from reverting to price-based schedules for the scheduled operating days. Most exposed are slow-start hydroelectric and pumped-storage units (>24-hour commitment lead time) in PJM's footprint, which lose offer flexibility during emergency conditions but gain certainty of commitment; the practical revenue effect depends on whether cost-based offer caps reflect true opportunity cost. Implementation timeline: MIC first read May 26, 2026; MRC first and second reads June 24, 2026; effective date and FERC notice scheduled thereafter.

issues.canonical_name: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market

documents.title: Consent Agenda B – 2 Manual 11 Revisions – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Cost-capping and offer-placement rules for resources with long startup/notification times (>24 hours) scheduled before the day-ahead market affect how hydro and pumped-storage operators with extended lead times structure bids and recover costs in PJM's energy market, with direct implications for revenue recovery under cost-based dispatch.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is conforming Manual 11 (Energy & Ancillary Services Market Operations) to FERC approval order ER26-527-000 (approved January 13, 2026) under Phase 1 of an issue charge addressing resources scheduled prior to the day-ahead market. The revisions clarify cost-capping of resources with startup or notification times exceeding 24 hours when called in advance of the day-ahead market, specify how such resources are placed on the cheapest available cost-based offer, and refine the application of the "switch to cost" flag in both day-ahead and real-time operations. Pumped-storage hydro with extended notification or startup times, run-of-river plants with multi-day scheduling constraints, and other hydro resources that may be committed ahead of the day-ahead market could be affected by these offer-placement and cost-recovery rules; the revisions were approved by the Market Implementation Committee on June 3, 2026 and are scheduled to take effect June 24, 2026.

documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 11 Revision 137 (effective June 1, 2026) incorporates three market-design updates relevant to hydro: (1) Advance Scheduled Resources Phase 1 updates (FERC ER26-0527, Section 2.3.3) affecting how scheduled resources participate in energy markets; (2) Regulation Redesign Phase 1 (FERC ER24-1772, Sections 2.5, 3, 4.4, 9.3) reshaping the regulation market that hydro traditionally supplies; and (3) Economic Demand Response parameter updates (FERC ER24-3135, Section 10.3) with secondary effects on energy and reserve clearing. The document confirms revisions to hydropower operation rules (Section 9.3) and pumped storage modeling assumptions (Attachment B) are now in effect. For conventional hydro and pumped-storage operators in PJM, the regulation redesign and ASR Phase 1 changes alter both offer/bid mechanics and mileage/opportunity-cost calculations; the effective date is June 1, 2026, with no comment deadline noted in this agenda item (revision was approved September 30, 2025).

documents.ai_summary: PJM is conducting periodic cover-to-cover reviews of Manual 20A (Resource Adequacy Analysis, Revision 3) and Manual 22 (Generator Resource Performance Indices, Revision 19), with administrative and definitional updates. Manual 20A adds two new definitions (Solved simulated annual peak load and Portfolio EUE) and reformats ELCC Class Rating and Resource Specific ELCC Rating calculations from plain text to formula presentation—clarifying but not changing the hydro-specific capacity accreditation methods. Manual 22 updates cross-references and adds pool-wide average Accredited UCAP Factor to its introduction. The manuals passed first read at PC on May 8, 2026; second read/endorsement vote is scheduled for June 24, 2026 at the Markets & Reliability Committee, with final timeline through June 2026.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ELCC accreditation study methodology directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are credited in PJM's capacity market and resource adequacy framework, determining their participation value and revenue exposure.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 20A (Revision 3, effective May 20, 2026) documents the Reserve Requirement Study (RRS), Effective Load Carrying Capability (ELCC) Study, and Capacity Emergency Transfer Objective (CETO) methodologies that determine capacity resource accreditation for the Reliability Pricing Model (RPM). Revision 3 adds two new definitions (Solved simulated annual peak load and Portfolio EUE), converts ELCC Class Rating and Resource Specific ELCC Rating calculations to formula-based presentations, and incorporates conforming changes from FERC Order 2222 on DER aggregation (Revision 2). The ELCC accreditation rating directly sets how much capacity credit hydropower and PSH can claim in PJM's auction, affecting both reserve-margin requirements and individual resource pricing signals—hydro's accreditation depends on how the study models seasonal availability and effective contribution to peak load carrying. No comment deadline or vote date is stated; this is an approved operational manual effective as of the stated dates.

documents.title: Consent Agenda C – 4 Manual 22 Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 22 defines generator resource performance indices (forced outage rates, availability factors, accreditation metrics) that directly affect how PJM calculates UCAP accreditation and capacity resource performance — a key revenue and compliance driver for all dispatchable generators including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 22 Revision 19 (effective May 20, 2026) updates generator performance-index definitions and calculations used to establish resource adequacy and capacity-market accreditation. The revision adds pool-wide average Accredited UCAP Factor metrics to the Introduction and cross-references updated PJM Manuals 20A and 21B governing resource adequacy analysis and capability determination. These indices — including Equivalent Availability Factor, Equivalent Forced Outage Rates, and accreditation factors — feed directly into UCAP calculation and capacity-auction participation for all generating units, including hydro and pumped-storage plants. Andrew Gledhill (Resource Adequacy Planning) manages the manual; no external stakeholder positions or comment deadlines are identified in this redline agenda item.

documents.title: Item 01 – 1 Schedule 2 Penalties – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MITIGATION-OFFERS: This modifies PJM's penalty formula for cost-based offers that violate approved Fuel Cost Policies, directly affecting how use-limited resources (including hydropower and pumped storage) are subject to default-energy-bid and opportunity-cost enforcement—a core market-power mitigation mechanism for storage.
documents.ai_summary: PJM proposes revising Operating Agreement Schedule 2 section 6.1 penalty formulae to prevent Market Sellers from receiving credits (negative penalties) when cost-based offers are non-compliant and real-time LMPs are negative. The revised formula introduces an error-identification factor (E = 0.25 for self-reported errors, 1 otherwise), a market-impact factor (I) that escalates penalties if non-compliance continues after PJM or MMU notice, and a duration multiplier (d) capping at 15 days. This change tightens enforcement of offer compliance for all dispatchable and storage resources subject to cost-based offer rules—hydro and PSH are typically among resources required to submit cost-based offers, so stricter penalties and elimination of negative-penalty loopholes increases the cost of offer errors and reinforces compliance discipline. First Read was May 20, 2026; Markets & Reliability Committee vote scheduled June 24, 2026.

issues.canonical_name: Schedule 2 Penalty Calculations

documents.title: Item 01 – 2 Schedule 2 Penalties Oa Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Cost-based offer penalties directly affect hydro operators' compliance obligations and how they manage offer submissions under PJM rules; the penalty structure and escalation mechanics impact use-limited resource mitigation and offer-development incentives.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Markets and Reliability Committee is revising Schedule 2 penalties for non-compliant cost-based offers. The redline text establishes a two-tier penalty structure: (1) a non-escalating penalty using a formula weighted by LMP, available capacity, a self-identification factor (0.25 if self-reported, 1.0 otherwise), and a market-impact factor that ranges from 0.1 to 1.0 depending on whether the resource cleared, ran, passed pivotal-supplier tests, or submitted offers above $1,000/MWh; and (2) a daily escalating penalty (multiplied by day count d, starting at 2 and capping at 15) for continuing violations after PJM notification. Hydro operators subject to cost-based offer requirements—particularly pumped storage and run-of-river resources with use-limited characteristics—face heightened compliance costs if offer errors persist; the escalating penalty's cap at day 15 and the incentive structure (lower penalties for self-reported errors) will shape whether operators err on the side of conservative offers or market-responsive pricing. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this redline.

documents.ai_summary: PJM is addressing a technical flaw in the penalty formula for Market Sellers who violate approved Fuel Cost Policies or Schedule 2 requirements: when negative LMPs occur, the existing penalty calculation under Operating Agreement Schedule 2, section 6.1 can produce negative (and thus uncollectible or perverse) penalties. The work is high-priority and immediate; the Market Implementation Committee will develop a proposed solution for tariff revision and manual updates. Hydro and storage operators subject to cost-based offer requirements (especially pumped storage in cost-based mode, and any unit filing a fuel-cost policy) should monitor the proposed fix to confirm it preserves penalty enforceability without creating unintended side effects in negative-price periods—a key risk during high-renewable, low-load hours when hydro's opportunity cost is most relevant.

documents.title: Item 02 – 1 Manual 13 Annual Review Revisions – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 13 Revision 98 updates PJM's emergency operating procedures with direct implications for hydro and PSH dispatch obligations, reserve contributions during capacity/transmission emergencies, and the new Capacity Advisory mechanism that signals scarcity conditions affecting real-time operations.
documents.ai_summary: PJM approved Manual 13 Revision 98 (effective July 23, 2026) updating emergency operations procedures to align with NERC EOP-011 standards and address capacity-deficient conditions occurring outside traditional weather-alert triggers. Key changes include: (1) a new Capacity Advisory mechanism providing advance notice when available generating capacity approaches demand requirements, leveraging forecasted peak load, day-ahead reserve requirements, and net interchange; (2) Attachment O establishing a conditional procedure for emergency use of backup generators (triggered only by DOE Section 202(c) orders) with coordinated PJM and Transmission Owner actions during capacity and transmission emergencies; (3) clarifications separating transmission-security emergency procedures from capacity-based procedures, removing capacity-specific actions (Emergency Bid Process, export recall, capacity load shed) from transmission-security triggers; and (4) updated language for Advanced Scheduled Resources under cold- and hot-weather alerts. Hydro operators must understand the Capacity Advisory's forecasting inputs and threshold logic, as early signals of tight capacity may activate voluntary or mandatory dispatch obligations; pumped-storage operators should note the manual's treatment of generation scheduling during emergencies and any implications for charging-window availability. Most exposed: all dispatchable hydro and PSH participating in PJM's real-time market and ancillary-services offerings. No member comment deadline is stated; the revision was endorsed by the Operating Committee on June 4, 2026.

documents.title: Item 02 – 2 Manual 13 Revisions – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 13 (Emergency Operations) revision covers procedural updates to emergency alert definitions, notifications, and administrative formatting with no bearing on hydro revenue mechanisms, participation rules, or strategic options.

documents.title: Item 02 – 3 Manual 13 Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 13 establishes PJM's emergency operations procedures including capacity emergencies, reserve requirements, and load management actions that directly govern how dispatchable resources including hydropower respond to system stress, affecting must-offer obligations and revenue opportunities during scarcity events.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 13: Emergency Operations (Revision 98, effective July 23, 2026) establishes procedures for capacity emergencies, reserve management, and transmission security including load shedding and emergency generation dispatch. The revision reorganizes transmission-security emergency procedures to separate them from capacity-focused actions, redefines several alert/action sequences, and adds a new attachment on emergency use of back-up generators. For hydro and PSH operators, Manual 13 defines the operational obligations during emergency conditions—including minimum generation requirements (Attachment H), load-dump capability assessment and allocation (Attachments E, F, N), and dispatch directives for transmission relief—that interact with revenue accrual during scarcity pricing events and state-of-charge management for pumped storage. Operators in the PJM footprint should review Revision 98 for changes to alert sequencing and reserve/generation requirements that may affect their emergency-response positioning and ancillary-service obligations.

documents.title: Item 03 – 1 Flexible Resource Loc Eligibility – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Flexible Resource definition refinement affects lost-opportunity-cost (LOC) crediting eligibility for dispatchable resources, including hydro with fast-start capability; the proposal tightens which resources qualify for LOC payments when held out of real-time operation, altering revenue certainty for flexible hydro units and changing the operational expectations PJM will impose on fast-start dispatch.
documents.ai_summary: PJM proposes to refine the definition of "Flexible Resources" to require that all available operating schedules meet the flexibility criteria (combined startup and notification time ≤ 2 hours, minimum run time ≤ 2 hours), rather than permitting flexibility on only some schedules. Under current rules, a unit committed day-ahead on a flexible schedule but holding an inflexible backup schedule can still receive Flexible Resource Lost Opportunity Cost (LOC) credits if not called in real-time, even though it may be unable to start under real-time dispatch due to its inflexible schedule(s). The proposal would classify such units as inflexible for real-time and LOC purposes, restricting LOC eligibility to units that meet flexibility on all schedules and are not called due to transmission or reliability constraints. Tariff and Manual updates (Attachments K, 11, and 28) are under review; the Markets and Reliability Committee is scheduled to endorse the proposal on July 28, 2026, with FERC filing to follow. Fast-start hydro and PSH units with multiple offer schedules are most exposed: those unable to configure all available cost-based schedules as flexible will lose LOC protection and be held to inflexible day-ahead commitments.

issues.canonical_name: Flexible Resource Lost Opportunity Costs Eligibility

documents.title: Item 03 – 2 Proposed Tariff Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Proposed tariff revisions redefine how hydroelectric resources are scheduled and compensated for lost opportunity costs when curtailed for reliability, directly affecting hydro revenue under PJM's pool-scheduling and real-time dispatch framework, with special provisions for hydroelectric and pumped-storage units.
documents.ai_summary: PJM proposes tariff changes to its pool-scheduling rules and lost-opportunity-cost (LOC) compensation framework. Key provisions: (1) hydropower units can only be pool-scheduled if they are pumped-storage units and use PJM's hydro optimization tool in the Day-ahead Energy Market; (2) hydroelectric resources that are pool-scheduled or self-scheduled and curtailed by PJM for transmission or reliability reasons receive LOC compensation under a formula detailed in section 3.2.3(f-2), aligned with methodology in sections 3.2.2(d) and 3.2.3A(f); (3) a new section 3.2.3(e-2) applies Tracking Balancing Net Revenues to Flexible Resources (startup and notification ≤2 hours, minimum run ≤2 hours) that are self-scheduled but committed in Day-ahead, modifying how their real-time compensation is calculated when PJM does not call them to run. The document is a redline tariff revision presented to the Markets and Reliability Committee on June 24, 2026; no comment deadline or vote date is stated in the excerpt.

issues.canonical_name: Flexible Resource Lost Opportunity Costs Eligibility

documents.title: Item 03 – 3 Manual 11 And 28 Revisions – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: PJM's changes to lost-opportunity-cost (LOC) eligibility rules for 'Flexible Resources' (fast-responding CTs and diesels) directly affect how PJM compensates use-limited resources—hydro and pumped storage—when held out of the market despite day-ahead commitment, a canonical revenue stream for dispatchable resources and the structural analog to how hydro's opportunity costs are treated under market power mitigation.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is amending Manual 11 (Energy & Ancillary Services Market Operations) and Manual 28 (Operating Agreement Accounting) to clarify lost-opportunity-cost (LOC) eligibility for 'Flexible Resources' (fast-response CTs and diesels) committed day-ahead but not dispatched in real-time, or denied startup for reliability/transmission reasons. The changes memorialize the expectation that Flexible Resources wait for PJM dispatch instructions before running, clarify offer-capping applicability, and specify LOC credit eligibility and ineligibility conditions. The tariff and manuals are in concurrent FERC approval, with MRC voting scheduled July 28, 2026. The LOC framework—compensating resources for foregone opportunity when constrained out despite a day-ahead commitment—is the same mechanism PJM uses for hydro and storage under mitigation rules; these revisions to Flexible Resource LOC treatment establish precedent and may signal how PJM intends to handle similar situations for dispatchable hydro, pumped storage, or other use-limited resources facing transmission or reliability constraints.

issues.canonical_name: Flexible Resource Lost Opportunity Costs Eligibility

documents.title: Item 03 – 4 Manual 11 Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 11 revisions to market seller rules, offer-capping procedures, and flexible-resource definition directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources submit offers, self-schedule, and face market power mitigation in PJM's energy and reserve markets.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Manual 11 redline updates the business rules for Market Sellers, with particular emphasis on Hybrid Resources and Energy Storage Resources (ESR) participation, flexible-resource definitions, and offer-capping procedures under the Three-Pivotal Supplier Test. Hybrid Resources and ESRs may now self-schedule only (not offer into day-ahead), must specify hourly operating mode and range, and trigger notification requirements when >10 MW. Offer-capping rules are clarified for combustion turbines and diesels designated as Flexible Resources—these units are now expected to await dispatch instruction rather than run automatically, preserving their flexibility value but exposing them to lost-opportunity-cost credits if not called. For non-CT resources and non-Flexible CT/diesel units, offer-capping carries through from day-ahead to real-time at the same schedule; Flexible Resources face re-evaluation for market power at real-time commitment. The revision enables cost-based offer stickiness (once dispatched on cost basis, unit remains cost-capped regardless of updated offers) and clarifies the initial capping determination period. Most exposed are pumped-storage and other dispatchable hydro resources that compete with CTs/diesels for flexibility revenue; any PSH owner using PJM's ESR model or treating a hybrid asset will need to audit self-scheduling constraints and mode-change notification procedures. The document does not state a comment deadline or vote date.

issues.canonical_name: Flexible Resource Lost Opportunity Costs Eligibility

documents.title: Item 03 – 5 Manual 28 Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 28 governs the accounting and settlement of ancillary services (regulation, operating reserves, synchronized reserves, non-synchronized reserves, secondary reserves), transmission congestion and losses, and energy storage charging — all critical revenue and obligation mechanisms for hydro and pumped-storage resources in PJM.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 28 (Revision 106, effective July 29, 2026) is the definitive operating manual for PJM's market accounting and settlement procedures. This redline revision updates rules for regulation accounting (Section 4), operating reserve accounting (Section 5), synchronized and non-synchronized reserve accounting (Sections 6–7), secondary reserve accounting (Section 19), transmission congestion and loss accounting (Sections 8–9), and notably for pumped-storage operators, charging energy accounting for open-loop hybrid and energy storage resources (Section 22). Because the document is a redline, specific substantive changes are not visible in the table of contents alone; hydro and PSH owners must review the marked-up text to identify which revenue-settlement formulas, accreditation criteria, charge/credit calculations, or metering reconciliation procedures have shifted. The effective date is July 29, 2026.

documents.title: Item 04 – 1 Gdecs Governing Document Revisions – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: GDECS April/May 2026 batch addresses obsolete-reference removal, FERC-docket alignment, and cross-reference corrections to PJM governing documents — routine tariff housekeeping with no stated impact on hydro revenue mechanisms, compliance obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: Item 04 – 2 Gdecs April 2026 Chart
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document is a technical tariff-revision tracking chart addressing NITS procedures, transmission rating methodology (AFC/ATC/TRM), and DER aggregation offer-cap compliance — none of which directly affect hydropower revenue mechanisms, storage participation rules, or strategic options for hydro operators.

documents.title: Item 04 – 3 Iprtf Gdecs Iii April 2026 Chart
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: interconnection queue and application-process reforms (IPRTF GDECS III) directly affect how new hydro and PSH projects navigate PJM interconnection requirements, queue cycles, and approval timelines — this is clean-up/clarification to Parts VII–IX tariff language governing the interconnection process that all new generation (including pumped storage) must comply with.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Legal Service Agreement Team has proposed clean-up, clarifications, and corrections to Tariff Parts VII–IX governing the interconnection reform process (Interconnection Reform Process Task Force GDECS Number III), with revisions submitted April 17, 2026. The document addresses definitional and formatting corrections across 43 numbered items spanning application requirements, interconnection facilities cost allocation, and generator interconnection agreement language. The proposals include minor grammatical edits, corrected cross-references, and clarifications to terms like "Application and Studies Agreement," "Cancellation Costs," and "Decision Point I" that establish timelines and cost responsibility rules for Project Developers (including hydro and PSH developers) entering new service requests. This is a tariff clean-up rather than a substantive redesign, but it codifies the procedural and cost-allocation framework through which all new hydro interconnections in PJM flow — corrections to Decision Point definitions, cancellation-cost allocation, and obligation language will apply uniformly to pumped-storage and conventional hydro developers submitting applications.

stakeholders.name: PJM Legal Service Agreement Team Attorneys

documents.title: Item 05a – Destf Phase Ii Scope Status Report – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's Deactivation Enhancement Task Force Phase II addresses RMR (Reliability Must-Run) contracts and alternatives including energy storage and transmission solutions, which directly affects how PJM will procure reliability services from retiring generators and whether hydro/storage assets can compete as alternatives—a strategic option and revenue mechanism for NHA members in PJM.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Deactivation Enhancement Task Force Phase II completed scope development for six work areas, with three proposals advancing: Scope Areas 4/5 (accelerating transmission solutions and alternatives to RMR contracts) failed to achieve >50% support and remain unresolved; Scope Area 6 (pro forma RMR arrangements and capacity market rule updates for retained generators) received PJM's proposal with >50% support and was offered for first read at the June 24, 2026 Markets & Reliability Committee meeting. The Phase II effort will determine PJM's framework for evaluating energy storage, demand-side solutions, and transmission enhancements as alternatives to long-term RMR contracts when generators retire—a direct pathway for hydro and pumped storage to compete for reliability-driven revenue in place of mothballed conventional units, though the lack of consensus on Scope 4/5 signals continued disagreement on how to fairly compare and procure such alternatives.

issues.canonical_name: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules

documents.title: Item 05b – 1 Pro Forma Rmr Agreement – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: RMR (Reliability Must-Run) agreements are a key strategic and financial tool for hydroelectric operators facing deactivation; this presentation establishes PJM's pro forma RMR framework, which determines the path by which hydro owners can recover costs and remain operational when transmission constraints require their continued service.
documents.ai_summary: PJM presented its pro forma Reliability Must-Run (RMR) Agreement framework, streamlining the process by which generators—especially old or transmission-constrained units—can receive cost recovery to continue operating after leaving the market to address thermal, voltage, or stability violations. The pro forma approach pre-approves non-rate terms and conditions, requiring FERC acceptance of only the rate component (either the Deactivation Avoidable Cost Credit formula or a full cost-of-service filing), eliminating the need for hearing and settlement judge procedures and reducing process friction. Hydroelectric facilities, particularly older reservoir-based units prone to becoming stranded as markets shift, may use this pathway to recover operating costs when transmission upgrades are pending; revenues from energy, capacity, and ancillary-services sales are credited against RMR charges, creating a hybrid cost-plus mechanism. The framework applies to units regardless of fuel type and is effective only upon FERC approval; PJM terminates the RMR when it determines the constraint is resolved. No vote date or comment deadline is stated in this presentation slide deck.

issues.canonical_name: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules

documents.title: Item 05b – 2 OATT Att Dd53 – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAPACITY-RA MECHANISM: this tariff redline modifies how generation capacity resources (including hydro) are accredited, committed, and compensated in PJM's capacity market (RPM), directly affecting resource-adequacy obligations, accreditation rules, and the revenue treatment of must-run/reliability-retained resources—a canonical hydro participation pathway.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's tariff amendment (Attachment DD, section 5.3) revises the commitment and compensation rules for Generation Capacity Resources retained under must-run or transmission-reliability cost-of-service agreements. The redline clarifies that such resources must offer their accredited UCAP at $0/MW-day into the Base Residual Auction (not subject to market-clearing mechanisms) and requires PJM to backstop any unmet capacity via the Third Incremental Auction. Critically, the amendment limits this automatic $0 offer through delivery year 2028/2029 (unless PJM and FERC extend it), and excludes such resources from performance-bonus eligibility and balancing-ratio calculations. All capacity revenues flow to the zone that funded the asset's reliability mission, not to the resource operator. This affects any hydro asset retained for transmission reliability or deactivation-avoidance—particularly reservoirs and run-of-river plants in constrained zones—by locking them into below-market revenue recovery and removing upside from performance incentives. The document is in first-read status at the June 24, 2026 MRC; next steps and comment deadlines are not stated in this excerpt.

issues.canonical_name: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules

documents.title: Item 05b – 3 Pro Forma Rmr – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: RMR (Reliability Must Run) agreements directly affect hydro and fossil generators facing deactivation — this pro forma tariff controls when generators can be forced to stay online, project-investment approval (critical for maintaining aging hydro units), compensation mechanisms (Section 4), and termination rights that constrain a hydro owner's exit decisions and asset management.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is revising its form Reliability Must Run Service Agreement for first read at the June 24, 2026 Markets and Reliability Committee meeting. The redlined pro forma establishes the terms under which PJM can require a generation owner to continue operating a resource designated as critical for transmission reliability, including scheduling/dispatch authority (Section 3), project-investment approval requirements (Section 4, not shown here but referenced), and termination triggers (Sections 2.2–2.3). Key hydro-specific provisions: Generation Owner may terminate only on "Catastrophic Force Majeure" or if PJM denies necessary Project Investment, with a 61-day notice requirement; PJM may extend the term unilaterally if reliability need persists; and the resource must be offered into Day-ahead and Real-time markets at "Unavailable" status until PJM dispatches. For hydro operators, this agreement determines whether aging run-of-river or reservoir plants facing retirement can be forced to remain online, what capital repairs PJM must fund, and how long the contract lock lasts — directly affecting retirement-planning flexibility and revenue certainty during the reliability-need window.

issues.canonical_name: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules

documents.title: Item 05b – 4 Tariff Part V Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Generation deactivation rules and mothballing/RMR processes directly affect hydro owner fleet strategy, retirement timing, and must-run revenue recovery — a core strategic option for asset owners facing obsolescence or economic pressure.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Part V tariff revisions (first read at June 24, 2026 MRC) govern generation deactivation notice, reliability-impact review, and continued operation under must-run arrangements. Generation owners must provide 12 months' notice; PJM then has 4–5 months to assess whether deactivation would harm transmission reliability. If reliability impacts are found, owners can elect either cost-of-service recovery or a Deactivation Avoidable Cost Credit (DACC) formula—a monthly payment that escalates from 110% in year one to 150% by year four of the deactivation-delay period, less actual net market revenues. The formula applies to generation (including hydropower) that continues operating beyond its desired retirement date to support grid reliability. Most relevant to aging hydro plants facing retirement but needed for transmission support or seasonal energy adequacy; the DACC structure and multiplier schedule directly determine whether an extended operating life is economically viable.

issues.canonical_name: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules

documents.title: Item 05c – 1 Destf Ceg Proposal – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's Reliability Must-Run (RMR) agreement framework directly governs retirement and mothball processes for dispatchable resources, a key strategic option for hydro and storage owners; the proposal affects cost recovery, termination rights, and capacity-market treatment of retained units.
documents.ai_summary: Constellation Energy presents proposed amendments to PJM's Pro Forma RMR Agreement (used when PJM retains a retiring unit for transmission system reliability) addressing cost-recovery certainty, termination rights, and capacity-market participation. The proposal would allow asset owners to terminate if PJM does not approve Project Investment, upon catastrophic force majeure, or with 90 days' notice under OATT Section 113.3, provided all prudently incurred costs are recoverable; it would also prevent blanket inclusion of RMR resources' capacity accreditation in RPM auctions unless specific resource-level terms are met and revenues credited to cost-payers. The changes aim to ensure RMR units do not face performance obligations without clear cost-recovery pathways prior to FERC rate-setting. This reshapes the economics of mothballing versus operational reserve retention for owners considering exit — a material decision point for aging hydro and storage assets in PJM.

issues.canonical_name: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules

documents.title: Item 05c – 2 Ceg Pro Forma Rmr – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: RMR (Reliability Must Run) agreements are a critical strategic option and revenue mechanism for hydropower and pumped-storage operators facing deactivation but offering essential grid stability services; this redline form shapes the terms under which hydro/PSH plants can be retained and compensated for reliability service.
documents.ai_summary: PJM has circulated a revised pro forma Reliability Must Run Service Agreement (tariff Part V, Subpart A) for discussion at the June 24, 2026 Markets and Reliability Committee. The agreement establishes the contractual framework by which PJM can compel continued operation of generation resources (including hydro and PSH) that have submitted deactivation notices but are deemed necessary for transmission system reliability. Key terms include: (1) Generation Owner may terminate only on Catastrophic Force Majeure, if PJM denies necessary Project Investment, or by exercising standard deactivation rights with 90 days' notice; (2) PJM may terminate on 61 days' notice if the RMR Resource is no longer needed, with full recovery of prudently incurred costs; (3) PJM may extend the term unilaterally, requiring Generation Owner response within 14 days and permitting renegotiation of compensation; and (4) PJM retains dispatch authority over the RMR Resource for transmission limits, system restoration, capacity emergencies, or operational testing, subject only to owner-disclosed operating limitations. The document is currently in working-draft, FOR DISCUSSION PURPOSES ONLY status. For hydro and PSH owners, this revised form directly affects exit optionality: the extension clause (Section 2.5) and PJM's unilateral termination right (Section 2.3) determine how long a resource can be held in operation and whether compensation can be renegotiated mid-agreement. Owners with plants facing retirement but offering essential voltage support, black-start capability, or ramping—a common profile for aging hydro and PSH—should monitor whether Section 4 (compensation mechanism, not fully excerpted here) provides adequate coverage of Project Investment (repairs, NERC compliance, maintenance with overtime) and whether the 60-day termination notice gives sufficient runway to execute alternative retirement or repurposing strategies.

issues.canonical_name: Enhancements to Deactivation Rules

documents.title: Item 05d – 1 Destf PJM Package April 2026 – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's Deactivation, Energy, Storage, Transmission, and Flexibility (DESTF) Phase 2 proposal directly affects hydro and storage RMR (Reliability Must Run) processes and creates expedited interconnection pathways that touch resource-adequacy and retirement-planning strategy for all generation including hydropower.
documents.ai_summary: PJM proposes a two-path framework to reduce or eliminate Reliability Must Run (RMR) obligations when generation deactivates. Path 1A/1B relies on PJM-led transmission alternatives (including demand-side and generation-from-transmission mechanisms like GET/ATTs and SATOA); Path 2 allows states to sponsor replacement generation with PJM technical support, with both paths requiring the deactivating unit to remain under RMR until the replacement is energized. The proposal incorporates Coalition stakeholder input on proactive 5-year deactivation analysis and dual competitive windows but explicitly excludes generation solutions from PJM's Path 1 procurement, citing FPA jurisdictional limits. Vote is scheduled for after May 19, 2026. This affects hydro owners subject to must-offer rules or considering retirement/deactivation by changing the duration and conditionality of RMR obligations and opening state-led generation procurement as an alternative to transmission reinforcement—relevant to both conventional hydropower and pumped storage considering fleet decisions in PJM.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a conforming Manual 11 update for demand-side economic load response regulation participation with net-energy metering capability — a demand-resource registration procedural rule with no bearing on hydro or pumped-storage revenue streams, obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: Item 06a – 2 Manual 11 Revisions – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document addresses Economic Load Response Regulation Only registration procedures and net-energy-metering customer injection safety — operational compliance matters for demand-side regulation resources that do not touch hydro revenue streams, obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: Item 06a – 3 Manual 11 Econregonlynem Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document is a conforming tariff revision to PJM Manual 11 implementing a FERC order on curtailment service providers (CSPs) and demand-resource registration—a demand-response mechanism with no direct bearing on hydropower or pumped-storage revenue, obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: Item 06b – 1 Manual M14d Revisions – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM Manual M14D Revision 71 mandates reactive power control requirements for all synchronous generators, including hydroelectric units; this directly affects hydro operators' compliance obligations and may constrain operational flexibility in reactive support provision, a traditional revenue stream.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual M14D to clarify and tighten reactive power (VAR) control requirements for all generators in response to the May 2026 NERC ERO Enterprise CMEP Practice Guide (which lowered IBR registration threshold to 20 MVA/60 kV) and emerging low-voltage stability risks highlighted by the 2025 Spain blackout. The revisions require: (1) generators operate exclusively in automatic voltage-control mode unless documented exceptions are approved by PJM and the Transmission Owner; (2) define acceptable volt-VAR boundaries and VAR control ranges per MW output (32.87% of max MW capacity); and (3) establish a three-party investigative process (PJM, TO, and generator owner/operator) for reactive control performance issues, with PJM empowered to direct AVR and inverter settings changes. For conventional hydroelectric and pumped-storage facilities, this narrows operational flexibility in reactive-power dispatch—generators can no longer operate in constant-MVAR, constant power-factor, or manual modes without PJM/TO approval—potentially reducing the ability to optimize reactive output for revenue or water management. The revision moves to OC and SOS for first read June 24, 2026, with MRC review June 24, and endorsement timeline extending through July.

documents.title: Item 06b – 2 Manual M14d Revisions – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: OBLIGATION: PJM is updating generator operational requirements for reactive power (volt-var and watt-var behavior) under NERC Category 2 GO standards, which applies to synchronous generators including conventional hydro and pumped-storage units operating in PJM's footprint, affecting their compliance obligations for reactive-control modes and VAR output.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual M14D (Generator Operational Requirements), sections 7.1.2 and Attachment D, effective July 23, 2026, to incorporate NERC's new Category 2 GO standard and clarify reactive-power control requirements. The revision adds references to Category 2 GO generators, specifies allowable automatic voltage regulator (AVR) modes, and introduces volt-var and watt-var performance graphs with acceptable operating ranges, along with monitoring procedures where PJM may direct gen owner/operators to address voltage issues. The change is driven by PJM's dual operational pressures: rising solar/IBR penetration and load growth exceeding new generation, both exacerbating voltage stability risks. Hydro operators in PJM (primarily PSH and conventional generation) must update their VAR control procedures and performance commitments to comply; MRC voting is scheduled for July 23rd, 2026.

documents.title: Item 06b – 3 Manual 14d Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 14D prescribes operational compliance obligations for all PJM generators including hydroelectric facilities—metering, dispatch, data exchange, ancillary-services participation, black-start capability, cold-weather readiness, and deactivation processes that directly affect hydro revenue and operational flexibility.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 14D Revision 71 (effective June 30, 2026) consolidates generator operational requirements across metering, data exchange, dispatch protocols, ancillary-services participation, black-start procurement, and resource deactivation. The redline updates sections on EMS data-exchange requirements (Section 4), ancillary-services marketing (Section 5.2), unit-commitment and scheduling (Sections 5.6–5.7), cold-weather resource readiness (Section 7.5 and Attachment N), and generator deactivation compensation (Section 9.2)—all of which set mandatory operational and reporting standards for conventional hydroelectric and pumped-storage facilities operating in PJM. Specific attention to cold-weather preparation for conventional generators and new sections on mixed-technology facilities and behind-the-meter/microgrid business rules (Appendices A–C) signal evolving compliance expectations. Hydro owners must comply with all must-offer and dispatch rules outlined, and those seeking black-start contracts or deactivation compensation navigate the defined processes in Sections 10 and 9 respectively.

documents.title: Item 06c – 1 Manual 14h Eit Revisions – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Expedited Interconnection Track (EIT) is a new queue-reform pathway affecting interconnection and deliverability timelines for eligible generators ≥250 MW UCAP, including hydro and PSH projects that can meet 36-month COD and secure state siting support.
documents.ai_summary: PJM has adopted a new Expedited Interconnection Track (EIT) as a parallel process to the standard interconnection queue, effective July 31, 2026. Eligible generators must have ≥250 MW accredited capacity, state siting-authority support, and achievable commercial operation within 36 months; the process compresses review/study/negotiation to approximately 300 days total. This creates a strategic option for pumped-storage and large conventional hydro projects with confirmed state backing and shovel-ready status to bypass queue congestion and accelerate deliverability. Hydro developers should assess whether existing or planned projects meet the 250 MW threshold and can secure the requisite siting support; the process is now open for applications.

documents.title: Item 06c – 2 Manual 14h Eit Revisions – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's new Expedited Interconnection Track affects hydro and PSH owners' ability to queue and connect generation to the grid, directly impacting interconnection timelines and deliverability — a strategic option for hydro fleet expansion and uprates.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is conforming Manual 14H (New Service Requests Cycle Process) to FERC Docket ER26-1563-000, which establishes an Expedited Interconnection Track as a parallel process option alongside the standard interconnection queue. The changes add a new Section 16 describing the expedited process and update process diagrams and milestones. The revisions take effect July 31, 2026; the Markets and Reliability Committee votes July 28, 2026. Hydro and PSH owners in PJM can potentially access faster queue processing and interconnection timelines under the expedited track, reducing capital-deployment delays, though the summary does not detail eligibility criteria or specific timeline reductions.

documents.title: Item 06c – 3 Manual 14h Eit Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Manual 14H revision updates the PJM interconnection queue process, which directly affects hydro and PSH interconnection, uprate, and surplus interconnection service pathways — core strategic and compliance obligations for hydro owners seeking to develop, expand, or repower facilities in PJM.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 14H (Revision 06, effective July 23, 2026) is a redline update to the New Service Requests Cycle Process, the standard governing interconnection applications and studies for generation, transmission, and merchant facilities in PJM. The manual covers application, phased system-impact studies (Phase I–III), deposit and site-control requirements, interconnection agreements, and special pathways including surplus interconnection service and replacement generation. The extracted text provides the table of contents and structural overview; the substantive revisions and their specific impacts are not visible in this excerpt. Hydro owners and PSH developers in PJM should review the full redline to identify changes to queue processing timelines, deposit rules, study scope, site-control timing, or surplus interconnection eligibility — all of which affect project economics and timing for uprates and repowering.

documents.title: Item 07 – 1 Storage As A Transmission Asset – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Storage-as-Transmission-Asset (SATA) framework directly affects PSH and storage revenue recovery, operational dispatch rules, and market participation eligibility—establishing a non-market pathway for storage discharge that bypasses capacity and energy markets but triggers automatic discharge to solve transmission violations.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Planning and Operating Committees have jointly approved a Storage As Transmission Asset (SATA) framework, now moving to Markets and Reliability Committee review (first read June 26, 2026, endorsement vote scheduled August 26, 2026) with FERC filing to follow. The design creates a distinct operational mode for energy storage—including pumped hydro—dedicated to mitigating RTEP-identified transmission violations: discharge occurs automatically when the violation occurs, charging is coordinated post-discharge, and the asset is settled under transmission cost-of-service (Schedule 12 Transmission Enhancement Credits) rather than energy or capacity markets. Critically, SATA resources cannot participate in PJM markets, earn no capacity value, and are excluded from the interconnection queue, effectively creating a bifurcated storage regime. Most exposed are pumped-storage owners considering or operating transmission-constrained locations; the framework could redirect PSH toward guaranteed cost-recovery transmission-relief roles at the expense of merchant energy and ancillary-service revenue streams. The mechanism locks storage owners into a single operational purpose (voltage/flow support) tied to planning-study assumptions, with tariff conforming language expected September–November 2026.

documents.title: 2026-06-24 TC A02 CAR-SA: Initial Deactivations Redlines Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE is implementing seasonal capacity accreditation as part of Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA), which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are accredited, deactivated, and compensated in the capacity market — a core revenue stream.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms–Seasonal Accreditation (CAR-SA) initiative is revising tariff provisions to align deactivation rules, cost-workbook submissions, conduct tests, and portfolio-benefit tests with a shift from annual to seasonal capacity commitment periods and marginal reliability impact (MRI) capacity accreditation. The deactivation redlines (WMPP ID 185), presented to the Transmission Committee in June 2026, conform existing provisions to seasonal terminology and introduce equivalent CNR Capability concepts, with a proposed Q2 2027 effective date. The revisions touch multiple sections of the ISO-NE tariff (II.48–II.55 and III.A.24) that govern how resources request and are approved for deactivation, how their deactivation cost workbooks are evaluated, and how the conduct and portfolio-benefit tests determine whether deactivation is economically justified. Hydro and pumped-storage operators in ISO-NE will need to recalculate opportunity costs, break-even prices, and seasonal cash flows to comply with the new submission requirements; the shift to MRI-based accreditation means their deactivation threshold (20 MW reliability review) and compensation mechanics are redefined in terms of seasonal marginal capacity contribution rather than annual ratings. Next steps include Markets Committee review of accreditation modeling (June–July 2026), tariff redline revisions, and anticipated FERC filing in Q4 2026.

documents.title: 2026-06-24 TC A02 CAR-SA Deactivations - Section II Redlines
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE's deactivation rules directly affect hydro and PSH capacity-market participation, retirement economics, and interconnection-rights termination — core revenue and strategic options for Northeast hydro owners.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE has redlined Section II of its tariff to establish a formal Deactivation Notification process for permanent resource retirements, effective for the Capacity Commitment Period beginning June 1, 2028. Generators seeking to retire must file a deactivation notice by the first business day within 12 months of each CCP start; resources >20 MW are subject to a 90-day reliability review, after which ISO may delay deactivation if needed for local transmission reliability and offer net going-forward-cost compensation. Key mechanics: (1) resources that never held Capacity Network Resource Interconnection Service (CNR or equivalent) can deactivate by simple written notice; (2) resources inactive for three calendar years are deemed automatically deactivated unless repower-application protections apply; (3) Lead Market Participants must submit detailed cost workbooks and officer affidavits, subject to Internal Market Monitor audit. Most exposed are Northeast hydro owners contemplating retirement, especially run-of-river or peaking units currently holding CNR capacity — the reliability-review gate and potential delayed-deactivation obligation (with only net-going-forward-cost recovery) can trap owners in the market longer than planned, and the three-year automatic-deactivation clock creates planning urgency for units on extended outages.

documents.title: 2026-06-24 TC A05 NEPOOL Counsel Memo Summarizing FERC Show Cause Order to ISO-NE & PTOs (Docket No. EL26-72)
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: FERC's show-cause order to ISO-NE on large-load and co-located-load integration directly affects hydro's strategic options — co-location rules, new transmission service definitions, and generator interconnection pathways for resources serving co-located load will reshape how hydropower (especially pumped storage and run-of-river) can participate in transmission service markets and capture value from load-proximate generation.
documents.ai_summary: FERC issued a show-cause order (June 18, 2026, Docket EL26-72) to ISO-NE and Participating Transmission Owners, preliminarily finding the tariff unjust and unreasonable for lacking provisions on large-load and co-located-load integration. ISO-NE has 60 days to propose tariff changes addressing five categories: application procedures for large loads (defined as ≥50 MW peak at single site, ≥69 kV), cost transparency and cost-recovery agreements, rates and terms for co-location (co-located load defined as end-use customer load physically connected to a generating facility), new transmission services (interim NITS, firm and non-firm contract demand), and generator interconnection for resources serving electrically proximate or co-located load. The order defines three interconnection approaches for generators: new study process matching output to load forecast, use of existing service at existing POI, or load-limited service with no injection. ISO-NE must also file a 30-day informational report on resource adequacy for new large loads. This order parallels concurrent FERC actions in PJM and SPP and builds on Secretary of Energy ANOPR RM26-4-000 (October 2025). Hydro owners should note that co-location rules and new transmission service architecture will determine whether existing or new hydropower facilities can serve co-located or electrically proximate loads, and how ancillary services and BTMG netting are treated—directly affecting revenue models for pumped storage and run-of-river plants positioned near emerging data-center loads in New England.

documents.title: 2026-06-24 TC A05 FERC Show Cause Order to ISO-NE & PTOs (Docket No. EL26-72)
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: FERC Show Cause Order directing ISO-NE to establish tariff provisions for large-load integration, co-located generation, and behind-the-meter generation—directly affecting how hydro and storage resources can interconnect, take transmission service, and participate in wholesale markets alongside large industrial loads.
documents.ai_summary: FERC issued a Show Cause Order (Docket EL26-72, 195 FERC ¶ 61,215) finding ISO-NE's Tariff preliminarily unjust and unreasonable for lack of large-load integration provisions, with ISO-NE and PTOs required to respond by August 17, 2026, or request abeyance by August 3 to file a Section 205 tariff proposal by November 16. The Order identifies five deficiency categories: transmission-service application procedures, cost transparency and cost-shifting, co-location and behind-the-meter generation rules, new flexible transmission services, and generator interconnection for load-serving facilities. Most material to hydro and pumped storage: FERC mandates new interconnection service options for generators serving electrically proximate or co-located large loads (50+ MW) and requires tariff rules governing how behind-the-meter generation nets against load for transmission charges and ancillary-service cost allocation. This directly enables co-located storage/hydro at large industrial sites—including data-center clusters—to be studied and interconnected under expedited or load-limited frameworks, but requires ISO-NE to clarify which ancillary-service costs (regulation, black start) large loads cannot avoid and to ensure cost allocation does not shift to other wholesale customers. ISO-NE must also file an informational report on resource-adequacy proposals by July 20, 2026.

documents.title: 03. NPRRs for review
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NPRR1319 and NPRR1326 touch hydro/storage operational and market obligations: seasonal mothball rules (resource fleet decisions) and energy-storage state-of-charge monitoring in ancillary-services markets (storage participation and dispatch).
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT is advancing four Nodal Protocol Revision Requests through its Credit Finance Sub Group. NPRR1319 expands the winter seasonal-mothball prohibition to December–February (beyond the existing summer ban June 1–Sept 30), and clarifies that ERCOT need not run a Must-Run Alternative RFP when a resource requests seasonal mothball status on short notice (90 days prior), streamlining the mothball evaluation. NPRR1326 adds Energy Storage Resource State of Charge (SOC) information to ERCOT's Ancillary Services Capacity Monitor, improving visibility into storage availability for reserves and ancillary-services procurement. NPRR1329 allows two Resource Entities to jointly manage injection/withdrawal limits at a Point of Interconnection through shared QSE responsibility and attestation. These changes affect seasonal fleet planning (mothball availability narrows in winter), storage dispatch and AS participation (SOC transparency in real-time reserve adequacy), and multi-entity coordination rules. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this agenda item.

documents.title: 02. CFSG_Minutes_May15_2026

documents.title: 03. NPRRs for review v2
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NPRR1319 (seasonal mothball restrictions) and NPRR1326 (energy storage state-of-charge monitoring) touch resource-adequacy and storage participation rules; NPRR1320 (PRM reporting) affects seasonal adequacy forecasting—mechanisms that set precedent for how other RTOs treat mothballing, storage dispatch, and hydro seasonal value.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Credit Finance Sub Group reviewed five Nodal Protocol Revision Requests. NPRR1319 expands prohibitions on seasonal mothballing to include December–February (winter), eliminating the ability to mothball during peak-demand seasons and clarifying that ERCOT need not conduct a Must-Run Alternative RFP process when a unit seeks seasonal mothball status with short notice. NPRR1326 adds Energy Storage Resource State of Charge information to ERCOT's Ancillary Services Capacity Monitor, improving visibility into storage dispatch constraints during AS procurement. NPRR1320 formalizes reporting of multiple Planning Reserve Margin scenarios (rather than a single point forecast) in ERCOT's capacity and reserves reports, better surfacing seasonal and year-ahead adequacy risk. For hydro-rich markets monitoring cross-RTO approaches: ERCOT's tightened mothball rules and storage-visibility protocols illustrate how RTOs handle use-limited resource retirement and storage operational transparency—relevant as precedent if other RTOs adopt similar frameworks.

documents.title: 1 Agenda
