documents.ai_summary: NYISO Management Committee meeting on June 24, 2026, will vote on Storm Watch Redispatch tariff revisions and review the 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook. Storm Watch Redispatch changes will alter how NYISO dispatches resources during severe weather, potentially shifting frequency-response and reserve-procurement rules that hydro is commonly called to supply; tariff language and vote are scheduled for this meeting. The 2025–2044 Outlook, presented by Potomac Economics, models seasonal resource adequacy and scarcity conditions across the forecast period, directly informing whether hydro and PSH are expected to be critical for meeting summer/winter reserve margins and energy sufficiency — a key input to hydro owners' decisions on capex, mothballing, and market-participation strategy. No comment deadline is stated in the agenda.

documents.title: 7 2025-2044 System Resource Outlook Report Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year System & Resource Outlook directly informs transmission-constrained deliverability of hydro and zero-emissions resources, identifies congestion chokepoints (Central East, Northern New York) where hydro exports and firm capacity face transmission limits, and shapes long-term resource-adequacy strategy affecting hydro's strategic siting and revenue opportunity.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook, presented to the Management Committee on June 24, 2026, projects 20-year system conditions, demand, generation mix, and transmission congestion across New York. Key findings emphasize that transmission capability increasingly determines whether new resources can serve load, and identify the Central East interface and Northern New York as targeted upgrade opportunities for bulk transmission to enable zero-emissions and firm-capacity development. The Outlook recommends establishing a path for firm, dispatchable capacity (including potential hydrogen-based generation) to provide grid services alongside renewables, coordinating resource additions and retirements to avoid tightening system conditions, and enhancing dynamic voltage support across Central East. Hydro owners in New York and the region exporting into NYISO should track the final Outlook (Board approval scheduled for July 2026) because transmission constraints identified here directly govern where new hydro projects achieve economic deliverability, and the emphasis on firm capacity and operational flexibility reaffirms hydro's strategic value in a high-renewable future—but only if interconnection and transmission planning prioritize hydro access.

documents.title: 7c 2025 - 2044 System Resource Outlook Appendices
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: System Resource Outlook appendices inform seasonal resource adequacy, hydro conditions, and energy sufficiency planning that directly shape scarcity pricing, capacity accreditation, and strategic reserve deployment — core revenue and operational drivers for hydro and PSH.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System Resource Outlook appendices provide detailed baseline and scenario-dependent projections of generation mix, load growth, transmission, and resource availability. The document serves as the foundation for NYISO's seasonal adequacy assessments and informs accreditation of hydro and storage resources, transmission-expansion justification, and long-term market-design assumptions. Hydro operators use SRO forecasts to benchmark their own conditions (e.g., precipitation and water-availability scenarios) against ISO projections and to anticipate changes in reserve-margin tightness, scarcity-pricing exposure, and ancillary-service demand. No specific proposals or votes are signaled in the metadata; this is reference material for the corpus.

documents.title: 7a 2025-2044 System Resource Outlook Draft Report
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year resource adequacy and transmission outlook identifies hydro imports from Québec as a key variable affecting NY's generation mix, energy pricing, and interregional flexibility — directly shaping hydro-export revenue and strategic positioning of Northeast hydropower in the largest Northeast market.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's draft 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook models New York's generation and transmission needs across demand and policy scenarios through 2044, explicitly examining 'Energy Interchange with Hydro-Québec' (p. 52) as a key variable affecting system flexibility and resource adequacy. The report identifies fossil-generation retirements, growing large-load demand (data centers, economic development), and transmission constraints in the Central East corridor as drivers of resource gaps; it evaluates how offshore wind, nuclear, and repowered fossil capacity fill these gaps under zero-emissions vs. limited-emissions policy paths. For Northeast hydropower exporters (Québec and regional run-of-river operators), the outlook signals sustained scarcity and interregional transmission bottlenecks that increase the value of flexible dispatch and cross-border energy exchange — directly affecting pricing in New York's energy market and the strategic case for transmission expansion. The report is in draft status for Management Committee discussion as of June 24, 2026; next steps and stakeholder feedback timelines are not specified in the excerpt.

documents.title: 7b 2025-2044 System Resource Outlook Draft Report redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year resource and transmission outlook directly shapes hydro's strategic options by projecting demand growth, resource-adequacy needs, transmission constraints affecting deliverability, and the interplay of policies (clean-energy targets, fossil retirements) that determine where and whether hydro capacity commands scarcity value; large-load growth and transmission pocket analysis are particularly material to hydro owners evaluating whether to invest in or maintain existing capacity.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook projects substantial generation and transmission investment needs across New York under multiple demand and policy scenarios. The draft report (dated June 24, 2026, presented to the Management Committee) models demand growth driven by emerging large loads (data centers), fossil retirements, and clean-energy policy stringency, finding that supply tightens under current commitments and creating upstate generation-pocket curtailment risk and Central East transmission congestion by 2035–2044. Key variables affecting resource value include Hydro-Québec interchange availability, offshore-wind deployment, and nuclear siting. For hydro owners, demand tightness and the scenario-dependent curtailment/congestion patterns establish the planning backdrop for whether existing and new capacity can command reliability and scarcity-driven revenue; the report flags transmission as a binding constraint in upstate generation pockets where much new solar (and by extension, where storage might be sited to firm it) would concentrate, directly affecting the economic case for PSH or reservoir hydro in those zones. No immediate comment deadline or board action is stated in this draft.

documents.title: 7c Motion
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook is a long-range adequacy and planning study that shapes hydro's strategic positioning on seasonal energy availability, resource-mix expectations, and transmission/deliverability assumptions driving dispatch and revenue.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Management Committee approves the draft 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook for Board consideration at its June 24, 2026 meeting. This multi-year outlook informs resource-adequacy construction, capacity-accreditation assumptions, and interregional transmission planning affecting hydro's energy-value trajectory and seasonal scarcity signals. The report consolidates input from ESPWG and TPAS stakeholder reviews; hydro owners should review the published outlook to assess whether seasonal hydro conditions, ELCC/RA accreditation parameters, and transmission-constrained scarcity scenarios align with their revenue and participation assumptions.

documents.title: 2 Draft March 25, 2026 Meeting Minutes
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Meeting minutes documenting approval of tariff revisions on interconnection queue processes (Cluster Study), deliverability testing, thunderstorm alert settlements, and ISO-NE coordination — none of which alter hydro's revenue streams, ancillary-service pricing, storage participation rules, or market-design obligations.

documents.title: 2 Draft May 27, 2026 Meeting Minutes
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ICAP capacity-market accreditation rules and energy/ancillary-services market-design alignment directly affect hydro revenue and resource valuation; the Market Monitor's emphasis on compensation alignment with resource value and improved market signals is substantive for hydro owners' strategic positioning in NYISO.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO Management Committee approved automatic resale of surplus UCAP in the ICAP spot market (tariff revisions to Market Administration and Control Area Services Tariff); Board approval and FERC filing recommended. The Market Monitor's 2025 State of the Market Report emphasized the need for better alignment of capacity compensation with actual resource value, improved coordination for large load interconnections, and stronger energy and ancillary-services market signals to support reliable outcomes. For conventional and pumped-storage hydro owners, this signals NYISO's intent to tighten the linkage between what capacity markets pay and what hydro actually contributes to reliability and flexibility — potentially reshaping how UCAP accreditation and ICAP auction clearing interact with real-time energy and ancillary-services value capture.

documents.title: 3a Operations Report
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Operations performance metrics reveal hydro and PSH operational patterns, curtailment trends, and dispatch interactions that affect revenue realization and strategic flexibility planning for NHA members.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's May 2026 operations report shows hydro (6,324 MW nameplate including PSH) provided 22% of NYCA's emissions-free generation mix. Wind and solar curtailment reached 2.7% of NYCA intervals in May (13,775 MWh wind, 6,208 MWh FTM solar), driven by transmission-constraint binding at specific interfaces (Meyer 230kV, Montfleur-Codington 115kV, and others). Hydro capacity unavailable in real-time (DAM to RTM) ranged 100–600 MW monthly average, a material baseline for understanding opportunity-cost and dispatch-constraint interactions. The report does not propose market-design changes but serves as reference material for cross-market analysis of how hydro's operational dispatch interacts with wind/solar ramping, transmission scarcity pricing, and reserve-activation patterns.

documents.title: 4 Rate Schedule 1
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Rate Schedule 1 allocates NYISO's annual budget between withdrawal and injection billing units; the allocation directly affects the cost recovery faced by pumped-storage hydropower (which both withdraws for charging and injects for discharge) and by conventional hydro operators, and hydro stakeholders voting in the Management Committee have ongoing interest in whether and how often this cost-allocation study is revisited.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO Rate Schedule 1 governs the allocation of the ISO's annual budget between withdrawal and injection billing units, currently set at 72% withdrawals / 28% injections since January 2012. The tariff requires a Management Committee vote in Q3 each year to decide whether to commission a cost-of-service study to evaluate whether the allocation should change; from 2015 through 2025, successive votes declined to fund a study (most recent study was Black and Veatch in 2011, costing ~$215K over 6 months). The next vote is scheduled for July 29, 2026. Pumped-storage owners are directly affected because the withdrawal/injection split determines their share of NYISO operating costs: a higher injection allocation would raise PSH's charging costs relative to discharge revenue, and a shift toward withdrawals would burden conventional hydro operators more heavily. Management Committee members representing hydro interests should track whether any party moves to commission a 2026–2027 study, since the allocation mechanism is active and the cost-of-service framework can shift under the tariff's standing provisions.

documents.title: 5a MST 26.4 redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Credit and collateral requirements for market participation touch all market participants including hydro operators; changes to Operating Requirement calculation methodology, ancillary services credit treatment, and external transaction (import/export) credit requirements affect hydro operator cash-flow obligations and cost of market entry.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is revising Section 26.4 of its Tariff governing Operating Requirements and Bidding Requirements—the credit and collateral amounts customers must post to participate in ISO markets. The redline modifies the Energy and Ancillary Services Component calculation, introducing a prepayment agreement pathway (reducing credit hold from 16 to 3 days for qualified customers) and altering the Import and Export Credit Requirement formulas to use 98th and 97th percentile price-differential thresholds respectively, with weighted lookback periods (1/3 weight to prior 1 year, 2/3 weight to prior 5 years). For hydropower and pumped-storage operators, lower credit collateral requirements reduce working-capital drag on dispatch flexibility and frequency/ancillary-service provision; the prepayment option may particularly benefit smaller hydro participants. The import/export methodology change affects trading margins for operators exporting power across seams. No vote date or comment deadline is stated in the excerpt provided.

documents.title: 6a MST 4.1 redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Tariff language on cost-recovery for reliability-committed units and energy storage resources, plus external capacity suppliers — mechanisms that determine revenue recovery for dispatchable hydro and pumped storage in NYISO commitment and real-time dispatch.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO tariff section 4.1.8 establishes cost-recovery rules for units committed by the ISO for reliability. Generating units, energy storage resources, and aggregations including withdrawal-eligible generators (which includes pumped storage) can recover start-up, minimum-generation, and incremental energy costs incurred for ISO reliability commitments when those costs exceed what was bid or recovered in day-ahead and real-time markets. External installed capacity suppliers responding to SRE (Special Reserve Event) requests may also recover incremental net costs including opportunity costs of lost revenue. The redline clarifies that payments are recovered through Rate Schedule 1 OATT and disputes through Rate Schedule 1 Section 6.1.13. This directly affects PSH revenue when dispatched for reliability outside economic dispatch — the rule determines whether owners recover the true cost of water release or lost opportunity value. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this excerpt.

documents.title: 3 CEO/COO Report
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Monthly energy market performance metrics show LBMP pricing trends, ancillary-services costs (regulation, reserves, voltage support, black start), and uplift/make-whole payments that directly affect hydro revenue streams; document establishes baseline for tracking real-time price formation and cost recovery mechanisms.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's May 2026 CEO/COO report presents monthly market performance metrics: LBMP averaged $43.63/MWh (up 21% from May 2025); year-to-date average cost is $102.99/MWh, a 27.7% increase over 2025 YTD. Ancillary-services costs include regulation ($0.27/MWh), reserves ($0.96/MWh), voltage support and black start ($0.14/MWh), and negative uplift of ($0.33)/MWh offset by local reliability share of $0.28/MWh. Document also tracks RGGI carbon-allowance auction price ($35.00/ton, up 40% from prior quarter) and natural-gas price trends (Transco Z6 down 13% YoY to $2.03/MMBtu). For hydro operators, rising LBMP and reserve-service costs reflect tightening conditions; the statewide uplift swing from negative to near-zero and the 27.7% YoY cost increase signal potential scarcity pricing opportunities, though negative uplift continues to depress make-whole revenues. This is a management-committee information report with no stated votes or comment deadlines.

documents.title: 6b Motion
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Storm Watch redispatch tariff revisions affect how NYISO operates and prices dispatch during weather emergencies, directly touching hydro's dispatch obligations, real-time pricing signals, and revenue recognition in emergency conditions.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO Management Committee voted June 24, 2026 to approve tariff revisions to the Market Administration and Control Area Services Tariff governing Storm Watch Redispatch operations, with recommendation to the Board to file under FPA Section 205. The document does not detail the specific tariff changes, but Storm Watch redispatch is a real-time dispatch override mechanism triggered during severe weather; revisions likely alter how resources (including hydropower) are called, dispatched, priced, and compensated during these events. The Board vote and FPA filing will determine whether the changes take effect. Hydro operators dispatched under Storm Watch should monitor the filed tariff language and any subsequent Board decision to understand revised compensation structures and mandatory dispatch obligations.

documents.title: 6 Storm Watch Redispatch
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Storm Watch redispatch cost calculation methodology directly affects settlement of Transmission Security Area (TSA) uplift charges, which are allocated to load in Zone J and rebated to NYISO-wide withdrawals — a mechanism that alters the effective energy price hydro and other resources face in real-time dispatch and settlement.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO proposes tariff and manual clarifications to formalize the 'Long-Term Improvement' to Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) cost calculation, which modifies how Day-Ahead and real-time flows across constrained lines are computed for settlement. The July 2025 TSA events generated over $53 million in settlement costs, triggering the review; the new methodology will use Day-Ahead schedules from the bid redispatch pass and real-time schedules, departing from the existing tariff language. Tariff revisions and Transmission & Dispatch Manual Section 4.2.9.1 have been approved by the BIC and OC and are proceeding to Board of Directors and FERC filing. Because TSA costs are ultimately allocated to Zone J load (with rebates to NYISO-wide withdrawals), changes to the settlement formula affect the effective price hydro resources receive in real-time and the competitiveness of their energy bids during constrained periods.

documents.title: 8 MMU Outlook Review
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO Market Monitoring Unit analysis of renewable curtailment, storage economics, and energy price signals directly affects hydro and pumped-storage revenue via Index REC/Storage Credit structures, technology and nodal discounts on LBMP, and the value of storage-based curtailment mitigation.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Market Monitoring Unit analyzed the 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook to evaluate how market signals guide renewable and storage investment. Key finding: energy storage becomes cost-effective for curtailment mitigation only as wind and solar penetration increases and marginal curtailment rates rise; under the Index Storage Credit structure, storage has strong incentives to locate where curtailment risk is highest, but may be unprofitable near profitable renewable sites. The MMU also identifies nodal and technology discounts (differences between zonal average LBMP and generation-weighted LBMP at renewable sites), which reduce wind and solar net revenues below Index REC strike prices—a signal that affects both the competitiveness of all-renewable portfolios and the relative value of storage as a complementary product. For pumped-storage owners, this analysis quantifies the regional and temporal distribution of curtailment opportunities and implied net revenue costs of storage across three policy scenarios (Contract Case 2030, 100×44 High Load 2035, 95×44 Baseline Load 2035), directly informing where PSH investment is most economically justified under NYISO market rules.

documents.title: Final Motions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Motion #4 approves the 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook, a long-range adequacy and resource planning study that shapes NYISO capacity/RA policy, transmission needs, and seasonal energy-sufficiency outlooks—foundational inputs to hydro fleet valuation, accreditation, and retirement/mothballing decisions.
documents.ai_summary: The NYISO Management Committee unanimously approved the draft 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook on June 24, 2026, following stakeholder review at prior ESPWG (Electricity System Planning Working Group) and TPAS (Transmission Planning Advisory Subcommittee) meetings and Business Issues Committee recommendation on June 10. The Outlook is a 19-year resource adequacy and transmission-planning forecast used to drive NYISO capacity market design, seasonal energy-sufficiency assessments, and infrastructure investment priorities. Hydro owners in NYISO should review this document to understand how their resources are modeled in the long-term adequacy baseline (seasonal accreditation assumptions, drought-stress scenarios, availability profiles) and how the Outlook's transmission and resource-mix conclusions may affect market scarcity signals, RA penalties, and the valuation of dispatchable/storage capacity relative to new renewables and storage.

documents.title: 7a 2025-2044 System Resource Outlook Draft Report - Revised
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Twenty-year resource and transmission outlook directly shapes hydro's strategic options through demand growth, large-load interconnection patterns, interregional transfer capability, fossil-retirements scenarios, and Quebec hydropower interchange—all central to hydro revenue sustainability and portfolio positioning.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook (draft for Management Committee discussion June 24, 2026) models New York's generation and transmission evolution across policy and demand scenarios to 2044. Key findings: growing peak and energy demand, emergence of large loads as significant growth driver, widespread fossil retirements accelerating, and substantial transmission needs—especially Central East congestion and renewable-pocket curtailment—all shape where and when new capacity (including hydro imports from Quebec) is economically viable. The outlook explicitly evaluates Quebec hydropower interchange availability and its system-flexibility impact, directly affecting the value of interregional hydro flows into NYISO. Most relevant to hydro owners are the demand-growth projections, large-load co-location patterns, transmission-congestion forecasts (which determine export/import economics), and scenarios testing new nuclear and offshore wind arrival—all of which reset the scarcity and locational signals hydro responds to in energy and capacity markets.

documents.title: 7b 2025-2044 System Resource Outlook Draft Report redline - Revised
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year resource and transmission outlook directly informs hydro's strategic options—load growth, resource adequacy constructs, transmission constraints affecting deliverability, large-load interconnection (data centers), and interregional flows with Québec hydro—and shapes how much capacity value existing hydro will capture.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook (draft for June 24, 2026 Management Committee discussion) projects substantial transmission and generation investment needs driven by demand growth, fossil retirements, and zero-emissions policy targets. The outlook models four scenarios with varying demand futures (95x and 100x2044 load cases) and policy stringency, examining resource mix, zonal concentration, and transmission congestion—particularly Central East bottlenecks—through 2044. Load growth emerges as a significant constraint driver, with large loads (data centers) cited as a new demand source, and interregional flows with Hydro-Québec are flagged as affecting "system flexibility and risk." For hydro owners, this frames the scarcity backdrop (tightened supply conditions drive higher downstate payments) and identifies where transmission limits will compress their deliverability to load centers; it also quantifies the scale of renewable and nuclear build-out that will compete for transmission headroom and RA accreditation. The document is in draft status for committee discussion with no stated vote date or comment deadline in the excerpt.

documents.title: 3a 2027 Project Prioritization Survey Draft
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Five-minute interchange scheduling with Hydro-Québec directly affects hydro revenue by improving price convergence and real-time flexibility value; demand-response baseline alignment and ICAP surplus rules alter capacity-market participation rules affecting all thermal and storage competitors; DER aggregation integration enables small-hydro participation pathways under Order 2222.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2027 project prioritization includes four proposals with direct hydro-market implications: (1) Five-minute interchange scheduling with Hydro-Québec to replace current 15-minute/hourly settlement, improving price convergence between RTC and RTD and increasing flexibility value during renewable ramp events; (2) Demand-response baseline alignment with LSE ICAP obligations to prevent demand-side resources from becoming net ICAP sellers, affecting the competitive position of hydro in capacity markets; (3) Automatic ICAP surplus resale to prevent uplift in ICAP spot prices from unsold capacity, lowering the cost to all capacity suppliers; (4) DER aggregation system integration via API to streamline Order 2222 participation, creating a pathway for small hydro and run-of-river resources to enter wholesale markets through aggregator enrollment. All four are listed as 2027 projects with deliverables ranging from market-design completion to deployment; no comment deadlines or vote dates are stated in this survey draft.

documents.title: 2 Qualifications for Capacity Imports from ISO-NE - LIPA Project Proposal
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAPACITY-RA MECHANISM: NYISO's categorical disqualification of ISO-NE capacity imports based on Connecticut's import-constrained-zone (ICZ) status directly affects which external hydro and thermal resources can qualify for ICAP accreditation in Zone K; the proposed Manual amendment would restore eligibility for ISO-NE resources (including hydro) whose deliverability transits constrained zones, preserving multi-year contracting certainty and the value of existing ISO-NE import pathways as least-cost capacity procurement.
documents.ai_summary: LIPA proposes an FY2027 NYISO Market Project to amend ICAP Manual §4.9.3.2(iii) to create a durable eligibility pathway for ISO-NE capacity imports to Zone K, addressing a collision between ISO-NE's planned elimination of the Capacity Export Through Import-Constrained Zones (CETICZ) mechanism and NYISO's categorical disqualification of resources in zones separated from the NYCA by constrained zones. Under current Path B rules, Cross Sound Cable (HVDC) and Northport–Norwalk Harbor Cable imports from Rest-of-Pool are automatically disqualified whenever Connecticut is modeled as an ICZ, regardless of actual price separation, and ISO-NE's transition to a prompt (1-month-ahead) capacity auction removes the ~3-year forward visibility that previously allowed hedging of this risk. LIPA seeks rolling documentation rules, seasonal CSO-netting stabilization, and grandfathering of existing contracts to preserve external ICAP eligibility and multi-year contracting certainty, noting that NYISO already accepts PJM imports under facility-level deliverability tests rather than categorical zonal exclusions. No vote date or comment deadline is stated; the request is for stakeholder support to designate this as an FY2027 project. Most directly exposed are LIPA and Zone K load-serving entities relying on ISO-NE capacity imports, and any generator (hydro, thermal, or storage) seeking to export from ISO-NE Rest-of-Pool or constrained zones into NYCA.

documents.title: 20260623 MISO-PJM JCM Agenda.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Freeze Date revision and interchange ramp rules affect interregional transmission of hydro and PSH power between MISO and PJM; interregional planning updates touch transmission deliverability of hydro resources across the seam.
documents.ai_summary: MISO and PJM are revising the 2004 Freeze Date used to determine firm transmission rights on flowgates between the RTOs—a foundational rule for long-distance hydro and storage exports across the MISO-PJM seam. MISO is also updating interchange ramp rules and retiring Coordinated Transaction Scheduling (CTS) processes. Parallel interregional planning updates via the PJM-MISO Interregional Planning Stakeholder Advisory Committee (IPSAC) will address long-term transmission capability. These changes alter the terms under which hydro operators schedule and export power across the seam and may improve transmission planning visibility for future hydro deliverability; no comment deadline or vote date is stated in this agenda, but the freeze-date and ramp-rules discussions are active. Most affected are reservoir and pumped-storage operators selling capacity into PJM or using the MISO-PJM interface for arbitrage.

documents.title: 20260623 MISO-PJM JCM Item 03 MISO Interchange Ramp Rules Update.pdf
documents.filename: 20260623 MISO-PJM JCM Item 03 MISO Interchange Ramp Rules Update.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO is removing interchange ramp restrictions for day-ahead offers, which directly affects how hydropower and other dispatchable resources can schedule power flows across MISO-PJM seams and influences flexibility/ramping product value.
documents.ai_summary: MISO is eliminating restrictions on interchange ramp calculations for day-ahead market submissions, effective Q3 2026. Previously, MISO enforced limits of ±1000 MW on increased imports/decreased exports and +500 MW on increased exports/decreased imports, enforced via the Ramp Reservation System (RRS) for quarterly schedule changes. Removing these caps increases the scheduling flexibility available to hydro and other resources moving power across the MISO-PJM interface, allowing larger and more frequent interchange adjustments without pre-reservation denials. This particularly benefits reservoir-based hydropower and pumped storage that rely on schedule flexibility to optimize operations across interregional pricing signals; the change reduces a non-price scheduling constraint that previously limited their ability to respond to real-time conditions. Implementation is targeted for Q3 2026.

documents.title: 20260623 MISO-PJM JCM Item 04 Interregional Planning Update.pdf
documents.filename: 20260623 MISO-PJM JCM Item 04 Interregional Planning Update.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO-PJM interregional transmission planning directly affects hydro deliverability, interregional energy flows, and the scarcity signals that drive hydro revenue; the 2026 Targeted Market Efficiency Project (TMEP) study will identify congestion-mitigation upgrades that reshape the value of hydropower export corridors between the two RTOs.
documents.ai_summary: MISO and PJM are conducting a 2026 Coordinated System Plan (TMEP) to study interregional congestion on the MISO-PJM seam, targeting the remaining $681 million of persistent congestion after eliminating $441 million deemed non-persistent or addressable by planned upgrades. Twenty-three TMEP study candidates are under evaluation to identify limiting equipment and potential upgrades; the JRPC will make a recommendation to the Boards by November 2026. For hydro owners with generation located near the MISO-PJM seam or exporting capacity across it, transmission upgrades selected for mitigation will alter locational scarcity pricing and transfer capability — potentially increasing or decreasing the value of that hydro's energy sales into either region depending on which flowgates are relieved.

documents.title: 20260623 MISO-PJM JCM Item 02 Retirement of CTS Product.pdf
documents.filename: 20260623 MISO-PJM JCM Item 02 Retirement of CTS Product.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Retirement of CTS affects interregional energy scheduling and price convergence between MISO and PJM, which directly impacts dispatch economics and revenue for hydro resources located on or serving the MISO-PJM seam; loss of an interchange optimization tool reduces locational price signals and flexibility value for assets arbitraging between the RTOs.
documents.ai_summary: MISO and PJM propose to retire the Coordinated Transaction Scheduling (CTS) product—a coordinated interchange optimization tool approved by FERC in 2016 and launched in 2017—due to minimal usage, underperformance ($740k in annual savings vs. $17–72M projected), and high migration costs ($1.4M to migrate to the new Market Clearing Engine, $0.8M to maintain over 5 years). Stakeholders submitted mixed feedback: some requested a modern replacement (e.g., SPP's Inter-Market Optimization) before retirement to avoid operational gaps, while others supported retirement given CTS inefficiencies. MISO responds that other interchange products (Fixed, Cleared Day-Ahead, and Dynamic schedules) already offer more granular, real-time scheduling (adjustable to 20 minutes before interval vs. 75 minutes for CTS), and MISO is open to pursuing replacement products with PJM and SPP after retirement. MISO and PJM intend to coordinate tariff and Joint Operating Agreement filings later in 2026 to remove CTS. Hydro owners on or serving the MISO-PJM interface lose a tool for optimizing interchange pricing and may see reduced locational value signals for flexibility; replacements under discussion are not yet defined.

documents.title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 01c Minutes 20260202.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) study and Reliability Based Demand Curves (RBDCs) directly inform capacity market accreditation and resource-adequacy requirements that affect how much capacity hydropower and pumped storage can sell into MISO's Planning Resource Auction.
documents.ai_summary: MISO presented PY 2026-2027 Marginal Reliability Impact (MRI) curves and preliminary Reliability Based Demand Curves (RBDCs) showing systemwide summer LOLE risk as highest, but subregional variation (North/Central region peaks in summer; South region peaks in winter, a seasonal shift from prior year). MISO confirmed battery-storage modeling will use 'even loss methodology' and is implementing an enhanced wind-profile model for the PY 2027-2028 LOLE study (near-term), with long-term coordination toward consistent renewable profiles across all planning studies. The wind-profile refinement showed preliminary no material change to LOLE outcomes. These LOLE and RBDC outputs flow directly into MISO's capacity accreditation framework and Planning Resource Auction parameters; seasonal subregional shifts in reliability risk can reset the demand curves that cap how much capacity owners—including hydro and pumped storage—can bid and clear at given prices. Next LOLEWG meeting is June 23, 2026.

documents.ai_summary: MISO's LOLEWG is reviewing the seasonal LOLE-target methodology and the load and capacity models that underpin Planning Year 2027–2028 resource-adequacy calculations. The agenda includes refreshers on seasonal LOLE targets, LOLE load-modeling results, capacity and outage-year assumptions, and wind-profile enhancements. Substantively: LOLE targets and load forecasts drive which resources are needed for RA compliance and how scarcity is priced; hydro's accreditation (seasonal and annual ELCC/UCAP) and the timing of peak-scarcity events are set within these models. Wind-profile enhancements may shift the seasonal shape of reserve requirements, changing when hydro's flexibility commands the highest value. Next meeting is August 11, 2026; the working group is requesting feedback on several items (load modeling, capacity assumptions, wind profiles), though no formal comment deadline is stated in this agenda.

documents.title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 01d Management Plan.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) analysis directly determines Planning Reserve Margin (PRM) requirements, zonal Local Reliability Requirements (LRR), and Capacity Import/Export Limits (CIL/CEL) — all foundational inputs to MISO's capacity accreditation, seasonal adequacy assessment, and locational pricing signals that drive hydro revenue and participation incentives.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation Working Group (LOLEWG) manages the LOLE analysis that calculates Planning Reserve Margin requirements, zonal Local Reliability Requirements, and Capacity Import/Export Limits per Module E-1 of the tariff, conforming to ERO and Regional Entity standards. The group held five meetings in 2025 (average 154 participants) and has scheduled work in 2026–2027 including annual wind/solar capacity-credit reviews, LOLE load modeling for PY2027–2028, planned-maintenance modeling, seasonal LOLE targets, and updates to CIL/CEL calculations. These LOLE-derived parameters feed into MISO's capacity accreditation framework and seasonal adequacy mechanisms; changes to load modeling assumptions, wind/solar profiles, or maintenance treatment directly affect the reserve margins and locational signals that determine how much capacity MISO needs from hydro assets and at what premium scarcity pricing may emerge, influencing both RA accreditation levels and energy-market scarcity incentives for dispatchable hydro.

documents.title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 06 Capacity and Outyear Model Assumptions.pdf
documents.filename: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 06 Capacity and Outyear Model Assumptions.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) model determines resource adequacy requirements and capacity accreditation thresholds that directly affect hydro and storage revenue through capacity-market clearing prices and RA obligation sizing.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation Working Group has updated capacity assumptions for the PY 2027-2028 LOLE study, making two key changes: (1) resource retirements from OMS-MISO Survey data will no longer be modeled in outyear LOLE projections—only retirements with approved Attachment Y filings will be reflected—and (2) ERAS resources will now be included in the model based on their Application In-Service date rather than earlier assumptions. These changes affect which resources are counted toward resource adequacy in MISO's planning year, directly influencing the capacity margin and thus the magnitude of capacity-market clearing prices and RA accreditation values for all dispatchable resources including hydro. The shift away from retirement-survey data means retiring units will be assumed available longer in the outyear, potentially depressing LOLE and lowering RA scarcity signals, while ERAS (Energy Resource Aggregation System) inclusion may improve visibility of emerging resources in adequacy. No comment deadline or vote date is specified in this working-group presentation.

documents.title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 07 Wind Profile Enhancement Update.pdf
documents.filename: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 07 Wind Profile Enhancement Update.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's enhanced wind-profile methodology changes how wind and storage resources are accredited in ELCC and DLOL frameworks, directly affecting capacity-value revenue for dispatchable resources including pumped storage competing in MISO's resource-adequacy market.
documents.ai_summary: MISO is adopting enhanced meteorology-driven wind profiles for its PY2027–2028 Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) studies, replacing status-quo profiles with 30-year synthetic data that better capture low-wind conditions and seasonal variability. The new profiles increase wind ELCC values (especially in Spring, rising from 19.3% to 43.8%), but also shift the distribution of critical-event durations by season, causing storage DLOL accreditation to rise significantly in Fall (+12.7 percentage points) while falling in Winter and Spring (−11.2% and −20.0% respectively). Thermal resources' accreditation is affected primarily by shifts in which maintenance-outage hours overlap with newly identified critical hours. MISO plans to move forward with the enhanced profiles subject to final review and will maintain a hybrid approach (synthetic pre-2012, observed post-2012). This directly reshapes the seasonal accreditation profile for pumped-storage hydro competing in MISO's capacity market, particularly for four-hour-plus storage duration valuations.

documents.title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 02 Liaison Report.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This liaison report covers MISO administrative announcements, workshop schedules, data system transitions, and operational alerts—none touching hydro revenue streams, use-limited resource rules, storage participation, capacity accreditation, or strategic market-design choices that affect hydro or PSH operators.

documents.title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 03 Refresher on Seasonal LOLE Targets.pdf
documents.filename: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 03 Refresher on Seasonal LOLE Targets.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's seasonal LOLE framework affects how hydro and other dispatchable resources are accredited in capacity adequacy studies and shapes seasonal RA requirements that determine hydro's reliability value and revenue exposure across quarters.
documents.ai_summary: MISO adopted a seasonal LOLE (Loss of Load Expectation) construct in 2023–2024, replacing a single annual 0.1 days/year target with per-season minima of 0.01 days/year. The framework accounts for seasonal variation in peak load (winter mornings, overnight cold events), forced-outage rates (thermal units fail more in extreme cold/heat), planned maintenance clustering (spring/fall), and ELCC values for wind and solar, which differ materially by season. MISO runs an annual LOLE study first, then upwardly adjusts any season falling below 0.01 days/year by adding perfect or proxy units. This document presents the construct as positioned between PJM's simple annual PRMR% approach and ISO-NE's proposed monthly granularity, and includes historical LOLE splits (PY25–26 all summer risk; PY26–27 split summer/winter/spring; PY29–30 nearly even summer/winter) showing how seasonal requirements dampen year-to-year volatility. Hydro operators are most affected because seasonal accreditation directly determines their RA contribution value in each quarter—winter peaking and spring outage windows matter independently rather than being smoothed into a summer-anchored requirement—and because the tariff filing referenced as of June 1, 2026 (adding a perfect positive unit to the LOLE algorithm) may change the study's outcome.

documents.title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 04 LOLE Load Modeling Process and Results.pdf
documents.filename: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 04 LOLE Load Modeling Process and Results.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) study drives resource-adequacy requirements and seasonal capacity accreditation for all dispatchable units including hydro; unprecedented load growth and forecast-uncertainty revisions directly affect the capacity value and RA showing requirements hydro operators must meet, and seasonal peak-demand modeling shapes winter/spring reliability signals that influence hydro dispatch and scarcity pricing.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Loss of Load Expectation Working Group is revising its load modeling process and load-forecast uncertainty assumptions for Planning Year 2027–2028, noting that actual loads have deviated from LOLE projections by 4% in shoulder seasons—exceeding current uncertainty bands. MISO is automating the load-development process (replacing NeuroShell with Histogram Gradient Boosting), standardizing extreme-temperature thresholds, and proposing to expand load-forecast error bands (e.g., from ±2% to a range including +3% to +4%) to reflect data-center and electrification-driven load growth. The key mechanism is that higher projected load peaks and wider uncertainty distributions increase the probability of scarcity conditions in LOLE runs, raising capacity-accreditation requirements for all dispatchable resources and making seasonal hydro availability more valuable in winter and shoulder months. MISO is requesting stakeholder feedback on model inputs and uncertainty-assumption options through July 7, 2026, via the Feedback Tool at misoenergy.org.

documents.title: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 05 CIL CEL Model and Input File Overview.pdf
documents.filename: 20260623 LOLEWG Item 05 CIL CEL Model and Input File Overview.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CIL/CEL study directly affects hydro's capacity accreditation and zone-to-zone transfer limits under MISO's Planning Resource Auction, which determines how much capacity hydro can export across Local Resource Zones and thus what cleared capacity and locational revenue it receives.
documents.ai_summary: MISO is conducting Capacity Import Limit (CIL) and Capacity Export Limit (CEL) studies for Planning Year 2027-2028 across four seasons (Summer 2027, Fall 2027, Winter 2027-2028, Spring 2028). CIL/CEL values constrain capacity transfers between MISO's Local Resource Zones in the Planning Resource Auction and are calculated using transfer-capability analyses that redispatch conventional generators and wind/solar (wind and solar are curtailed, not ramped up). The study uses PSSE power-flow models and contingency files now posted on ShareFile for stakeholder review. MISO requests stakeholder feedback on input files by July 7, 2026, with preliminary results expected in September 2026 and final values in October 2026. For hydro operators, tighter CIL/CEL values reduce their zone's ability to export capacity—lowering locational RA value and export revenue—while looser limits enhance those streams; the redispatch methodology's treatment of hydro as curtailable (not dispatchable) may understate the transfer capability available when hydro can flex output. Most exposed are multi-zone hydro fleets and pumped storage units in export-constrained Local Resource Zones.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a conference agenda focused on general economic outlook and EV load-impact modeling; it does not address any hydro/PSH revenue stream (pricing, ancillary services, capacity accreditation), compliance obligation, or strategic option.

documents.title: 04 Geospatial Modeling of EV Load Impacts at National Grid
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Load growth from EV electrification drives demand forecasting inputs to NYISO's resource-adequacy and operational-planning processes, which directly affect hydro's strategic value and dispatch scheduling; co-located load and feeder-level demand patterns also inform transmission and storage deployment decisions that touch hydro assets and deliverability.
documents.ai_summary: National Grid's Load Forecasting & Analytics team presented geospatial modeling methods to forecast electric-vehicle load impacts on NYISO's distribution and transmission network at feeder and system levels through 2035+. The work integrates EV adoption propensity models (driven by socioeconomics, vehicle-registration data, program enrollment) with vehicle-specific charging profiles (light-duty, medium/heavy-duty, transit/school bus) to project unconstrained load impacts by location and time. The presentation outlined a hybrid allocation approach (census-tract and parcel-level granularity for subsets like depot-based MHDVs and queued storage/PV) and flagged ongoing challenges in data integration, propensity-model robustness, and governance consistency. No near-term policy decision or vote was announced, but this forecasting framework feeds NYISO's system-planning and resource-adequacy studies—which determine seasonal reserve requirements, transmission expansion triggers, and the scarcity value of dispatchable hydro and PSH.

title: Installed Capacity Working Group
meeting_hydro_reason: The Installed Capacity Working Group addresses capacity-market design, accreditation rules, and performance standards that directly affect how hydro and PSH resources are valued, qualified, and compensated in NYISO's capacity market.

documents.title: 1 Agenda
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO is discussing capacity accreditation modeling and resource-adequacy improvements that directly affect how hydro and storage resources are credited toward ICAP requirements, plus intermittency-balancing rules and transmission-security backstops that shape dispatch and revenue.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Installed Capacity Working Group is convening to address three mechanisms affecting hydro and storage operators. First, Mike Swider will lead a session on 'Improving Capacity Accreditation and Resource Adequacy Modeling' (10:05–11:05), which signals active revision of how NYISO calculates ELCC or capacity value for dispatchable and storage resources — the foundation of ICAP showings and revenue. Second, Amanda Myott will discuss 'External SRE Opportunity Cost & Penalty' (11:05–11:35), addressing how NYISO applies mitigation to out-of-market resources and opportunity-cost-based offer floors, a chronic pinch point for storage and use-limited hydro. Third, Vijay Kaki's session on 'Balancing Intermittency: Manual Updates' (11:35–11:55) suggests NYISO is refining how it handles forecast error and variability, which affects flexible ramping procurement and settlement. A fourth agenda item, 'Seasonal TSL Floor Kick Off' (Keegan Guinn, 1:10–1:55), signals the opening of a seasonal transmission-security limit study that may alter locational dispatch signals or scarcity pricing. The meeting is structured across the ICAP Working Group, Market Issues Working Group, and Price-Responsive Load Working Group, indicating multi-stakeholder engagement. No specific vote or comment deadline is stated in the agenda itself.

documents.title: 4 Balancing Intermittency: Manual Updates
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Uncertainty Reserve Requirements (URR) design directly affects hydro's revenue from ancillary services and flexibility products; URR adds new locational reserve requirements calculated for Net Load, Wind, and Solar forecast uncertainty, requiring hydro and other dispatchable resources to supply additional reserves on top of existing requirements, with deployment in August 2026.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is implementing Uncertainty Reserve Requirements (URR)—a new 24-hour reserve product to account for forecast uncertainty in Net Load, Land-Based Wind, Offshore Wind, and Front-of-Meter Solar. The URR design was accepted by FERC in July 2025 (ER25-1998) and is scheduled for August 2026 deployment. NYISO has incorporated stakeholder feedback from June 2nd meeting and updated the Ancillary Services Manual and a new Locational Reserve Requirements document; notably, the Long Island 30-minute total reserve requirement will vary from 270 MW (off-peak) to 540 MW (on-peak). These additional, locational reserve requirements create new demand for the ramping and flexibility services that hydropower traditionally supplies, raising the value of flexible hydro capacity during high-uncertainty periods (high wind/solar variability). All resources—including hydro and pumped storage—will be obligated to contribute to URR supply. Stakeholder vote scheduled for July 15–16, 2026, at BIC and OC meetings; deployment begins August 2026.

documents.title: 3 External SRE Opportunity Cost & Penalty
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO proposes to cap opportunity-cost recovery for external ICAP suppliers (including hydropower and storage) called for emergency service, and to add FERC referral penalties for non-compliance — changes that directly affect how external hydro and PSH can recover costs when required to respond to emergency calls, and the penalties they face for non-compliance.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO proposes two changes to external ICAP supplier obligations during System Reserve Events (SREs): (1) cap opportunity-cost recovery at the applicable proxy-generator-bus LBMP minus ATC interface congestion, eliminating pass-through of revenues external resources could earn in neighboring control areas; (2) retain existing ICAP Shortfall Penalties for SRE non-response but add FERC referral threat if an external supplier delivers to another control area while failing to meet its NYISO energy obligation. The proposal reframes opportunity costs as compensation for NYISO's mandatory -$1,000/MWh bidding requirement, aligning external treatment closer to internal reliability suppliers. Next step: NYISO will return to ICAPWG/MIWG with draft tariff and manual language following stakeholder feedback. For external hydro and PSH owners operating at NYISO seams, this eliminates the option to recover the full spread between NYISO and neighboring-market prices during an SRE call, while the FERC referral adds reputational/regulatory risk to non-compliance.

documents.title: 2 Improving Capacity Accreditation and Resource Adequacy Modeling
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: NYISO is evaluating alternative capacity accreditation methodologies (moving from LOLE to Expected Unserved Energy) that will directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are credited toward resource adequacy requirements and capacity payments, with implications for accreditation factors (CAFs) and UCAP contributions across different resource classes.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is conducting a study to increase transparency in resource adequacy modeling and evaluate whether Expected Unserved Energy (EUE)—a magnitude-based metric capturing duration and depth of capacity shortfalls—should replace or supplement the traditional 1-day-in-10-years LOLE criterion for calculating Capacity Accreditation Factors (CAFs). The CAF determines how much Unforced Capacity (UCAP) each resource can provide; EUE would capture reliability contributions differently across resource types, particularly affecting hydro's seasonal contribution and ability to mitigate prolonged weather events as NYISO's winter-risk profile increases (4.4 GW of intermittent resources expected in five years). NYISO has engaged GE to model CAF sensitivity under EUE scenarios and assess accreditation stability; the study is due to complete in 2026, with broader stakeholder assessment of which methodology better reflects NYISO's evolving resource mix to follow.

documents.title: 4a Locational Reserves Requirements
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO locational reserve requirements define the operating-reserve products (spinning, 10-minute, 30-minute) that hydropower and pumped-storage units are eligible to supply, and establish locational and magnitude thresholds that affect their reserve-procurement revenue and dispatch obligations.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO specifies locational reserve requirements across five zones (NYCA, EAST, SENY, NYC, LI) for three reserve products: spinning reserve (most restrictive, synchronous units only), 10-minute non-synchronized reserve, and 30-minute reserve. NYCA requires 655 MW spinning and 1,310 MW 10-minute total; EAST requires 330 MW spinning and 1,200 MW 10-minute; SENY requires 1,300–1,800 MW 30-minute (varying by hour, reduced to zero during thunderstorm alerts); NYC and LI have localized requirements of 625–1,250 MW and 120–540 MW respectively. Hydropower and pumped-storage units eligible for spinning reserve (synchronous generation) are the primary suppliers of the 655 MW NYCA spinning requirement and regional spinning reserves; conventional hydro can also supply 10-minute and 30-minute reserves. The document does not indicate a pending vote or comment deadline but establishes the baseline rules governing reserve accreditation and revenue eligibility for reservoir hydro and PSH in New York.

documents.title: 4b Draft Ancillary Services Manual redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's redlined Ancillary Services Manual directly governs how hydro and pumped-storage resources bid, qualify, and are compensated for regulation, reserves, voltage support, and frequency response — hydro's core revenue streams and compliance obligations.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO has released a draft (May 2026) redline to its Ancillary Services Manual 2, governing qualification, performance tracking, settlement, and demand curves for regulation and frequency response service, operating reserves (spinning, non-spinning, quick-start), voltage support, and reactive power capability testing. The manual includes new or revised sections on energy storage resources' reactive power testing (3.6.5), synchronous condenser testing (3.6.3), HVDC VSC technology (3.6.4), and regulation service performance audits (4.11.3). The document is in draft for discussion; it was accepted by BIC on 04/15/2026 and OC on 04/16/2026, with an effective date of 05/01/2026. Hydro and pumped-storage operators must track revisions to regulation demand curves (4.8), performance adjustment rules (4.5–4.6), and resource qualification thresholds (4.11), as these directly affect the value of regulation and reserve services — historically hydro's largest ancillary-service revenue source in NYISO. The manual's treatment of energy storage resources in reactive power testing and performance frameworks may also signal NYISO's direction on storage parity rules relevant to pumped-storage PSH participation.

documents.title: 3 External SRE Opportunity Cost & Penalty - Revised
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO proposal to cap opportunity-cost recovery and strengthen defection penalties for external ICAP suppliers affects how hydro and other dispatchable resources located outside NY (notably in PJM or ISO-NE) value their NYISO capacity obligations and manage the trade-off between NYISO SRE calls and higher-priced neighboring-market opportunities — a direct revenue and strategic participation lever for multi-region hydro portfolios.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO proposes to cap opportunity-cost recovery for external ICAP suppliers responding to Supplemental Resource Evaluations (SREs) at the applicable proxy-bus LBMP, excluding ATC interface congestion, and to add FERC referral as a sanction for suppliers that defect to sell energy elsewhere during an SRE obligation. Currently, external suppliers can claim any opportunity costs; the proposal aligns external cost recovery more closely to internal reliability-commitment rules (startup, minimum generation) and removes the subsidy for selling at neighboring-market prices. Status: draft for ICAPWG/MIWG discussion; next step is stakeholder feedback and return with tariff/manual language (no deadline stated). External ICAP suppliers — especially multi-region hydropower and gas operators serving NYISO capacity markets from PJM, ISO-NE, or IESO — face lower opportunity-cost compensation in high-priced hours in neighboring control areas, reducing the financial benefit of maintaining NYISO ICAP obligations; simultaneously, the threat of FERC referral for non-compliance strengthens the incentive to answer SREs even when outside-market prices spike.

documents.title: 6 Seasonal TSL Floor Kick Off
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's seasonal TSL floor methodology directly affects ICAP accreditation and capacity requirements for all resources including hydro and storage, with implications for winter peak conditions and derating factors for limited-control run-of-river hydro.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is implementing seasonal Transmission Security Limit (TSL) floor values as part of its Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements proposal (filed with FERC February 18, 2026, Docket ER26-1431, effective date requested June 28, 2026, for 2027-2028 capability year onward). The seasonal methodology will replace annual calculations with winter- and summer-specific peak loads, bulk transmission limits, equipment ratings, and derating factors. Critically, NYISO proposes to include winter performance data for Limited Control Run of River hydro resources in the seasonal derating factor calculation and will establish winter-specific Special Case Resource (SCR) enrollment, which raises the ICAP addback component. The document indicates that 5-year derating factors will consider winter EFORd for thermal units and seasonal adjustments for intermittent and run-of-river hydro per updated ICAP Manual Attachment N (revised June 2026), and that seasonal External Unforced Deliverability Rights (UDR) elections would be implemented starting 2027-2028. Pumped storage and battery-based energy storage resources (ESRs) currently face no specific UCAP duration-limit adjustment in the TSL floor calculation, though the document notes ESRs are modeled in resource adequacy with full availability subject to duration limits. Run-of-river hydro owners should monitor the winter derating factor methodology to understand how winter-season performance data may reduce their seasonal ICAP accreditation; reservoir and pumped-storage operators should track the seasonal UDR election framework and any future ESR adjustments proposed.

documents.title: 5 Transmission Security Background
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's derating-factor methodology and transmission-security-assessment practices directly affect ICAP accreditation for all generators including hydropower, and the document addresses seasonal EFORd calculations and limited-control run-of-river hydro treatment in capacity-adequacy studies.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO staff present transmission-security assessment background and derating-factor recommendations for the Installed Capacity market, including treatment of seasonal forced outage rates (EFORd) for thermal units and reference to run-of-river hydro derating under ICAP Manual Attachment N. The document identifies a methodological gap: 5-year seasonal EFORd averages understate zonal derating factors by roughly 2% compared to 5-year continuous EFORd, and NYISO recommends developing an automated tool to support multi-year continuous seasonal EFORd calculations. The implications for hydro are indirect but material—derating factors determine Locational Minimum Installed Capacity Requirements (LCRs) that shape the ICAP demand curve and pricing hydro and other resources bid into. Run-of-river resources are explicitly noted as following Attachment N methodology, though the document does not propose changes to that treatment in this June 2026 working-group meeting.

title: LLWG Meeting
meeting_hydro_reason: Large load interconnection and co-located load rules directly affect hydro's strategic options for customer value capture and market participation, and ERCOT's approach sets precedent for other RTOs where hydro operates.

documents.title: LLWG Meeting Agenda 2026-06-19
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's Large Load Interconnection Queue and related power variation/compliance discussions directly affect large-load participation and co-location options that hydro owners may pursue as strategic opportunities in Texas, and establishes comparison precedent for how non-hydro RTOs handle interconnection and load-coupled resource design.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Large Load Working Group convenes to discuss queue readiness, interconnection batches (PGRR145, PGRR144, PGRR146), power variation limits on large loads (LCL), and site-development compliance (NOGRR282). Agee Springer (ERCOT) will present the Large Load Interconnection Queue status and readiness of Batch Zero; Matt Mereness will cover PGRR145 readiness; Jimmy Zhang will address power variation limits. No specific proposals or decisions are detailed in the agenda, but these mechanics directly shape how large loads (data centers, industrial consumers) can interconnect and operate in ERCOT, which is strategically relevant to NHA members considering load-following or co-located hydro strategies in competitive markets and to cross-RTO comparison of how large-load participation rules influence real-time flexibility and transmission constraints.

documents.title: June 19 LLWG Report
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large load interconnection and integration directly affects hydro strategic options: load growth (especially data centers) raises scarcity value of existing hydro output, and ERCOT's interconnection queue rules set a comparison point for how RTOs handle resource deliverability under surge demand.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT reports on the status of its Large Load Interconnection process as of June 2026, tracking approximately 8,927 MW that have received Approval to Energize, with observed simultaneous peak consumption of 3,675 MW in June 2026. The bulk of the queue comprises data centers and cryptocurrency mining operations, with most loads concentrated in the South Central and West zones. This load surge represents a material change in ERCOT's resource adequacy outlook: rapid influx of synchronous, year-round consumption increases the scarcity premium for dispatchable generation, including hydropower exports to Texas if any interregional seams are extended. The presentation updates queue mechanics and validation processes for Batch Zero but does not propose tariff or participation-model changes; it serves as a monitoring document for how sustained load growth reshapes the marginal value of flexible, on-demand resources.

title: Business Issues Committee
meeting_hydro_reason: NYISO Business Issues Committee addresses tariff changes, market-design proposals, and participation rules that directly affect hydro revenue, capacity accreditation, and operational obligations in the Northeast's largest hydro market.

documents.title: 1 Agenda
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO is proposing changes to ICAP Manual rules governing seasonal capacity accreditation (UDR/EDR elections) and must-offer requirements for dispatchable resources, which directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage facilities are accredited and obligated to participate in the capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee will vote on proposed revisions to the ICAP Manual and attachments governing Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements, specifically seasonal UDR (Unforced Demand Response) and EDR (Emergency Demand Response) elections and must-offer requirements. The presentation and vote are scheduled for June 18, 2026, 10:05–10:35 am. The mechanic affects hydro and PSH accreditation methodology: changes to seasonal capacity elections and must-offer obligations determine whether and how run-of-river, reservoir, and pumped-storage facilities count toward winter RA showings and what dispatch obligations attach to that capacity. Owners of dispatchable hydro and PSH should monitor this vote, as the revised rules will reset their seasonal capacity election rights and operational requirements.

documents.title: 2 Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements – Seasonal UDR/EDR Elections and Must Offer Requirement: Proposed ICAP Manual and Attachment Revisions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO establishes seasonal must-offer requirements and penalties for holders of External Unforced Capacity Deliverability Rights (UDRs) and External-to-Rest of State Deliverability Rights (EDRs), which directly affects how external capacity—including hydropower—can be exported from and must be offered to meet NYCA obligations, and creates an enforcement mechanism (penalty methodology in new Attachment Q) that changes an owner's compliance exposure and dispatch obligations.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is implementing seasonal elections for External UDRs and EDRs starting May 1, 2027 (2027–2028 Capability Year), with elections due August 1, 2026. Holders of External UDRs or EDRs must submit distinct seasonal elections (one for Summer, one for Winter) to return capacity to the NYCA, or face a must-offer requirement: capacity not returned must be offered in each ICAP Spot Market Auction or certified to meet LSE minimum UCAP requirements, and failure triggers a penalty calculated using Market Clearing Price and a new Attachment Q methodology converting ICAP to UCAP. This reshapes the options available to external capacity owners (including hydro exporters to NYCA) by forcing them to commit seasonally to either export or domestic service, with financial penalties for non-compliance; pumped-storage and reservoir hydro operators holding external rights into New York will need to evaluate seasonal dispatch strategies against the penalty risk. The proposal was filed with FERC in Docket No. ER26-1431 with requested effective date June 28, 2026; ICAP Manual revisions become effective one business day after the 10-day appeal period if approved (by July 7, 2026 absent appeal or FERC rejection).

documents.title: 2b Motion
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO proposes seasonal capacity accreditation and must-offer requirement changes affecting hydro and PSH revenue recognition and operational obligations in the Installed Capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee approved revisions to the Installed Capacity Manual establishing seasonal Unforced Demand Response (UDR) and Energy Demand Response (EDR) elections and must-offer requirements tied to Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements (FERC Docket ER26-1431). The proposal changes how hydro and storage assets are accredited for capacity in seasonal winter conditions and imposes new must-offer obligations during those periods. The revisions become effective only upon FERC acceptance; rejection triggers automatic rescission. Reservoir hydropower and pumped-storage operators face potential changes to their capacity revenue recognition and dispatch obligations during the winter season, with implementation contingent on FERC's decision in ER26-1431.

documents.title: 2a Draft ICAP Manual
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's ICAP Manual governs capacity accreditation, duration-adjustment factors, and reporting requirements that directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are valued and compensated in NYISO's capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's draft Installed Capacity (ICAP) Manual 17.0, effective April 2, 2026, specifies minimum capacity requirements, unforced-capacity calculations, and resource-specific test conditions and reporting obligations for all capacity suppliers. The manual explicitly addresses energy-limited resources (including hydro), co-located storage resources (directly relevant to PSH), duration-adjustment factors, and DER aggregations. Sections 4.1.1 and 4.1.4 detail Energy Duration Limitations and Duration Adjustment Factors for installed-capacity suppliers and co-located storage; Section 4.2 governs Demonstrated Maximum Net Capability (DMNC) testing that determines hydro's accredited capacity; and Section 4.4 establishes monthly operating-data reporting requirements (including intermittent power resources and energy-limited resources). The document is in draft for discussion at the Business Issues Committee (BIC) meeting on June 18, 2026, following circulation at the June 9, 2026 ICAPWG meeting (yellow-highlighted changes noted). Hydro and PSH owners need to review the updated duration-adjustment methodology, DMNC test procedures, and state-of-charge rules for co-located storage to confirm accreditation treatment and compliance deadlines.

documents.title: Final Motion
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO is approving installed capacity manual revisions for seasonal capacity accreditation elections and must-offer requirements affecting hydro and storage resource participation and revenue obligations.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee approved on June 18, 2026 revisions to the Installed Capacity Manual implementing Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements, specifically addressing seasonal UDR/EDR (Unforced Demand Response / Equivalent Demand Response) elections and must-offer requirements (FERC Docket ER26-1431). The revisions change the rules governing when and how capacity resources, including hydropower and pumped storage, must be available and offer energy during winter scarcity periods — affecting both their accreditation methodology and their operational obligations. The approval is conditional on FERC acceptance of the filing; if FERC rejects the proposal, the manual revisions will be rescinded without further action. Most exposed are owners with winter-dependent capacity portfolios and PSH facilities subject to new seasonal election and dispatch mechanics.

documents.title: 2c Draft ICAP Manual edited during BIC
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's ICAP Manual revision defines unforced-capacity accreditation, duration-adjustment factors, and participation rules for energy-limited resources (hydro) and co-located storage—direct determinants of how much capacity hydro and pumped-storage plants can sell and at what revenue.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO has issued a draft ICAP (Installed Capacity) Manual 04, effective April 2, 2026, for BIC (Business Issues Committee) discussion and acceptance. The manual governs how much unforced capacity hydro, pumped storage, and other resources can supply to the NYCA, including duration-adjustment factors for energy-limited resources (§4.1.1), co-located storage provisions (§4.1.4), and DMNC/DMGC testing procedures (§4.2). Hydro and PSH owners must comply with operating-data reporting (§4.4), bidding/scheduling (§4.8.7 for co-located storage), and maintenance-scheduling rules; the manual sets the technical and temporal basis for their capacity accreditation and revenue in NYISO's ICAP auctions. The document is in draft form and was under BIC discussion as of June 18, 2026—no comment deadline or vote date is stated in this excerpt.

documents.title: Resource Integration Topics
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT interconnection queue mechanics and dynamic-model requirements for generation resources set cross-market precedent for how RTOs handle queue reform, model validation, and resource-integration standards that affect storage and flexible-resource participation pathways hydro monitors for applicability elsewhere.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Resource Integration Working Group presented Q2 2026 status on the Quarterly Stability Assessment (QSA) process, including model-validation deadlines (45 days prior to QSA deadline for FIS studies, 15–30 days for dynamic model review), PSCAD model-quality test requirements effective August 1, 2026, and active revision requests on ESR maintenance exceptions and in-kind generation definitions. As of May 31, 2026, ERCOT has 1,960 active generation interconnection requests totaling 458 GW: 172 GW of battery storage, 163 GW solar, 71 GW natural gas, 48 GW wind, and 5 GW other (including 65 small-gen projects, 37 model-ready). The queue has grown from ~409 GW (May 2025) to ~458 GW (May 2026), with battery capacity in interconnection remaining near 172 GW. For hydro and storage operators, this document signals ERCOT's evolving requirements for dynamic-model validation and queue-management timelines; the large battery-storage queue (172 GW / 412 GWh) demonstrates how a non-hydro RTO is accommodating storage participation and sets a benchmark for comparable participation rules elsewhere. The QSA deadline structure and prerequisite schedules may inform how other RTOs design interconnection-process certainty for flexible resources. No immediate FERC filing or NHA member vote is present, but the document is useful reference material for understanding queue and stability-assessment mechanics in a hydro-free market.

documents.title: ERCOT-RIWG-2026-06-18-RIOO-INR Workflow Enhancements
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: INTERCONNECTION: ERCOT is enhancing its Interconnection Request and operational-equipment workflow to support repowering and unit modifications at existing substations, which directly affects how hydro and storage owners modify or upgrade existing plants and pursue strategic repower opportunities in ERCOT's interconnection process.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Grid and Market Solutions team is deploying enhancements to the Interconnection Request (INR) workflow on July 10, 2026, allowing Market Participants to select operational substations managed by the Resource Scheduling system and add new equipment on top of existing deployed infrastructure. Repowering and unit-modification functionality will be delivered in October 2026. This workflow change enables hydro and storage operators to pursue uprates, conversions, and repower projects more flexibly by building on existing operational infrastructure rather than being limited to greenfield substation creation, reducing barriers to life-extension and capacity-enhancement projects. Most relevant to owners of existing plants with operational equipment seeking to add capacity or transition to new generation technology; no comment deadline or vote is noted in this status update.

documents.title: ERCOT-TWG-2026-06-18-Large-Load-Curtailment-Manager
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's Large Load Curtailment Manager implements demand-side flexibility dispatch that creates a cross-market comparison point for how RTOs can integrate dispatchable load as a reliability resource — relevant for understanding alternative approaches to the flexibility and ramping products hydro typically supplies, and for tracking load-growth dynamics that affect hydro scarcity value.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT is deploying a Large Load Curtailment Manager (LLCM) tool to identify and instruct curtailment of specified large loads under SB6 policy. The minimum viable product goes live June 25, 2026, with full scope expected in Q4 2026. LLCM will monitor real-time margin, flag when large-load curtailment is warranted, and send XML dispatch instructions to loads with net-metering arrangements; curtailed load will be accounted in SCED reliability deployment price-adder calculations. This represents demand-side deployment capacity competing with traditional generation ramping and flexibility — a structural change to how ERCOT procures the flexibility function that hydro dominates in other RTOs. NHA analysts tracking frequency response, ramping, and flexibility procurement should monitor whether ERCOT's use of large-load curtailment as a reliability tool constrains the value of conventional flexibility resources or creates new settlement risk.
