documents.title: ERCOT-TWG-2026-06-DIP Updates
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT data-product updates include price-adder reporting for real-time dispatch and ancillary-service clearing prices (NPRR1290/NPRR1323), which establish precedent for how storage and flexible resources are priced and accredited in a major non-hydro RTO — directly comparable to NHA members' revenue mechanisms elsewhere.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT is rolling out data-product enhancements across releases 6–11 (June–December 2026) to support market-design changes. Release 7 adds a new Real-Time Deployed Large Load (RTDLL) price-adder column to historical and real-time pricing reports (NP6-323-CD, NP6-792-ER) under NPRR1238. Release 8 implements uncapped versions of System Lambda and LMPs (NP6-322-CD, NP6-787-CD) and unbounded ancillary-service clearing prices (MCPC) by type under NPRR1290/NPRR1323, exposing both capped and uncapped pricing for transparency. These changes affect how real-time scarcity signals propagate to dispatchable resources and how ancillary-service revenue is calculated. For NHA members tracking ERCOT as a comparison point: ERCOT's move to dual-publish capped and uncapped prices lets market participants see the full opportunity cost of dispatch, a framework PSH operators elsewhere use to justify opportunity-cost-based offer development and to critique reference-level mitigation rules that cap their bids below true scarcity.

documents.title: PRS_June_2026_Project_Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Energy storage resource state-of-charge rules and controllable/curtailable load resource dispatch and frequency response are anchoring operational structures that will affect how pumped storage and other use-limited resources compete in ERCOT's nodal market.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT is advancing several projects that will reshape how flexible and storage resources operate in its markets. NPRR1188 and NPRR1244 will implement nodal dispatch and energy settlement for controllable load resources (CLRs) and clarify their primary frequency response responsibilities, with go-live targeted for January 2027; separately, NPRR1326 proposes adding energy storage resource state-of-charge information to the ancillary services capacity monitor (impact assessment ongoing). NPRR1329 addresses resource entity requirements for self-limiting facilities. These projects establish the technical and operational framework into which ERCOT's storage and flexible-load assets must fit; pumped storage operators in ERCOT should monitor the December 2026 completion of the impact assessments for NPRR1326 and NPRR1329 and track the January 2027 go-live of NPRR1188/1244 as they will govern how storage dispatch interacts with frequency response obligations and energy settlement.

title: Commitment Cost Bidding Fexibility
meeting_hydro_reason: Commitment cost bidding rules directly affect how dispatchable resources including hydropower and pumped-storage units recover fixed costs and participate in energy and ancillary services markets, influencing revenue adequacy and bidding strategy.

issues.canonical_name: Commitment cost bidding flexibility

documents.title: Presentation - Commitment Cost Bidding Flexibility - Working Group - Jun 18, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO is designing a local market power mitigation test for commitment costs that directly affects how hydro and other dispatchable resources can bid their start-up costs — a use-limited resource revenue stream — by implementing phased caps (300% in competitive conditions, 110% in uncompetitive) and testing commitment decisions against a three-pivotal-supplier test with reference levels.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Commitment Cost Bidding Flexibility (CCBF) working group is developing a market power mitigation framework for commitment cost offers, replacing a static cap with a competitiveness-based test that identifies uncompetitive conditions using a three-pivotal-supplier test for commitment-specific constraints. The framework will mitigate commitment costs identified as exercising market power by assessing them against the resource's cost-based reference level, with phased caps of 300% in competitive conditions and 110% in uncompetitive conditions. Dispatchable hydro and pumped-storage operators face direct revenue exposure: inflated commitment-cost bids that currently clear under the static cap could be suppressed or capped when the test identifies the resource as pivotal to binding transmission constraints; conversely, owners with low reference levels could bid more competitively. Workshop 2 (this meeting, June 18, 2026) identifies policy choices on structural competitiveness testing and mitigation criteria; next steps and comment deadlines not stated in this presentation excerpt.

title: FERC Launches Aggressive Targeted Action to Speed Large Load Integration
meeting_hydro_reason: Auto-flagged: FERC markets-policy item; doc-level screener decides per-article relevance.

documents.title: FERC Launches Aggressive Targeted Action to Speed Large Load Integration
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: FERC's show-cause orders to all six RTOs/ISOs on large-load interconnection and co-located generation directly affect hydro's strategic options for siting, co-location, behind-the-meter service, and competitive positioning against data-center load aggregators; also affects transmission planning and deliverability of hydro export capacity.
documents.ai_summary: FERC issued tailored show-cause orders (June 18, 2026) to PJM, MISO, SPP, CAISO, ISO-NE, and NYISO, giving each 60 days to justify or reform tariffs governing large-load interconnection, including data centers and co-located generation. The orders target five categories: transmission study processes, cost-shifting prevention, co-location and behind-the-meter rules, flexible transmission services for large loads, and generation-serving-load study processes. Within 30 days, each RTO/ISO must report how it will ensure adequate generation for existing and new large loads. For hydro owners, this opens strategic pathways: PSH and reservoir hydro can compete to serve large co-located loads, especially where hydro-sited generation can meet 'electrically proximate' criteria; behind-the-meter service rules may create new revenue streams from data centers or manufacturing co-location. However, the rules also intensify pressure on transmission planning—hydro's export capability may face new demand-study scrutiny, and transmission costs will be more transparent, affecting long-distance export margins. Biggest exposure: Northwest hydro seeking interregional transmission for remote load service, and existing PSH/run-of-river plants considering co-location partnerships.

title: Fact Sheet | FERC Takes Action to Supercharge America’s Grid for Efficiency, Reliability, and a Bold Energy Future
meeting_hydro_reason: Auto-flagged: FERC markets-policy item; doc-level screener decides per-article relevance.

documents.title: Fact Sheet | FERC Takes Action to Supercharge America’s Grid for Efficiency, Reliability, and a Bold Energy Future
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: FERC show-cause orders to six RTOs/ISOs on large-load integration touch hydro's strategic options: transmission access rules, co-location and behind-the-meter generation frameworks, resource adequacy provisions for new loads, and potential tariff changes affecting how hydro and storage are dispatched and compensated in footprints where load growth and data-center siting are reshaping scarcity and system design.
documents.ai_summary: FERC issues six tailored show-cause orders (one to each RTO/ISO: PJM, MISO, SPP, CAISO, ISO-NE, NYISO) requiring each to justify within 60 days why current tariffs remain just and reasonable in the absence of provisions for large load customers, or propose changes. Orders identify five reform categories: efficient transmission application/study processes, cost-transparency and prevention of cost shifting, co-location and behind-the-meter generation, flexible transmission services for large loads, and study processes for generation serving large co-located loads. In parallel, each RTO/ISO must report within 30 days on how it will ensure adequate generation to serve existing and new large loads, including resource-adequacy proposals under consideration, milestone schedules, and stakeholder processes to accelerate capacity additions. The orders account for regional differences (e.g., SPP's High Impact Large Load Assessment process, CAISO's non-Order 888 transmission model) and preserve state authority over resource siting and retail rates. Large loads and data-center siting are reshaping scarcity and transmission-constraint patterns—existing hydro's deliverability and value hinge on these tariff structures and RA adequacy designs, and pumped-storage operators will face new rules on co-location, behind-the-meter charging/discharge, and how storage capacity is accredited against large-load resource adequacy showings.

documents.ai_summary: PJM's Deactivation Enhancements Senior Task Force (DESTF) held its May 19, 2026 meeting to advance voting on scope areas 4/5 and 6, which address long-term transmission solutions and alternatives to Part V arrangements for deactivating generators. Michael Herman, Clara Summers, and Claire Lang-Ree presented solution options for accelerating transmission completion and modifying Part V terms. The group is in a consensus-based issue-resolution process with next DESTF meeting scheduled June 11, 2026. Hydro and PSH owners using Part V arrangements (particularly those with limited water/dispatch seasons or facing transmission constraints) may see revised cost-allocation or exit-timing rules that affect retirement decisions and the cost of remaining online; the June 11 meeting will advance scope-area voting.

documents.title: 20260617 AC Item 06 MISO Strategy Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's strategy update addresses load growth integration, resource adequacy mechanisms, operational risks tied to fleet evolution and large-load interconnection, and storage/hybrid resource participation — all of which touch hydro revenue (seasonal accreditation, RA pricing), strategic options (large-load co-location, transmission planning), and obligations (operational risk categories, interconnection queue management).
documents.ai_summary: MISO reports record 14.7 GW nameplate capacity additions in 2026, with solar and storage dominating the resource mix, while facing accelerating large-load additions (24.9 GW proposed, 8.9 GW approved in Expedited Project Reviews) and evolving operational risks — particularly net-load variability, fewer synchronous resources, and load-pocket adequacy. The Reliability Imperative initiative includes market redesign targeting accreditation/peak-risk alignment, optimized storage participation, dynamic reserves, and congestion-pricing reform, alongside transmission evolution and large-load integration speed-to-power. For hydro owners, the strategic significance lies in (1) seasonal accreditation and capacity-market design — MISO notes summer/winter capacity accreditation at 9.4%–21.2% planning reserve margins, directly affecting what flexibility and seasonal-storage products hydro can sell into RA frameworks; (2) load-pocket adequacy analysis and transmission planning to support large loads, which opens opportunities for run-of-river and reservoir hydro to serve co-located or regionally constrained load; and (3) storage-participation optimization, which will likely reshape how pumped storage competes with batteries in accreditation and ramping products. No specific vote or comment deadline is stated; this is a strategic briefing to the Advisory Committee with implementation timelines for ERAS and market reforms to follow.

documents.title: 20260617 AC Item 01d Legal Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Document previews near-term tariff filings on co-located resource interconnection limits, capacity accreditation deliverability (DLOL methodology for SAC calculation), large-load reliability requirements, and zone-based generation interconnection (ZGIA) — four filings that will directly affect how hydro (especially pumped storage) competes for capacity accreditation, how co-location affects revenue, and how new load interconnection rules alter dispatch and deliverability constraints.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Legal Update lists 13 tariff filings anticipated through late July 2026, including four with material relevance to hydro and storage operators: (1) delay of the Interconnection Service Limit Constraint for Co-Located Resources effective date (06/12/2026, docket ER26-1915-000); (2) tariff revisions to establish DLOL-based accreditation methodology for capacity resources beginning Planning Year 2028–2029, with new provisions for how deliverability is accounted for in SAC values for both traditional and co-located resources (06/19/2026); (3) Large Load reliability and informational requirements (06/30/2026); and (4) a non-queue Zone-based Generation Interconnection Approach (ZGIA) for generation co-located at the same substation as load (06/30/2026). The accreditation deliverability filing (item 3) is the most directly material to PSH and reservoir hydro, as it resets how MISO calculates seasonal accreditation value when transmission-constraint-based deliverability limits are incorporated into the accreditation formula — a shift that could reduce accreditation for resources in tight delivery zones. Co-location rules (items 1 and 4) affect PSH and small hydro co-located with load or industrial facilities. All filings are estimated dates unless tied to a FERC deadline; no comment deadlines are stated in the outlook.

documents.title: 20260617 AC Item 08 Main Parent Entity Status Updates
documents.filename: 20260617 AC Item 08 Main Parent Entity Status Updates.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Multiple MISO subcommittees report on accreditation rules, resource adequacy, reserve deliverability, interconnection procedures for co-located load and generation, and transmission planning that affect hydro and pumped-storage participation, capacity value, and strategic deployment options.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Resource Adequacy Subcommittee is addressing two accreditation issues that affect all dispatchable and storage resources: (1) Solar accreditation hour-window adjustment for Planning Year 2027/2028, realigning risk hours with observed real-time performance as a glide path to Direct Loss of Load (DLOL) methodology in 2028/2029—a precedent for how MISO recalibrates accreditation as resource mix shifts; (2) Lead-time rules for offline resources, where MISO is tightening the 24-hour lead-time accreditation threshold because some resources have changed lead times approaching or during emergencies, potentially masking unavailability. MISO intends a FERC filing in Q3 or Q4 2026 on the lead-time issue. The Market Subcommittee is also reviewing reserve deliverability in day-ahead and real-time markets (MSC-2026-2), noting that changing resource mix has affected regulation performance and created clearance disparities when the reserve-delivery test (RDT) binds—feedback was due June 5. On the interconnection front, the Planning Advisory Committee is advancing a Zero-Injection Generation Interconnection Agreement (ZGIA) proposal for co-located load and generation, with modifications to require substation-level connection and automatic disconnection of injections when load trips; FERC filing is planned before end of June. These mechanisms directly affect accreditation certainty for hydro and pumped-storage capacity showings, regulation/AS revenue exposure if reserve-delivery rules tighten, and strategic options for co-locating PSH or run-of-river hydro with large loads or behind-the-meter applications.

documents.title: 20260617 AC Item 05a Current Issue Discussion - Preventing Fraud and Manipulation Questions
documents.filename: 20260617 AC Item 05a Current Issue Discussion - Preventing Fraud and Manipulation_Questions.pdf

documents.ai_summary: MISO's Advisory Committee is discussing fraud and manipulation risks in demand-side resource (DSR) registrations and accreditation, prompted by FERC enforcement actions (Ketchup Caddy LLC, $25M penalty, December 2024; American Efficient LLC, $722M penalty, April 2026). The committee is exploring upcoming MISO accreditation changes and how fraud vulnerabilities in DSR programs intersect with the recent proliferation of large-load interconnection requests. For hydro owners, the relevance is indirect but material: if MISO tightens DSR accreditation rules or validation standards, the capacity value and ancillary-service revenue opportunities for demand-side flexibility may shrink, reducing competition for RA accreditation and improving pricing signals for dispatchable hydro. Conversely, if fraudulent DSRs have inflated RA accreditation or depressed prices, correcting those practices could revalue hydro's true contribution. No specific accreditation proposals or vote dates are stated in this discussion document.

documents.ai_summary: MISO Advisory Committee and Board session reviewing resource adequacy maintenance strategy in light of accelerated load-growth projections since June 2023. MISO has implemented seasonal capacity auctions, reliability-based demand curves, resource accreditation changes, dynamic loss-of-load methodology, and expedited interconnection processes (ERAS). The session will assess whether these changes are sufficient and what additional measures are needed. Directly relevant because accreditation methodology, demand-curve design, and seasonal auction structure set the capacity value hydro and pumped storage receive in MISO's planning reserve auctions; the shift to seasonal accreditation and dynamic loss-of-load modeling will alter how hydropower's seasonal contribution is recognized, particularly in high-stress periods where hydro's flexibility has highest value.

documents.title: PDCWG Meeting Agenda 2026-06-17
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT frequency control and ancillary-services methodology development is a cross-market comparison point for hydro's role in fast-frequency response, regulation, and reserves — the same products hydro supplies in WECC/Eastern RTOs, and methodological changes in ERCOT often precede or inform similar proposals elsewhere.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's PDCWG meeting agenda includes discussion of 2027 ancillary-services methodology (presenter Hinojosa), frequency-control and regulation reporting (Lowe), and analysis of disturbances. While the agenda itself contains no substantive proposals, the 2027 AS methodology review is directly relevant to how ERCOT will price and accredit frequency response and regulation products going forward — the same ancillary services hydro supplies in other markets. Analysts tracking cross-RTO frequency-response design should monitor the outcomes when detailed proposals are published.

documents.title: PDCWG Frequency Control Report for May 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT frequency control and inertia metrics establish a cross-market comparison point for hydro's role in frequency response and synchronous generation — showing how a low-hydro system manages frequency stability and minimum inertia thresholds that hydro-rich RTOs can benchmark against.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's May 2026 frequency control report documents CPS1 performance of 184.80% (12-month rolling average 181.08%), zero BAAL exceedances, and minimum system inertia of 138.8 GW·s (down from 143 GW·s in 2016 despite 81% non-synchronous generation penetration). The report reveals that ERCOT has reduced its critical inertia threshold from 100 GW·s to 87 GW·s between 2016 and 2026, while deploying Fast Frequency Response (FFR) awards totaling 430 MW by March 2026 to maintain frequency stability as synchronous generation declines. For hydro-rich RTOs, this demonstrates that systems with negligible conventional hydropower must artificially lower inertia requirements and increasingly rely on synthetic-inertia products (FFR), whereas hydro operators in NERC regions with reservoir storage maintain naturally higher inertia margins — a competitive advantage that regulatory design should preserve. The threshold reductions and FFR ramp-up illustrate the cost of losing synchronous capacity; hydro's inherent inertia contribution to regional frequency stability remains a quantifiable asset.

documents.title: Regulation Analysis PDCWG May-26
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's regulation service analysis—deployed volume, bias patterns, and 5-min interval performance—is a cross-market comparison point for how ERCOT's ancillary-services market design differs from hydro-rich RTOs and sets precedent for frequency-response procurement and pricing elsewhere.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT Operations Planning presented a regulation bias and performance analysis covering May 2026 data: regulation deployment trends (yearly and monthly), wind and solar ramp-rate forecast errors, regulation bias across consecutive 5-min SCED intervals, time error accumulation, and load/net-load/ramp profiles. The analysis examines which factors (load variability, wind/solar ramps, startup/shutdown cycles, short-term load forecast error, non-SCED qualified large load ramping) drive time-error accumulation and regulation need. No policy proposals or decisions are stated; this is a diagnostic report to the Performance, Disturbance, Compliance Working Group. Significance for hydro asset owners: ERCOT's regulation procurement model and ramp-rate-driven AS demand are structural comparisons—ERCOT's real-time balancing relies heavily on deployed regulation volume to absorb wind/solar variability, whereas hydro-rich RTOs (WECC, NERC) often use different flexibility mechanisms (flexible ramping, energy imbalance markets). The data provides a market-monitor-like baseline for cross-RTO analysis of ancillary-services value and design; no imminent rule change or NHA member action is signaled.

documents.title: Non-FMEs for May-26_PUBLIC
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT frequency event analysis informs cross-market comparison of how RTOs handle frequency response and synchronous-resource ride-through — a key ancillary service and operational-compliance obligation that shapes hydro and storage dispatch across all markets.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT Operations Planning presented analysis of two frequency events in May 2026: one FME (Frequency Measurement Event) on 5/20 at 23:17:35 UTC and one Non-FME on 5/27 at 12:29:39 UTC, reviewed at the PDCWG on June 17, 2026. The document does not disclose the magnitude, duration, cause, or mitigation outcome of either event in the excerpt provided. As a cross-market reference point, this analysis contributes to understanding how ERCOT — a synchronous-machine-rich, hydro-limited RTO — detects and responds to frequency disturbances compared to hydro-dependent RTOs (WECC, NYISO, PJM); such comparisons inform NHA members' assessment of whether frequency-response procurement and ride-through standards are calibrated consistently across interconnections.

documents.title: 2027_AS_Methodology_PDCWG_061726
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's 2027 ancillary-services methodology changes, particularly to Regulation Service procurement driven by renewable ramping and large-load behavior, establish a cross-market comparison point for how non-hydro-rich RTOs structure flexible-ramping products and frequency-response requirements — relevant to NHA's understanding of AS design alternatives and hydro's competitive position in frequency-response markets.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT is finalizing 2027 ancillary-services (AS) methodology, with one substantive change to Regulation Service computation: incorporation of a potential Large Load adjustment factor to account for extreme 5-minute ramps (up to 400 MW in instances) from data centers and steel mills, which exceed the current methodology's reliance on net-load forecast error and wind/solar ramping adjustment tables. The change reflects ERCOT's focus on dynamic solar-ramp thresholds (now 280 MW/min for sunrise/sunset, up from 80 MW/min when implemented in June 2023) and intra-hour renewable-variability capture via GTBD. Responsive Reserve Service (RRS) methodology remains unchanged pending NERC's 2027 BAL-003 Interconnection Frequency Response Obligation assessment; current analysis uses the 2026 minimum RRS-PFR floor of 1,377 MW. Board vote scheduled September 15, 2026; PUC submission November–December 2026. For hydro asset owners, this demonstrates how RTOs outside the West are handling frequency-response procurement in high-renewable, low-inertia conditions — ERCOT's emphasis on large-load ramping as a regulation driver differs from traditional net-load variability models and may inform future WECC or NYISO redesigns.

documents.ai_summary: PJM's Market Implementation Committee will review 2026 updates to Gross CONE (Cost of New Entry) and Gross ACR (Avoidable Cost Rate) values on June 16, 2026, with Brattle Group presenting methodology changes from the prior study. Gross CONE and ACR underpin PJM's capacity auction demand curve and set the reference prices against which all resource bids are evaluated; changes to these values directly affect the price floor/ceiling in capacity auctions and can shift accreditation outcomes for hydro and pumped-storage resources. The agenda confirms a working session (9:05 a.m.–12:00 p.m. EPT); no comment deadline or vote date is stated in this agenda document, but the 2026 study review suggests parameters will be finalized for implementation in the next capacity auction cycle.

documents.title: Agenda Doc
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: PJM Gross CONE and Gross ACR updates directly affect capacity-market accreditation and auction parameters for all capacity resources, including hydro and pumped-storage hydro participating in PJM's RPM (capacity market).
documents.ai_summary: PJM Special MIC is reviewing updates to Gross CONE (Cost of New Entry) and Gross ACR (Accredited Capacity Rating) — the two foundational parameters that drive RPM capacity-auction demand curves and reserve-margin calculations. Gross CONE is the levelized cost benchmark for a new reference resource; Gross ACR is the proxy capacity value of existing resources. Changes to either reset the economic entry/exit threshold for new capacity and redraw existing-resource accreditation levels — directly affecting reserve-margin adequacy determinations and capacity revenue for all dispatchable and storage units. The meeting agenda indicates updates are under discussion, but the extracted text does not include substantive proposals, comment deadlines, or next steps; the full meeting materials (slides, proposals) would be needed to determine specific impacts on hydro and PSH accreditation or revenue exposure.

documents.title: Item 01 1 – Gross CONE And Gross Acr Updates – Preliminary Update Of Default CONE Values
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CONE and ACR are the foundational inputs to PJM's Minimum Offer Price Rule (MOPR) and Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC), which directly set offer-floor rules for all capacity resources including hydropower and pumped storage; this periodic review updates default values for the 2030/2031 delivery year that affect how hydro operators must bid and what the floor to market participation is.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is updating its default Gross Cost of New Entry (CONE) and Gross Avoidable Cost Rate (ACR) values, effective for the 2030/2031 capacity auction, informed by consultant analysis of capital and fixed O&M costs from EIA and prior Quadrennial Review data. These default values set the floor for existing hydro and storage offers under the Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC) and for new-entrant MOPR bids; PJM will file final updated values with FERC by October 1, 2026, following an Advisory Vote at the MRC/MC in September 2026. The update applies the same methodology as prior cycles but includes preliminary CONE values for all resource types (e.g., Battery Energy Storage $502–$799/MW-day for 2026/27 and 2030/31); existing hydro assets will be affected to the extent their marginal avoidable costs are benchmarked against these updated ACR defaults when calculating unit-specific or default-option MSOC bids.

documents.title: Item 01 2 – Gross CONE And Gross Acr Updates – Avoidable Cost Study Methodology
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM is updating Gross Avoidable Cost Rates (ACRs) used in the Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC), which directly affects how hydro resources' market-power mitigation default offers are calculated and whether unit-specific cost reviews are triggered — a core revenue-protection mechanism for dispatchable resources.
documents.ai_summary: PJM and Brattle/Sargent & Lundy are updating the Gross ACR study (last done in 2022) to take effect in delivery years beginning 2030/31. The 2022 study covered ~94% of the PJM fleet and established resource-type median costs (e.g., coal at $94/MW-day, natural gas CC at $113/MW-day, onshore wind at $147/MW-day) that feed into default Net ACRs, which are then applied as the MSOC threshold for supplier-side market power mitigation in the capacity market. The 2026 update will review whether current fleet characteristics have shifted the median cost profiles, solicit stakeholder feedback on whether to add a 4-hour BESS as a reference type, and present draft ranges and fleet analysis at the July 8, 2026 MIC meeting. Hydro resources are not explicitly listed as a reference type in either study; the mechanism is indirect — if the MSOC thresholds shift downward relative to hydro's actual avoidable costs, hydro owners will face more frequent unit-specific cost reviews to justify offers above the default, raising compliance burden and regulatory uncertainty around scarcity-related revenues.

documents.title: 20260616 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 05 Five Year Plan.pdf
documents.filename: 20260616 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 05 Five Year Plan.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's five-year plan addresses storage participation optimization, accreditation reform, ramping and flexibility products, demand response accreditation, large-load interconnection effects on grid stability, and resource adequacy—all direct levers on hydro and PSH revenue and operational strategy in MISO.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's 2026–2029+ five-year strategic plan prioritizes reliability imperatives driven by solar growth, load growth (especially large commercial/industrial loads), and net-load-shape steepening. Key market-design initiatives affecting storage and dispatchable resources include: (1) storage optimization in markets and multi-interval storage optimization (2027–2029), improving accreditation alignment with peak-risk periods and refining accreditation to include external resources (2026–2029+), and dynamic reserves; (2) ramping-product pricing adjustments, transmission constraint demand curves, linear ramping for fast-ramp resources, and enhanced regional ramp deliverability to mitigate 4–6% ramp shortages during morning/evening peaks; (3) demand-response reforms (2026–2028) with accreditation and participation standardization; and (4) FERC Order 2222 foundational work for DER aggregation (2026–2029+). Operationally, MISO plans centralized control-room modernization (2026–2027) and Ambient Adjusted Ratings (AAR) implementation under FERC Order 881 (2027–2029+). Resource-adequacy ecosystem modernization includes LOLE modeling refinements, large-load adequacy impact assessments, and co-optimized seasonal capacity auctions. Pumped-storage and reservoir hydro owners—especially those holding RA positions or supplying flexibility into ramp markets—should monitor accreditation-reform specifics (Q2–Q3 2026) and storage-optimization market-design details (2027 design phase) for impacts on capacity value and dispatch incentives. Large-load interconnection studies (2026–2027) and ambient-adjusted-ratings deployment affect system stability margins and may require hydro dispatch changes or create scarcity-driven revenue opportunities.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's operations report flags net-load ramp challenges from 20+ GW of solar growth, summer resource adequacy outcomes, and emergency procedure changes that affect hydro dispatch obligations and revenue opportunities from ramping/regulation services.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's spring 2026 operations report shows mild conditions but identifies summer challenges: over 20 GW of solar creating net-load ramp risk, above-average temperature forecasts, and drought conditions in parts of the footprint. The region cleared sufficient capacity (142 GW procured against 132 GW reserve requirement) in the 2026/27 Planning Resource Auction, but real-time metrics show congestion forecasting gaps and RSG (uplift) volatility—in March, $1.7M RSG was paid across three trade dates with 66% concentrated in a single high-priced resource in the South. MISO rolled out revised emergency procedures (June 1, 2026) with simplified capacity-emergency steps and a new Transmission Warning alert to provide earlier non-emergency signaling before load shed becomes necessary. For hydro operators, the ramp-challenge callout signals growing dispatch value from flexible resources; the highlighted congestion forecasting misalignments and congestion-cost shortfalls (ECF ~$10.6M shortfall in March) suggest potential revenue volatility in real-time energy and congestion markets, particularly for resources in the South load pocket.

documents.title: 20260616 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 07 IMM Quarterly Report.pdf
documents.filename: 20260616 Markets Committee of the BOD Item 07 IMM Quarterly Report.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO Independent Market Monitor quarterly report identifies energy storage resource (ESR/battery) operational flaws and price-formation issues affecting dispatch efficiency, and assesses capacity adequacy and resource mix shift toward renewables, all of which establish competitive context and market-design reference points for PSH and conventional hydro revenue and strategic positioning in MISO.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Independent Market Monitor reports Spring 2026 performance: real-time energy prices fell 7% despite higher load, but price volatility surged with 20 shortage intervals (vs. 11 last year), driven by evening net-load ramps averaging 15 GW as solar penetration increases and system experiences 187 negative-price intervals in daytime hours. The Monitor identifies a critical ESR state-of-charge (SOC) software flaw that removed 7% of ESR capability from dispatch when outside SOC limits, scheduling discharge during negative prices and charge during $200+/MWh scarcity — a July software fix is underway. Existing solar is over-accredited at 68% summer output (designated 2–5 pm hours) versus actual 4–7 pm peak-stress window, inflating solar's capacity value by ~2.6 GW and depressing summer PRA clearing prices by ~$240/MW-day; the Monitor recommends aligning accreditation to 4–7 pm hours for next year's auction. These findings directly affect hydro's relative market position: expanded solar and ESR penetration intensify evening ramp demand (where conventional hydro excels), yet dispatch rules and accreditation design are not yet optimized for the new resource mix. Pumped storage, if subject to similar SOC constraints, faces the same inefficient dispatch penalty; conventional hydro benefits from scarcity but faces sustained low daytime prices from solar excess.

documents.title: 20260616 System Planning Committee of the BOD Item 05 Reliability Imperative_Transmission Evolution.pdf
documents.filename: 20260616 System Planning Committee of the BOD Item 05 Reliability Imperative_Transmission Evolution.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's transmission and load-growth planning directly affects hydro deliverability, interregional transfer capability, and strategic positioning in evolving seams-governance arrangements (PJM-MISO TMEP, SPP-MISO CSP); long-range studies and interconnection queues determine which resources benefit from coordinated upgrades and transmission-as-option value.
documents.ai_summary: MISO is executing a three-layered planning framework (local, regional, interregional) to accommodate 26.4 GW of large-load additions projected in MTEP26, with load-growth forecasts ranging 1.1–3.0% CAGR through 2046 (data-center CAGR at 103% through 2030). The South and Midwest Long Range Transmission Planning studies are launching this quarter using updated Futures scenarios to guide solutions for reliability and load-serving needs; outcomes will determine transmission and/or generation solutions in subsequent MTEP cycles. Parallel interregional coordination is underway with PJM (2026 Targeted Market Efficiency Project studying PJM-MISO congestion), SPP (2024–25 CSP yielded two portfolio options improving reliability and transfer capability; 2026–27 CSP announced), and broader alignment with FERC Order 1920. This acceleration of transmission and interregional planning directly raises the strategic value of existing hydro capacity and expands opportunities for hydro to serve as a flexibility and transfer solution, particularly for units near MISO seams or capable of serving co-located load; the 28 GW of joint capacity enabled by JTIQ transmission projects (19 GW approved in MISO-SPP) signals growing reliance on coordinated transmission to unlock interconnection participation.

documents.title: 20260616 System Planning Committee of the BOD Item 04 Five-Year Plan.pdf
documents.filename: 20260616 System Planning Committee of the BOD Item 04 Five-Year Plan.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's five-year plan directly addresses hydro and storage revenue, obligations, and strategic options through storage resource participation optimization, resource accreditation reforms, large-load interconnection enabling, dynamic reserves design, and capacity-adequacy modeling that will reshape how existing hydro and pumped-storage plants are dispatched and valued.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's System Planning Committee approved a five-year strategic plan (2025–2029+) with major workstreams in market redefinition, transmission evolution, and operations modernization. In Market Redefinition, MISO will redesign storage participation models (including co-located storage optimization), reform resource accreditation to improve peak-risk-period alignment and reflect evolving resource risks, develop dynamic ramp and regulation reserves, and modernize resource adequacy modeling and loss-of-load expectation (LOLE) tooling to account for climate-change impacts and seasonal interdependencies. In Transmission Evolution, MISO will accelerate large-load interconnections (with "speed-to-partial-power" processes), implement FERC Order 2023 generation interconnection reforms, and conduct interregional transmission planning with PJM and SPP. These changes mean storage operators (especially pumped-storage owners) will face new accreditation rules that tie capacity credit to peak-risk periods rather than nameplate, new co-location optimization rules that can improve or constrain dispatch economics, and expanded opportunities to serve fast-growing data-center and large-load demand. Reservoir and run-of-river operators will be affected by changes to dynamic reserves procurement, ramp-product design, and seasonal resource adequacy modeling. No specific vote or comment deadline is stated in this overview document; the plan is framed as approved board guidance for 24–36 month execution cycles beginning 2025.

documents.title: 20260616 System Planning Committee of the BOD Item 07 Resource Adequacy and GI Queue Update.pdf
documents.filename: 20260616 System Planning Committee of the BOD Item 07 Resource Adequacy and GI Queue Update.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's interconnection queue update and resource-adequacy outlook directly affect hydro and storage owners' interconnection timelines, accreditation pathways (particularly storage in ERAS cycles), and the region's capacity-margin trajectory—which shapes scarcity pricing and PSH dispatch value.
documents.ai_summary: MISO reports record 2026 capacity additions (14.7 GW nameplate, 10.7 GW accredited) offsetting 2.6 GW of retirements, with 86 GW of approved projects still awaiting commercial operation and 43 GW experiencing delays. Storage projects represent 18–40% of active queue and ERAS applications across cycles 1–4 (as of 6/1/26), advancing through the Expedited Resource Addition Study process designed to accelerate generation additions amid load surge. However, 30 GW (218 projects) have exceeded the standard GIA grace period, with regulatory, contractor, and PPA issues cited as primary delay drivers; projects are securing federal waivers extending timelines 2–7 years beyond COD. For hydropower and PSH owners, the bottleneck between approved capacity and online generation prolongs scarcity conditions that support energy prices and ramping-value realization, while storage's prominent role in ERAS (12–17% of cycles studied) demonstrates MISO's reliance on storage flexibility—though the report does not quantify storage-specific accreditation treatment or ancillary-services pathways. Next steps include MISO exploring modifications to ERAS study cycles (expected Q2 2026) to maximize utilization and GIA negotiations expected over the next two years (920 projected total across Q2 2026–Q2 2027).

Key agenda items include preliminary MTEP26 review (September 15), substantive comments review (November 12), and board recommendation (November 12). For hydro and PSH owners in MISO, these meetings will address transmission capacity available for resource delivery, interconnection wait times and queue status, and seasonal/long-term resource adequacy forecasting that determines the scarcity signals affecting energy and reserve pricing. documents.ai_summary: MISO is deploying SUGAR (Suite of Unified Grid Analyses with Renewables) to automate interconnection study workflows and compress Phase 1 processing from 93 business days (2022 cycle) to 47 days (2023 cycle) to 54 days (2025 cycle), with plans to extend automation to Phases 2 and 3. The system uses agentic AI to automate power flow analysis and network upgrade costing, achieving >99% accuracy match with legacy tools. Faster queue processing reduces time-to-decision for both generator and storage interconnection requests—a material advantage for PSH and conventional hydro seeking expedited network studies and cost determination. MISO's next stage expands automation to stability and short-circuit analysis (Phases 2–3), which historically consumed months; no comment deadline is stated, but this is a board-approved initiative moving into deployment (Q2 2026 status shown).

documents.ai_summary: This document establishes or revises the governance charter for the Western Energy Imbalance Market (WEIM) and lays groundwork for the Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) operational structure. The charter defines stakeholder representation, decision-making authority, and institutional design for a multi-state market footprint that will govern energy pricing, resource accreditation, and participation rules for all generators including hydropower and pumped storage across California and adjacent regions. Hydro owners in WEIM-participating states (Oregon, Washington, Nevada, Utah, Arizona, New Mexico, Idaho) and those considering EDAM entry need to track this charter's provisions on voting power, cost allocation, rate design, and dispute resolution — particularly how use-limited resources like hydro and PSH will be treated in capacity and ancillary-services frameworks under the expanded governance structure. documents.title: Final Market Surveillance Committee Opinion on Gas Resource Management - Dec 15, 2025
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO's Gas Resource Management proposal affects how gas-fired resources' commitment costs and default energy bids are calculated — reference levels that are the canonical mitigation tool for use-limited resources including hydropower, and directly impact EDAM participation by Western resources including hydro operators in the WEIM/EDAM footprint.
documents.ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee issued a final opinion (Dec. 12, 2025) supporting three of four enhancements in CAISO's Gas Resource Management proposal: improved reference-level change-request processes, expanded after-the-fact cost-recovery rules, and multi-day advisory dispatch forecasts to help gas resources anticipate EDAM schedules and procure fuel more efficiently ahead of morning gas nominations. The MSC supports these changes to address EDAM coordination challenges as EDAM expands westward. However, the MSC emphasizes a critical gap: the absence of any market-power test for commitment-cost mitigation means all gas units are indiscriminately mitigated in every dispatch interval, creating inefficient offer bounds that may favor units with actual market power while constraining flexible suppliers. The MSC calls development of a workable commitment-cost market-power test "long overdue and should be a high priority." This is material to hydro owners because reference-level design directly affects whether hydro (a canonical use-limited resource) can offer flexibility without artificial caps, and the expansion of EDAM brings Western hydro into closer coordination with gas-constrained EDAM pricing and scheduling — misaligned gas-cost accounting upstream could distort the scarcity signals and ancillary-service values hydro depends on.

documents.title: Final Market Surveillance Committee Opinion - Extended Day-Ahead Market Congestion Revenue Allocation - Jun 16, 2025
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: EDAM congestion revenue allocation rules directly affect how hydro operators recover transmission-congestion hedges and how parallel-flow costs from hydro schedules are reallocated across balancing authorities — a core revenue-protection mechanism for dispatchable and use-limited resources in the Western interregional market.
documents.ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee issued a final opinion (June 16, 2025) on EDAM's proposed congestion revenue allocation scheme for parallel flows, evaluating a Year 1 interim design that would reallocate congestion revenues from BAAs where congestion occurs to BAAs that sold firm point-to-point and NITS transmission rights when those holders self-schedule. The MSC has significant reservations about the Year 1 design because it creates perverse incentives to self-schedule instead of participating in economically efficient co-optimized dispatch, potentially distorting bidding and causing unintended cost shifts — a concern particularly acute for hydro and other dispatchable resources whose flexibility value depends on competitive economic dispatch. The opinion does not block the proposal but calls for detailed pre-EDAM-launch empirical analysis of dispatch distortion and market-price effects from self-scheduling incentives, and notes that Year 2 alternatives linked to flexibly-bid resources would worsen below-cost-offer incentives; the framework recommended for future exploration is financial flowgate rights rather than transaction-linked reallocation.

documents.title: Market Surveillance Committee Final Opinion - Storage Bid Cost Recovery - Nov 01, 2024
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO proposes changes to real-time bid cost recovery (BCR) calculations for battery storage, directly affecting how storage resources recover losses and manage state-of-charge — a canonical storage-revenue mechanism that sets precedent for pumped-storage participation and cost allocation in real-time markets.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Market Surveillance Committee (MSC) evaluates CAISO's revised proposal to modify real-time bid cost recovery for storage resources, targeting elimination of 'phantom losses' — BCR payments on losses that don't reflect actual economic harm — and improving incentives for storage operators to manage state-of-charge efficiently across the day. The MSC conditionally supports implementation of CAISO's draft final proposal as a near-term check on inflated BCR but notes that empirical data on phantom-loss materiality is incomplete; the MSC recommends immediate follow-up in Phase II to achieve deeper reforms and eliminate BCR on phantom losses before expanding storage bidding flexibility. Pumped-storage owners relying on cost-recovery mechanisms for charging losses and state-of-charge management should monitor whether CAISO's approach—which changes how discharge offers and charging bids factor into BCR calculations—narrows or preserves their ability to recover legitimate operating losses, and should engage if Phase II designs begin to collapse BCR scope more broadly.

documents.title: MSC Final Opinion - Rules for Bidding Above the Soft Offer Cap - Price Formation Enhancements - May 2024
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO proposes revisions to Order 831 soft-offer-cap rules for battery storage and hydropower, directly affecting how energy-limited resources price their opportunity costs and manage state of charge — a core revenue mechanism for hydro and especially pumped storage.
documents.ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee (MSC) issued a final opinion (May 15, 2024) supporting CAISO's proposed changes to Order 831 bidding rules for summer 2024, specifically allowing battery storage and hydropower to bid above the $1000/MWh soft cap by reflecting future-hour opportunity costs rather than only same-hour alternatives. The MSC found that current rules force premature discharge and systematic underestimation of stored energy value, creating inefficiencies that risk curtailments under tight supply conditions; the root problem is that Order 831's Maximum Import Bid Price (MIBP) mechanism was designed for thermal resources and mischaracterizes the temporal opportunity cost of energy-limited resources. The changes apply to both CAISO and Western EIM resources; the MSC also flagged that deeper reforms to local market power mitigation as applied to energy-limited resources and the real-time market's 75-minute offer-update lag remain unresolved and require future work, but the summer 2024 proposal is a necessary interim step.

documents.title: Market Surveillance Committee Addendum to Final Opinion on Day-Ahead Market Enhancements
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO's Imbalance Reserves cost-allocation mechanism affects how hydro and storage operators are charged for uncertainty-procurement costs and how those costs flow through nodal pricing and congestion settlement — touching energy revenue and price-formation outcomes.
documents.ai_summary: The Market Surveillance Committee clarifies its May 2023 opinion on CAISO's Day-Ahead Market Enhancements (DAME), specifically correcting its description of how Imbalance Reserves (IR) costs will be allocated across Balancing Area Authorities. IR costs are now understood to be calculated per BAA based on nodal prices and cleared quantities of uncertainty, less payments for IR supplied at locations within the BAA — with net charges/payments aggregated across the EDAM and any resulting congestion rents allocated to transfer and transmission constraints in the same manner as energy-flow congestion. This nodal cost-allocation mirrors energy settlements, making the mechanism transparent and competitively neutral. For hydro and PSH operators, this clarification is material because it determines how uncertainty-procurement costs flow through the nodal price signal they face and how their own IR supplies (especially from PSH) are valued and credited at their location — affecting both the cost side of operations and the compensation for flexibility supply.

documents.title: Market Surveillance Committee Final Opinion on Day-Ahead Market Enhancements - Attachment 2 to Memo - May 3, 2023
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO's Day-Ahead Market Enhancements (DAME) proposal redesigns real-time procurement and co-optimization of imbalance reserves, uncertainty products, and storage state-of-charge modeling — directly affecting hydro and pumped-storage revenue from ramping/flexibility products, energy co-optimization pricing, and storage dispatch constraints.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Market Surveillance Committee issues a final opinion supporting the DAME proposal (as of May 3, 2023), which introduces a co-optimized imbalance reserve (IR) product, updates the residual unit commitment (RUC) into a reliability capacity (RC) process, and improves storage state-of-charge modeling in day-ahead and hour-ahead optimization. The proposal aims to replace ad-hoc operator load-biasing with transparent market signals and will affect how hydro and pumped storage compete in energy and flexibility procurement, especially through new nodal IR pricing and storage-provision constraints. The MSC emphasizes substantial testing before implementation and calls for a rapid-cycle monitoring and tuning process (not traditional multi-year stakeholder proceedings) to adapt IR quantity calculations, penalty values, and storage state-of-charge parameters — all of which will materially affect PSH flexibility revenue and constraints on charging energy treatment. Implementation timing and parameter lock-in deadlines are not specified in this excerpt, but the opinion signals that CAISO will move faster than historical pace, exposing PSH and long-duration hydro to mid-course design changes.

documents.title: Market Surveillance Committee Update - May 2023
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO's Day-Ahead Market Enhancements (DAME) proposal directly affects energy price formation, imbalance-reserve procurement (a ramping/flexibility product), storage state-of-charge modeling, and resource-adequacy interactions — all core revenue and operational mechanisms for hydro and pumped storage in CAISO's footprint and the emerging west-wide EDAM.
documents.ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee (MSC) issued a May 4, 2023 opinion supporting the Day-Ahead Market Enhancements (DAME) initiative, which proposes new co-optimized imbalance-reserve products, updates to residual unit commitment (RUC), and improved storage state-of-charge modeling to replace ad-hoc operator load-biasing and improve market transparency. The MSC endorses the proposal as a necessary step toward a west-wide day-ahead market (EDAM) but flags substantial uncertainties: nodal imbalance-reserve calculation methods, storage state-of-charge parameter-setting, and 30-minute ramp-constraint limits on reserve supply are untested design elements with no precedent in other RTOs. The MSC recommends conservative implementation with guardrails, pre-implementation software testing, and a rapid feedback cycle with stakeholders to adapt the design; the imbalance-reserve demand curve — which caps up-reserve prices at $55/MWh and sets penalty values influencing energy prices — will be critical to monitor. Hydro and pumped-storage operators should track parameter tuning during testing and go-live, as the storage state-of-charge equations and imbalance-reserve locational requirements will reshape the value of flexibility and multi-hour discharge capability; the MSC notes this framework has no ISO precedent and will evolve based on operational experience.

documents.title: Market Surveillance Committee Final Opinion on Day-Ahead Market Enhancements
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO's Day-Ahead Market Enhancements (DAME) proposal fundamentally restructures co-optimization of energy and flexibility products, creating new imbalance-reserve procurement rules and state-of-charge constraints on storage that directly affect how pumped-storage hydro earns revenue from flexibility supply and interact with residual unit commitment pricing.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Market Surveillance Committee (MSC) issued a final opinion on May 3, 2023, supporting the Day-Ahead Market Enhancements proposal, which introduces a new Imbalance Reserves (IR) co-optimized product to replace ad-hoc operator load-biasing, updates residual unit commitment into a Reliability Capacity (RC) process, and improves storage state-of-charge modeling in day-ahead and RUC runs. The proposal moves forward with stated intent to consult stakeholders during testing and implementation, but the MSC identifies substantial untested elements: nodal IR demand-curve calculation, locational uncertainty estimation, IR deliverability constraints, and storage state-of-charge equation parameters all lack operational precedent outside CAISO and may require rapid iterative adjustment. Pumped-storage operators face direct exposure through IR constraints limiting 30-minute rampable capacity supply, storage participation rules in IR procurement, and potential changes to RUC/RC pricing interaction; the design's success depends on whether operators' flexibility needs are genuinely met by IR pricing or whether hidden costs migrate to uplift.

documents.title: MSC Final Opinion on Extended Day-Ahead Market
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: EDAM's day-ahead market coordination across western balancing areas directly affects hydro's revenue from inter-BAA energy and reserve arbitrage, co-optimization of dispatch across large footprints, and participation incentives for flexible resources like pumped storage; the MSC opinion on RSE, GHG accounting, and transmission design shapes whether hydro's scarcity-pricing and flexibility value is preserved or diluted in the expanded market.
documents.ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee issues a final opinion (January 27, 2023) endorsing the Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) as a beneficial expansion of RTO-style coordination into a day-ahead voluntary market across western BAAs, while flagging unresolved design ambiguities in resource-sufficiency evaluation (RSE), greenhouse-gas accounting, and inter-BAA transmission pricing that require detailed simulation before implementation. The MSC emphasizes that EDAM will pool scarcity pricing and reserve requirements across participants—meaning a shortage in one BAA will raise prices and impose reserve penalties across the entire footprint—and notes that design choices allowing bilateral contracts and demand-reduction programs to count toward RSE requirements, combined with pooled WEIM RSE treatment, could create perverse incentives for resource-insufficient BAAs to lean on the pool. For hydro and PSH operators, EDAM's inter-BAA optimization and removal of 'pancaking' transmission charges will increase dispatch flexibility and inter-regional arbitrage opportunities, but the MSC warns that unresolved details on export constraints, shortfall allocation (especially when multiple BAAs cannot meet their EDAM schedules simultaneously), and GHG export formulation could materially affect scarcity-pricing signals and the value of flexible ramping; the committee recommends flexibility in software design to allow parameter adjustments as EDAM begins operations with an initial small set of BAAs and expands.

documents.title: Agenda - Market Surveillance Committee Meeting - Mar 06, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Day-ahead market congestion revenue allocation affects price formation and the distribution of scarcity rents that hydro supplies depend on for revenue; this is a scheduled discussion on an active CAISO mechanism affecting energy pricing.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Market Surveillance Committee will hold a general session on March 6, 2026, with a substantive agenda item on extended day-ahead market congestion revenue allocation (CRA). The session runs 9:00 a.m.–12:00 p.m. Pacific Time. No details are provided in the agenda itself on the specific proposals or alternatives under discussion, but CRA design directly affects how scarcity rents from congestion are allocated to generators—a key revenue source for hydro at constrained nodes, particularly in the California system where many hydro plants face binding transmission limits. The document does not indicate a decision or voting outcome; it is notice of an advisory committee discussion whose details would appear in supporting materials not provided here.

documents.title: Presentation - Congestion Cost Analysis from Historical WEIM Performance - Mar 06, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Congestion revenue allocation (CRA) methodology for EDAM go-live affects how transmission rights exercised by PacifiCorp and internal generation self-schedules create congestion rents on CAISO constraints — directly touching the revenue from transmission-dependent hydro exports and the incentive structure for scheduling flexibility within the footprint.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Market Surveillance Committee presented analysis of how the revised CRA methodology will distribute congestion rents when EDAM goes live, using historical WEIM performance (Mar 2024–2025) as a proxy. The analysis finds that if PacifiCorp exercises PACE-to-PACW transmission rights and self-schedules internal generation under EDAM, revised allocation could shift $1.6–$2.5 million per month in congestion charges, with estimated monthly congestion rents ranging $2.34–$6.96 million depending on transfer-capability assumptions. The outcome is stated as 'no worse than current DA solution for CAISO congestion rents.' Hydro owners in PACE/PACW and those exporting through CAISO constraints should track the final CRA rule and EDAM implementation timeline (go-live date not stated in document) to quantify how parallel flows from PacifiCorp interarea transfers will alter nodal pricing signals and congestion revenue distribution affecting their scheduling and capacity value.

documents.title: Presentation - Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) Congestion Revenue Allocation - Mar 06, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: EDAM congestion revenue allocation design directly affects how hydro and other dispatchable resources' energy sales are priced and settled across the Western balancing-area seam; Phase 2 evaluation of self-schedule incentives and parallel-flow revenue allocation will reshape revenue capture for hydro exports and intra-EDAM transactions.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO is executing Phase 2 of EDAM congestion revenue allocation design, following FERC's August 2025 approval of a transitional Phase 1 design that launched EDAM in May 2026. The Phase 1 design allocates internal constraint congestion to the balancing area where the constraint sits, and allocates parallel-flow congestion from balanced self-schedules to the exercising balancing area. Phase 2 stakeholder working groups (two sessions completed as of this presentation) are now evaluating design concepts to address two key issues: (1) eliminating or reducing self-schedule incentives that discourage economic bidding, and (2) creating further symmetry in parallel-flow congestion allocation for the CAISO balancing area. The Market Surveillance Committee recommends exploring a negotiated-entitlement approach and has flagged questions about how negotiated entitlements would apply to Western market structures, existing inter-balancing-area operational agreements managing loop flow, and the basis for establishing entitlements (historical flows, dollar limits, etc.). The next phase of stakeholder working groups runs late March through May 2026. Hydro operators exporting across EDAM seams or managing run-of-river dispatch with balanced schedules face direct revenue exposure: parallel-flow congestion allocation affects the net LMP received, and the Phase 2 pivot away from self-schedule incentives toward economic bidding may reshape the value of storage and flexible hydro in managing loop-flow congestion. Owners of reservoir hydro with flexible dispatch and pumped-storage facilities in the Western Interconnection are most exposed.

documents.title: Presentation - Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) Congestion Charge Allocation Impacts - Mar 06, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: EDAM congestion charge allocation design directly affects hydro and storage dispatch incentives, transmission revenue recovery, and interregional wheeling economics—a core revenue mechanism for hydro with transmission-dependent value.
documents.ai_summary: Scott Harvey (MSC member) presents CAISO analysis of how EDAM's go-live congestion charge allocation design differs from Western EIM base-schedule treatment and the original EDAM proposal, using PacifiCorp East-to-West transactions as a case study. The presentation compares settlement outcomes across three designs using historical congestion data from June 2024, January 2025, and July 2025, finding that go-live EDAM produces materially different charge allocations depending on prevailing vs. counterflow conditions and the magnitude of internal vs. interregional congestion. The analysis is part of the Market Surveillance Committee's evaluation of EDAM design impacts; no immediate vote or deadline is stated, but the document supports ongoing MSC deliberation on EDAM revenue allocation fairness across the Western footprint—directly relevant to how hydro and PSH resources sited on transmission bottlenecks (especially in the Pacific Northwest interconnecting to California) will be paid for energy and congestion services under EDAM go-live.

documents.title: Presentation - Future Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) Congestion Revenue Allocation - Mar 06, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: EDAM congestion revenue allocation rules directly affect how hydro (especially pumped storage and dispatchable reservoir hydro) is incentivized to self-schedule vs. participate in real-time dispatch, and how loopflows from hydro export and renewable-heavy regions are priced and allocated across balancing areas—touching both revenue from congestion-related dispatch signals and the efficiency of hydro's participation in EDAM/WEIM markets.
documents.ai_summary: Scott Harvey presents CAISO's analysis of loopflow congestion pricing and allocation in EDAM, arguing that the go-live design's requirement for self-scheduling to receive congestion-charge rebates creates inefficient use-it-or-lose-it incentives and may shift costs between market participants. Harvey notes that loopflows—inter-balancing-area power transfers that create congestion on parallel constraints—can significantly impact market efficiency and proposes moving toward a long-run financial-congestion-hedge design (akin to PJM/MISO firm-flow entitlements and joint congestion management) that decouples rent allocation from self-schedule requirements. An interim step would assign internal-constraint congestion rents to balancing areas based on flow entitlements rather than self-scheduling. This is relevant to hydro because reservoir and pumped-storage hydro participating in EDAM would face distorted dispatch incentives under the current self-schedule-based rebate structure, particularly in scenarios with large new loads (data centers) and remote renewable generation; loopflow-impact allocation also affects the transmission value and export economics of hydro-rich regions like the Northwest. The presentation does not identify a formal comment deadline or board vote date.

documents.title: Agenda - Market Surveillance Committee Meeting - Feb 06, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a bare meeting agenda with no substantive proposal, discussion materials, or findings touching hydro revenue streams, compliance obligations, or strategic options.
documents.ai_summary: The Market Surveillance Committee will discuss extended day-ahead market configurable parameters, a topic relevant to hydropower operations, bidding flexibility, and real-time dispatch optimization in CAISO's wholesale markets.

documents.title: Presentation - Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) Configurable Parameters - Feb 06, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: EDAM configurable parameters directly affect imbalance reserve (IR) pricing, procurement, and the opportunity-cost bidding environment for all dispatchable resources including hydro and pumped storage, plus storage-specific envelope constraints and state-of-charge treatment.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's configurable-parameters working group completed sensitivity analyses on five EDAM design parameters: envelope constraint multiplier, IR bid cap, IR/RC default availability bids, deployed imbalance reserve factor, and transmission constraint enforcement. Key findings: higher envelope multipliers reduce battery IR procurement and raise IR prices (storage-specific impact); DIR factor and constraint enforcement interact to affect congestion and local pricing; bid caps temper clearing prices. Parallel operations (extended through April 30, 2026) will test envelope multipliers (starting at 85%, assessing lower values), DIR factor (starting at 100%, assessing reductions), and transmission constraint enforcement hierarchies; IR bid cap and DAB cap remain fixed at $55 per tariff. For pumped-storage operators, the envelope multiplier and DIR factor tuning during parallel ops directly affects how much IR PSH can procure and at what price — these govern the opportunity-cost treatment of state-of-charge constraints. Results will be assessed using parallel-ops market cases; no go-live decision date is stated, but the extended parallel window and commitment to post-go-live monitoring suggests continued parameter adjustment risk through mid-2026.

documents.title: Presentation - Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) Imbalance Reserve Costs Market Surveillance Committee - Feb 06, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: EDAM imbalance reserve market design and pricing directly affects storage and dispatchable hydro revenue from flexibility products and reserve provision; the analysis identifies design choices (15-minute start constraint, 30-minute ramp limits, 0.85 attenuation factor for storage energy usage, reserve procurement levels) that materially drive up reserve costs and reshape which resources are economical to deploy.
documents.ai_summary: Scott Harvey (Market Surveillance Committee member) presents analysis of EDAM imbalance reserve pricing in simulations, finding average reserve-up costs of ~$20–$40/day appear materially higher than efficient-market levels. The high costs stem from five key market design choices: 15-minute dispatch requirement, 30-minute ramp limits, 0.85 attenuation factor on storage energy usage, tying storage state-of-charge to reserve schedules (disconnected from real-time RTD dispatch), and the reserve procurement level and demand-curve shape. These design features inflate thermal commitment costs and create high opportunity costs for storage, making both thermal and storage sources expensive providers of imbalance reserves. Harvey notes some costs may be simulation artifacts (low storage energy offer prices, high reserve offer prices, constraint modeling differences), but warns the high prices may accurately reflect actual EDAM design if storage energy usage must be tied to reserve schedules. The analysis does not recommend specific changes but flags the disconnect between high reserve prices and relatively low energy prices as requiring investigation before market launch.

documents.title: Agenda - Market Surveillance Committee General Session - Jan 16, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO is discussing price formation enhancements including BAA-level market power mitigation and scarcity pricing, which directly affect energy revenue and cost-allocation mechanisms for hydropower across the ISO.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Market Surveillance Committee will discuss a straw proposal on price formation enhancements balancing balancing-authority-area (BAA) level market power mitigation (MPM) and scarcity pricing on January 16, 2026. The proposal touches how energy is priced under locational scarcity conditions and how market power is mitigated across BAA boundaries—both material to hydro revenue in CAISO's footprint and to the design of opportunity-cost offers that large hydro operators must develop. This is a straw-proposal discussion stage; no vote date or comment deadline is stated in this agenda notice.

documents.title: Presentation - Scarcity Pricing - Market Surveillance Committee - Jan 16, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Scarcity pricing mechanisms directly affect hydro and storage revenue through energy price formation and penalty-value settings that influence investment signals and intra-day dispatch incentives.
documents.ai_summary: James Bushnell (CAISO Market Surveillance Committee) presents an analysis of scarcity-pricing approaches in CAISO's pricing-enhancements initiative, arguing that penalty-value reassessment is more straightforward than adding new reserve products. The power-balance constraint (PBC) penalty currently stands at $1,000/MWh ($2,000 during 831-trigger conditions) and has remained unchanged for decades; at 2010 value it would equate to roughly $1,500 today. Bushnell argues that higher penalty values would strengthen incentives for storage resources to retain energy for scarce conditions, incentivize delivery of scheduled imports, and improve EDAM's ability to compete for scarce import MWh during tight western conditions. His recommendation is that penalty values be raised and coordinated across markets, potentially stepped up over time once a schedule is agreed, and that major new reserve products should await live EDAM experience. The implication: PSH and conventional hydro with storage capability would face stronger economic incentives to withhold energy for peak scarcity, increasing real-time dispatch revenue during tight periods but potentially affecting energy-market pricing dynamics in both day-ahead and real-time.

documents.title: Presentation - Market Power Mitigation Issues - Market Surveillance Committee - Jan 16, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Market power mitigation design directly affects how hydro and storage are tested for market power and when their offers are capped to default energy bids — the document critiques proposed changes to CAISO's three-pivotal-supplier test and discusses conduct-and-impact alternatives that would reshape which use-limited resources (battery, pondage hydro) face mitigation triggers and cost-cap enforcement.
documents.ai_summary: Scott Harvey (CAISO Market Surveillance Committee member) presents critical analysis of CAISO's August 22, 2025 straw proposal on market power mitigation, arguing that the ISO has not demonstrated non-competitive outcomes in recent years and that proposed system-level market-power tests applied to constrained regions lack evidence of actual withholding. Harvey contends that counterfactual simulations conflate volatile gas-cost methodology (understated gas input prices) with genuine market power and that applying mitigation to constrained-down regions is unwarranted. He separately explains conduct-and-impact test mechanics, noting that CAISO's competitive-LMP approach caps offers to default energy bids even when a resource's output would have zero price impact — a key distinction for batteries and pondage hydro. The presentation does not state a formal recommendation or next step, but signals Harvey's view that CAISO should require empirical proof of withholding days and accurate cost bases before expanding mitigation design. For PSH and battery owners, this is a critical opportunity to submit comments opposing overly broad mitigation triggers; the August 2025 straw proposal appears to be the active consultation document being critiqued.

documents.title: Presentation - Updated Analysis on Price Formation Enhancements - Market Surveillance Committee - Jan 16, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO is updating its Bidding Adjudication Algorithm (BAA) under the Mitigation and Pass-Through (MPM) mechanism — a market-power mitigation rule that directly affects how hydro and storage operators' opportunity-cost offers are tested and potentially rejected or accepted; changes to the grouping logic, LSO treatment, and CAISO testing parameters alter the revenue risk and bid acceptance likelihood for use-limited resources.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Market Performance and Advanced Analytics team presented updated analysis of eight algorithm variants for the BAA-MPM test, evaluated on Q3 2025 data (July–September). The variants combine three design factors: adjusted grouping logic, addition of a Load Serving Obligation (LSO) component to the Residual Supply Index calculation, and testing CAISO as a balancing-area entity. Adding the LSO component materially increased pass rates across most balancing areas, particularly in import-constrained midday hours in the Pacific Northwest and Central/Mountain regions (PSEI, SCL, TPWR saw +19–21% improvements), while testing CAISO reduced pass rates for several BAAs in peak evening hours (19–21). The presentation does not state a next step or decision date, but the analysis supports continued refinement of offer-acceptance criteria for resource-limited suppliers.

documents.title: Presentation - Price Formation Enhancements - Market Surveillance Committee - Jan 16, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO is redesigning BAA-level market power mitigation (affecting mitigation of storage and use-limited hydro offers) and advancing scarcity pricing — two mechanisms that directly shape hydro revenue through default energy bids, opportunity-cost offer treatment, and energy price formation during tight supply.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO is pursuing two parallel tracks in its Price Formation Enhancements initiative. Track 1 advances BAA-level market power mitigation (BAAMPM) by incorporating load-serving obligations (LSO) into the competitiveness test, mitigating only net-pivotal suppliers rather than all suppliers in a failed BAA group, and formalizing a grouping algorithm that includes the CAISO BA itself. This refinement directly affects how CAISO identifies whether hydro and storage operators have market power and thus whether their offers face mitigation — the LSO adjustment should reduce false positives by exempting capacity genuinely needed to serve an affiliate's retail load. Track 2 defers comprehensive scarcity pricing redesign, with CAISO planning incremental improvements (ensuring scarcity prices when load is shed, enhancing pricing when load is 'armed') before undertaking more expansive work; the Joint LSEs (SCE on behalf of IOUs, POUs, and Six Cities) oppose Track 2, while the Western Power Trading Forum urges full development of an energy supply margin framework. The LSO proposal has outstanding design questions for WEIM-only BAAs on handling merchant affiliate allocation. Most exposed are pumped-storage operators and large reservoir hydro with significant market power footprints in multi-affiliate BAAs, and any hydro owner relying on opportunity-cost mitigation exemptions; the competitiveness test changes could tighten or relax mitigation exposure depending on how LSO allocation evolves in stakeholder discussion.
