documents.title: Presentation - Price Formation Issues - Market Surveillance Committee - Jan 16, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO Market Surveillance Committee presentation on price-formation issues and reserve-product design directly affects hydropower revenue through scarcity-pricing mechanisms, reserve-procurement rules, and co-optimization of reserves with energy—all core inputs to hydro dispatch value and ancillary-service compensation.
documents.ai_summary: Scott Harvey, CAISO Market Surveillance Committee member, presents two critical price-formation issues for the California ISO balancing area: (1) real-time price signals in stressed conditions (August 2020 case study shows low real-time prices failed to incent import supply despite high day-ahead prices, and current rules cap prices at $1,000 during non-spinning-reserve dispatch, understating shortage value to suppliers and consumers); and (2) absence of explicit 30- or 60-minute reserve products, forcing CAISO to procure thousands of MWs of buffer capacity via expensive load-conformance purchases (energy bought in FMM and settled at RTD imbalance prices, with costs varying wildly). Harvey recommends CAISO adopt a tiered reserve product with graduated demand-curve pricing (referencing NYISO's 10-step design: $0–$750 depending on shortage MW), co-optimized with energy, but cautions that existing flexiramp and imbalance-reserve implementation flaws (battery state-of-charge accounting, nodal-delivery tests, locational modeling) must be fixed first—work that will take years. Hydropower operators (especially pumped storage and flexible dispatchable units) are directly affected: better-designed reserve markets with co-optimization and scarcity-responsive pricing would raise the value of fast-response capacity and reduce reliance on out-of-market load conformance, increasing revenue certainty and reducing demand-side rationing risk.

documents.title: Market Surveillance Committee Draft Opinion - Gas Resource Management - Dec 12, 2025
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Gas-fired resource offer rules (reference levels, commitment cost caps, default energy bids) directly affect CAISO's mitigation framework for dispatchable generators; the proposal's expansion to EDAM participants and its implications for offer flexibility and cost recovery touch hydro's competitive position in energy pricing and dispatch, plus the proposal's stated concern about not enabling locational market power is foundational to how use-limited resources like hydro are treated in cost-based offer caps.
documents.ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee issued a draft opinion (Dec. 5, 2025) on the CAISO's Gas Resource Management (GRM) proposal, which addresses gas price volatility challenges for gas-fired generators participating in EDAM, including changes to reference-level calculation methods, after-the-fact cost recovery, and multi-day advisory market runs to improve fuel procurement forecasting. The MSC supports most enhancements as low-cost measures to help EDAM participants reflect true gas costs in offer prices, but flags a critical gap: the absence of any market-power test for commitment cost mitigation means all gas units are mitigated in every interval, creating inefficient caps for non-dominant units while potentially over-protecting dominant ones—a structural issue that directly parallels hydro's chronic mis-treatment under opportunity-cost-based mitigation rules designed for thermal units. The MSC identifies EDAM timing (day-ahead schedules posted after morning gas nominations close) as exacerbating these challenges across the expanded Western footprint, and recommends development of a workable market-power test for commitment costs as a high priority going forward. No vote or comment deadline is stated in this draft opinion.

documents.ai_summary: The CAISO Market Surveillance Committee will vote to adopt an MSC opinion on CAISO's extended day-ahead market congestion revenue allocation final proposal at its June 16, 2025 General Session (9:00–10:00 a.m. PT). Congestion revenue allocation is a component of price formation that determines how locational price differences flow to generators and load; changes to CRA rules can shift the scarcity signals and locational rents hydro facilities capture when they relieve transmission constraints. The agenda does not specify the substance of the proposal or MSC's recommendation, so the actual hydro impact cannot be assessed from this notice alone; hydro operators should obtain the underlying MSC opinion and CAISO proposal documents to evaluate whether allocation rule changes favor or penalize their geographic position.

documents.title: 05a Summer 2026 Operations Study - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Changes in transmission infrastructure (CHPE HVDC addition, Smart Path Connect upgrades, interface limit redistribution) and generation retirements affect hydro dispatch patterns, interregional transfer capability, and revenue opportunity from exports and locational scarcity pricing—all of which touch hydro's strategic deliverability and the value of flexible ramping capacity.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Summer 2026 operations study reports 1,225 MW of generation retirements (including large thermal units like Danskammer 532 MW and Narrows 352 MW) offset by 225 MW of solar and storage additions. Major transmission projects—Champlain Hudson Power Express HVDC, Smart Path Connect 345 kV stations, and Hertel–Astoria additions—are now modeled in service. These changes redistribute flows and alter interface thermal limits: Total East interface limit decreased 125 MW, UPNY-ConEd increased 250 MW, UPNY-SENY increased 225 MW, and Moses South increased 250 MW. Sprain Brook Dunwoodie South interface limit was raised to 4,225 MW and Total East to 4,050 MW. The study's status is a June 2026 presentation update to the Operating Committee; no forward deadlines are stated. For hydro owners, the net effect is mixed: retirement of thermal generation reduces dispatchable competition and may elevate real-time energy prices and ramping value, while the HVDC and transmission upgrades improve northern import capability and may compress NY price separations, affecting both in-state dispatchable hydro revenue and Northwest-to-NY export economics. Pumped-storage operators should monitor how the reduced Moses South thermal limits and increased UPNY interfaces change the voltage-support and frequency-response service opportunities in those zones.

documents.title: 05b Summer 2026 Operating Study Report Draft - Redline

documents.ai_summary: This Summer 2026 operating study evaluates thermal transfer limits on NYISO interfaces, with notable impacts from new transmission projects (CHPE and SPC) that redistribute power flows and affect total East and external interface transfer limits. These transmission changes directly influence how hydropower resources in New York and neighboring areas (ISO-NE, PJM, Ontario) can be dispatched and delivered to load centers.

documents.title: 05c Summer 2026 Operating Study Appendices Draft - Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Summer operating study details interregional transmission flows, HVdc converter schedules, and phase-angle regulator dispatch that directly affect hydropower export deliverability and revenue from cross-border power transfers — particularly from TransÉnergie (Quebec hydro) into NYISO and ISONE.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Summer 2026 Operating Study appendices specify base-case assumptions for generation dispatch, load, and interregional transfers. TransÉnergie is modeled exporting 2,362 MW to NYISO, 1,500 MW to ISO-NE via Sandy Pond HVdc, and 1,250 MW to Ontario (IESO) — all major hydro corridors. HVdc converter schedules list in-service status for Champlain Hudson Power Express (1,250 MW), Sandy Pond (1,500 MW), and four other TransÉnergie HVdc links (total ~3,300 MW). Phase-angle regulator dispatch on key NY interfaces (Ramapo, Uniondale, and cross-Sound) is specified to manage congestion. This study informs summer transfer limits and is foundational for RA adequacy and reserve requirement calculations; export-dependent hydro owners (Northwest suppliers to WECC/California, Quebec hydro exporters) should verify their assumed dispatch levels match actual operating conditions and test sensitivity to transmission constraints.

documents.title: 06a Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO Expedited Deliverability Study determines which generation and storage projects can deliver power without system upgrades — directly affecting interconnection timelines, costs, and deliverability certainty for new hydro, PSH, or co-located resources seeking NYISO queue status.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 analyzes three projects (Curtis/Palmer natural gas/hydro hybrid, Soltage battery energy storage, RP New York BESS) for deliverability at requested CRIS (capacity resource interconnection service) levels without triggering system-wide upgrades. All three projects passed their respective interface and byway tests and are deemed fully deliverable at requested capacity: Curtis/Palmer at 58.8 MW summer, Soltage at 20 MW, and RP New York BESS at 5.1 MW. The study is on track for Operating Committee approval with a 5-business-day decision window for each interconnection customer to accept or reject its deliverable MW; non-acceptance triggers re-study of remaining projects. For any hydro or hybrid generator seeking interconnection in NYISO, this procedural pathway offers faster deliverability determination than full Cluster Study if transmission constraints are minimal — but only for projects already through Class Year or Cluster study phases for ERIS (energy resource interconnection service).

documents.title: 06b Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 - Report
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO interconnection deliverability study determines whether a hydropower project (Curtis/Palmer Hydro) can inject its requested capacity without network upgrades — directly affecting hydro interconnection timelines, costs, and strategic feasibility of delivery into high-value zones.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 concludes that the Curtis/Palmer Hydro Project in the Rest-of-State Capacity Region is fully deliverable at its requested CRIS level without requiring system upgrades. The study evaluated three projects (Curtis/Palmer Hydro, Soltage battery, RP New York BESS) using 2029 summer peak conditions, transfer-limit analysis across capacity regions, and contingency screening under NPCC/NYSRC criteria. For hydro interconnection customers, a 'fully deliverable' determination removes the risk of being forced to undersize their injection or fund costly network upgrades to achieve their requested MW — streamlining path to commercial operation. The analysis covers methodology, base-case modeling, and transfer-capability assessments but contains no stated comment deadlines or next procedural steps in this excerpt.

documents.title: 07a Storm Watch Redispatch - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Tariff and settlement methodology changes to Storm Watch Redispatch (Transmission Security Analysis) cost allocation affect how real-time dispatch costs are computed and allocated to load zones, which can influence uplift/make-whole charges borne by dispatchable resources including hydro.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is clarifying its Services Tariff (MST 4.1.8) and revising the Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual to document a long-term improvement to Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) cost calculation. The enhancement will use Day-Ahead schedules from the bid redispatch pass (instead of initial Day-Ahead schedules) and real-time schedules to determine flows across TSA constraints, while keeping the real-time shadow-price methodology and Zone J customer allocation unchanged. This refinement stems from review of July 2025 TSA settlements that exceeded $53 million and aims to more accurately reflect the actual redispatch obligation. NYISO is seeking votes on the proposed tariff revisions at the June 2026 Membership Committee meeting.

documents.title: 07b Storm Watch Redispatch - T&D Manual - Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Storm Watch Redispatch procedures affect real-time dispatch operations, transmission constraints, and emergency redispatch authority — core operational obligations and dispatch mechanisms that touch hydro's real-time pricing exposure and must-offer/dispatch-instruction compliance.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual (Manual 12) redlines the Storm Watch Redispatch procedure, a real-time operational protocol for managing transmission constraints and emergency conditions. The document is in DRAFT status, accepted by the BIC on 04/15/2026 and Operating Committee on 04/16/2026, with an effective date of 05/01/2026. Storm Watch Redispatch is a corrective control strategy that allows NYISO to issue real-time dispatch instructions to manage transmission violations and security constraints — a tool that can override normal market clearing and compel hydro resources to adjust output. For hydro operators, the relevance lies in the operational obligation to accept Storm Watch redispatch directives, which may suppress market-cleared prices during constraint periods or require uneconomic output changes. Reservoir and run-of-river hydro are equally exposed to these dispatch obligations; the mechanism affects real-time revenue predictability and compliance burden.

documents.title: Proposed Motions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Motion #2 approves an Expedited Deliverability Study that affects interconnection and transmission deliverability for new and existing resources, and Motion #3 modifies dispatch operations manual rules that govern how generation responds to grid conditions — both touch hydro's operational obligations and strategic positioning for interconnection and uprates.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO Operating Committee approves the Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 Report and revisions to the Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual related to 'Storm Watch Redispatch.' The deliverability study directly affects which new and existing generation (including hydro uprates and storage projects) can physically deliver power; study approval clears the path for resource interconnection decisions. The Storm Watch redispatch manual revisions change how NYISO instructs synchronous generation to operate during weather events — a compliance obligation for conventional hydro units. No comment deadline or vote date beyond the June 11, 2026 approval is stated in this motion summary.

documents.title: 03 Operations Report - May 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Monthly operational metrics tracking NYISO generation mix, hydro/pumped-storage capacity and availability, intermittent resource forecasting, curtailment, and transmission constraints that directly affect hydro dispatch and revenue opportunities.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's May 2026 operations report shows hydro (including pumped-storage hydro) at 6,324 MW nameplate capacity, comprising 12% of NYCA generation resources. The report tracks month-to-month generation mix, day-ahead market capacity unavailable in real-time (broken by fuel type including hydro), wind and solar curtailment by transmission constraint, net load ramps, and reserve activations. Two new transmission projects (Smart Path Connect and additional 345 kV facilities) came online in May, and the Champlain Hudson Power Express merchant transmission facility entered the real-time energy market on 5/13, potentially improving deliverability for hydro exports. Hydro owners use this baseline data to assess system balance, transmission constraints limiting their dispatch, and competitive positioning versus wind/solar capacity (11,547 MW intermittent nameplate vs. 6,324 MW hydro). The report shows May 2026 wind and FTM solar curtailment at 2.7% system-wide, with specific transmission constraints listed, affecting revenue for hydro resources competing in the same nodal zones.

documents.title: Final Motions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Motion #2 approves the Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02, which directly addresses interconnection and deliverability—a core strategic option for hydro owners evaluating uprates, new resources, and transmission-constrained resource placement.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO Operating Committee approved three routine motions on June 11, 2026: prior-meeting minutes, the Expedited Deliverability Study 2025-02 Report, and revisions to the Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual for Storm Watch Redispatch. The deliverability study approval is substantive for hydro owners: deliverability studies determine whether generation—including hydro uprates and new hydro interconnection—can physically export power and receive full capacity accreditation, and expedited studies typically flag resource-constraint relief needed for queue-clearing. The Storm Watch manual revisions affect real-time dispatch and redispatch authority during extreme weather, a standing operational obligation for synchronous hydro units. No comment deadlines or further votes are stated in this motion document.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: SEAMS GOVERNANCE: This is a foundational Markets+ Seams Working Group meeting establishing the forum and process for regional market integration, which directly affects hydro owners' strategic options for participation across multiple RTOs and their ability to access different market designs and revenue mechanisms.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ Seams Working Group convened June 11, 2026, with introductory slides establishing meeting procedures, attendee roles (panelists vs. observers), accessibility, and governance. This is the operational kickoff of Markets+ — the multiregional market-seams coordination initiative NHA members track as an alternative/complement to EDAM. The working group's decisions on data sharing, operational coordination, and interregional product design will determine which hydro assets can participate in which markets and under what rules. Hydro owners with operations or contract exposure across the SPP, ERCOT, and other affiliated regions should monitor MSWG outputs for changes to transmission use, dispatch authority, capacity accreditation portability, and ancillary-service procurement that affect asset monetization and operational flexibility.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: this agenda covers Markets+ seams governance and interregional transaction/export processes (MIR60-R MITSPS, MIR83 eTag, SPP-CAISO dialogue) that directly affect how hydropower and PSH operators participate in and settle interregional energy transfers, which is critical to Northwest hydro exports and revenue.
documents.ai_summary: The Seams Working Group is meeting June 11, 2026, to address three operational seams topics: Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) scheduling, eTag status coordination, and an SPP-CAISO dialogue on seams issues. The agenda also includes policy questions on High Priority System Sales. These administrative and governance items directly govern how hydro owners execute interregional transactions, settle exports, and allocate transmission points across the SPP-CAISO boundary — core to monetizing hydro generation in multi-RTO footprints. The working group will also discuss future meeting logistics, including a possible September in-person joint meeting in Denver with the broader Seams Advisory Group.

documents.title: 02-MIR75-R SPP Comments-20260520 (1).docx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Establishes e-Tag adjustment rules for high-priority export transactions when a Resource's maximum output (Max ECOL for conventional units, discharge limit for MSRs, or VER forecast) is less than the export bid amount. This directly affects hydro and pumped-storage export revenue by setting when and how export clearings are reduced pro-rata across multiple tags using the same source — a critical rule for dispatchable export scheduling.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ proposes mechanics (MIR75-R) for adjusting high-priority export e-Tags when a Resource's maximum available capacity (Max ECOL/discharge limit) or VER forecast falls below the sum of all Normal-transaction export bids. When reduction is required, adjustments are made pro-rata across all tags sharing the same source, rounded to whole MW, with a 1 MW minimum threshold. The rule applies in Day-Ahead Market (adjustment posted at market close) and Real-Time Balancing Market (adjustment in Pre-RTBM SCED study). This clarifies policy intent from prior MSWG/MDWG decisions (April 16, 2026 meeting) by specifying the pro-rata allocation method — eliminating ambiguity that could have forced discretionary cuts. Most affected are conventional hydro and pumped-storage operators with multiple simultaneous export tags from a single resource, where the pro-rata rule now guarantees fair allocation rather than first-come-first-served exposure. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in the document.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Markets+ System Points (MPSP) and MITSPS establish new interchange transaction pathways for imports/exports across the Markets+ boundary; this directly affects how hydropower resources in participating BAAs can schedule and tag energy transactions into/out of the footprint, potentially altering revenue streams and operational flexibility for hydro-rich members considering or already committed to Markets+ governance.
documents.ai_summary: SPP staff (Kelli Schermerhorn) submitted MIR 60-R to implement Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS), a framework enabling Market Participants to schedule and e-Tag import and export transactions using generic Markets+ System Points (MPSP) as sources and sinks, rather than resource- or load-specific points. MITSPS will allow hydropower operators and other dispatchable resources in participating BAAs to route energy to and from the Markets+ boundary with greater operational ease, with the MPSP functioning as a generic scheduling node that must be registered and used for all import/export transactions crossing the footprint. The design is classified as low complexity and expected implementation in Q1 2027 after MPEC approval in Q3 2026; this modification to interchange scheduling and tagging directly affects revenue attribution, transaction flexibility, and portfolio management for hydro and storage resources participating in Markets+ or considering accession to it, particularly relevant for Northwest and Southwest operators evaluating Markets+ membership versus EDAM or other seams options.

documents.title: 06_High Priority Exports Surplus Calculation Change BHE Presentation Final.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611.zip]
documents.filename: 06_High Priority Exports Surplus Calculation Change BHE Presentation Final.pptx
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Interregional export rules and surplus-capacity calculations directly affect hydropower's ability to contract and schedule firm exports across seams, touching both revenue streams (bilateral trade liquidity and firmness classification) and strategic options (Markets+ participation and cross-border transmission utilization).
documents.ai_summary: Black Hills Energy presents a proposal to align Markets+ High Priority Export surplus calculations with Operations Program obligations, rather than keeping them static across the entire WRAP binding season. Currently, surplus capacity is calculated once per season against forward RA obligations but cannot be updated as operational conditions change, forcing high-priority exports to be classified as non-firm (uncommitted) even when actual operational capacity exists. The proposal would allow surplus recalculation on a near-term basis to reflect real availability, addressing a misalignment between WSPP Schedule C (firm-service language) and Markets+ treatment of uncommitted exports as economically dispatchable. This affects hydropower owners relying on firm bilateral export trades across the SPP-WEIM seam, particularly in constrained regions like Rocky Mountain; the change would restore liquidity of firm bilateral trades and planning certainty for load-serving entities. Status and next steps are not stated in this presentation.

documents.title: 07_2026_Q2_JUNE_MSWG_StakeholderTrainingUpdate_1.0.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Training update on Markets+ participant readiness program establishes how hydro and PSH operators will comply with the new SPP Markets+ market system — a strategic governance choice with revenue and operational implications for NHA members considering or already committed to Markets+ participation.
documents.ai_summary: SPP is rolling out a phased training program (Levels 1–3) to prepare market participants for Markets+ operations, with Level 1 foundational content, Level 2 topic-specific instructor-led sessions, and Level 3 task-based training. The Level 2 curriculum includes sessions on day-ahead market, RTBM, RUC, must-offer obligations, market storage resources (MSR), dispatchable demand response, and variable energy resources — all directly relevant to how hydro and PSH register, bid, and settle under Markets+. Trainers and market participants must complete readiness milestones; the training team is gathering feedback from entity trainers and collecting input on Level 3 development. Next working-group meeting is August 13, 2026.

documents.title: 04_MPSP_MITPSPS and NSI.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: The document explains how Markets+ seams constructs (MPSPs, MITSPS, MTIs, CISLs) affect net scheduled interchange (NSI) and real-time dispatch, which determines how interregional power transfers are scheduled and constrained — directly impacting hydro deliverability, interregional export revenue, and operational flexibility for hydro-rich regions seeking to participate in Markets+.
documents.ai_summary: This Markets+ Seams Working Group presentation illustrates how Multi-Party Scheduling Points (MPSPs), Market-Initiated Transfers through SPP Scheduling (MITSPS), Market Transfer Interchange (MTI), and Constraint Interchange Scheduling Limits (CISLs) function in real-time net scheduled interchange (NSI) calculation and dispatch. The document walks through examples showing how bilateral transactions, economic MITSPS cleared in day-ahead, normal and dynamic MTIs, and MPSP constraints combine to determine a BAA's total NSI and how real-time redispatch occurs when constraints bind. The mechanism is directly relevant to hydro owners evaluating Markets+ membership because it defines how hydropower exports cross seams, how interregional transfer capability is allocated and constrained, and how dynamic real-time adjustments may increase or reduce revenue opportunities from selling flexibility across balancing-authority boundaries. The presentation contains no action items, deadlines, or decision dates — it is explanatory material for stakeholders learning Markets+ architecture.

documents.title: 00-Markets+ Intro Slides 20260611.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Markets+ is a seams-governance initiative directly affecting Western hydro's strategic options for RTO participation, regional market integration, and revenue-recovery opportunities across SPP footprint expansion.
documents.ai_summary: This is an introductory presentation for the Markets+ Seams Working Group (MSWG) net conference on June 11, 2026. The document establishes meeting logistics, anti-trust protocols, and engagement rules for panelists (task-force members) versus attendees. No substantive market-design proposals or decisions are presented in these slides; the document serves as a procedural framing for the working group's ongoing deliberations on seams issues and market integration. For hydro owners tracking Markets+ as a potential alternative or complement to EDAM/WEIM membership, this confirms active engagement by Western utilities (BPA via proxy, Idaho Power via proxy) in the MSWG, signaling continued development of the SPP-led seams framework.

documents.title: 01-MSWG Agenda 20260611.docx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Markets+ Seams Working Group agenda addresses interregional market coordination, export scheduling, and system operations affecting hydro deliverability and revenue across SPP–CAISO boundary; specific action items on export tag adjustments, interchange transaction scheduling (MITSPS), and high-priority export surplus calculations directly shape how hydro exports are priced and procured at the seam.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ Seams Working Group (MSWG) convenes June 11, 2026, to advance several seams-coordination mechanisms affecting export scheduling and pricing between SPP and CAISO. Agenda items include MIR75-R (export tag adjustments), MIR60-R (Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules—MITSPS), MIR83 (eTag status), and high-priority export surplus calculation methodology. These mechanisms govern how hydro and other resources schedule and price power flowing across the SPP–CAISO boundary. The working group is coordinating with a planned September in-person joint MSWG–Seams Advisory Group meeting in Denver and will maintain regular monthly meetings through December 2026. Owners with generation near or exporting through the SPP–CAISO seam should monitor export tag rule changes and interchange scheduling impacts on revenues.

documents.title: 04-MIR60-R Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) submission.docx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
documents.filename: 04-MIR60-R Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS) submission.docx
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Establishes Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS), a new transaction pathway enabling hydropower and other dispatchable resources to schedule and tag export/import transactions across the Markets+ boundary without pre-scheduling bilaterals, affecting how hydro participates in interregional power flow and market clearing within the emerging Markets+ seam governance structure.
documents.ai_summary: SPP staff submitted MIR 60-R to implement Markets+ Interchange Transaction System Points Schedules (MITSPS), creating a framework for generic Markets+ System Points (MPSPs) to facilitate unscheduled interchange transactions cleared through day-ahead and real-time markets rather than bilateral pre-scheduling. The framework establishes that MITSPS transactions are classified as 'Uncommitted' imports/exports and do not count toward a participant's must-offer obligation, while each Balancing Authority Area may set optional hourly import/export/net limits on MPSP interchange via the Market User Interface. Implementation is targeted for Q1 2027, with MPEC approval sought in Q3 2026. For hydropower owners participating in Markets+—particularly those seeking to export renewable generation or manage interchange flexibility across the SPP-region boundary—this creates a new operational pathway that could alter revenue stacking (energy cleared in DAM/RTBM plus potential bilateral premiums) and strategic choices around scheduling vs. market participation; the mechanism is most relevant to larger hydro and PSH facilities managing outflow across seams into non-Markets+ balancing areas, since it enables market-cleared rather than pre-scheduled boundary transactions.

documents.title: 05_MIR83-R e-Tag Status.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: e-Tag confirmation requirements affect interchange transaction eligibility in Markets+, which directly impacts how hydro (especially run-of-river and variable output) participates in interregional energy markets and settlement accuracy — a core revenue mechanism for hydro exports and flexibility sales across seams.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ is evaluating whether to accept e-Tags at the 'Valid' (pending approval) or 'Confirmed' (approved) stage for import/export/through interchange transactions. SPP and its vendor recommend 'Confirmed e-Tags' — only those with completed multi-party approvals — to avoid settlement discrepancies, divergence between day-ahead awards and real-time operations, and failed transactions affecting market assumptions. The 'Valid' option offers earlier visibility and participant flexibility but creates operational and settlement risk: ~10% of Western Interconnect e-Tags in May 2026 were ultimately denied, and using unapproved schedules in market solutions could force settlements on transactions that never become operational. The recommendation aligns with existing Integrated Marketplace practices and reduces vendor development costs. Markets+ protocol sections 4.2.2.7, 4.2.3.3, and 4.2.4 currently reference 'valid' e-Tags; this decision will either codify or revise that language. Discussion topics and a decision timeline (MSWG → MDWG → MPEC) are outlined but no comment deadline or vote date is stated in the excerpt.

documents.title: 06_High Priority Exports Surplus Calculation Change BHE Presentation Final.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
documents.filename: 06_High Priority Exports Surplus Calculation Change BHE Presentation Final.pptx
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Proposes changes to Markets+ export surplus calculation that would affect how hydropower and other dispatchable resources can qualify for firm export revenue and planning certainty across the SPP/Markets+ seam.
documents.ai_summary: Black Hills Energy proposes realigning the Markets+ High Priority Export surplus calculation to reflect near-real-time operational availability rather than static seasonal peak-load forecasts, allowing resources with zero Operations Program obligations to classify exports as firm rather than being forced into non-firm/uncommitted status. The proposal highlights a misalignment between WRAP Forward Showing (seasonal, static) and Operations Program (hourly, dynamic), and a tension between Markets+ Export constructs and WSPP Schedule C firmness requirements — uncommitted exports are currently reducible for economic dispatch and congestion despite Schedule C limiting interruptions to mutual agreement and native-load reliability. Hydropower and other dispatchable resources operating in Rocky Mountain constrained-path regions rely on firm bilateral trades; this change would restore export eligibility for operators with seasonal RA obligations but zero hour-ahead commitments, increasing firm-trade liquidity and planning certainty for load-serving entities. Next step not stated in presentation materials.

documents.title: 07_2026_Q2_JUNE_MSWG_StakeholderTrainingUpdate_1.0.pptx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: SPP Markets+ is a proposed multi-RTO governance and market-design seam affecting Western hydro operators' strategic options and compliance obligations; this training material establishes what participants must learn to engage with the new framework, including storage resource registration, must-offer rules, congestion and GHG modeling, and settlement mechanics that directly shape hydro revenue and dispatch control.
documents.ai_summary: SPP is rolling out a tiered stakeholder training program for Markets+, a multi-RTO consolidation framework affecting governance, market design, and operations across the Southwest and potential expansion regions. The training curriculum covers foundational market architecture (Level 1), topical deep-dives on transmission, congestion rent, GHG accounting, must-offer obligations, day-ahead/real-time/RUC mechanics, market monitoring, and settlements (Level 2), plus task-based implementation training (Level 3). Hydro and pumped-storage operators in or considering SPP Markets+ must complete Level 1 foundational training and select Level 2 and 3 courses to participate; the curriculum explicitly includes modules on Market Storage Resources (MSR), resource registration (including Jointly Owned Units and Combined Interest Resources), and dispatchable demand response—frameworks that will govern how PSH charges, how hydro's must-offer obligations apply, and how congestion and greenhouse-gas costs flow through settlement. Next stakeholder-training update is August 13, 2026; training registrations are open in the SPP Learning Center.

documents.title: 03-MIR75-R SPP Comments-20260520 (1).docx  [in MSWG Meeting Materials 20260611 (1).zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Defines e-Tag adjustment mechanics for high-priority exports from hydro and storage resources (MSRs) when resource capacity limits are exceeded; directly affects export revenue certainty and intra-day settlement for run-of-river hydro and pumped-storage discharge exports in Markets+ footprint.
documents.ai_summary: SPP submitted a Markets+ Initiative Request (MIR75-R) specifying the operational mechanics for adjusting export e-Tags when a resource's maximum economic limit or (for pumped-storage and variable energy resources) available output falls below the MW amount specified on the e-Tag. The proposal establishes that adjustments occur in day-ahead (at market close) and real-time (pre-RTBM SCED study) windows, with pro-rata allocation across multiple e-Tags using the same resource source, rounded to whole-MW increments with a 1 MW minimum threshold. This clarifies language from an April 2026 MSWG/MDWG policy decision and applies to high-priority exports (WRAP forward showings, WRAP holdbacks, and NERC Priority 6–7 transmission service from internal resources). The proposal distinguishes between conventional resources (limited by Max ECOL) and pumped-storage/hybrid resources (limited by Maximum Discharge Limit or VER forecast). Most exposed are pumped-storage operators exporting during tight resource conditions and run-of-river hydro operators whose unforecasted generation changes mid-day; the pro-rata settlement rule means their export revenue depends on how many other same-source e-Tags are active. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this submitter-comment form.

documents.title: 8a 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook Report
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year resource and transmission outlook directly affects hydropower revenue through capacity-adequacy accreditation, generation-pocket curtailment risk, transmission congestion signals that alter locational value, and strategic location decisions for new capacity — all core inputs to hydro asset valuation and dispatch strategy in the Northeast.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook examines how New York's grid will evolve under multiple policy and demand scenarios through 2044, focusing on generation adequacy, resource-mix composition, and transmission constraints. The analysis finds that renewable generation concentration in upstate pockets creates curtailment risk (especially by 2035–2044) and drives bulk-transmission congestion through the Central East interface, with fossil-generation retirements and large-load growth (data centers, etc.) tightening system conditions and raising downstate capacity payments. For hydropower owners, this outlook establishes the transmission-planning baseline affecting deliverability and locational scarcity value; for pumped-storage operators, it identifies whether seasonal adequacy stress and interregional export opportunities (low-cost clean-energy export potential noted in 2035 scenarios) will raise PSH cycling revenue and RA accreditation premiums. The document is a draft for NYISO Business Issues Committee discussion (June 2026); no formal comment deadline is stated, but future comprehensive reliability plans and transmission procurements will flow from these scenarios.

documents.title: 8 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: 20-year NYISO transmission and resource outlook identifies firm dispatchable capacity requirements, transmission constraints affecting generator deliverability, and resource adequacy challenges in a high-decarbonization future — all determinants of hydro's system value, dispatch patterns, and investment conditions.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook projects electricity demand outpacing resource additions, with firm dispatchable capacity remaining essential across all decarbonization scenarios to provide energy, operating reserves, ramping, regulation, and voltage support during periods of low renewable output. The study identifies Central East and Northern New York transmission interfaces as bottlenecks constraining renewable deliverability and system efficiency; targeted dynamic voltage support upgrades are recommended. Coordinating the timing of resource retirements and new additions is flagged as critical to avoid tightening system conditions. The findings inform NYISO's biennial Comprehensive System Planning Process and identify scenarios for Economic Transmission Project Evaluation and Requested Economic Planning Studies. Board approval is scheduled for July 2026; the full report and 12 technical appendices (including capacity expansion and production cost simulations, renewable generation pocket analysis, and sensitivities) are available in the data catalog.

documents.title: 6a Accounting & Billing Manual Incremental Highlight
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a procedural manual update covering settlement invoicing, meter data management, and dispute processes—operational/administrative mechanics with no impact on hydro revenue streams, market-design obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: 6b Accounting & Billing Manual redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Settlement and billing manual redlines affect how hydro operators' energy and ancillary services revenues are calculated, metered, invoiced, and disputed—foundational operational and financial obligations.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO has issued a draft redline (version 7.0, effective 04/30/2026) of its Accounting and Billing Manual for Business Issues Committee approval (scheduled 06/10/2026). The manual governs settlement processing, invoicing schedules, meter data management, challenge and dispute procedures, and supplier settlements for energy (DAM and real-time), ancillary services, and demand response. Sections 4 and 5 detail energy and ancillary-services settlement mechanics for generators (including hydro and energy storage resources), encompassing Bid Production Cost Guarantees, balancing-market uplift, and voltage-support and regulation payments—the revenue streams on which hydro operators depend. Hydro operators and pumped-storage owners must track redline changes to settlement rules, metering timelines (including meter-data revision sunset provisions and resettlement procedures in section 1.4–1.5), and dispute deadlines, as errors in these procedures directly affect cash settlement.

documents.title: 2 Draft April 15, 2026 Minutes
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: NYISO capacity accreditation and winter ICAP demand-curve methodology directly affects pumped-storage and conventional hydro revenue through seasonal accreditation factors (CAFs) and locational capacity-market clearing prices; the CHPE UDRs decision also touches interregional hydro deliverability and market-seam governance.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's BIC approved tariff revisions to align winter ICAP demand curves for 2026–2027 to account for potential capacity supply via CHPE (Champlain Hudson Power Express) UDRs from HydroQuébec into New York City, contingent on CHPE commencing summer participation. The proposal resets winter-peak and shoulder ICAP demand curves and their UCAP translations using capacity-accreditation factors (CAFs) that assume CHPE participation ('CHPE-In' parameters). Stakeholders raised concerns that actual winter capacity supply via CHPE could diverge from the 0-MW assumption for winter peaks, potentially inverting NYC winter-to-shoulder price signals and reducing accreditation revenue for existing capacity suppliers. The NYISO rejected a lower-curve stakeholder alternative and declined to impose a prohibition on CHPE winter sales, asserting the proposal maintains revenue sufficiency for the reference technology. The proposal is pending FERC approval under ER26-1431 and will become effective on FERC's permit date; it resets baseline assumptions for the broader Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements design scheduled for 2027–2028. Most exposed are existing hydro and PSH owners in NYC and rest-of-state capacity zones whose seasonal RA valuations and clearing-price signals are recalibrated.

documents.title: 2 Draft May 13, 2026 Minutes
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: capacity market accreditation and participation rules (CHPE UDRs, capacity parameter changes), automatic ICAP surplus-offer rules affecting capacity availability and revenue, and RA/reliability planning updates all touch hydro's capacity revenue streams and strategic options in NYISO; also load-forecasting and DER participation developments relevant to hydro's competitive position.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee approved an Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers proposal (Motion #1, May 13, 2026) requiring automatic offering of surplus capacity at $0.00/kW-month with clearing price payment, with May 2027 implementation. Separately, CHPE transmission line commenced real-time energy-market operation May 13, 2026 and capacity-market participation July 2026 delivery month, triggering changes in NYISO capacity-market parameters that will affect existing resources' capacity availability ratings starting July 2026. The committee also reviewed ongoing Electric System Planning and Installed Capacity Working Group activities addressing reliability enhancements (FERC Order 1920 compliance), 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook updates, DER unavailability methodologies, and flexible large-load wholesale participation. For hydro owners, the surplus-offer rule eliminates the option to withhold resale capacity (historically 2.1 MW unoffered Jan 2026), forcing systematic market clearing at spotauction prices; the CHPE parameter shift reallocates capacity value across the system, requiring owners with reduced ICAP ratings to secure makeup capacity or release excess through July 2026 auctions.

documents.title: 7 Proposed Changes to Attachment N of the ICAP Manual
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO proposes seasonal translation factors for run-of-river hydro and intermittent resources affecting UCAP accreditation and capacity-requirement calculations in IRM/LCR studies, directly impacting hydro's RA value and seasonal revenue recognition.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO proposes revisions to Attachment N of the ICAP Manual to establish separate summer and winter translation factors for Limited Control Run-of-River Hydro Resources and Intermittent Power Resources. Currently, translation factors (which convert Installed Capacity to Unforced Capacity for RA calculations) are computed using June–August production data; the proposal adds winter factors using December–February data, weighted by hourly LOLE factors from the most recent LCR study base case. The revision aligns with NYISO's Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements proposal (filed with FERC on 2/18/2026, effective 6/28/2026, targeted for 2027–2028 Capability Year implementation) and responds to observed winter LOLE in the 2026–2027 IRM study. The document also proposes a formula clarification to better document the current hourly LOLE-weighted calculation method. Run-of-river and seasonal-variable hydro owners will see their RA accreditation shift between seasons—winter production now counts toward winter capacity needs separately—which may raise or lower total RA credits depending on the seasonal production profile relative to winter LOLE weighting.

documents.title: 7c ICAP Manual Appendix Attachment N clean
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Attachment N specifies the NYISO procedure for calculating translation factors for intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources, directly affecting how these units' unforced capacity is determined and accredited in NYISO's capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: This is NYISO's Installed Capacity Manual Appendix, issued June 2026, covering capacity-market procedures and definitions. Attachment N (referenced in the table of contents but not fully excerpted here) addresses the translation-factor calculation procedure for intermittent power resources and limited-control run-of-river hydro—the mechanism by which NYISO converts such resources' nameplate or maximum output into their contribution to installed capacity (unforced capacity, or UCAP). This translation-factor methodology directly determines capacity-accreditation and revenue for variable-output hydro; changes to the procedure affect both how a plant's UCAP obligation is calculated and its market revenue under NYISO's mandatory participation rules. The document also covers external capacity import rights (including Hydro-Quebec deliveries via Champlain Hudson Power Express and Cedars interface) and DMNC testing protocols for hydro generation. No immediate action deadline is stated in the excerpt, but this is the operative manual governing NYISO's treatment of hydro resources in capacity markets.

documents.title: 7a ICAP Manual Appendix Attachment N redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ATTACHMENT N establishes the procedure to calculate translation factors for intermittent power resources and limited-control run-of-river hydro in NYISO's capacity market, directly affecting hydro's installed-capacity accreditation and thus revenue.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's ICAP Manual Attachment N (redlined draft, dated June 2026) governs how intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources translate their nameplate capacity into unforced capacity (UCAP) for capacity-market accreditation. This translation-factor methodology is the core mechanism by which variable hydro (which cannot be reliably dispatched at full output across all conditions) receives a derated ICAP credit versus synchronous resources, directly reducing the capacity revenue such hydro can claim in NYISO's auctions. The attachment specifies the procedure by which hydro owners request and substantiate the calculation of these factors, affecting which hydro plants qualify as energy-limited resources (Attachment M) and how their seasonal or weather-dependent output profile is translated into a single UCAP value for RA showings. No comment deadline or next step is stated in the excerpt provided.

documents.title: 7b ICAP Manual Appendix Attachment N incremental redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ICAP Manual Attachment N describes the procedure for calculating unforced-capacity translation factors for run-of-river hydro and intermittent resources—directly affecting how NYISO accredits run-of-river hydro in the capacity market and thus hydro's RA participation and revenue.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is revising ICAP Manual Attachment N, which governs the calculation of translation factors (the fraction of installed capacity that counts as unforced capacity) for intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources in NYISO's capacity market. This redline was circulated for the June 2, 2026 ICAP WG meeting and is flagged for discussion at the June 10, 2026 Business Issues Committee. The translation-factor methodology determines what fraction of a run-of-river plant's nameplate capacity NYISO credits toward a seller's capacity obligation—a critical parameter for run-of-river owners' RA revenue and portfolio planning. The document is in draft-for-discussion status; specific redline content is not fully visible in the excerpt, but the procedural revision is scheduled for committee action.

documents.title: 8c Motion
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook is a long-term resource-adequacy and transmission study directly affecting hydro fleet planning, seasonal energy adequacy, and strategic generation-mix decisions across NYISO's footprint.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee recommends Board approval of the draft 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook, a 20-year resource-adequacy and transmission study. This outlook informs seasonal energy-adequacy assessments, transmission expansion priorities, and resource-mix planning that shape scarcity conditions, transmission deliverability constraints, and the revenue environment for existing and new hydro and pumped-storage capacity in NYISO. The motion advances to the Management Committee and Board for final approval; the document itself carries no explicit stakeholder comment deadline, but the prior ESPWG and TPAS meetings cited indicate the outlook has been through iterative stakeholder review.

documents.title: 4a MST 26.4 redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This tariff redline modifies NYISO's credit and collateral requirements for market participants, including specific provisions affecting energy and ancillary services bids, import/export transactions, and capacity market participation — revenue streams and operational obligations that directly shape hydro and PSH participation economics.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO has redlined tariff section 26.4 governing Operating and Bidding Requirements — the credit collateral framework that participants must post to trade energy, ancillary services, and participate in capacity and TCC auctions. The revision clarifies calculation formulas for the Energy and Ancillary Services Component (with separate factors for prepaid vs. non-prepaid customers), refines Import and Export Credit Requirements using 98th and 97th percentile price differentials respectively over rolling one- and five-year lookback windows, and specifies how historical price data is weighted and applied when insufficient data exists at a location. For hydro and PSH operators, this affects the upfront credit burden to bid flexible energy and ramping products, particularly for facilities engaged in import/export arbitrage or frequent real-time adjustment — the collateral requirement formulas directly reduce available capital to deploy in energy or AS markets. Most exposed are PSH and run-of-river operators using interregional transactions to optimize revenue, and any facility with volatile net-settlement exposure. No explicit deadline or next-step action is stated in this redline excerpt.

documents.title: 9a OATT Proposed Revisions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: No document text was extracted; screening is based on title only ('OATT Proposed Revisions'). OATT tariff revisions may touch hydro obligations or market design, but cannot be evaluated without substantive content.

documents.title: 9 Order No. 1920 Long-Term Regional Transmission Planning
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: FERC Order 1920 transmission planning and cost allocation framework directly affects hydro deliverability, interconnection, and strategic transmission expansion that determines whether existing and new hydro resources can reach markets; long-term scenarios and resource-mix assumptions will drive transmission investment decisions that enable or constrain hydro export capability.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is implementing FERC Order 1920 (and subsequent clarifications 1920-A and 1920-B) through a redesigned Long-Term Transmission Planning Process that expands the 20-year planning horizon, incorporates New York Department of Public Service scenario input, and establishes an ex ante cost-allocation methodology for selected transmission projects. The process creates a new Catalog of Transmission Expansion Opportunities, solicits private-sector project proposals competing against NYISO baseline solutions using seven FERC-mandated benefit metrics (avoided reliability facilities, LOLE reduction, production cost savings, reduced losses, outage-congestion mitigation, extreme-weather resilience, and capacity benefits), and gates selection through NYPSC declaration of Long-Term Transmission Needs before cost recovery is allocated. The framework directly affects where transmission investment flows: hydro owners in New York face a new, longer-term planning horizon and competing non-wires alternatives that may or may not prioritize inter-area hydro exports versus local reliability; the cost-allocation methodology (details not yet finalized) determines whether transmission enabling hydro delivery is socialized regionally or assigned to specific beneficiaries. The compliance mechanics are active — NYISO Board approval of the planning structure is imminent, and the first Outlook cycle and PSC filings will begin shortly thereafter.

documents.title: 5b Motion
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Storm Watch Redispatch Costs tariff revisions may alter dispatch procedures, cost allocation, and operational obligations for dispatchable resources including hydropower during weather emergencies — a category of dispatch instruction and settlement changes that affect revenue and compliance.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Business Issues Committee recommends approval of tariff and manual changes for 'Storm Watch Redispatch Costs,' covering both Market Administration and Control Area Services Tariff revisions and updates to the Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual. The motion advances the proposal to the Management Committee for further approval. The document does not specify the substance of the redispatch-cost changes, their impact on dispatch procedures or settlement, or affected resource classes; the presentation titled 'Storm Watch Redispatch Costs: Tariff and Manual Revisions' (referenced but not included here) would contain the operational and financial mechanism details necessary to assess whether hydro operators face new cost-allocation rules, dispatch constraints, or state-of-charge/operational mandates during storm conditions.

documents.title: 5 Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) Costs: Tariff & Manual Revisions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) cost settlement and uplift allocation directly affects NYISO energy market price formation and cost recovery mechanisms that apply to all dispatchable resources including hydropower.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO proposes tariff and manual revisions to clarify the calculation of Storm Watch Redispatch (TSA) costs—uplift charges triggered by emergency transmission constraints during severe weather. The Long-Term Improvement will enhance Day-Ahead and real-time flow calculations by using Day-Ahead schedules from the bid redispatch pass and real-time schedules to determine flows across TSA constraints, while keeping the real-time Shadow Price and allocation to Zone J customers unchanged. NYISO will seek votes on manual revisions at the June Oversight Committee and tariff revisions at the June Management Committee. This affects how NYISO recovers emergency redispatch costs from the market and thus the magnitude of uplift/make-whole payments that hydro and other dispatchable resources encounter during scarcity events—clarifying the calculation methodology may reduce settlement volatility following extreme events like the July 2025 storms that triggered $53 million in initial costs.

documents.title: 5a Transmission & Dispatch Manual redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: This Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual redline update governs NYISO real-time dispatch, commitment, monitoring, and transmission operations — the core operational procedures that determine when and how hydro resources are called upon, constrained, and compensated for reliability services and energy delivery.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's redlined Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual (Manual 12) updates operational procedures for real-time commitment, dispatch monitoring, transmission operations, and voltage/reactive power control. The manual covers reserve calculations, regulation requirements, corrective control strategies, and phase-angle regulator operations — all of which affect when hydro must respond, how its flexibility is valued in real-time dispatch, and revenue exposure to constraint relief and emergency operations. The document is in draft for Business Issues Committee discussion (BIC acceptance dated 04/15/2026, Operations Committee 04/16/2026) and effective 05/01/2026. Most directly material are sections on real-time commitment procedures (6.1), reserve and regulation requirements (3.1.4), and voltage/reactive support via STATCOM and SVC guidance (4.2.3) — all operational levers that determine whether hydro is selected for ancillary services or energy dispatch in competitive real-time markets.

documents.title: 6c Motion
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a routine settlement automation and accounting-manual housekeeping vote unrelated to hydro revenue formation, obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: 6 TSA Settlements Automation: Manual Revisions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document addresses only the technical automation of NYISO's internal billing and settlement system for thunderstorm alert redispatch costs — a procedural change to invoice processing that does not affect hydro revenue streams, market-design obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: 8a 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook Report - Revised
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year resource and transmission outlook directly shapes hydro's strategic positioning in New York, including load-growth impacts on scarcity and revenue, interregional hydro-Québec interchange, transmission constraints affecting hydro deliverability, and seasonal resource-adequacy outlooks that inform whether existing hydro retains or loses value.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook is a biennial scenario-based planning study examining how demand, resource mix, and transmission evolve under different policy futures. The Outlook models two demand scenarios (95x and 100x) and varying policy stringencies, projecting load growth driven by large loads (data centers and economic development), fossil retirements, and clean-energy targets; it identifies curtailment risk in renewable-generation pockets, bulk transmission congestion (especially on Central East corridor limiting upstate generation export), and strategic value of interregional flows and energy interchange with Hydro-Québec. For hydro asset owners, this study anchors NYISO's capital planning and can influence resource-adequacy constructs, transmission investment priorities, and the marginal value of flexible/dispatchable generation as load-growth tightens supply conditions. The Outlook was presented to the Business Issues Committee on June 10, 2026 (draft status); formal adoption and next-step timeline are not stated in this excerpt.

documents.title: 8d Draft Appendix B 2025-2044 Outlook Production Cost Model Assumptions Matrix - Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's production-cost modeling assumptions directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage resources are valued in long-term resource and reliability planning; the document specifies how PSH dispatch, conventional hydro modeling, and battery-storage precedent shape the inputs to capacity-expansion models that drive NHA members' strategic options.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO released a draft Production Cost Model Assumptions Matrix for its 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook, establishing modeling parameters for long-term reliability and resource-adequacy studies. The matrix specifies that conventional hydro (excluding Niagara) is modeled as hourly modifiers based on 5-year historic data with 2018 weather shapes; Niagara is modeled as a pondage unit with monthly energy targets; and pumped-storage hydro is modeled with endogenous optimization of pumping and dispatching. Battery storage similarly undergoes endogenous optimization by the production-cost model. These assumptions feed into capacity-expansion models that determine new-generation requirements and economic retirement risk by scenario (Base, Contract, and Policy cases with lockdown dates ranging 9/1/2025–10/31/2025). The document is a draft for NYISO Business Issues Committee discussion on June 10, 2026, and represents a critical methodological snapshot for how NYISO values existing and prospective PSH and conventional hydro in RA showings and long-run planning.

documents.title: 8e Draft Appendix G 2025-2044 Outlook Production Cost Model Results - redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Production cost modeling of NYCA generation fleet, LBMPs, and energy storage utilization through 2044 directly informs hydro asset owners' long-term revenue outlook, reserve-margin tightness, and the competitive positioning of energy storage relative to conventional hydropower in the region.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's draft Appendix G to the 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook presents production cost model results across four scenarios (Base Case, Contract Case, and two Policy Cases) for study years 2030, 2035, 2040, and 2044. The modeling shows load growth concentrated in downstate zones (NYC and Long Island), with LBMPs rising across the study period and becoming sharply elevated in later years (2040–2044) under the Contract Case due to assumed fossil-generator retirements outpacing new renewable additions; Policy Cases with higher capacity additions substantially mitigate LBMP growth and reduce net imports. Energy storage utilization analysis reveals that battery capacity additions exhibit high zone-level correlation with utilization but experience diminishing marginal utilization per unit as total storage capacity rises, suggesting a saturation point in battery efficacy. This outlook is a reference document for NHA members modeling demand-side scarcity, transmission congestion effects on zonal pricing, and the substitution dynamics between storage and dispatchable hydro in a low-carbon NYCA.

documents.title: 8f Draft Appendix I 2025-2044 Outlook Operational Analysis - redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's 20-year operational outlook characterizes hydropower's flexibility role and ramp-following capability relative to fossil generation and renewables, directly informing revenue opportunities for hydro's dispatchable and ramping services as the grid evolves under high-renewable scenarios.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 operational analysis (part of its System & Resource Outlook) models the generation fleet's hourly behavior under Base and Contract Cases, analyzing how different resource types respond to load and net-load (load minus intermittent renewables) changes across daily, hourly, and seasonal timescales. Hydro is characterized as serving both a baseline and dispatchable role with 'limited capability to respond to changes in load/net load' compared to fossil generation, which dominates ramp-following. The analysis shows hydro–load correlation is the second-strongest among generation types (after fossil), and hydro ramps track load ramps more reliably than weather-dependent renewables, positioning hydro as a secondary flexibility resource. This operational characterization — which NYISO will use to assess future resource needs and market design — affects how NYISO values hydro's ramp and ramping-product contributions and shapes transmission and flexibility adequacy planning that influences hydro owners' ability to monetize flexible output. Most exposed are hydro owners with ramp-capable resources; the analysis provides baseline data NYISO may use to set demand curves or capacity factors for flexibility products in forthcoming market-design reviews.

documents.title: 8g Draft Appendix J 2025 - 2044 Outlook Renewable Generation Pockets - redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's renewable pocket analysis identifies transmission constraints affecting hydro deliverability in northern and western New York; hydro resources compete with wind and solar for shared transmission paths, directly impacting energy-delivery value and strategic interconnection/transmission planning.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2025–2044 System & Resource Outlook Appendix J analyzes renewable generation pockets and finds that hydro resources in Pockets W and X (western and northern New York) face rising curtailment and congestion in high-penetration renewable scenarios. Pocket X1 (St. Lawrence hydro corridor) exhibits the second-highest congestion among all pockets; hydro curtails before wind and solar because hydro is ineligible for Tier 1 RECs and therefore carries no negative-bid cost in the production-cost model, putting it at a competitive disadvantage when transmission is constrained. The analysis is draft (dated June 10, 2026 BIC) and used to inform longer-range transmission-upgrade planning; approved Phase 1 and Phase 2 transmission projects in Northern New York (Smart Path Connect) are expected to alleviate some congestion, but Policy Case scenarios with 13–35 GW of additional land-based renewable capacity by 2035 will intensify resource-concentration pressures on hydro energy deliverability.

documents.title: 3 Market Operations Report
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO Market Operations Report contains energy price formation data, uplift/make-whole mechanisms, ancillary services pricing (regulation, reserves, voltage support, black start), and cost-allocation developments directly affecting hydro revenue.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Market Operations Report (June 2026) shows energy and ancillary services cost composition for NYISO customers through May 2026, revealing material shifts in price formation and uplift structures. LBMP (locational energy price) has risen sharply to $43.63/MWh in May 2026 YTD average vs. $36.99/MWh in May 2025; year-to-date NYISO average cost/MWh stands at $102.99 (May 2026) versus $80.72 (May 2025). Uplift (make-whole payments) shows month-to-month volatility, with statewide uplift share swinging between positive $1.68/MWh (January 2026) and negative values in subsequent months. Voltage support and black start services remain stable at approximately $0.14–$0.38/MWh monthly, while reserve and regulation costs track energy scarcity. The report's detailed monthly breakdown by LBMP, NTAC (transmission), reserves, regulation, uplift components, and ancillary services provides visibility into how NYISO's price-formation mechanisms and market-mitigation rules are translating to customer costs — critical for hydro operators managing opportunity costs and dispatch decisions.

documents.title: 04) Proposed Changes Must Offer.potx  [in MSUF_Meeting_Materials_20260610_v2.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Must-offer is a core compliance obligation for dispatchable hydropower and pumped-storage operators under Markets+ tariff; changes to settlement calculation and reporting directly affect how hydro owners are charged or credited for must-offer compliance and how those outcomes are tracked and billed.
documents.ai_summary: Markets+ is revising how must-offer charges and credits are calculated and reported in settlement systems. Currently, must-offer assessment occurs at the Market Participant (MP) level only, creating gaps in Asset Owner (AO)-level determinants for shadow settlement and invoicing. The proposed resolution allocates MP-level must-offer charges proportionally by load ratio share across each AO modeled under an MP, preserving MP-level assessment (and financial responsibility) while enabling AO-level reporting. Changes affect sections 2.6.17, 2.6.18, 2.7.26, and 2.7.27 of the Markets+ Settlement Calculation Guide. A two-week public comment period runs through June 24, 2026, via the SPP RMS system; comments require no formal tariff vote if the guide changes stand alone. Most exposed are hydro operators managing multiple asset owners under a single market participant profile, since the new load-ratio-share allocation will determine how aggregate must-offer penalties or credits flow back to individual facilities for purposes of settlement reporting and invoice tie-out.

documents.title: 05) Markets+ Program Update.pptx  [in MSUF_Meeting_Materials_20260610_v2.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Markets+ is SPP's new RTO market design launching in the western Great Plains; this program-status update documents settlement system development, testing timelines, and operational readiness milestones that directly affect how hydro and storage assets will be settled, must-offer, and participate in SPP markets post-go-live.
documents.ai_summary: SPP's Markets+ program (the new RTO market design for the western footprint) is in development with core settlement and financial systems in active build. The Settlements & Financial Apps Tower shows Core Settlement Management System (SMS) in wave 3 (of 3) development, with calculation sets 1–6 complete and sets 7–8 (including Must Offer calculations) in progress, targeted for delivery in parallel with market-system functionality. The program published its Participant Test Plan v1.0 in May 2026, outlining connectivity testing, data exchange testing, market trials, and parallel operations phases through 2027. A Markets/Operations Tower faces schedule risk (yellow status) due to vendor delays on detailed design; SPP has established a strike team to assess design complexity from supporting both CPT (SPP legacy) and PPT (M+) time zones simultaneously. Hydro and storage operators in SPP's footprint will need to complete connectivity and data exchange testing before market trials; the Must Offer calculation finalization (set 8, in progress) directly affects how dispatchable resources—including run-of-river and pumped storage—will be treated in the new settlement framework. Next steps include continued vendor coordination, SPP Integration Testing Period adjustments, and stakeholder training.

The training addresses registration, operational configurations, and market participation requirements essential for hydro and pumped-storage assets in the SPP Markets+ platform.

documents.title: 07) MDWG Updates.potx  [in MSUF_Meeting_Materials_20260610_v2.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Make-whole payment rules for dispatchable resources and GHG attribution directly affect hydro and storage revenue recovery under Markets+ energy price formation and cost disallowance mechanisms.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ approved MIR52, which specifies that GHG adders in real-time unit commitment (RUC) make-whole payments (MWP) should be included in the Manual Status Cost Disallowance for non-dispatchable injections, ensuring costs above a resource's economic operating point are not recovered. MIR77 addresses removal of inapplicable Integrated Marketplace language regarding dispatchable variable energy resource (DVER) deployment in the real-time balancing market, clarifying that Markets+ does not co-optimize for operating reserves (a responsibility left to balancing authorities). For hydro and pumped-storage operators, these rules affect how startup, shutdown, and GHG-related costs are treated in make-whole uplift payments — a key revenue recovery pathway when energy prices fall below operating costs. The mechanism is tightened cost disallowance for manual-status resources, which may constrain recovery for storage units or hydro plants operating outside economic dispatch.

documents.title: 08) RMS Overview.pptx  [in MSUF_Meeting_Materials_20260610_v2.zip]

documents.title: 09) Calibration Testing Overview.pptx  [in MSUF_Meeting_Materials_20260610_v2.zip]
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Calibration testing and metering/settlement rules are direct obligations for all hydro and PSH participants in SPP Markets+; this document details the settlement-area meter-data submission processes and calibration exercises that will govern billing adjustments for generation, load, and interchange ties during market trials.
documents.ai_summary: SPP Markets+ is conducting preliminary calibration testing in July–August 2026 to validate meter-data submissions and settlement-area constructs ahead of data exchange testing and market trials. Market participants and meter agents in each settlement area must submit meter data via SPP's portal, participate in calibration exercises to identify and resolve unaccounted-for-energy issues, and agree to share meter data during settlement-area-specific troubleshooting meetings. Hydro and PSH operators will be required to submit accurate generator meter data and tie-line data (with correct sign convention for imports/exports) and to resolve calibration disputes at the settlement-area level; high unaccounted-for-energy balances will shift settlement MWhs via load-ratio-share distribution, creating financial impacts if meter data is inaccurate or submission deadlines are missed. No formal vote or comment deadline is stated; next steps include SPP identifying meter agents and market participants by settlement area, distributing spreadsheet templates and exercise intervals in July, and scheduling settlement-area meetings for August.
