title: Planning Subcommittee (PSC) - June 10, 2026
meeting_hydro_reason: MISO Planning Subcommittee meetings typically address transmission planning, resource adequacy, and interconnection issues that directly affect hydropower and pumped-storage project deliverability and market participation.

documents.title: 20260610 PSC Item 06 MTEP Reliability Sensitivities of NERC TPL
documents.filename: 20260610 PSC Item 06 MTEP Reliability Sensitivities of NERC TPL.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MTEP scenario changes affecting transmission planning reliability assessment directly impact the operational and dispatch conditions under which hydro resources are modeled for interconnection, deliverability, and resource-adequacy accreditation purposes.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes to replace the 2-year Light Load, 0% wind planning sensitivity with a 2-year Winter Peak, low wind case (0% solar, wind TBD but below 67%) in its MTEP reliability sensitivities to better reflect winter operating conditions and stress the transmission system under NERC TPL compliance. The change affects how MISO models generation dispatch and transmission adequacy in planning studies used for both MTEP and generator interconnection (DPP), ERAS, and surplus-service reviews. Stakeholder feedback is due June 26, 2026, before BPM-020 and the Planning Modeling Manual are updated for MTEP27. Hydro owners should consider whether the winter-peak dispatch assumptions (low solar, lower wind) accurately represent the seasonal value and dispatch patterns of their resources in MISO's planning studies, particularly regarding accreditation in seasonal resource-adequacy analyses and interconnection deliverability assessments.

documents.title: 20260610 PSC Item 02 Liaison Report
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Storage charging treatment in interconnection studies and transmission planning directly affects PSH (and battery) participation economics; removal of charging limitations in GIAs and model changes to Phase 3 injection studies signal MISO's evolving approach to how stored-energy resources are studied and integrated into grid planning, with downstream impact on PSH project viability and cost allocation.
documents.ai_summary: MISO has decided to remove storage charging limitations in Phase 3 of its Distributed Generation Proposal (DPP), shifting the modeling approach from SH to LL methodology to include battery charging in interconnection studies; however, MISO will not remove charging constraints in Phase 1 of DPP. This decision elevates to the Planning Advisory Committee (PAC). The change directly affects how pumped-storage hydro and battery resources model their charging footprint in interconnection impact assessments, potentially lowering their study costs and revealing more favorable network impacts when charging is explicitly accounted for. Additionally, MISO posted feedback responses confirming its Annual Review of Dispatch Wind/Solar Assumptions and Transmission Cost Estimation Guide for MTEP26, which will inform transmission expansion studies and affect whether existing hydropower resources receive congestion-relief value or face new constraint headroom requirements. Pumped-storage projects seeking interconnection approval and existing PSH facilities relying on congestion revenue should monitor the PAC review timeline and MTEP27/DPP2027 modeling implementation.

documents.title: 20260610 PSC Item 01d Management Plan
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: TRANSMISSION PLANNING: The PSC oversees MISO's MTEP reliability planning, transfer-capability analysis, and multi-year transmission projects that directly affect deliverability and revenue for hydro generators; storage-charging transmission service requirements (PAC-2023-2) and removal of storage-charging limitations in grid interconnection agreements (PAC-2024-3) materially alter the operational and economic constraints on pumped-storage hydro.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Planning Subcommittee manages the Multi-Year Transmission Evaluation Process (MTEP) and reliability assessments across 2026–2027, with recurring quarterly reviews of approved transmission projects, voltage stability, and transfer-capability analyses. Two active initiatives directly affect storage and hydro: PAC-2023-2 (Transmission Service Requirements for Charging Electric Storage Resources from the Grid) and PAC-2024-3 (Removing Storage Charging Limitations in GIAs) are under discussion at the June 10, 2026 meeting and scheduled for final review through Q3 2026. PAC-2024-3 removes contractual caps on charging volume in generator interconnection agreements, potentially unlocking incremental pumped-storage dispatch and charging flexibility; PAC-2023-2 addresses how the grid must serve charging load, affecting cost allocation and dispatch freedom for PSH. The PSC's four quarterly cycles through 2027 include dispatch-model assumption reviews and sensitivity studies that will incorporate updated wind/solar forecasts and storage assumptions, shaping the transmission baseline against which hydro's operational value and export capability are evaluated.

documents.title: 20260610 PSC Item 05 Removing Storage Limitations in GIAs (PAC 2024 3)
documents.filename: 20260610 PSC Item 05 Removing Storage Limitations in GIAs (PAC-2024-3).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO is modifying generation interconnection study procedures for storage charging analysis, which directly affects how pumped-storage hydro interconnection impacts and cost allocations are evaluated in the DPP process.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes to replace the 'Summer Shoulder' charging scenario with a 'Light Load' charging scenario in generation interconnection (DPP) studies, effective DPP 2026 (pending approval by December 2026). The Light Load model represents early-morning hours at 45–65% loading, continuing to evaluate both storage charging and injection of queued generation across all three DPP phases. This change affects how network upgrades triggered by storage charging are identified and cost-allocated; PSH developers will now see upgrades tied to morning charging rather than shoulder-period charging, potentially altering which interconnection service categories bear upgrade costs. The mechanism matters because interconnection study procedures determine whether PSH charging access is operationally feasible and cost-justified; shifting the charging scenario from shoulder to light load could reduce or defer upgrade requirements, lowering interconnection costs for PSH projects. Most exposed are pumped-storage developers in the queue; MISO requests feedback by July 15, 2026 (PAC meeting) on the proposed BPM-015 language, with legal review and posting of updated BPMs targeted for Q4 2026.

documents.title: 20260610 PSC Item 01b Agenda
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Storage charging treatment in generator interconnection agreements (GIA) directly affects pumped-storage hydro revenue and operational flexibility, and MTEP reliability planning with storage sensitivities shapes the transmission and adequacy environment where PSH competes.
documents.ai_summary: MISO Planning Subcommittee will address two storage-relevant items: (1) removal of storage charging limitations in generator interconnection agreements (PAC 2024-3, item 5, presenter Grant Larson at 9:55 ET), which expands the operational envelope for PSH charging; and (2) MTEP reliability sensitivities for NERC TPL update (item 6, presenter Joe Dunn at 10:15 ET) incorporating storage in transmission adequacy modeling. The first item signals MISO's shift toward lifting GIA restrictions that have historically constrained PSH charging-mode operations and revenue capture. The second embeds storage (including PSH) into the transmission planning baseline, potentially improving the visibility of storage's contribution to reliability and the case for new transmission or storage-backed reliability solutions. Both are scheduled for discussion at the June 10, 2026 meeting; no explicit vote or comment deadline is stated in this agenda.

documents.title: 20260610 PSC Item 03a Modeling Update - Planning Process
documents.filename: 20260610 PSC Item 03a Modeling Update - Planning Process.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's multi-year planning model series (MISO26, 2026 ERAG MMWG) underpins resource-adequacy studies, transmission expansion, and seasonal energy-adequacy analyses that directly affect hydro revenue projections, ELCC accreditation, and deliverability planning.
documents.ai_summary: MISO Planning Modeling team has completed the 2025 ERAG Multiregional Modeling Working Group (MMWG) series and is advancing the 2026 and MISO26 series, which serve as the backbone for NERC transmission planning (TPL) and MISO's Planning Resource Auction (resource-adequacy) studies. The MISO26 power-flow models are on track for June 2026 submission to MMWG, with final Pass 4 models released April 1, 2026; dynamics models use PSSE v35.6.4 and include summer-peak, light-load, and shoulder-load cases across 2026, 2030, and 2035 horizons, with wind and solar capacity-credit penetrations specified for each case. These model series are the inputs to seasonal energy-adequacy studies and transmission needs assessment that determine whether existing hydro plants' RA contributions (ELCC values) and transmission-delivered energy values remain stable or erode under future generation mixes; changes to model structure or resource assumptions in future years (e.g., solar saturation levels, minimum-load scenarios) cascade into accreditation and planning-reserve-margin calculations.

documents.title: 20260610 PSC Item 01c Minutes 20260429
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's proposed changes to storage charging study requirements in interconnection and reliability planning (BPM-015 updates and DPP process modifications) directly affect how pumped-storage and energy-storage projects are modeled, studied, and cost-allocated, touching both interconnection obligations and revenue exposure through queue treatment.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposed updates to BPM-015 (Business Practice Manual) to remove storage charging limitations in generator interconnection agreements (PAC-2024-3), replacing the Shoulder off-peak charging case with a Light Load charging case and limiting charging study phases to II and III of the Definitive Planning Process. Stakeholders raised concerns about how these changes interact with existing queue positions, cost exposure, and study treatment across all generators including storage. The changes do not affect DPP cycles already underway and are expected to begin with DPP-26 if approved by year-end; feedback is due May 20, 2026, and MISO will introduce language to the Planning Advisory Committee in Q3. Pumped-storage operators should scrutinize how Light Load charging assumptions affect their accreditation and cost allocation during interconnection studies, particularly for projects already queued or in mid-study phases.

title: Large Load Working Group (LLWG) - June 10, 2026
meeting_hydro_reason: The Large Load Working Group focuses on issues affecting large industrial consumers and demand-side participation, which typically does not overlap with hydropower and pumped-storage generation or market rules that govern hydro resource participation.

documents.title: 20260610 LLWG Item 05 Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation.pdf
documents.filename: 20260610 LLWG Item 05 Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's Zero-Injection GIA (ZGIA) proposal directly affects how co-located generation and load participate in capacity adequacy, resource registration, market offerings, and interconnection — touching revenue accreditation, market participation obligations, and strategic participation pathways for owners with on-site generation or hybrid assets.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes a Zero-Injection GIA framework to formalize market participation and interconnection for co-located generation serving on-site load at the same substation and voltage level. The proposal addresses interconnection planning (using familiar surplus/replacement/ERAS processes), resource adequacy accreditation (ZGIA resources offset only their associated local load's capacity obligation, accredited under current MISO rulesets and eligible for zonal resource credits limited by Schedule 53 Direct Loss of Load accreditation or local load obligation), market registration (independent metering and telemetry required; market resources must maintain offer limits ≤ local load demand; MISO will monitor injections and consider charges for overage), and transmission planning (ZGIA included as Non-Transmission Alternative in MTEP). Draft tariff language has been posted; formal feedback is requested from the PAC (pending) and LLWG (June 10, 2026), with a FERC filing planned for late June 2026. Most exposed are owners of on-site generation (including small hydro or pumped storage sited at industrial or utility facilities) seeking to participate as co-located resources or hybrid assets; the framework clarifies accreditation caps and injection limits that will govern revenue from capacity and energy markets.

documents.title: 20260610 LLWG Item 07 Firm Service Step Up Study Process.pdf
documents.filename: 20260610 LLWG Item 07 Firm Service Step Up Study Process.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large load interconnection and interim service step-up procedures materially affect hydro's strategic options for co-located load participation, transmission deliverability, and resource-adequacy obligations, and establish precedent for how RTOs structure partial-service pathways that could extend to storage and other dispatchable resources.
documents.ai_summary: MISO is formalizing a Firm Service Step Up process to allow large load customers to begin service on partial load blocks before full transmission upgrades are complete, using only interim available capacity. The process requires each partial load addition to pass NERC TPL-001 reliability studies (90-day cycle, quarterly submissions, first-ready-first-served prioritization) and mandates that the load-serving entity procure generation capacity in MISO's Planning Resource Auction to cover the interim load. Approved partial loads become effective at the start of the planning year and persist until the full load interconnection is operational; service is revoked if a load block fails to energize within 90 days of its approved date. For hydro and pumped-storage operators, this is strategically relevant: (1) co-located hydro can now supply large-load customers incrementally rather than waiting for full transmission readiness, shortening payback and improving investment returns; (2) the requirement that interim loads participate in the PRA means the resource-adequacy framework now extends to partial-service loads, establishing that generation must be procured at each step, a model that could define how storage and other flexible resources are accredited in segmented operations; (3) hydro deliverability and transmission-constraint management now explicitly account for multiple overlapping interim-service blocks, which affects zonal scarcity pricing and ramping-reserve demand. MISO requests stakeholder feedback on the prioritization methodology, submission granularity, study frequency, and generation-availability accounting by Monday, June 24, 2026, with tariff language to follow in August.

documents.title: 20260610 LLWG Item 01b Agenda.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO is addressing co-located load and generation participation rules, interconnection reliability requirements for large loads, and firm service standards that directly affect how hydro and other dispatchable resources can compete for large-load contracts and serve behind-the-meter applications.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group is advancing three initiatives: (1) a zero-injection GIA framework (PAC-2024-2B) governing market participation and registration of co-located load and generation, with tariff review scheduled for June 10, 2026; (2) interconnection reliability requirements (LLWG-2026-3) including ramp capability, ride-through, and stability standards for large loads, with transitional language, data/modeling, and ride-through requirements under review; and (3) a large load firm service step-up study (LLWG-2026-1) with study-process design details due June 10. These establish the rules under which conventional hydro and pumped-storage hydro can compete to serve large loads co-located at or behind load centers, and define the operational obligations (ramp, stability, ride-through) that such service requires. The firm-service framework may create new contracting or capacity-accreditation pathways for dispatchable resources, particularly in load-growth regions where large loads are interconnecting.

documents.title: 20260610 LLWG Item 06 Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements Presentation (LLWG-2026-3).pdf
documents.filename: 20260610 LLWG Item 06 Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements Presentation (LLWG-2026-3).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large load interconnection requirements directly affect hydropower and pumped-storage operators' strategic options for co-located load development, their transmission deliverability, and may impose new operational modeling/telemetry/ramp obligations on facilities with computational loads or adjacent generation.
documents.ai_summary: MISO is finalizing tariff language for Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements, targeting a June 30, 2026 filing. The framework distinguishes between Computational Loads (digitally driven, requiring advanced modeling, PMU, ramp, and ride-through requirements) and Non-Computational Loads (baseline data and telemetry only), with applicability triggered at 50 MW or expansions of 25+ MW. For hydro owners, this matters because (1) co-located data centers, cryptocurrency mining, and HPC workloads with which hydro may be sited or co-operate are now subject to detailed telemetry, PMU, dynamic modeling, and forecasting requirements that will flow upstream to the interconnecting hydro facility; (2) large-load definition and transition provisions now allow flexibility for in-flight projects, creating windows for strategic positioning; and (3) the distinction between computational and non-computational behavior may allow run-of-river or variable hydro paired with load to avoid some advanced-modeling burdens if properly characterized. Stakeholder feedback significantly shaped the revised language, particularly on applicability date, transition timelines, and load-type distinctions.

documents.title: 20260610 LLWG Item 06 MISO Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements_Technical Compendium Draft.pdf
documents.filename: 20260610 LLWG Item 06 MISO Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements_Technical Compendium Draft.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large-load interconnection reliability requirements establish grid-performance and operational-readiness obligations that will affect hydropower's ability to maintain system stability during rapid load-consumption changes and disturbances, and may constrain hydro's operational flexibility or impose new coordination/telemetry burdens; also signals that MISO's rapid load growth (data centers, AI computing) creates scarcity conditions that raise the market value of existing hydro's dispatchability and ramping capability.
documents.ai_summary: MISO published a technical compendium establishing reliability requirements for large transmission-connected loads (data centers, AI computing, crypto mining) that are growing rapidly across its footprint. The framework mandates telemetry and PMU requirements, data/modeling disclosures, short-term load forecasting, ramp-rate limits, voltage and frequency ride-through standards, and transient-stability and oscillation assessments for new large loads. This affects hydro owners in two ways: (1) large-load behavior—rapid changes in power consumption and sudden disconnections during disturbances—will drive more volatile and unpredictable demand patterns that hydropower must balance in real time, potentially increasing the operational value of hydro's ramping and regulation services; and (2) ride-through and stability rules imposed on loads may shift grid-stability burdens away from demand-side and back onto traditional generation (including hydro), creating new obligations for synchronous units. The document does not indicate a comment deadline or vote schedule; it appears to be a draft technical standard (marked as compendium draft) with stakeholder-engagement appendix noted but not shown in excerpt.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large load interconnection and co-located load/generation rules directly affect hydro owners' ability to serve loads behind the meter and their market participation pathways, especially for pumped storage and run-of-river resources seeking direct offtake arrangements.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group (LLWG) is developing policies on large load interconnection, firm service options, reserve products, and operational requirements (forecasting, network modeling, telemetry/PMU standards). A June 2026 agenda includes discussion of "Market Participation and Registration of Co-Located Load and Generation / Consideration of Zero-Injection GIA" (PAC-2024-4), which directly governs how hydro owners (especially those with on-site load or storage) register and participate in MISO markets. The group is also addressing "Large Load Firm Service Step Up" (LLWG-2026-1) and "Large Load Reserve Product Enhancements" (LLWG-2026-2), with draft tariff redlines and final recommendations expected by August–November 2026. These initiatives reshape the interconnection, market registration, and reserve-procurement framework for resources co-located with large loads—a key strategic pathway for PSH and run-of-river facilities seeking firm offtakes or behind-the-meter service.

documents.title: 20260610 LLWG Item 01c Action Items.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's Large Load interconnection and reserve-product enhancements directly affect how large hydro facilities and pumped-storage plants manage interconnection deliverability, participate in reserve markets, and compete with co-located loads for network access and capacity accreditation.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group is tracking three active tariff initiatives: (1) ZGIA proposed draft tariff (PAC-2024-2B), (2) Large Load Interconnection Reliability Requirements (LLWG-2026-3), and (3) Large Load Reserve Product Enhancements (LLWG-2026-2), with feedback deadlines stated as May 29, May 26, and June 4 respectively. The interconnection-reliability and reserve-product initiatives directly affect how large existing hydro plants and pumped-storage facilities must operate alongside new large loads competing for interconnection service and regional reserve procurement. Hydro owners with generation in MISO should monitor whether these rules tighten synchronous-machine requirements, alter reserve accreditation for dispatchable units, or create preferential treatment for co-located load that would displace wholesale-market revenue from grid-serving generation.

documents.title: 20260610 LLWG Item 01c Minutes 20260514.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: LLWG discussions of zero-injection GIAs, co-located load-and-generation participation models, and interconnection reliability requirements directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage resources can register, dispatch, and participate in MISO markets — a strategic option for existing and new hydro capacity.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Large Load Working Group approved a charter and reviewed draft tariff language for Zero-Injection Generator Interconnection Agreements (ZGIAs), which govern how co-located generation and load behind a single point of withdrawal participate in the MISO market. The proposal addresses resource adequacy, market participation, and reliable operations for large-load campuses with on-site or nearby generation (including hydro). MISO will post final tariff language prior to filing at end of June 2026, with business practice manual language expected Q3/Q4. The LLWG also discussed Interconnection Reliability Requirements (LLWG-2026-3). Hydro owners operating or developing capacity co-located with large loads should track this mechanism: ZGIAs and campus-level participation models create revenue pathways for hydro serving behind-the-meter load, but tariff design choices on scheduling, resource-adequacy accreditation, and storage state-of-charge treatment will determine whether existing flexibility value transfers to the co-located arrangement.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT energy-storage participation rules, frequency-response ancillary services, capacity/resource-adequacy mechanisms, and price formation — all cross-market design precedents for hydro and PSH — are under revision in multiple NPRRs on this agenda.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Protocol Revisions Subcommittee is reviewing six active proposals affecting storage and flexibility markets, plus ten tabled proposals. Most directly relevant to hydro: NPRR1326 (state-of-charge transparency in ancillary-services capacity monitor), NPRR1310 (dispatchable reliability reserves plus energy-storage participation and release factors — marked URGENT), NPRR1328 (generation firming program — marked URGENT), and NPRR1255 (energy-storage mitigation rules, addressing market power in dispatchable resources). NPRR1214 (reliability deployment price adder for locational signals and uplift reduction), tabled at PRS, represents a broader ERCOT pivot toward scarcity pricing and away from out-of-market uplift — a model relevant to PSH revenue treatment. Status: proposals are in review and revision phases; tabled items may be revisited. These NPRRs will set participation rules and price-formation mechanics that establish ERCOT precedent for cross-RTO storage participation design.

documents.title: Agenda_PRS_20260610
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT energy storage resource (ESR) mitigation, state-of-charge ancillary services rules, and dispatch participation mechanics directly parallel pumped-storage hydro design choices in other RTOs and merit cross-market monitoring.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Protocol Revisions Subcommittee meeting on June 10, 2026 includes several tabled and active proposals affecting energy storage participation: NPRR1326 adds state-of-charge (SOC) information to the Ancillary Services Capacity Monitor; NPRR1255 introduces mitigation of ESRs (energy storage resources); and NPRR1310 expands Dispatchable Reliability Reserve Service (DRRS) participation to ESRs with release factors. These rules govern how storage resources bid, accredit capacity, offer ancillary services, and interact with dispatch and pricing — mechanics that set precedent for pumped-storage treatment in RTOs benchmarking against ERCOT. The agenda lists multiple proposals tabled at PRS awaiting votes and review of revision-request language, with no comment deadline specified in the agenda itself.

The other items are listed without substance. Texas has no hydropower fleet, and while ERCOT market design is tracked for cross-market comparison, this document provides no analysis of mechanism, impact, or market-design principle; it is a procedural attendance and voting record.

documents.title: 05. NPRR1214_ERCOTComments_20260610 v6
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's proposed changes to NPRR 1214 directly affect price formation (LMP and AS MCPC determination) and indifference-payment settlement mechanics that would apply to any dispatchable or storage resource participating in a similar market structure; the pricing and settlement innovations are instructive cross-market comparison points for how RTOs handle reliability deployments and the distortion between dispatch (actual) and pricing-run (counterfactual) awards.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT proposes revisions to NPRR 1214 that eliminate energy and ancillary-service price adders by using SCED Pricing Run prices (LMPs and AS MCPCs) directly when a pricing run is active, rather than calculating adders as Pricing Run minus Dispatch Run prices. In parallel, ERCOT introduces an ancillary-services indifference payment (previously energy-only) using actual offer/bid curves instead of linear approximations, and extends indifference-payment consideration to RUC, RMR, and MRA resources whose real-time revenues were previously overlooked. The proposal also expands locational modeling of reliability deployments to Load and SOG resources under certain dispatch scenarios. Although ERCOT has no hydropower, the design—separating binding dispatch awards (from actual grid state) from binding prices (from hypothetical state absent reliability deployments), and compensating the resulting financial mismatch via indifference payments—illustrates an alternative settlement architecture that contrasts with how other RTOs (including those with hydro fleets) handle the same tension between dispatch realism and scarcity-signal accuracy.

documents.title: 05. NRR1214_ERCOT_Paper_20260610
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: ERCOT's proposed changes to reliability deployment indifference-payment calculation and locational modeling of out-of-market actions affect how energy and ancillary-service prices are set and how dispatchable/storage resources receive compensation when ERCOT invokes reliability deployments—a price-formation rule that sets precedent for how other RTOs (including hydro-rich markets) structure make-whole payments and scarcity pricing when operators override market dispatch.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT proposes modifications to NPRR1214 that change how indifference payments compensate resources for reliability deployments (out-of-market actions). Currently, ERCOT calculates energy and ancillary-service price adders to reflect the difference between dispatch-run and pricing-run market clearing; ERCOT proposes to eliminate those adders and instead use pricing-run LMPs and AS MCPCs directly when deployments are active, and to refine the indifference-payment formula to use SCED pricing-run prices rather than approximations. The change applies locational modeling to additional deployment categories (VECL, non-controllable load, must-run and capacity contracts). These modifications simplify settlement accounting and improve accuracy of make-whole payments for resources whose output is constrained by ERCOT dispatch overrides—relevant to storage and dispatchable hydro that compete for scarcity premiums and uplift when system operators invoke out-of-market controls. No vote date or comment deadline is stated in this draft (version 0.7, dated May 21, 2026); this is a technical comments document being developed for PRS review.

title: NEPOOL Markets Committee Meeting
meeting_hydro_reason: The NEPOOL Markets Committee addresses market design and tariff changes across all generation types including hydropower and storage resources, making it likely to contain content relevant to hydro/PSH participation rules, capacity accreditation, ancillary services, or resource adequacy.

issues.canonical_name: Capacity Auction Reforms
Order No. 2222

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A06 - Manuals - Order No. 2222 Conforming Changes and SATOA Conforming Changes - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: SATOA and Order 2222 conforming changes directly affect how pumped-storage hydro (and storage generally) registers, participates in energy markets, and settles; Order 2222 also opens DER aggregation pathways for small hydro.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes conforming updates to Manuals M-11, M-28, and M-RPA to implement Storage as Transmission-Only Asset (SATOA) revisions (effective September 1, 2026) and FERC Order 2222 requirements (effective November 1, 2026). The SATOA changes establish registration, metering, and settlement accounting for storage assets that operate as transmission-only resources on the 115 kV+ network, affecting how pumped-storage operators register, retire, update SATOA data, and account for injections and withdrawals in the settlement model. Order 2222 integration adds two new DER aggregation models (Demand Response DERA and Settlement Only DERA) into Manual M-11 market operations, creating new participation pathways for distributed and smaller hydro resources. The presentation previews the redlines without a formal comment deadline or vote date stated; next step is NEPOOL Markets Committee discussion and approval of the manual revisions.

issues.canonical_name: Order No. 2222

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A06 - Manuals - Order No. 2222 Conforming Changes - Manual 11 Revision 63
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Order 2222 conforming changes to ISO-NE Manual 11 establish DER aggregation participation rules that create a new pathway for small hydropower and pumped-storage resources to participate in wholesale markets, affecting revenue opportunities and operational obligations for distributed hydro assets.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE Manual 11 Revision 63 implements FERC Order 2222 conforming changes for the NEPOOL markets, establishing rules for distributed energy-resource (DER) aggregation participation. The document defines how small hydro, pumped storage, and other DERs can aggregate and bid into energy, capacity, and ancillary-services markets through a lead DER aggregator. Small and medium hydro owners in the ISO-NE footprint gain a new participation model that does not require individual resource registration, potentially lowering barriers to wholesale-market entry and creating revenue streams previously unavailable to sub-threshold hydro units. Manual 11 Revision 63 is being presented to the NEPOOL Markets Committee on June 9, 2026; the specific compliance deadline and effectiveness date are not stated in the extracted text.

issues.canonical_name: Order No. 2222

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A06 - Manuals - Order No. 2222 Conforming Changes - Manual 28 Revision 68
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 28 revision conforming to FERC Order 2222 affects DER aggregation participation rules, which establish pathways for distributed and small hydropower resources to participate in organized markets.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE Manual 28 Revision 68 implements conforming changes to Order 2222 requirements governing DER aggregation participation in the wholesale markets. Order 2222 creates a participation model for distributed energy resources and aggregators, which extends to small hydropower and run-of-river sites that would otherwise lack economic access to RTOs. The revision establishes technical and operational standards for how aggregated DER (including small hydro) can bid, dispatch, and settle in energy and ancillary-service markets. This mechanism expands the universe of hydro assets that can monetize their output — small facilities previously too small to qualify for direct ISO participation now gain a participation pathway through aggregation, though the detailed operational rules (telemetry, dispatch latency, state-of-charge tracking for any hybrid DER) will constrain how flexibly they can operate relative to bulk generators.

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE Manual RPA (Resource Participation Agreement) Revision 22 updates ISO-NE's tariff to conform to FERC Order 2222 requirements for DER aggregation participation. The manual revision governs how distributed energy resources, including small hydro, can aggregate and participate in wholesale markets under a single operating agreement. This is a compliance filing establishing the operational and settlement framework; no comment deadline or vote date is stated in the extracted title. Small hydropower owners considering aggregation as a path to ISO-NE market participation should review the revised manual for eligibility requirements, state-of-charge or operating rules specific to hydro, and settlement treatment of aggregate hydro facilities.

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04c - CAR-SA: Storage and Energy-Limited Resource Modeling and Accreditation Design Updates Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE proposes an accreditation adjustment formula for energy-limited and storage resources that directly affects how pumped storage and run-of-river hydro are valued in the capacity market, potentially increasing their accreditation values and revenue.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes a post-RAA accreditation adjustment for energy-limited and storage resources (including pumped storage and long-duration hydro) to account for a model limitation: the RAA model currently prohibits these resources from charging other storage during charging-constrained hours, understating their reliability value. The adjustment formula scales each resource's rMRI upward by a factor that grows with charging-constrained hours (15% winter, 1% summer in the Near-Term Base Case); for a hypothetical 1,000-hour storage resource with zero forced outage rate, the adjustment would boost accreditation from 0.85 (winter) to 1.0. The adjustment is projected to be finalized Q4 2026 with FERC filing and Q2 2027 effective date. Stakeholders should submit feedback to Jasleen Singh (jsingh@iso-ne.com) by June 24, 2026. Most exposed segment: pumped-storage operators and seasonal/long-duration hydro owners in ISO-NE's capacity market.

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A03a - DA/AS Post-Implementation Adjustments - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Day-ahead ancillary services price formation and demand-quantity adjustments directly affect the value of hydro's regulation and reserve products, and the strike-price floor methodology using combustion-turbine marginal cost creates a reference-level precedent for use-limited resource mitigation.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes targeted adjustments to its day-ahead ancillary services (DA A/S) design, effective October 22, 2026, based on February 2026 Internal Market Monitor recommendations. The changes include: (1) reducing the Forecast Energy Requirement Demand Quantity (FER DQ) by expected uncleared front-of-the-meter wind and solar supply to better reflect actual reliability needs; (2) setting a daily floor for the DA A/S strike price (K) based on the marginal cost of an efficient, distillate-fired combustion turbine, including regional emissions costs; and (3) establishing a $3/MWh floor on the Impact Test Threshold. For hydro operators, the strike-price floor methodology is the key mechanic—it ties ancillary-services scarcity pricing to a fossil marginal-cost reference, which will raise the opportunity cost against which hydro's must-offer obligation is evaluated and may increase procurement volume during scarcity events when hydro's regulation and reserve products clear at higher prices. The FER DQ reduction for variable wind/solar will also shift the composition of reliability-based demand, potentially favoring the fast-ramping and continuous-availability profile that hydro supplies. ISO-NE Markets Committee votes on these changes June 9–10, 2026, with Participants Committee vote scheduled June 16–18, 2026.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE proposes adjustments to day-ahead ancillary services market design—specifically non-performance factor, strike price floor, and forecast energy requirement demand—which directly affect the revenue hydro and other dispatchable resources receive for supplying reserves and flexibility.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes post-implementation adjustments to its Day-Ahead Ancillary Services market following the Internal Market Monitor's February 2026 review. The changes include: reducing the non-performance factor from 120% to 115% (already in effect April 30, 2026); adding a strike price floor tied to distillate-fired combustion turbine costs to reduce the range between short-run marginal costs and strike price; implementing a $3/MWh floor on the Impact Test Threshold to address over-mitigation risk; and reducing Forecast Energy Requirement Demand Quantity by forecasted uncleared wind and solar to better align day-ahead procurement with reserve adequacy analysis. The proposal also modifies Net Commitment Period Compensation to prevent ineligible imports from receiving the FER price. ISO-NE targets implementation October 22, 2026, and is requesting Markets Committee vote. Hydro operators supplying day-ahead ancillary services—especially those exposed to closeout cost risk when the strike price exceeds short-run marginal cost—will see adjusted revenue signals and reserve procurement levels; operators in ISO-NE's footprint should monitor the mechanics of the strike price floor and its impact on their day-ahead AS clearing economics.

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04l - CAR-SA: Initial Tariff Redlines - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Capacity Auction Reforms—Seasonal Accreditation (CAR-SA) deactivation tariff redlines fundamentally reshape how ISO-NE's capacity market accredits and clears seasonal capacity commitments, directly affecting how hydro resources (especially reservoirs managing seasonal supply) will be valued, offered, and potentially deactivated under the new framework.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is revising its tariff to implement seasonal capacity accreditation and deactivation rules as part of the broader Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA), with a proposed Q2 2027 effective date. The presentation covers initial draft tariff changes to conform deactivation processes from annual to seasonal Capacity Commitment Periods, including revisions to cost workbooks, conduct-test thresholds, portfolio benefit tests, and proxy capacity offer structures, all now indexed to seasonal Marginal Reliability Impact Capacity rather than annual metrics. Hydro owners—particularly those with multi-seasonal storage assets—will see their accreditation, offer-construction flexibility, and deactivation economics reframed around seasonal scarcity and clearing prices; affected segments include reservoir-based resources and pumped storage. The stakeholder schedule shows tariff review and committee votes pending through Q3 2026, with a FERC filing target in Q4 2026.

issues.canonical_name: Capacity Auction Reforms

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04k - CAR-SA: Capacity Bilaterals - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Capacity IBTs and exports rules directly affect how hydro and storage operators can hedge capacity-market revenues and manage export bids—both central to revenue optimization under ISO-NE's seasonal capacity market redesign (CAR-SA).
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes to implement Capacity Internal Bilateral Transactions (IBTs)—an ISO-administered financial instrument allowing participants to hedge capacity-market price risk by fixing capacity payments—and to simplify capacity-export rules under the seasonal Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA), effective Q2 2027. Under CAR-SA, capacity clearing occurs ~1 month before each 6-month season (vs. 40+ months ahead under the old FCM), shortening the forward-price visibility window and raising hedging demand. The Capacity IBT would allow a supplier and LSE to exchange fixed-for-floating swaps on capacity obligations, with the ISO settling the floating leg (pegged to seasonal clearing prices or CLO charge rates) and netting it within ISO settlement, while the fixed leg stays off-ISO books. Separately, ISO-NE would discontinue rarely-used export-bid curtailment exemptions (III.1.10.7(f) and III.15.8.1.2) and consolidate export functionality under simpler rules (III.1.10.7(i)), allowing capacity exporters to submit opportunity costs that determine MRIC eligibility. Hydro and PSH operators using export strategies or managing capacity revenues through bilateral hedges will see changes to both pricing instruments and export curtailment priority; the proposal suggests March 2027 implementation and is under stakeholder feedback.

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04j - CAR-SA: Interactions with PFP - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA) reshape accreditation methodology and Pay-for-Performance settlement mechanics — directly affecting capacity market revenue and performance-payment exposure for all resource types, including hydropower and pumped storage, through changes to the Balancing Ratio cap, CPP calculations, and seasonal stop-loss limits.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE presents three approaches to cap the Balancing Ratio (BR) under its seasonal capacity auction reform (CAR-SA), addressing a FERC order requiring the BR cap at 1.0 to prevent resources from being obligated beyond physical capability. The migration from Qualified Capacity to Marginal Reliability Impact (MRI) accreditation will reduce total capacity supply obligations system-wide, raising BRs and causing the 1.0 cap to bind more frequently, which shrinks Capacity Performance Payments (CPPs) to overperformers due to funding deficiencies. ISO-NE's preferred Approach 3 would cap the BR at a seasonal ratio tied to net maximum capability divided by net MRIC (1.355 in summer, 1.444 in winter), preserving stronger performance incentives for overperformance while complying with FERC's logic. Additionally, ISO-NE proposes conforming the annual PFP stop-loss limit to a seasonal market by capping seasonal losses at two times (instead of three times) a resource's maximum monthly net loss. Stakeholder feedback is due to Jasleen Singh (jsingh@iso-ne.com) by June 24, 2026, with ISO-NE targeting July for its final proposal. Large-duration and seasonal-flexibility resources (including pumped storage and multi-week-capable reservoirs) are most exposed, as their value in CAR-SA depends on accreditation during scarcity hours; how the BR cap settles will determine whether they receive full or reduced CPP rates during performance.

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04h - CAR-SA: Resource Qualification Process Follow Up - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAR-SA capacity accreditation methodology directly affects how hydro and storage resources qualify for and are compensated in ISO-NE's seasonal capacity market, including novel provisions for forced outages and ambient-air deratings that reshape the qualification process hydro owners must navigate.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE presented refinements to its Capacity Accreditation and Reconfiguration – Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) resource qualification process, effective Q2 2027. Three areas affect hydro and storage operators: (1) Ambient Air treatment allows thermal generators to offer de-rated MWs above the cost-offer threshold without justification—hydro owners need to track how this shapes capacity-market offer strategies for their thermal neighbors; (2) the 100 kW minimum MRIC requirement is retained, but small hydro and storage can meet it via aggregation as Distributed Energy Capacity Resources; (3) a new "unexpected full forced outage" provision lets resources remove themselves from the seasonal auction if they face season-long full outages, but only if the outage duration covers the entire six-month season and only before the seasonal auction offer window. The mechanism affects seasonal capacity revenue for any resource subject to forced outages (including hydro with unplanned failures) and clarifies qualification deadlines. Feedback deadline: June 24, 2026; next discussion at July Markets Committee on resource qualification follow-up and resource-attribute update processes.

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A03b- DA/AS Post-Implementation Adjustments - FirstLight Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Strike Price Floor in day-ahead ancillary services pricing directly affects hydro and pumped-storage revenue from reserves (regulation, spinning reserve); FirstLight's challenge to the Floor definition and cost–benefit tradeoff is a position-taking document on a tariff amendment in active discussion at ISO-NE Markets Committee.
documents.ai_summary: FirstLight opposes ISO-NE's proposed Strike Price Floor in the Day-Ahead Ancillary Services (DAAS) tariff amendment. The Floor would set a minimum day-ahead reserve clearing price based on an "efficient distillate-fired combustion turbine," but FirstLight argues the tariff language is too vague (heat rate, emissions cost index, fuel-price index unspecified), creates long-term investment uncertainty, and delivers poor cost–benefit: a 2% reduction in DAAS costs while reducing financial incentives for reserves in 41–47% of cleared resources during 50% of hours. FirstLight proposes instead striking the Floor language and replacing it with a filing-letter commitment to improve the Gaussian Mixture Model used to forecast real-time LMP. The document presents ISO-NE's own data showing the Floor would increase strike prices by an average of 238% outside winter months (Dec–Feb), with greatest impact during shoulder seasons when supply is tightest. No explicit vote or comment deadline is stated in this presentation.

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04a - CAR-SA: Transition Mechanism Introduction - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE's CAR-SA capacity accreditation reform directly alters how seasonal capacity values are calculated and compensated for all resources including hydro, shifting from Qualified Capacity to MRIC-based seasonal accreditation with a multi-year transition glide path that affects revenue stability and clearing outcomes.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes a Capacity Auction Reforms – Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) transition mechanism (target Q2 2027 effective date) that will gradually shift resource accreditation from current Qualified Capacity to proposed seasonal MRIC (Minimum Reliability Impact Contribution) values over two transition years (two summer and two winter seasons), with full phased design taking effect in Capacity Commitment Period 21. The transition uses a weighted-average approach: in Year 1, a 100 MW QC resource with 70 MW summer MRIC and 40 MW winter MRIC would be accredited at 90 MW (summer) and 80 MW (winter); by Year 3 it falls to the full MRIC floor (70/40 MW). Hydro owners will face material accreditation volatility during transition because MRIC values depend on seasonal performance during modeled Minimum Reliability Interest hours, potentially creating seasonal mismatches between what resources can offer and what demand curves procure. ISO-NE is still assessing demand-curve transition mechanics and whether to transition the winter gas constraint; comments and questions are due to Markets Committee Secretary Jasleen Singh (Jsingh@iso-ne.com) by June 24, 2026, with further stakeholder meetings scheduled through Q3 2026 before FERC filing.

issues.canonical_name: Capacity Auction Reforms

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04b - CAR-SA: Resource Accreditation Modeling cont. - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA) establish resource accreditation modeling that directly determines how much capacity hydro and storage resources can sell and at what value — the foundation for their RA revenue.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is modeling accreditation outcomes under its proposed CAR-SA design using a 70-year weather-adjusted load dataset (weighted subset of years 2000/01–2022/23 for winter; 2007–2023 for summer) to capture long-term weather variability and peak-load scenarios. Sensitivity analysis shows that explicitly modeling all 70 years of winter hourly loads produces nearly identical net ICR, reliability metrics (LOLE, LOLH, EUE), and accreditation results (rMRI) across all resource types — including Daily/Weekly Hydro (96.1–96.2%), Energy Storage (20.9–21.7%), and IPR-Hydro (59.9–61.0%) — compared to the ISO's proposed weighted-year approach. The ISO assessed and rejected FirstLight Resource Management's alternate block-based weighting methodology, concluding it ignores inter-block weather correlations and temporal heterogeneity, understating the likelihood of severe winters and distorting the upper tail of the load distribution where resource adequacy is most critical. The proposed framework will remain in place for CAR-SA implementation targeting Q2 2027; FirstLight's alternative approach was a stakeholder submission rather than ISO proposal.

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04h - CAR-SA: Non-Firm Gas Capacity Demand Curve Follow Up - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Capacity auction design for gas resources affects the demand curve methodology and clearing prices that determine system clearing in ISO-NE's capacity market, which directly impacts hydro's energy and capacity revenue through system-wide price signals and accreditation treatment of energy-limited resources.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes not to adjust the non-firm gas capacity demand curve in response to firm gas contracts, treating all firm contracts as fully incremental. The proposal is part of the Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA), with effective date proposed for Q2 2027 and expected filing in the coming months. This design prevents gas resources from artificially inflating capacity market clearing prices through uneconomic firm gas arrangements; however, it may result in procurement of more gas capacity than the actual gas available during tight system conditions. The proposal also links gas contract requirements (16-hour hourly minimum for December–February) to energy-limited resource accreditation methodology, meaning hydro and other energy-limited resources will be treated analogously to gas capacity with similar fuel-availability thresholds. Hydro owners in ISO-NE should monitor whether this gas-constraint treatment influences how seasonal accreditation is applied to conventional hydro and how winter capacity pricing evolves.

issues.canonical_name: Capacity Auction Reforms

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04m - CAR-SA: Seasonal Offer Construction and Mitigation - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: ISO-NE's Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR-SA) establish seasonal capacity-market offer formation rules and seller-side market power mitigation affecting how all resources, including hydropower and pumped storage, must construct and submit capacity bids, with direct implications for revenue recovery and mitigation risk.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE presented seasonal offer construction methodology and seller-side market power mitigation rules for its Capacity Auction Reforms – Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) proceeding. The ISO proposes applying a Capacity Offer Price Threshold (COPT) methodology conformed from the FCA's Dynamic Delist Bid Threshold, capped at 25–75% of net Net CONE, plus a pivotal supplier test that automatically reduces bids above the threshold if the supplier is deemed pivotal. For the first seasonal auctions in CCP19, the ISO will use an initial COPT of 37% of annual Net CONE (derived from historical FCA16-18 ratios) rather than prior-auction pricing. The presentation illustrates offer formulation for generalized resources and gas-constrained resources by showing how next-best-alternative analysis drives capacity bids — resources whose NBA is temporary exit must cover netGFC plus expected PFP costs, while resources profitable in energy markets offer only the PFP common-value component. Hydro operators with seasonal revenue constraints (e.g., run-of-river or PSH facing winter/summer energy-market disparities) will need to validate whether their avoidable-cost calculations and PFP recovery assumptions survive the pivotal supplier test; if mitigated improperly, FERC cost-recovery procedures are available. Status and next steps are not stated in this presentation; the proceeding remains in detail-design phase.

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04d - CAR-SA: FirstLight Power Alternative Load Weighting cont. - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: FirstLight Power proposes revised methodology for winter load-shape and weather-year selection in ISO-NE's Capacity Accreditation Resource (CAR) framework, directly affecting how energy-storage (especially pumped-storage hydro) accreditation is calculated and thus revenue under winter-peak capacity commitments.
documents.ai_summary: FirstLight Power presented a revised approach to winter load-shape and weather-year selection for ISO-NE's CAR-SA (Capacity Accreditation Resource – Storage Accreditation) at the June 2026 Markets Committee. The proposal revises FirstLight's earlier methodology to incorporate aspects of both its own and ISO-NE's original approaches: it targets 5-day blocks (not single days) to capture multi-day consecutive-cold-day effects critical to storage discharge modeling, and it optimizes fit across the entire load distribution rather than only the extreme tail (>95th percentile). FirstLight argues ISO-NE's current methodology over-represents frequency of extreme multi-day events and under-represents fit in non-extreme conditions, leading to material but not massive discrepancies in storage accreditation outcomes, with larger potential impacts under different risk splits or future resource-mix scenarios. The document shows no explicit next-step date or vote, but positions FirstLight as a technical stakeholder in an active accreditation-methodology debate directly affecting how pumped-storage hydro is credited for winter resource-adequacy showings.

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04f - CAR-SA: Impact Analysis, Market Clearing Preliminary Results - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAR-SA fundamentally redesigns ISO-NE's capacity auction accreditation and payment structure for seasonal resources, directly affecting hydro and storage revenue recognition, clearing prices, and capacity market participation rules.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is analyzing market-clearing outcomes under its proposed Capacity Auction Reforms–Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA), with preliminary results presented for three stress scenarios (Lower, Medium, Higher). The reform splits the annual capacity demand curve into separate summer and winter curves with distinct payment rates ($8.82/kW-day summer vs. $19.01/kW-day winter) and introduces a gas-supply constraint in winter clearing. Hydro and storage resources face altered accreditation formulas tied to seasonal peak contribution rather than annual ELCC, and the model shows winter price separation increases as non-firm gas resources displace other capacity. The ISO expects to present sensitivity analyses (80/20 risk split, alternative gas assumptions) in July; the proposed effective date is Q2 2027. Pumped-storage operators and seasonal-variable hydro owners should monitor how the seasonal split and storage accreditation methodology affect their cleared capacity and winter-vs.-summer revenue streams.

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A03 - DAAS Post-Implementation Adjustments - MA AGO Comments in Support
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Day-Ahead Ancillary Services market design changes directly affect reserve procurement costs and pricing — hydro's core revenue stream for regulation and reserve provision — through adjustments to reserve requirements, renewable-integration parameters, and strike-price thresholds.
documents.ai_summary: Massachusetts Attorney General's Office supports ISO-NE's proposed post-implementation adjustments to the Day-Ahead Ancillary Services (DA A/S) market, citing cost overruns in the market's first year (actual incremental cost $921–974 million vs. $140 million forecast). The proposals are: (1) reduce the Non-Performance Factor for Total Ten-Minute Reserves from 120% to 115% to right-size reserve quantities; (2) adjust the Forecast Energy Requirement downward to count full expected real-time Front-of-the-Meter renewable generation, not just cleared DA quantities; and (3) set a Strike Price floor based on efficient Combustion Turbine short-run marginal costs, which would better align with actual generator costs and reduce closeout risk. ISO-NE targets October 23, 2026 implementation. The adjustments would have reduced costs by an estimated $292 million (30% of incremental costs) if applied retroactively. These changes alter the supply curves and clearing prices for reserves hydro typically supplies, with greatest impact during low-load hours and periods of tight system conditions.

stakeholders.name: Massachusetts Office of the Attorney General

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04e - CAR-SA: Flatiron RAM cont. - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: ISO-NE's proposed resource accreditation model (rMRI methodology) directly affects capacity revenue for all resources by changing how marginal reliability contribution is calculated and how seasonal payment rates are set; the proposal fundamentally alters whether hydro and pumped storage can earn capacity payments based on their actual energy availability and flexibility value during extreme/scarcity conditions versus synthetic stress scenarios.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE and Flatiron RAM consulting present a proposal to align resource accreditation modeling with expected system conditions rather than synthetic 'at-criteria' stress scenarios in the Capacity, Accreditation, and Reliance (CAR-SA) initiative. The core concern is that current modeling assumptions over- or under-accredit resources (especially those whose value depends on energy availability in the GE MARS software), and risk paying for a resource mix optimized for conditions that never occur; Flatiron proposes accrediting based on 'expected clearing' or 'forecasted load' scenarios where system visibility is better and resource mix is known. The proposal includes three options to address situations with few simulated resource-adequacy events: load scaling to reliability targets, scaling based on forecasted load growth beyond the delivery year, or setting accreditation floors based on empirical data (e.g., EFORd) when simulated risk falls below threshold. The document flags that any marginal accreditation approach creates volatility and forecasting burden for market participants, since rMRI values are sensitive to resource mix, penetration, interactions, and load shape—factors outside individual participant control. Next steps and comment dates are not stated in this presentation excerpt.

documents.title: 2026-06-09-11 MC A04n - CAR-SA: AEU Conceptual Amendments - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ISO-NE's seasonal accreditation methodology directly affects how storage and flexible resources are valued in the capacity market, with specific proposals on weather-year weighting and intertie modeling that would alter the MRIC (Minimum Revenue Impacting Capacity) accreditation signal for pumped storage and other flexible resources.
documents.ai_summary: Advanced Energy United presents two conceptual amendments to ISO-NE's seasonal accreditation reform (CAR-SA). Amendment 1 argues ISO's weather-year weighting based on peak-load days overstates the frequency of sustained cold snaps, causing storage (especially pumped storage) to be disproportionately penalized in accreditation; CAU proposes weighting years by the frequency of multi-day cold-weather blocks instead. Amendment 2 contends ISO's flat import-capacity block for intertie modeling obscures when actual imports are available (often overnight, between MRI hours), understating storage's charging opportunities and thus its MRIC value; CAU proposes modeling hourly import-availability profiles tied to load shape. The submission also flags a potential inconsistency between ISO-NE's use of LOLE for resource adequacy but EUE for accreditation, which could systematically undervalue storage. Storage accreditation is directly material to investment signals and RA sufficiency in New England; these amendments are being advanced in the context of ISO-NE's broader seasonal accreditation reform process.

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE's Markets Committee approved post-implementation adjustments to the Day-Ahead Ancillary Services market and related tariff sections (III.3.2.1(q)(4), III.1.8.2, III.1.8.4, III.A.8.1.2, III.F.2.3.1.3) on June 9, 2026. FirstLight Power submitted an amendment requesting restoration of a strike-price floor in Section III.1.8, arguing against its removal in the ISO proposal; that amendment failed with only 20.83% support (Generation sector split 8.33% for / 8.33% opposed). The unamended main motion passed with 64.54% support. The strike-price floor affects how PSH offering strategies in day-ahead ancillary services are constrained; its removal reduces offer flexibility for storage resources that rely on floor protection to manage opportunity costs. The vote reflects sector disagreement — Generation (including hydro/PSH) largely opposed the unamended version, while Transmission and other sectors favored it.

documents.title: 1 Agenda
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's seasonal capacity accreditation (Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements) and must-offer rules directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage resources are credited toward RA obligations and when they can be dispatched.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Installed Capacity Working Group will discuss Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements (seasonal UDR/EDR accreditation), must-offer requirement changes, and ICAP Manual revisions on June 9, 2026. Seasonal accreditation mechanisms determine how much RA value hydro and PSH claim in winter versus summer, a critical lever for use-limited resources whose output is constrained by water availability. The meeting also covers must-offer obligations that force hydro units into certain market states. Potomac Economics will present highlights from the 2025 State of the Market Report, which typically includes performance assessments of capacity accreditation models. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in the agenda.

documents.title: 2 Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements – Seasonal UDR/EDR Elections and Must Offer Requirement: ICAP Manual and Attachment Revisions (Incremental Changes)
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO capacity market design adds seasonal must-offer requirements and UDR/EDR elections that directly affect external resource commitments and penalties for underutilized capacity rights — a novel compliance obligation touching revenue and strategic flexibility for hydro and storage resources holding deliverability rights.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO proposes seasonal (Summer/Winter) elections for Unforced Capacity Deliverability Rights (UDRs) and External-to-Rest-of-State Deliverability Rights (EDRs) starting with the 2027-2028 Capability Year (effective May 1, 2027), filed with FERC February 18, 2026 in Docket ER26-1431 with requested effective date June 28, 2026. Holders must submit separate seasonal return-to-NYCA elections by August 1 prior to each capability year; capacity associated with external UDRs/EDRs not returned must be offered in ICAP spot auctions or certified for LSE compliance, with sanctions for failure — including a revised must-offer penalty calculated monthly. This reframes deliverability-rights holders' compliance posture: seasonal elections create a new obligation to commit or forfeit, converting a flexible holding into a binary choice with penalties. Pumped storage and large reservoir hydro holding external rights face the most direct impact; small hydro with external positions must track August elections and ensure compliance or incur monthly penalties.

documents.title: 2c ICAP Manual Appendix Att. Q Incremental Edits (single redline)
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ICAP Manual updates governing NYISO capacity accreditation, external import rights, and deliverability for hydro and storage resources affect revenue recognition and participation mechanics for NHA members.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO Capacity Market Products has issued incremental edits to ICAP Manual Appendix Q, updating maximum allowances for external installed capacity and deliverability rights for 2025–2027. The document revises ICAP allocation limits for neighboring control areas (PJM, ISO-NE, Ontario, Quebec), details Unforced Capacity Deliverability Rights (UDRs) for major transmission interfaces including 1,250 MW Champlain Hudson Power Express (Hydro-Quebec to NYC Zone J) and 660 MW Neptune Cable (PJM to Long Island), and specifies External-to-ROS Deliverability Rights (EDRs). Hydro and storage resources in NYCA seeking external-area capacity credits or import rights must navigate these transmission-constrained allocations; the inclusion of intermittent-resource and run-of-river translation factors (Attachment N) and energy-limited resource procedures (Attachment M) indicates the manual also governs accreditation mechanics for variable hydro. This is a draft for discussion; no comment deadline or vote date is stated in the excerpt.

documents.title: 2c ICAP Manual Appendix Att. Q Incremental Edits (with highlighting)
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO capacity market rules for external imports and deliverability affect hydro's ability to export power (especially Quebec HQ and Northeast hydro) into New York and its accreditation revenue under ICAP; the manual specifies UDRs and EDRs that govern reservoir hydro export paths and seasonal capacity allocations.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO Capacity Market Products issues an updated ICAP Manual Appendix Q reflecting 2025–2026 and 2026–2027 maximum external installed-capacity allocations by control area and interface, including grandfathered and conditional rights. Key routes for hydro exports: Quebec via Chateauguay (1,110 MW of External CRIS Rights, with seasonal variation November–April); Hydro-Quebec Cedars Interface (80 MW EDR to Rest of State Zone D); Champlain Hudson Power Express from HQ (1,250 MW UDR to Zone J); Cross Sound Cable from New England (330 MW UDR to Zone K). The document is in draft form for discussion by the Installed Capacity Working Group (meeting 2026-06-09) with highlighted revisions from the 2026-05-27 version. Changes affect which hydro suppliers can qualify for capacity revenue and at what seasonal limits into high-value zones (NYC Zone J, Long Island Zone K); owners exporting Quebec or run-of-river Northeast hydro must track these allocations and any ICAP auction implications.

documents.title: 2b ICAP Manual Incremental Revisions (single redline)
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO is revising its Installed Capacity Manual to update accreditation rules for co-located storage resources, energy-duration limitations, and DER aggregation — all direct mechanisms for how pumped storage and hybrid hydro resources are valued and accredited in the capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Installed Capacity Manual (Manual 04) is being revised with incremental changes effective April 2, 2026, after BIC acceptance on March 18, 2026. Key sections touch energy-duration limitations and duration-adjustment factors for ICAP suppliers (4.1.1), dual-participation rules (4.1.2), DER and aggregation provisions (4.1.3), and dedicated treatment for co-located storage resources (4.1.4). For pumped storage and hybrid hydro owners, the document signals that NYISO is codifying distinct accreditation pathways for storage paired with generation: co-located resources face specific DMNC/DMGC testing requirements (4.2), operating-data reporting obligations (4.4.10), bidding procedures (4.8.7), and capacity-supply calculations that differ from standalone generators. Storage owners should review sections 4.1.1 (duration factors that constrain RA value) and 4.1.4 (state-of-charge and co-location rules) for direct impact on accreditation and revenue; the manual's treatment of energy-limited resources and intermittent resources also establishes the framework by which run-of-river hydro is accredited.

documents.title: 2a ICAP Manual Incremental Revisions (with highlighting)
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO ICAP manual revisions directly govern unforced capacity accreditation, duration adjustment factors for energy-limited resources (hydro's core issue), co-located storage rules, and operating data/certification requirements — all determinants of how much capacity revenue hydro and PSH can claim.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO has revised its Installed Capacity Manual (version 17.0, effective 04/02/2026, Committee Acceptance 03/18/2026 BIC), with incremental changes highlighted as of the 06/09/2026 ICAPWG meeting. The manual governs minimum unforced capacity requirements for LSEs, accreditation calculations for ICAP suppliers, duration adjustment factors for energy-limited resources, and co-located storage participation rules. Key sections address DMNC (Demonstrated Minimum Net Capacity) testing procedures, operating data reporting requirements (including for municipally owned generation and intermittent/energy-limited resources), and provisions for co-located storage resources — all mechanisms that directly affect how much capacity hydro and pumped-storage facilities can supply and at what accreditation level. The revisions appear to be in draft/discussion phase as of the June 2026 working group meeting; the document provides the current manual text with yellow highlighting indicating changes from the prior 05/27/2026 version.

documents.title: 3 Highlights from the 2025 State of the Market Report for the NYISO Markets
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO Market Monitoring Unit analysis of energy price formation, ancillary-services co-optimization, shortage pricing (ORDC), reserve-requirement allocation, and demand-response settlement rules that directly affect hydro revenue through energy and reserve pricing, uplift mechanisms, and locational scarcity signals.
documents.ai_summary: Potomac Economics (NYISO's Market Monitoring Unit) presents highlights from the 2025 State of the Market Report addressing energy and ancillary-services market performance. Key findings: energy prices rose 66–79% in 2025 due to higher gas prices, peak loads, reduced OOM commitment, increased exports to Quebec, and reduced hydro availability; the MMU identifies $221M in misallocated costs annually, including $40M from statewide allocation of local reserve charges and $38M from largest contingencies not paying their impact on reserve requirements; the MMU recommends urgent reforms to shortage pricing (Rec #2017-2: raising NYCA 30-min ORDC to $2000/MWh up to 150% of largest contingency) to prevent economically unjustified exports to ISO-NE and PJM, and to dynamically adjust reserve requirements and model locational constraints in day-ahead commitment. Hydro operators benefit from higher scarcity pricing and better locational reserve signals, but face exposure to large downward corrections if demand-response baseline issues and cryptocurrency-miner over-consumption (paying full LMP despite uneconomic curtailment) are fixed; the report documents $34M annual uplift from paying demand response as if it generates electricity, which competes with traditional ramping and flexibility revenue.

Attachment GGG modifications and the DPP study cadence directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage projects navigate the queue, secure deliverability, and compete with other resources for interconnection positions. The agenda does not specify which segments are most exposed or include comment deadlines, but the tariff redlines phase indicates active rule-setting; hydro owners should monitor the next IPWG meeting (July 21, 2026) for substantive tariff proposals and any formal comment windows.
