documents.ai_summary: MISO launched the Generation Interconnection Application Automation (GIAA) portal in November 2025 and is executing incremental releases (April, May 2026, with July/September/November 2026 planned) to improve application processing automation, data migration from the legacy Grid Unity system, and user experience. The portal now supports Surplus and Replacement applications, Inverter-Based Resource (IBR) uploads, and expanded transformer and Point of Interconnection voltage-level fields. Known issues include file-size limitations, lack of project-list sorting/filtering, and unclear user-type account options—problems that can slow application review or create friction for interconnection customers (generators including hydro and pumped-storage developers). A dedicated product team was established March 1, 2026, with ongoing focus on bug resolution and data cleanup from the legacy system. Generators relying on timely queue processing should monitor the portal's usability and watch for the next release cycle (tentatively July 18, 2026) to assess whether validation and automated-review improvements reduce application review delays.

documents.title: 20260609 IPWG Item 04 Att GGG (PAC-2024-6)_redlines.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's Merchant HVDC Transmission Connection Procedures (MHCP) govern interconnection of external HVDC lines to the transmission system, establishing study, cost-allocation, and injection-rights processes that directly affect deliverability and transmission-access economics for hydro resources seeking to interconnect or export across MISO's footprint.
documents.ai_summary: MISO has posted redlined revisions to Attachment GGG (Merchant HVDC Transmission Connection Procedures) for its June 9, 2026 IPWG meeting. The document defines the terms, scope, and procedural framework for entities seeking to interconnect merchant HVDC transmission lines to MISO's transmission system, including definitions of Connection Facilities, Network Upgrades, Injection Rights, and cost-allocation mechanisms (Common Use Upgrades, Multi-Party Facilities Construction Agreements). While the excerpt provided contains only definitions and scope language (effective July 19, 2018), redlines indicate updates are under review. Hydro owners and independent power producers using HVDC interconnection for long-distance export of generation—particularly from low-hydropower-density regions into MISO or across MISO seams—are directly affected by the study timelines, upgrade cost-sharing rules, and injection-rights certification procedures these procedures establish.

documents.title: 20260609 IPWG Item 04 Attachment GGG Improvements (PAC-2024-6).pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's clarifications to the MHVDC interconnection procedure (Attachment GGG) affect how non-MISO generation, including hydropower and pumped-storage resources, can interconnect and inject power into MISO — touching deliverability, interconnection pathways, and the cost-recovery treatment of network upgrades that directly impact project economics.
documents.ai_summary: MISO proposes final tariff and business-practice-manual changes to Attachment GGG (MHVDC Interconnection Procedure) to clarify the Module B transmission-service injection pathway as an alternative to Attachment X external Network Resource Interconnection Service, distinguish between Necessary Upgrades and Network Upgrades cost recovery, and update the Multi-Party Facility Construction Agreement. Stakeholders in the April 28 IPWG meeting expressed general support but raised concerns about the scope of Necessary Upgrades in connection studies and cost-recovery pathways for Module B versus Attachment X. MISO plans to file the first tariff change to FERC within 30 days (introducing Limited Operations provisions and clarifying injection/withdrawal-rights scope) and a second filing in Q3–Q4 2026 (updating application forms and clarifying Module B as injection-only, not restricted to External NRIS). Most affected are outside generators—including remote hydropower and pumped-storage facilities seeking to export into MISO—for whom these pathway and cost-allocation rules determine interconnection feasibility and return on investment.

documents.title: 20260609 IPWG Item 02 Liaison Report.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's interconnection queue reform, DPP cycle scheduling, and Attachment GGG improvements directly affect interconnection timelines and cost-recovery pathways for all generators including hydropower and pumped-storage projects seeking network access.
documents.ai_summary: MISO reports progress on Attachment GGG improvements (PAC-2024-6) addressing cost-recovery clarifications for Necessary vs. Network upgrades, with stakeholder feedback incorporated and PAC review pending. The DPP 2026 cycle has an application deadline of September 10, 2026, with a $7,600 application fee (recalculated triennially for inflation); study-cycle kick-off is December 9, 2026. MISO's restructured Resource Planning Team consolidates interconnection project management, DPP studies, ERAS studies, and tariff administration, with new roles in surplus interconnection and replacement-generation queuing. Hydro and PSH owners in MISO should flag the September 10 DPP deadline and monitor Attachment GGG clarifications on how Network-upgrade costs are recovered when their projects trigger system improvements.

documents.title: 20260609 IPWG Item 01b Management Plan.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: FERC Order 2023 compliance and interconnection queue management directly affect hydro and PSH owners' ability to build or uprate plants and the timing/cost of grid interconnection, a core strategic and operational lever.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Interconnection Process Working Group (IPWG) is tracking multiple Order 2023 implementation workstreams: BPM-015 (project-ownership-transfer rules), interconnection queue-cap mechanics (PAC-2023-1), Attachment GGG improvements affecting study timelines and cost allocation, and electromagnetic-transient (EMT) system-strength screening for inverter-based resources. The group is also implementing SUGAR (presumably Standardized Unified Generator Aggregation Review) to standardize application-model data and reviewing the MISO/SPP Joint Operating Agreement. Order 2023 penalty rules are scheduled for October 2026 discussion. For hydro and PSH owners in MISO, these queue and study-process reforms directly shape interconnection lead times, uprate feasibility, and the cost of surplus interconnection service — and the June 9, July 21, October 13, and December 8, 2026 meetings are the immediate decision gates. Small and run-of-river hydro seeking DER-aggregation pathways (Order 2222) should monitor queue-standardization and portal-product updates.

documents.title: 20260609 IPWG Item 01c Action Items.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Interconnection Process Working Group action items track interconnection queue reform, IBR conformance requirements, and deliverability standards that directly affect hydro plant interconnection, upgrade pathways, and operational compliance obligations.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Interconnection Process Working Group closed three action items as of June 9, 2026: feedback on Attachment GGG interconnection improvements (PAC-2024-6), follow-up on Inverter Based Resource (IBR) performance and modeling conformance with OEM compliance by June 1, 2026 (status update issued May 11), and administrative updates to the SUGAR implementation slides. The IBR conformance deadline is most material: it sets modeling and performance standards that may establish precedent for or constrain how energy-storage and hybrid hydro resources qualify in MISO's interconnection process. The Attachment GGG improvements (status closed by May 12) likely address queue reform, surplus interconnection, or interconnection service rules that directly affect upgrade economics and delivery timelines for conventional hydro uprates and new PSH interconnections. Hydro owners should monitor MISO filings for final Attachment GGG language and IBR conformance criteria to assess impact on their own interconnection applications and existing plant compliance obligations.

documents.title: 20260609 IPWG Item 03b DPP Study Schedule.pdf

documents.ai_summary: MISO's Distributed Project Portfolio Study Schedule (version 5/1/2026) shows systematic delays across multiple study cycles through 2027, driven primarily by stability-model audits, transmission-operator (TO) mitigation delays from Ameren, PPI, and NIPSCO, and cascading effects from the 2022 Phase 2 cycle. DPP cycles are progressing through DPP 2 and DPP 3 phases with GIA execution dates extending into 2027; for example, DPP-2022 Central (121 projects, 23,908 MW) is delayed 3–7 calendar days monthly due to stability analysis and mitigation delays. The schedule notes that MISO's Reduced GIP Timeline (effective March 15, 2022) applies to all active cycles, and final reports post 15 business days before completion dates. For hydro and storage interconnection customers, these delays directly extend the time to GIA execution and commercial operation; projects queued in 2021–2025 cycles should monitor their specific region (Central, East ATC/ITC, South, West) and track whether stability or TO-mitigation bottlenecks are the binding constraint on their advancement.

documents.title: 20260609 IPWG Item 01c Minutes 20260428.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's interconnection queue reforms (ERAS, Surplus Interconnection, Generator Replacement) and IBR modeling/system-strength screening directly affect hydro and PSH developers' ability to interconnect, study timelines, and grid-interaction requirements for new and uprated plants.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's Interconnection Process Working Group meeting on April 28, 2026, covered three interconnection-related initiatives affecting all generators: (1) Expedited Resource Addition Study (ERAS) program has 53 of 68 allowed projects active, with Cycles 1–2 complete and Cycle 3 ongoing; (2) Surplus Interconnection and Generator Replacement rules received FERC approval April 14, 2026, with new $150,000 study-deposit amounts effective immediately, and BPM-015 redlines pending finalization; (3) IBR Performance, Modeling, and Conformance (PAC-2024-2) is developing system-strength screening for electromagnetic-transient (EMT) studies to target weak-grid interconnection cases as IBR penetration grows. For hydro and PSH, the Surplus Interconnection approval and revised deposit structure directly lowers barriers to uprates and replacement generation at existing hydroelectric sites; ERAS eligibility and study-cycle progression affect delivery timelines for new run-of-river and reservoir projects. IBR modeling requirements (June 1, 2026, deadline) impose conformance obligations on synchronous machines co-located or competing for queue position, though hydro's synchronous generator status may exempt traditional plants from inverter-specific rules. Feedback on Attachment GGG MHVDC improvements was due May 12, 2026; stakeholders should track the June 2026 BPM posting and Q3–Q4 FERC filings.

Key projects affecting hydro and storage include: 5-Minute Transaction Scheduling with HQ (expanding real-time interchange frequency to improve price convergence and flexibility); Balancing Intermittency Phase 2 (evaluating duration in reserves, directly relevant to PSH scheduling); Dynamic Reserves Phase 1 (reviewing cost recovery for operating-reserve suppliers); Storage-as-Transmission (broadening PSH use cases); Capacity Zone Redesign and Enhancing Locational Pricing of Capacity (affecting hydro siting value); Seasonal ICAP Market Improvements and ICAP Demand Curve Reset (capacity accreditation); FERC Order 2222 Compliance (DER/hybrid participation); Voltage Support Service for Inverter-Based Resources (expanding ancillary-service procurement); and Energy Market Mitigation screening enhancements. documents.title: 3 2027 Proposed Enterprise Projects
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Projects 4 (Activate VSA Application), 14 (Demand Forecasting Machine Learning), 40 (REFIRE), and 48 (Uncertainty Response Tool) touch hydro-specific market operations (variable-resource forecasting, advanced dispatch tools, energy-market modeling) that affect real-time co-optimization, flexibility-product procurement, and settlement accuracy — core revenue and compliance domains for hydro operators in NYISO.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Budget & Priorities Working Group is evaluating 49 proposed Enterprise projects for 2027, including four that directly affect hydropower market participation and dispatch: (1) Activate VSA Application (Energy Markets, deployment phase); (2) Demand Forecasting Machine Learning (Operations & Reliability, study complete); (3) Renewable Energy Forecast Integration and Reporting Enhancements / REFIRE (Operations & Reliability, software design phase); and (4) Uncertainty Response Tool / URT (Operations & Reliability, $0.45M, software design phase). These projects improve forecasting accuracy for variable renewable and hydro resources, real-time dispatch modeling, and uncertainty quantification — directly affecting how hydro's flexibility and ramping capability are valued in energy and ramping-product markets, and how settlement of forecast-error charges is computed. The committee will conduct cost revisions and NYISO scoring on June 24, 2026, and stakeholder presentations and advocacy are scheduled through August; final project prioritization and NYISO budget recommendation are due to the Markets Committee on October 5, 2026, with Board approval on November 16, 2026. Hydro operators should monitor how these tools are designed to avoid systematic undervaluation of hydro flexibility and overcharging of hydro operators for forecast error.

documents.title: 2027 NYISO Project Prioritization Survey
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO is soliciting prioritization of six 2027 projects including one directly affecting hydro/PSH transmission capability (5-minute interchange scheduling with Hydro-Québec) and others touching DER aggregation, demand response baselines, and capacity market rules that create revenue and compliance precedent for storage and flexible resources.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's 2027 project-prioritization survey solicits stakeholder input on six initiatives, due July 15, 2026. Project 1 (5-Minute Transaction Scheduling) expands a 2020 study to develop market rules and mechanisms for scheduling interchange with Hydro-Québec at five-minute frequency, aligning with internal real-time dispatch and improving price convergence and ramping flexibility — directly increasing hydro's strategic options in cross-border trade and within-hour balancing. Project 3 (Aggregation System Integration) streamlines DER and aggregation enrollment processes via API integration, supporting Order 2222 participation pathways for small hydro. Project 4 (Demand Response Baseline Alignment) investigates whether demand-response baselines can be net suppliers of ICAP capacity, following MMU recommendations — the rules examined also govern how DSRs and storage interact with capacity markets. Project 5 (Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers) proposes automatic resale of unforced-capacity surplus to reduce market-clearing prices. These collectively reshape how hydro and storage interact with capacity, flexibility, and cross-border revenue mechanisms; survey responses and comments will be made public and posted with Budget & Priorities Working Group materials.

The list includes projects directly material to hydropower: 'Dynamic Reserves – Review Operating Reserve Supplier Cost Recovery' (energy market, touching reserve product revenue), 'Improve Capacity Accreditation and Resource Adequacy Modeling' (capacity market, affecting hydro's ICAP qualification), 'Enhancing Locational Pricing of Capacity' (locational signals for hydro dispatch value), and 'Balancing Intermittency Phase 2: Evaluating Needs for Duration in Reserves (ENDURE)' (duration/flexibility product design, central to hydro's operational edge). NYISO has also flagged a forthcoming mandatory project responding to FERC's Large Load Interconnection rulemaking (order expected end of June 2026), which will reshape hydro's strategic options for co-located load serving and baseline accreditation. Hydro owners should prioritize survey response by July 15, 2026, and track the mandatory project scope once FERC rules, as large-load interconnection rules will affect hydro's participation pathways and capacity value in the coming year.

documents.title: 20260608 MISO-PJM IPSAC Agenda
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO-PJM interregional transmission planning and FERC Order 1920 implementation directly affect hydro deliverability, interregional transfer capability, and market-seam governance—all strategic options for hydro owners operating across or exporting from the MISO-PJM footprint.
documents.ai_summary: The MISO-PJM IPSAC agenda covers three topics: (1) a Targeted Market Efficiency Project Study update, (2) MISO-PJM FERC Order 1920 compliance progress, and (3) interregional transmission planning status. FERC Order 1920 requires improved interregional coordination and transmission planning between RTOs; implementation here affects how hydro resources in or serving the MISO-PJM seam can compete for capacity, how interregional energy flows are priced and scheduled, and whether transmission constraints limit hydro export capability. The June 8 meeting includes verbal reports on these items with no public comment period advertised in this agenda, but the transmission-planning update and Order 1920 progress are material to hydro owners evaluating long-term positioning in the MISO-PJM region and to any with portfolio exposure across the seam.

documents.filename: 20260608 MISO-PJM IPSAC Item 01 TMEP Study Update.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: TMEP (Transmission and Market Enhancement Projects) study identifies and evaluates interregional transmission solutions between MISO and PJM, directly affecting the deliverability and revenue opportunity for hydro and storage resources at the seam.
documents.ai_summary: MISO and PJM are conducting a coordinated Transmission and Market Enhancement Projects (TMEP) study to address ~$681 million in persistent congestion on Market-to-Market flowgates between the two RTOs (2024–2025 data). The study, initiated following a March 13, 2026 Issues Review IPSAC decision, will evaluate 23 flowgate candidates and assess whether non-transmission solutions (operator adjustments, market redesigns, or asset-owner flexibility) can relieve binding constraints more cost-effectively than capital projects. Study steps run June–November 2026, with outcomes informing which interregional bottlenecks impede hydro export capability and which may be solvable via operational or market changes rather than new wires. For hydro owners with resources near the MISO-PJM seam, the study outcomes determine whether transmission constraints limiting their revenue will be addressed via infrastructure investment, demand-response/flexibility products, or remain as persistent opportunity costs. The next IPSAC meeting is scheduled for August 17, 2026.

documents.title: 20260608 MISO-PJM IPSAC Item 03 Interregional Transmission Planning Status Update
documents.filename: 20260608 MISO-PJM IPSAC Item 03 Interregional Transmission Planning Status Update.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Interregional transmission planning between MISO and PJM directly affects hydro and PSH deliverability, interregional transfer capability, and strategic options for resources sited in or serving both RTOs, particularly regarding long-range adequacy, benefit-metric development for transmission that may indirectly influence scarcity pricing and capacity accreditation frameworks.
documents.ai_summary: MISO and PJM are jointly developing an expanded interregional transmission planning framework to overcome structural limitations in their Joint Operating Agreement's ability to identify and evaluate interregional solutions, with emphasis on long-range scenario-driven benefit identification under FERC Order 1920. They are stress-testing three benefit metrics (reliability, production-cost, and extreme-weather benefits) through an Interregional Transmission Coordinated Study (ITCS), with a compliance filing expected in Q4 2026 and ongoing coordination of planning assumptions and horizons across MTEP/RTEP cycles. This effort is material to hydro and PSH owners in the MISO-PJM seam because transmission expansion projects evaluated under the new framework could alter interregional transfer limits, change the economic value of storage flexibility across the seam, and shape how long-duration or seasonal hydro resource adequacy is credited in joint reliability studies — owners should monitor the Q4 2026 filing and the benefit-metric definitions MISO and PJM adopt, as they may reframe how interregional hydro exports and PSH cyclic operation are valued in future planning.

documents.filename: 20260608 MISO-PJM IPSAC Item 02 FERC Order 1920 Interregional Compliance - Correction.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: FERC Order 1920 interregional long-term transmission planning compliance will reshape how MISO and PJM coordinate on transmission expansion, directly affecting interregional deliverability of hydro and pumped-storage resources and their strategic transmission-planning participation opportunities.
documents.ai_summary: MISO and PJM are implementing FERC Order 1920/1920-A through two-phase compliance: Phase 1 (regional planning, MISO June 12, 2026; PJM Dec. 12, 2025) and Phase 2 (interregional coordination, due Dec. 12, 2026). The RTOs assert their existing long-term regional planning processes (MISO LRTP, PJM Long-Term Planning) and interregional procedures are generally compliant; key reforms include explicit long-term transmission-needs posting, joint evaluation of interregional facilities, and allowance for any entity to propose interregional projects within regional planning processes. Stakeholder review (redlines July–August 2026, regional committee engagement through October–November 2026) precedes the December 2026 FERC filing. For hydro owners, interregional facility eligibility and transparent cost-benefit evaluation of cross-regional transmission solutions directly affect how PSH and reservoir hydro assets can be sited and valued for export; the expanded entity-proposal rights create a formal pathway for hydro interests to nominate interregional transmission solutions to meet long-term needs, with December 12, 2026 as the RTO filing deadline and stakeholder feedback windows in July–October 2026.

documents.title: Draft Final Proposal - Interconnection Service Capacity and Deliverability Retention for Non-Operating Generating Facilities - May 11, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Establishes mandatory timelines and conditions for retaining interconnection service capacity and deliverability rights when hydroelectric or pumped-storage facilities cease operations, mothball, or repower — directly affecting whether a hydro owner can preserve its grid position and capacity value during outages or strategic transitions.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Draft Final Proposal (May 11, 2026) codifies rules requiring non-operating generating facilities to either return to service, enter the generator interconnection queue, or begin repower construction within specified timeframes to retain deliverability allocations and interconnection service capacity rights; failure to act within these windows forfeits the rights, releasing capacity back to the system. MECHANISM: The proposal creates a hard-deadline retention regime that affects hydro owners planning extended maintenance, mothballing, or repowering. Facilities reaching physical derate (reduced maximum output) lose deliverability commensurate with the loss if the derate exceeds 3 years; those exiting wholesale markets to serve behind-the-meter load are treated as permanent retirements triggering reliability reviews and potential RMR (Reliability Must Run) obligations. SCOPE: All hydroelectric resources in CAISO territory, particularly those contemplating maintenance windows beyond 3 years, repower projects, or transitional mothball periods, are exposed. Generators choosing not to follow notification processes forfeit their rights, and the proposal strengthens ISO enforcement of 90-day advance notice and retirement affidavits. Next step: Draft Final Proposal status indicates Board action is pending (typical CAISO timeline ~Q3 2026); stakeholders should monitor for final tariff vote and implementation timeline.

documents.ai_summary: PJM and Constellation jointly proposed a SATA Phase II solution, with Monitoring Analytics also presenting a competing solution proposal, both reviewed at the May 7, 2026 Operating Committee meeting. The Operating Committee will vote to endorse one or both proposals at the next meeting (June 4, 2026). SATA defines how pumped-storage hydro and other storage can be compensated and operated when functioning as a transmission asset rather than a conventional generator or ancillary-services provider—this directly affects PSH revenue streams and the strategic choice between traditional dispatch and transmission-service roles. Pumped-storage owners in PJM should monitor the June endorsement vote and any tariff filings that follow, as the Phase II framework will govern long-term cost recovery and operating obligations for storage deployments.

documents.title: Item 04 – 1 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's revision to Manual 14d establishes binding reactive power control requirements for all generators, including hydroelectric units, affecting their compliance obligations and operational constraints on voltage support capability provision.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14d Section 7.1.2 to clarify and strengthen reactive power (voltage support) control requirements for all generators in its footprint, driven by NERC's May 2026 lowering of IBR registration thresholds to 20 MVA and PJM's identification of increasing low-voltage risk (citing the 2025 Spain blackout). The revision mandates automatic voltage control mode as the default for all generators; defines volt-var control boundaries and reactive output ranges tied to MW output; specifies two new required reactive capability curve data points ('Maximum AVR capable MW Output' and 'Unit Minimum MW Output with Reactive Control'); and establishes a three-party investigative process (PJM, transmission owner, generator owner/operator) for reactive control performance issues. For hydroelectric owners, this tightens the operating envelope for reactive power supply: hydro units must now document and receive explicit approval for any AVR mode other than voltage control, and must define their reactive capability limits with new precision, constraining dispatch flexibility and potentially requiring retrofits or AVR reconfiguration on legacy units. The revision proceeds through SOS (June 15), MRC (July 1 and July 9), and OC endorsement (July 23), with education and Q&A sessions between June 4 and July 9. The compliance timeline is not yet stated in this first-read presentation.

documents.title: Item 04 – 2 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: reactive power and voltage support obligations — PJM is clarifying VAR control requirements and AVR mode directives in Manual 14d, which directly affects how synchronous hydro and PSH units must operate their excitation systems and reactive-capability dispatch; this tightens an existing compliance obligation and may constrain flexibility in real-time volt-var/watt-var behavior.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14d (Generator Operational Requirements), sections 7.1.2 and Attachment D, effective July 23, 2026, to add NERC Category 2 GO references, clarify allowable AVR (automatic voltage regulator) modes, specify acceptable volt-var and watt-var control ranges, and formalize PJM's monitoring and engagement authority over reactive-control behavior. The revision responds to NERC's May 2026 Category 2 GO initiative (expanding inverter-based resource reliability standards) and PJM's operational challenges from rising solar/IBR penetration and load growth outstripping generation, both elevating voltage risk. For synchronous hydro and PSH operators, this tightens the specification of reactive-capability dispatch and AVR operating ranges — reducing ambiguity about allowable volt-var curves but also narrowing operational flexibility in voltage control. Most affected are large reservoir hydro and PSH facilities with synchronous generators supplying reactive power in constrained voltage zones; smaller or run-of-river plants with limited reactive margin may face tighter conformance audits. MRC voting is scheduled for July 23rd, 2026.

documents.title: Item 04 – 3 Manual 14d – Revision 71 – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 14D governs PJM generator operational and data-exchange requirements, metering standards, and dispatch procedures that directly affect hydro and storage operators' compliance obligations, participation pathways, and black-start procurement eligibility.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 14D (Revision 71, effective June 30, 2026) is a comprehensive operational and technical standard for all generating resources in PJM, including hydro, storage, and hybrid facilities. The redline reflects updates to control-center requirements, EMS data exchange and synchrophasor protocols, metering standards (with specific exhibits for energy-storage and hybrid resources), cold-weather resource preparation checklists for conventional and inverter-based units, black-start procurement processes, and behind-the-meter and microgrid business rules. Hydro and pumped-storage operators must comply with metering precision, reactive-capability testing and reporting (Attachments D–E), dispatch-instruction protocols, outage-scheduling, and cold-weather operational readiness (Attachment N) — and may be selected for black-start service under the revised incremental RFP and reliability backstop processes (Section 10). Mixed-technology facilities (solar–storage hybrids) now have dedicated marketing and metering rules (Section 13, Exhibit 7–8), setting a template that extends to other hybrid and co-located configurations. Revision 71 is already effective as of the meeting date, with no further comment period or vote indicated in the excerpt.

documents.title: Item 06 – Security Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a routine cybersecurity briefing on threat monitoring and notification procedures — it touches no hydro revenue stream, market obligation, or strategic option.

documents.title: Item 08 – Regulation Performance Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Regulation performance and pricing are ancillary-services revenue streams core to PJM hydro and storage operators; this operational update tracks performance scoring, mileage-based pricing (RMMCP), and regulation requirement changes that directly affect hydro's compliance and compensation in PJM's regulation market.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Performance Compliance team reports on regulation-market operations as of May 2026, including a nomenclature change (Regulation Market Mileage Clearing Price, effective October 1, 2025) and an update to regulation requirements for summer 2026 (effective May 1). Hydro resources averaged 89% performance score in May 2026 with 967 MW qualified for regulation, compared to storage at 94% score and 230 MW qualified. This metric directly affects hydro's regulation-market revenue; operators should track the new mileage-based pricing framework and summer requirement levels to assess whether pricing or dispatch patterns have shifted under the post-October 2025 rules.

documents.title: Item 09 – Synchronous Reserve Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Synchronous reserve performance and penalty administration directly affect ancillary-service revenue and compliance obligations for dispatchable resources, including hydropower units that carry synchronized-reserve assignments in PJM.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's 2026 Q1 synchronized-reserve performance report shows 8 events (1 ≥10 minutes) with 48% average generation response to assignment and 66% demand-side response. March 1 event triggered $330K in penalties (first of the quarter), indicating tightened enforcement of reserve-response obligations. The report documents response-performance metrics by resource type (generation vs. demand-side resources) and region (RTO vs. MAD zone), establishing baseline compliance data that operators—particularly synchronous units like conventional hydro—must track for performance assessment and penalty exposure.

documents.title: Item 10 – Reliability Compliance Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NERC large-load interconnection guidance and inverter-based resource ride-through standards directly affect how synchronous hydropower (and competing generation) must operate during grid disturbances, and large computational loads affect hydro's strategic positioning in a scarcity-prone grid.
documents.ai_summary: NERC issued a Level 3 Essential Action Alert (due August 3, 2026) requiring registered entities to address risks from customer-initiated large load reductions and oscillations that threaten BPS reliability, alongside a Reliability Guideline recommending risk mitigation for emerging large loads. NERC also released implementation guidance for PRC-029-1 (inverter-based resource ride-through design), which sets standards hydro must comply with if required to operate in grid-support modes. The large-load alert is strategically relevant because rapid load swings create scarcity windows that reward flexible, synchronous resources like hydro; however, the ride-through standard applies primarily to inverter-based resources and does not directly change hydro's existing obligations. Hydro operators in PJM should monitor August 3 deadline compliance requirements and the June 5 MSPP Rules of Procedure webinar for any emerging standards that might extend ride-through or frequency-support mandates to synchronous units.

documents.title: Item 11 – Mtopoc Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MTOPOC coordinates major transmission outages that constrain interregional flows and affect generator dispatch flexibility and deliverability — directly impacting hydro owner outage scheduling options and revenue dependability during periods when transmission is constrained.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Multi-TO Project Outage Coordination (MTOPOC) process manages ~$21 billion in transmission upgrades and associated major outages (230 kV and above, 20+ days) across 2026–2027 to minimize system reliability risk and construction conflicts. Generator outage coordination is becoming critical as load growth and generation retirements outpace new capacity additions; PJM is encouraging generators to submit outages early with detail and to maintain scheduling flexibility, as transmission constraints and emergent system conditions are triggering increased outage adjustments and recalls. Hydropower and pumped-storage operators in PJM face tightening outage windows and must coordinate outage timing with major transmission work affecting their deliverability corridors (Peach Bottom nuclear, northern Dominion, North-South flows); generators should engage with Emanuel Bernabeu and Joe Rushing early in the outage-planning cycle.

documents.title: Item 12 – Regulation Market Phase Ii Simulation Results
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: regulation market bifurcation (splitting single bidirectional product into separate RegUp and RegDown products) changes the offer, clearing, and settlement mechanics that hydro and PSH use to compete for and monetize regulation revenue in PJM, a major market where hydro operates.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is implementing Phase 2 of its Regulation Market redesign, effective October 1, 2026 (pending FERC approval), splitting the single bidirectional regulation product into two separate RegUp and RegDown products, each with its own requirement, clearing price, and settlement. The simulation, using FESTIV software across representative weeks in all four seasons, tested area control error performance, controller saturation, and generator movement under the bifurcated design versus the current unified product. Key findings show separate regulation products improve ACE (Area Control Error) performance and reliability metrics, though summer conditions remain challenging; improvements stem from changing resource mix and generator movement behavior. The implication for hydro and PSH operators is that the new product structure will change how their regulation capacity awards are determined and priced — previously a single award covered both up and down service; under Phase 2, RegUp and RegDown awards and prices are independently cleared, potentially altering the revenue profile for units providing regulation. The document does not contain comment deadlines or further implementation milestones beyond the October 1, 2026 effective date.

documents.title: Item 13 – Generation Operational Testing Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's Generation Operational Testing program establishes must-offer compliance obligations and performance penalties that directly affect dispatchable resource operators, including hydroelectric generators committed to RPM or FRR plans, through failure charges and retest requirements tied to start-time and minimum-run-time parameters.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Performance Compliance team reported on the Generation Operational Testing program, which subjects all capacity resources (except variable resources) committed in RPM or FRR to unannounced real-time operational tests up to twice per season. Winter 2025 saw 54 total tests with a 40% failure rate (vs. 24% in summer 2024), driven mainly by delayed start times, mechanical issues, and telemetry errors; retests failed at 64% versus 36% for initial tests. PJM emphasized that generators face financial implications from failed retests and must proactively validate operational readiness and parameter accuracy (start-up time, minimum run time, notification delay) in Markets Gateway. Hydroelectric and pumped-storage operators committed as capacity resources in PJM fall squarely under this obligation; dispatchable hydro must maintain accurate start-time parameters and be capable of reliable real-time response or face test failure and associated penalties.

documents.title: Item 02 – Storage As A Transmission Asset – Sata – Phase 2 – Package Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: SATA establishes a new participation model for energy storage as a regulated transmission asset in PJM, fundamentally altering how storage resources (including pumped-storage hydro) can be deployed and valued — either enrolled in SATA (transmission-only, cost-of-service recovery, no market participation) or remain in competitive markets, creating a strategic fork for PSH owners.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Operating Committee reviewed the Phase 2 framework for Storage as a Transmission Asset (SATA), which allows energy storage (including pumped-storage hydro) to operate as regulated transmission facilities to address localized RTEP violations without participating in competitive markets. Under SATA Phase 2, eligible storage assets would discharge automatically to mitigate identified contingencies, charge via owner coordination afterward, and recover costs through transmission rates (Schedule 12 Transmission Enhancement Credits) rather than market revenue, with metering and telemetry matching standard energy-storage requirements. The framework explicitly prohibits SATA resources from queuing for interconnection or claiming capacity value, and expects assets to be small (≤10 MW) and short-duration (≤4 hours). The document advances to MRC review in July 2026, with FERC submission expected September–November 2026. This creates a new revenue pathway for pumped-storage owners willing to accept regulated-utility-like cost recovery in exchange for operational control and transmission-rate certainty, but forecloses market-participation optionality (energy, AS, capacity) for enrolled assets.

documents.title: Item 03 – 1 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 13 governs PJM emergency operations procedures that directly affect hydro and other generating resources' obligations during system emergencies, capacity shortages, and transmission security events — particularly dispatch instructions, must-offer requirements, and emergency bid processes that touch hydro revenue and compliance.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 13 (Emergency Operations), Revision 98, underwent annual review with substantive changes to emergency procedure taxonomy and triggering thresholds effective July 23, 2026. Key changes separate transmission-security emergencies from capacity emergencies, clarify that transmission-security procedures do not trigger Energy Emergency Alerts (EEAs) or Persistent Advisories (PAIs), and add a new Capacity Advisory to provide advance notice when forecasted capacity approaches operating requirements. A new Attachment O codifies procedures for emergency use of backup generators under DOE 202(c) Orders. The revision also updates Advanced Scheduled Resources language for cold/hot-weather alerts and adds RCIS notification to Pre-Emergency Load Management procedures. For hydro operators, the distinction between transmission- and capacity-triggered emergencies affects when they will receive dispatch instructions and emergency bid solicitations — transmission emergencies no longer trigger EEAs, which may narrow the scope of emergency-reserve procurement that traditionally favors hydro's rapid-response capability; the Capacity Advisory may provide earlier signaling of system stress, allowing better strategic positioning. Most affected are large, flexible generators and storage operators participating in PJM's emergency-bid and reserve markets. Revision endorsed by Operating Committee June 4, 2026, with final approval timeline shown through June 24, 2026 (MRC second read).

documents.title: Item 03 – 2 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Establishes procedures for emergency dispatch of large loads (data centers) on backup generation during capacity/transmission emergencies, creating new operational constraints and dispatch prioritization rules that affect hydro's role in reserve provision and emergency response.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 13 Revision 98 (effective July 23, 2026) introduces a new Emergency Use of Back-up Generators procedure allowing data centers and large loads to disconnect from the grid and operate on-site backup generation during capacity or transmission emergencies—after NERC EEA2 is declared and a DOE 202(c) Order is obtained. PJM will direct Transmission Owners to coordinate disconnection of large loads within 15 minutes, with capacity-deficient control zones exhausting all available large-load capacity before requesting disconnection in zones with excess capacity. This reshapes hydro's strategic value during emergencies: where previously all generation resources (including hydro's full output) would be called to meet contingency reserves, large-load disconnection now becomes an alternative resource, potentially reducing dispatch calls on conventional generators and changing the economic incentive structure for hydro emergency participation. Most exposed are reservoir and pumped-storage operators supplying contingency reserves in constrained zones; run-of-river hydro with fixed output faces less direct impact. The manual becomes effective immediately; no comment deadline is stated.

documents.title: Item 03 – 3 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 13 covers emergency operations including capacity emergencies, weather/environmental procedures, and transmission security — obligations that hydro operators must comply with during system stress, plus strategic implications for hydro revenue during scarcity events (shortage pricing, load shedding, emergency procedures that affect dispatch and pricing).
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 13 Revision 98 (effective July 23, 2026) updates the operator emergency procedures manual with changes to capacity emergency classifications, weather alerts, transmission security procedures, and minimum generation requirements. Key changes include addition of a Capacity Advisory level to the emergency-level structure (Section 2.3), updated language for Advanced Schedule Resources during cold and hot weather alerts, clarification that transmission security procedures do not trigger PAIs or EEAs, and a new Attachment O on emergency use of backup generators. For hydro operators, these procedures determine obligations during system stress (e.g., must-offer and minimum generation requirements), affect the timing and magnitude of scarcity-pricing events and emergency bidding, and establish the dispatch scenarios in which hydro's flexibility is most valued or constrained. All hydro-owning utilities in PJM footprint must comply with these operating standards.

documents.title: Item Xx – May Hot Weather Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a real-time operations report on a specific heat-wave event (May 2026) with no bearing on hydro revenue streams, participation rules, accreditation, or strategic market design—it documents dispatch decisions, outage coordination, and load forecasting performance during emergency conditions.

documents.title: Item 05 – Operating Metrics
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Document reports on PJM operational metrics including a specific shortage-case approval where hydro switching to pumping mode triggered a shortage event, directly touching hydro's dispatch flexibility and pumped-storage operational constraints in real-time balancing.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's June 4, 2026 Operating Committee report documents system operations and performance metrics for May 2026. One finding is material to PSH owners: on May 15, 2026, PJM approved a shortage case (09:50 interval) explicitly citing 'hydro switching to pumping' as a factor contributing to the shortage event. This incident highlights how pumped-storage transition from discharge to pumping mode can tighten system energy balance and trigger emergency procedures — a risk PSH operators face when participating in energy markets and providing flexible ramping. The report also notes spinning-reserve response performance, forecast-error tracking, and BAAL (Balancing Authority ACE Limit) compliance, all relevant to the real-time operating environment in which hydro and PSH dispatch decisions occur.

documents.title: Item 07 – Primary Frequency Response Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Primary Frequency Response (PFR) is an ancillary service hydro plants are required to supply under NERC BAL-003-2, and this PJM performance update directly measures hydro unit compliance with PFR standards and identifies performance gaps that may trigger must-offer rules or operational penalties.
documents.ai_summary: PJM reports on compliance with NERC BAL-003-2 frequency response obligations for 2025 operating year (FRO = −235 MW/0.1 Hz) and reviews Manual 12 Primary Frequency Response performance across six low-frequency events between March and May 2026. In the 04/13/26 event, 25 hydro units were evaluated; the stacked results across all fuel types show hydro at 71% responding with expected PFR, 13% with no response when expected, and 16% flagged for further evaluation. This puts hydro at or below the fleet-wide average (81% PFR compliance), suggesting potential compliance risk or dispatch/operating constraints limiting hydro's frequency-response capability in PJM. No specific proposal or deadline is stated; this is a monitoring report to the Operating Committee.

documents.ai_summary: PJM is convening a TEAC Special Session on Order 1920 compliance to address transmission cost allocation. documents.title: 02. ROS Agenda 20260604
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT is advancing multiple rule changes affecting energy storage participation (NPRR1264 Energy Attribute Certificate Program, NOGRR273/PGRR123/RRGRR038), storage resource operations (NOGRR284 on Dispatchable Reliability Reserve Service Plus and ESR participation), large-load interconnection reform (PGRR141), and seasonal resource mothballing (NPRR1319) — mechanisms that set precedent for storage participation models and interconnection practices NHA tracks cross-market, and ERCOT's treatment of these design choices informs hydro-rich RTOs' approach to similar issues.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Reliability Operations Subcommittee is voting on multiple revisions to operations protocols scheduled for June 4, 2026. Key items for storage and system flexibility include: NPRR1264 and related revisions (NOGRR273, PGRR123, RRGRR038) establishing a new Energy Attribute Certificate program (status: language and impact analysis pending); NOGRR284 on Dispatchable Reliability Reserve Service Plus and Energy Storage Resource participation and release factors (tabled, status uncertain); PGRR141 on Large Load Interconnection Study Reform (tabled); and NPRR1319 on Seasonal Mothball Periods (tabled). While ERCOT has no hydropower fleet, its treatment of storage participation rules, interconnection pathways for dispatchable resources, and seasonal resource availability mechanics create comparative precedent for hydro-rich RTOs designing similar policies. The Energy Attribute Certificate program design, particularly as it applies to storage and generation, and the release-factor rules for storage participation bear watching as CAISO, NYISO, and other markets adopt analogous frameworks.

documents.title: 06. April 2026 ERCOT Operations Report Public
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT operations report documents net-load ramping, frequency response performance, and reliability unit commitment — cross-market comparison points for how ERCOT manages flexibility and grid stability without hydropower, informing hydro-rich RTO market design alternatives.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's April 2026 operations report shows largest net-load ramps nearly tripled since 2015 (18,089 MW in 60-minute ramp vs. 5,330 MW in April 2015), driven by growing wind and solar penetration (80.27% IRR maximum, 69.15% wind record). The system managed two frequency events and 16 RUC commitments without automatic load-shedding via under-frequency relays. Congestion rent on the HCKSW_MR2L constraint alone reached $51.7M for the month. As a hydro-poor system increasingly dependent on fast ramping and frequency stabilization from conventional generators, ERCOT's experience provides a contrast case: where hydro-rich markets (CAISO, NYISO, BPA) use ramping products and flexible capacity mechanisms to harness dispatchable hydro, ERCOT compensates through higher RUC frequency and larger intra-hour ramps. This benchmarks the value proposition of dedicated ramping and flexibility products that hydro markets have adopted.

documents.title: 11. PDCWG-Report-to-ROS_060426
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT frequency response and inertia management, particularly FFR procurement and PFR performance monitoring, demonstrate a market-design approach to frequency support that contrasts with hydro-rich RTOs and informs NHA's cross-market analysis of how different regions value synchronous and fast-response capability.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Primary Frequency Response (PFR) performance monitoring under BAL-001-TRE has stalled due to too few Frequency Measurable Events (FMEs) — the criteria used to evaluate PFR performance are generating only fewer than the desired 20–35 events per year. ERCOT has proposed revised FME selection criteria to increase event counts and improve oversight of Responsive Reserve Service providers; revised criteria have been shared with the PDCWG and ERCOT has begun using them immediately while retaining discretion in selection. The ERCOT system inertia trends show a structural decline: synchronous inertia at minimum-load points has fallen from 143 GW·s in 2016 to 114.7 GW·s in March 2026, while non-synchronous generation has grown to 81% of system load and Fast Frequency Response procurement has expanded from zero to 430 MW. This illustrates a cross-market contrast — whereas hydro-rich RTOs rely on synchronous hydro for inertia and primary frequency response, ERCOT's low-inertia, high-renewable grid is increasingly dependent on FFR procurement and stricter frequency-event metrics to maintain reserve adequacy and response capability.

documents.ai_summary: The Planning Working Group update covers multiple interconnection and grid-planning reforms underway at ERCOT, most material to hydro: (1) PGRR 140 (Non-Settled Generator and DG registration) moved to ROS for vote June 4, 2026, with Vistra's May 28 comments on Lone Star Infrastructure Act conflicts still pending discussion; (2) PGRR 141 (Large Load Interconnection Study Reform) tabled pending further discussion with sponsor; (3) PGRR 142 (In-Kind Definition for Generation) moved to ROS with ERCOT, LCRA, and Oncor opposing vague "same or better performance" language that could bypass normal interconnection review; and (4) PGRR 144 (Dynamic Model Submission for Large Computational Loads) tabled one more month, now clarifying that ERCOT will not impose battery-ride-through requirements on loads themselves. These rules govern how storage, dispatchable resources, and large loads enter the queue and achieve operational status—interconnection delays and definitional gaps directly erode the strategic value of building new or uprated capacity. The votes at the June 4 ROS meeting will determine whether these pathways remain predictable or create discretionary bottlenecks.

documents.title: Item 01 1 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Phase 1 – Manual 11 Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 11 revision 137 includes updates to hydropower operation scheduling rules (Section 9.3) and pumped-storage modeling (Attachment B) under FERC compliance, plus regulation-market redesign affecting how hydro supplies ancillary services; this touches both hydro obligations (scheduling/dispatch) and revenue mechanisms (regulation pricing and settlement).
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 11 revision 137 (effective July 1, 2026) incorporates three market-design updates relevant to hydro: (1) Advance Scheduled Resources Phase 1 updates (FERC ER26-0527) affecting resource scheduling prior to day-ahead, reflected in sections 2.3.1, 2.3.3, and 9.1; (2) Regulation Redesign Phase 1 (FERC ER24-1772) introducing revised regulation market clearing, pricing, and obligation rules in new Section 3 and Section 4.4; and (3) updates to hydropower operation procedures in Section 9.3 and pumped-storage modeling in Attachment B. Hydro operators face new scheduling windows and must-offer obligations under the Advance Scheduled Resources framework, while regulation revenue and mileage settlement mechanisms are restructured. Pumped-storage owners are directly affected by modeling changes and participation rules in the revised regulation market. The manual is now effective; no comment period is noted in this redline document, indicating implementation is underway.

issues.canonical_name: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market

documents.title: Item 01 2 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Phase 1 – Manual 11 Revisions – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's advance resource-scheduling rule for units with >24-hour start-up times locks cost-based offers and prevents price-based rebidding, affecting how dispatchable hydro and other resources can position themselves in energy markets and real-time rebidding windows.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is implementing Manual 11 revisions (Phase 1) to formalize advance scheduling of resources with notification or start-up times exceeding 24 hours, triggered by lessons from Winter Storm Elliott. Resources pre-scheduled in advance of the Day-ahead Market on binding cost-based offers will have their offer prices locked and the 'Switch to Cost Schedule' flag set by PJM; they cannot update to price-based schedules for the locked days. Resources with start-up/notification <24 hours may update cost-based offers only through the Day-ahead bid period and during real-time rebidding windows. The rule prevents advance-scheduled units from exercising price-based flexibility once committed. Implementation is scheduled for June 2026, with MIC first read May 26 and second read/endorsement in June.

issues.canonical_name: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market

documents.title: Item 01 3 – Resource Scheduling Prior TO The Day Ahead Energy Market – Phase 1 – Manual 11 Revisions – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 11 revisions clarify dispatch and cost-offer rules for resources called prior to the day-ahead market, affecting how conventional hydro and other dispatchable units are scheduled and compensated when operated outside normal market gates.
documents.ai_summary: PJM approved conforming changes to Manual 11 (Energy & Ancillary Services Market Operations) effective June 24, 2026, under FERC order ER26-527-000, to implement Phase 1 of its Resource Scheduling Prior to Day-Ahead Market initiative. The revisions clarify cost-capping rules for resources with start-up or notification times exceeding 24 hours when called in advance of the day-ahead market, specify how such resources are placed on cheapest-available cost-based offers, and address application of the "switch to cost" flag in day-ahead and real-time operations. For conventional hydro and dispatchable resources operated outside standard day-ahead/real-time windows, the changes affect how dispatch costs are calculated and offers are applied—particularly relevant to run-of-river or reservoir hydro units with long notification times that may be pre-scheduled for reliability or system needs, with voting completed June 3, 2026.

issues.canonical_name: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market

documents.title: Item 02 1 – Manual 18 Periodic Review – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM Manual 18 Revision 63 governs the Reliability Pricing Model (RPM) capacity market, including accreditation rules, performance assessment penalties, and participation frameworks that directly affect how hydroelectric and pumped-storage resources qualify for and are compensated in PJM's capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: PJM has released Revision 63 of Manual 18 (effective September 1, 2026), a periodic review update to the RPM capacity market rulebook. The revision spans resource accreditation, performance testing, non-performance assessment, seasonal capacity resources, and DER participation—all mechanisms that establish how hydro and PSH assets earn capacity revenue and face compliance penalties. Key sections address Summer/Winter Capability Testing (Section 8.5), Non-Performance Assessment (Section 8.4A), and Seasonal Capacity Performance Resources (Section 4.10), which directly determine whether hydro meets PJM's qualifying-MW standards and penalty exposure. The document shows this is a formal Manual revision in effect as of the meeting date; no comment period or vote deadline is stated in the excerpt, but this redline indicates the revision has been approved and is now operative.

documents.title: Item 02 2 – Manual 18 Periodic Review – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 18 periodic review includes conforming changes to PJM's capacity market accreditation rules (AUCAP factor penalties, VRR curve parameters, sell-offer requirements) that directly affect how hydro and storage resources are valued and must participate in PJM's RPM auction.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Manual 18 periodic review incorporates conforming changes to the Capacity Market Manual approved via several tariff filings, including a new penalty for derate caused by decrease in final Accredited AUCAP factor (ER25-2002-000), updates to Variable Resource Requirement curve plotting and parameters (ER26-455-000), and clarifications to must-offer exception processes and resource-specific sell-offer requirements. The document reflects housekeeping and compliance work, with a FERC order expected on June 5th on GDECS changes (ER26-980) that may trigger additional conforming edits; the updated manual is expected for MIC endorsement in July 2026. Most exposed are hydro and storage operators subject to AUCAP accreditation assessment and must-offer obligations; the new penalty structure for AUCAP derate could alter effective capacity value for peaking or seasonal hydro resources whose output factors change year-to-year.

documents.title: Item 04 – Periodic Review Of Default Gross CONE And Gross Acr Values – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's periodic review of default Gross CONE and Gross ACR values directly affects the Minimum Offer Price Rule (MOPR) and Market Seller Offer Cap (MSOC) calculations that apply to all resources, including hydropower and pumped storage; these values set the floor for offers and determine whether units face mitigation, which is the canonical market-power rule applied to use-limited resources like hydro.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is conducting its mandatory periodic review of default Gross CONE and Gross ACR values for the 30/31 delivery year, with external consultants Brattle Group and Sargent & Lundy analyzing updates. These values drive MOPR calculations for new resources and ACR-based MSOC calculations for existing units; they directly determine the cost floors against which hydro offers are tested for mitigation. PJM will present detailed methodology and revisions to the Market Implementation Committee on June 16, 2026, seek first read at the MRC, conduct an Advisory Vote at MRC/MC in August, and file changes at FERC no later than October 1, 2026. All resource types—including any hydro or pumped-storage plants in the capacity market—are subject to these mitigation rules, making the updated values material to revenue defensibility and offer strategy for PJM members.

documents.ai_summary: PJM's Market Implementation Committee is developing Phase 2 of a 'Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market' framework, with design work spanning mid-2025 through mid-2026. The work plan shows PJM will identify design components, document their relative importance, develop and narrow options, and construct and compare alternative packages for stakeholder approval around June 2026. The mechanism would reshape when and how hydro (and all dispatchable resources) lock in commitments relative to the day-ahead market, potentially affecting both the value of real-time flexibility and the cost of unit-commitment, particularly for run-of-river and reservoir resources that depend on hour-ahead optionality; the outcome will be critical to PJM-area hydro operators' ability to optimize dispatch timing against price signals. No vote date or public-comment window is yet specified in this work plan.

documents.title: Informational Only – PJM Esr Model Enhancements Position Paper
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's proposed enhancements to energy storage resource market participation rules—particularly opportunity-cost offer methodology, state-of-charge management, and must-offer requirements—directly affect how pumped-storage hydro and battery storage compete for energy and ancillary-services revenue and how their dispatch is constrained by SOC accounting, setting precedent that will shape storage treatment across RTOs.
documents.ai_summary: PJM staff proposes enhancements to its Energy Storage Resource Participation Model to accommodate rapid battery-storage growth (4,800 MW expected by 2030). The proposal addresses four issue areas: (1) a standardized opportunity-cost calculation for real-time energy offers capping cost at max(incremental energy cost, maximum DA LMP) up to $2,000, with emergency override to $2,000; (2) SOC accounting in day-ahead dispatch (rolling calculation to prevent infeasible dispatch) and ancillary-services clearing (recognizing SOC depletion from AS deployment), but explicit rejection of centralized SOC optimization; (3) no change to inter-day offer rules; and (4) complementary work via the Reserve Certainty Senior Task Force. A stakeholder process begins July 2026. The opportunity-cost cap directly affects how pumped storage (and batteries) offer into real-time markets—tying the cap to max DA LMP rather than real-time conditions or a multiplier makes cost-based offers more predictable but may suppress PSH discharge bids during high-priced hours when SO-C is low. SOC accounting prevents infeasible dispatch but stops short of RTO-led optimization, preserving operator discretion. The proposal affects both standalone storage and MTF configurations (including hydro-battery hybrids). Comments and next steps are embedded in a July 2026 stakeholder launch.

documents.ai_summary: MISO has completed the MISO26 series power-flow models and is building dynamic models (pre-final review due early June 2026, final release mid-June). These models include base and sensitivity cases across 2028, 2031, and 2036 horizons with specific wind and solar capacity-credit assumptions and load profiles (summer peak, light load, winter peak, shoulder). The models feed MTEP transmission-expansion studies, Generation Interconnection queue assessments, System Support Resource studies (which determine forced-operation obligations on existing generation for reliability), and Planning Resource Auction adequacy calculations. Hydro owners' ability to negotiate must-offer exemptions, avoid reliability-driven RMR or mothball processes, and establish RA capacity credits depends on these baseline assumptions; changes in model inputs (load forecasts, wind/solar penetration, transmission paths) shift scarcity signals and the effective capacity value of existing hydro. The document also flags that ERAG MMWG 2026 power-flow model development begins June 2026, affecting Eastern Interconnection–wide resource-adequacy and interregional transfer studies in which hydro participates.

documents.ai_summary: MISO's MTEP26 (2026 Transmission Expansion Plan) targets 574 new projects, with 98 proposed in the South region, driven predominantly by load growth and large data-center load additions; the plan includes 4,000+ MW of new spot load in the South and sharp growth in generator interconnection projects, particularly in Arkansas. The reliability assessment scope is consistent with NERC TPL standards and covers steady-state, transient stability, voltage, transfer capability, and nuclear plant assessments across 2, 5, and 10-year horizons. For hydro owners, massive load growth and queue expansion directly increase scarcity and system stress, raising energy-price and ancillary-service value, but also compress interconnection timelines and deliverability thresholds for new or uprated hydro capacity; the South region's concentration of load-growth projects and generator interconnection requests elevates competition for transmission headroom that existing reservoir hydro may need to serve or export energy. Key deadlines: August 19, 2026 (data-entry cutoff for SPM3 presentation), September 28–October 12 (stakeholder feedback window), December 3 (full Board approval).

documents.title: 02. WMS Agenda 20260603
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT is advancing multiple market-design proposals affecting energy storage participation, ancillary services, and price formation—developments NHA tracks as cross-market comparison points for hydro-rich RTOs, particularly storage participation models (NPRR1278 on Advanced Grid Support Service), mitigation frameworks (NPRR1255 on ESR mitigation), and energy attribute certificate programs (NPRR1264) that set precedent for how storage and flexible resources are credited and priced.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's Wholesale Market Subcommittee is advancing several proposals relevant to storage and flexible-resource revenue and dispatch. NPRR1278 (Establishing Advanced Grid Support Service as an Ancillary Service) and NPRR1255 (Mitigation of Energy Storage Resources) are tabled for vote, addressing how dispatchable storage can participate in ancillary-service markets and how mitigation rules apply to use-limited resources—a canonical problem for pumped-storage hydropower in other RTOs. NPRR1264, which creates a new Energy Attribute Certificate program with three related revision requests (COPMGRR051, SMOGRR031, VCMRR043), is also on the agenda; this shapes how clean-energy attributes are accredited, directly affecting how hydro's environmental value is recognized and monetized in competitive markets. Additionally, NPRR1278 (Generation Firming Program) and ongoing work on Real-Time Ancillary Service duration parameters, ASDC floors, and System Lambda capping signal ERCOT's focus on pricing flexibility and ramping capacity—mechanisms that mirror problems hydro operators face in other markets. WMS open action items include establishing performance-review cadences for ancillary-service procurement and investigating impacts of DOE 202(c) emergency authority on pricing transparency, which affects all flexible resources. No specific comment deadlines or vote dates are stated in this agenda extract; the next steps are the WMS meeting votes on June 3, 2026.

documents.title: 05. NPRR1214_ERCOTComments_20260610 v6
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's NPRR 1214 proposes changes to energy price formation, ancillary-service pricing, and indifference-payment mechanics for reliability deployments — a cross-market comparison point for how non-hydro-rich RTOs handle price-adder elimination, locational pricing of out-of-market actions, and settlement consistency between dispatch and pricing runs, mechanisms that affect how hydro-dominant RTOs (WECC, NYISO, PJM) structure scarcity pricing and make-whole payments.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT proposes to eliminate separate energy and ancillary-service price adders by using SCED Pricing Run prices (LMPs and AS MCPCs) directly as binding prices when reliability deployments are active, replacing a previous approach that added the difference between pricing and dispatch runs. ERCOT also refines indifference-payment calculations to use actual offer/bid curves instead of linear approximations, and extends indifference payments to ancillary services and RUC/RMR/MRA resources. The proposal addresses a settlement inconsistency: dispatch-run awards (reflecting real-time conditions) paired with pricing-run prices (reflecting a hypothetical grid absent deployments) can financially harm participants and create perverse incentives to chase prices. This represents ERCOT's approach to neutralizing distortions from out-of-market actions — a design problem that hydro-rich markets handle differently (e.g., PJM's opportunity-cost framework, CAISO's reliability-deployment uplift). No explicit comment deadline or vote date is stated in the slides.

documents.title: 05. NPRR1214_ERCOT_Paper_20260610
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's proposal to reform energy and ancillary-service price formation during reliability deployments — replacing price adders with direct SCED pricing-run prices and introducing AS indifference payments — sets a cross-market precedent for how out-of-market actions distort dispatch awards and prices, a design question that affects how hydro assets (especially flexible resources) are compensated when system operators override market outcomes.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT proposes reforms to NPRR1214 that modify how reliability deployments (out-of-market dispatchable loads, reserve curtailments, and generation adjustments) are priced and compensated. The proposal replaces energy and ancillary-service price adders with direct use of SCED pricing-run locational marginal prices and market clearing prices, and introduces ancillary-service indifference payments to offset the gap between binding dispatch-run awards (reflecting actual reliability actions) and hypothetical pricing-run prices (reflecting the counterfactual absent those deployments). ERCOT positions this as more accurate than prior approximations and states it will be discussed at the Wholesale Market Subcommittee; no vote or implementation date is stated in this draft. Although ERCOT has no significant hydropower fleet, this precedent is relevant to NHA members in other RTOs (especially CAISO, NYISO, and PJM) where similar tensions arise between must-run or must-offer generation commitments and competitive pricing, and where storage and hydro operators increasingly face indifference-payment regimes to offset scarcity pricing or out-of-market dispatch.

documents.title: 06. CMWG-update-WMS-May-2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ERCOT's ancillary-services deployment factor (ASDF) methodology and frequency-response pricing directly parallels hydro's ancillary-service revenue streams; the document also addresses congestion and transmission constraint activation affecting locational price signals — both mechanisms hydro monitors cross-market for comparative design.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT updated the Wholesale Market Subcommittee on three transmission and ancillary-service topics: (1) Congestion Revenue Rights (CRR) auction performance (80 hours for peak weekday, within 100-hour target) and Point-to-Point obligation growth causing system performance issues — staff plan to file NRPP language targeting 'inefficient' bids and seek stakeholder feedback on transaction-volume limits; (2) NPRR 1301, which clarifies that when multiple contingencies overload the same transmission element, only the highest-overload constraint activates, preventing the shadow-price (LMP adder) from being counted multiple times — LCRA supports the change, filing expected as formal comments; (3) Ancillary Service Deployment Factors (ASDFs) for Summer 2026 using the same methodology as Winter 2025/Spring 2026, with Primary Frequency Response and Fast Frequency Response both set to zero for all hours. The ASDF treatment is the cross-market comparison point: ERCOT's zero-cost FFR reflects a different frequency-response cost allocation than hydro-rich RTOs (e.g., PJM, NYISO) that price fast frequency response; constraint-activation deduplication in NPRR 1301 reduces artificial LMP inflation, a mechanism hydro owners track as it affects scarcity-pricing signals at hydro delivery nodes.

documents.title: 09. wmwg-update-to-wms-of-May-26-meeting
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NPRR1328 (Generation Firming Program) and NPRR1278 (grid-forming ancillary service) establish new market mechanisms that affect resource participation, penalty structures, and ancillary service procurement—cross-market precedents for storage and flexible resources; ESR state-of-charge analysis directly parallels PSH operational constraints in RUC/AS procurement.
documents.ai_summary: ERCOT's WMWG advanced two revised proposals in development. NPRR1328 (Generation Firming Program, required by Texas HB1500) implements capacity-firming with revised penalty formulas to better align penalties with actual transfer quantities and resource performance; stakeholders (TSSA, NextEra, TCPA) raised concerns about disproportionate penalties and notice timelines, with September Board approval targeted. NPRR1278 proposes an RFP-based ancillary-service procurement framework for grid-forming capability, now under discussion with a companion proposal (NPRR1333) for voluntary one-time payments to inverter-based resources—the interaction between these two approaches is still being defined. Separately, ERCOT analyzed January 28 RUC outcomes showing that Energy Storage Resources' state-of-charge constraints and inaccurate COP submissions caused severe ancillary-service shortfall signals in RUC that exceeded real-time conditions; ERCOT is reassessing whether RUC AS demand curves and SOC constraints are properly calibrated. These mechanisms directly affect how flexible and storage-like resources are committed and priced in ERCOT's unit-commitment and ancillary-service markets, with penalty and SOC-constraint rules setting precedent for similar storage participation rules in other RTOs.
