documents.title: Item 03 – 1 Manual 14h Revisions Ieee 2800 – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM is adopting IEEE 2800 inverter-based resource interconnection standards that will establish ride-through, frequency response, and voltage support requirements applicable to synchronous hydropower and all new generation — creating new operating obligations and potentially raising compliance costs for hydro facilities during disturbances.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14H to adopt IEEE 2800-2022 requirements for inverter-based resources (Phase 1), including voltage and frequency ride-through, primary frequency response, and enter-service criteria, with application to VSC-HVDC transmission facilities and IBR interconnections. The revision adds Section 9.15 and new Attachment K, specifying mandatory ride-through times (e.g., 6 seconds at 0.70–0.90 p.u. voltage) and default frequency-response parameters (droop 5%, dead-band 0.06% × fnom). While Phase 1 targets IBRs, the standards establish precedent for synchronous resource requirements; PJM endorsement is scheduled for June 2, 2026, with MRC endorsement August 19, 2026. Hydro operators should monitor whether these ride-through and frequency-response mandates extend to conventional generators in Phase 2, as compliance during voltage/frequency disturbances will become mandatory, and deviation penalties may apply.

documents.title: Item 03 – 2 Manual 14h Revisions Ieee 2800 – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: IEEE 2800 adoption establishes operating standards (ride-through, frequency response) that synchronous hydro units must meet, directly affecting compliance obligations and potentially the technical feasibility and cost of hydro participation in PJM.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 14H to adopt IEEE Standard 2800-2022 requirements for inverter-based resources, with Phase I covering general definitions, voltage and frequency ride-through, ride-through protection, and primary frequency response. The manual changes are effective July 23, 2026, pending Planning Committee approval (MRC first read June 24, 2026; MRC voting July 23, 2026). While IEEE 2800 is designed for inverter-based resources, PJM's alignment with industry standards may establish precedent for how synchronous resources—including conventional hydropower and pumped-storage hydro—are expected to meet similar technical standards in future compliance phases, affecting both capital requirements and operational dispatch.

documents.title: Item 03 – 3 Manual 14h Revisions Ieee 2800 – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual revision addresses interconnection queue procedures, surplus interconnection service, and generator requirements (including capacity vs. energy resource elections) — core obligations and strategic options for hydro interconnection and deliverability.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 14H (New Service Requests Cycle Process) Revision 05 becomes effective July 23, 2026. This manual governs the interconnection queue process for generation, transmission, and merchant transmission requests in PJM. For hydropower and pumped-storage operators, the key provisions include: Section 9 sets requirements for generating facilities (power factor, capacity vs. energy resource status elections, behind-the-meter generation, inverter-based resource rules); Section 12 covers surplus interconnection service, the mechanism by which existing hydro plants can access previously-allocated but unused interconnection capacity for uprates or expansion; and Sections 1–5 establish the phased study and agreement process that determines project viability and cost allocation. The manual revision incorporates IEEE 2800 standards (synchronous inverter modeling), affecting how hydro and hybrid resources are studied. Hydro developers and PSH operators in PJM should review Section 9's resource-status elections and Section 12's surplus service process, which determine how much capacity value their projects can claim and how efficiently they move through the queue.

documents.title: Item 04 – 1 M20a M22 Periodic Review – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM Manual 20A addresses ELCC Class Rating and Resource-Specific ELCC Rating—the core accreditation mechanism for capacity value and resource adequacy showings—which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are credited toward peak-load obligations and RA requirements.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is conducting a periodic cover-to-cover review of Manual 20A (Resource Adequacy Analysis, Revision 3) and Manual 22 (Generator Resource Performance Indices, Revision 19), with administrative and formulaic updates. Manual 20A now presents ELCC Class Rating and Resource-Specific ELCC Rating as formulas rather than plain text, and adds definitions for Solved Simulated Annual Peak Load and Portfolio EUE. Manual 22 updates references and adds pool-wide average Accredited UCAP Factor and effective dates for PRISM reliability calculations. First read scheduled June 2, 2026 (Planning Committee); second read and endorsement scheduled June 24, 2026 (Members Resource Committee). These revisions affect how PJM calculates capacity accreditation for all dispatchable and storage resources, including hydropower and pumped storage, which depend on accurate ELCC methodology to establish their contribution to system adequacy.

documents.title: Item 04 – 2 M20a M22 Periodic Review – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a routine administrative periodic review of PJM Manuals 20A and 22 covering resource adequacy analysis and generator performance indices — the document summary indicates only administrative updates with no substantive changes to revenue mechanisms, obligations, or strategic options affecting hydropower or pumped-storage operators.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is updating Manual 20A (Resource Adequacy Analysis) and Manual 22 (Generator Resource Performance Indices) effective 06/24/2026. These manuals govern how PJM evaluates generator performance and resource adequacy, which determines how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are accredited for capacity markets and rated for participation in reliability assessments.

documents.title: Item 04 – 3 Manual 20a Periodic Review – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ELCC accreditation methodology for capacity resources directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage facilities are valued and compensated in PJM's capacity market; the periodic review updates to ELCC calculation formulas and DER conforming changes impact hydro revenue recognition.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 20A Revision 3 (effective May 20, 2026) updates the Resource Adequacy Analysis procedure governing ELCC class ratings and resource-specific ELCC ratings used to accredit capacity in PJM's Reliability Pricing Model (RPM). Key changes: two new definitions (Solved simulated annual peak load and Portfolio EUE) are added, and ELCC calculations are now presented as mathematical formulas rather than text, plus DER conforming changes based on FERC Order 2222 are incorporated (as of Revision 2, December 17, 2025). Hydro and PSH operators rely on ELCC ratings to determine their capacity offering value; reformulated accreditation methodologies can shift which resource types (dispatchable vs. variable; duration-limited vs. unlimited) receive higher accreditation factors, directly altering revenue in PJM's annual capacity auction. The manual governs RTO-wide (0.1 days/year LOLE) and LDA-level (40% × Portfolio EUE) adequacy criteria that drive capacity procurement.

documents.title: Item 04 – 4 Manual 22 Periodic Review – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 22 defines generator performance indices and accredited capacity factors (UCAP) used in PJM's resource adequacy and capacity market, which directly affect how hydroelectric and pumped-storage facilities are valued and compensated for capacity accreditation.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 22 (Revision 19, effective May 20, 2026) establishes the technical framework for calculating generator resource performance indices, including forced outage rates, availability factors, and accredited capacity (UCAP factors) used in planning and capacity-market studies. The revision updates references to Manual 21B and 20A (resource adequacy and capability rules) and adds pool-wide average Accredited UCAP Factor to the Introduction. These metrics directly determine how much capacity credit hydro and pumped-storage units receive in PJM's capacity market and reserve-margin calculations — a core revenue stream for storage and seasonal-limited resources. The manual itself is procedural and defines no new policy, but changes to UCAP accreditation methodology, outage-rate data collection, or reliability-calculation tools (PRISM) flow through this manual and affect what capacity revenue hydro owners can claim.

documents.title: Item 05 – 1 Manual 14h Revisions Eit – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Expedited Interconnection Track (EIT) establishes a new parallel queuing process for large generators (≥250 MW UCAP) with compressed timelines, affecting interconnection strategy and deliverability for pumped storage and conventional hydro projects seeking to compete in PJM resource-adequacy procurement.
documents.ai_summary: PJM has filed a tariff amendment (FERC Docket ER26-1563) creating a temporary Expedited Interconnection Track for generators meeting three criteria: ≥250 MW accredited capacity, ability to achieve commercial operation within 36 months, and Primary Siting Authority support. EIT operates as a parallel track outside the standard interconnection queue cycle, compressing the process into ~300 days (Application Review ~180 days, Study Phase ~60 days, Final Agreement Negotiation ~60 days). Manual 14H is being revised to reflect EIT procedures; PJM's Board ruled on the process in June 2026, with FERC ruling anticipated July 31, 2026 and a requested effective date of July 31, 2026. For PSH and large run-of-river projects with construction-readiness and state support, EIT offers a faster path to interconnection and earlier capacity accreditation timing relative to standard queue, affecting project-timing and RA-portfolio strategy; however, EIT projects forfeit the one-time milestone-extension option available to standard-queue projects, tightening execution risk.

documents.title: Item 05 – 2 Manual 14h Revisions Eit – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Interconnection queue reform and expedited interconnection track design directly affect hydro and PSH project deliverability timelines and strategic project-development options in PJM.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is conforming Manual 14H (New Service Requests Cycle Process) to implement an Expedited Interconnection Track, pending FERC approval in Docket ER26-1563-000, effective July 31, 2026. The revision adds a new section 16 describing the expedited process and reorganizes sections to treat it as a parallel pathway alongside the standard interconnection queue. This creates a faster-track option for qualifying projects (specifics on eligibility criteria are not detailed in this executive summary), potentially enabling hydro and PSH developers to achieve COD sooner than the standard process — a strategic advantage in tight RA markets and for repositioning existing generation or pumped-storage capacity additions. MRC voting is scheduled for July 23, 2026.

documents.title: Item 05 – 3 Manual 14h Revisions Eit – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 14h revisions establish interconnection queue procedures and surplus interconnection service rules—core obligations affecting hydro and PSH uprate, new-plant, and deliverability pathways in PJM.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 14h (Revision 06, effective July 23, 2026) governs New Service Requests Cycle Process for generation and transmission interconnections, including system impact study phases, agreement negotiation, deposits, and an expedited interconnection track (EIT). The manual covers generator classification, capacity vs. energy resource election, surplus interconnection service (including deactivation of existing facilities), and modifications at cycle decision points—all mechanics that determine how hydro and PSH plants queue, reserve interconnection capacity, and pursue uprates. This redline version shows the current procedural framework; hydro owners must track revisions to site-control requirements (Section 7), deposit timelines (Section 6), and surplus service deactivation rules (Section 12.3) as they affect project economics and timelines. Most exposed are PSH facilities seeking new queue slots or surplus capacity, and run-of-river plants pursuing uprates via the surplus interconnection pathway.

documents.title: Item 07 – Reliability Compliance Update – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NERC's Level 3 Alert and Reliability Guidelines on large computational loads directly affect PJM synchronous and inverter-based resource dispatch, ride-through obligations, and the revenue/operational stability of dispatchable hydro and pumped storage relied upon to manage the second-scale oscillations and frequency disturbances these loads cause.
documents.ai_summary: NERC issued a Level 3 Essential Action Alert (responses due August 3, 2026) requiring registered entities to implement seven immediate actions addressing risks posed by computational loads (data centers) causing customer-initiated large load reductions and significant oscillations within seconds — outpacing real-time response capability and threatening BPS reliability. PJM members must comply with this alert and the accompanying NERC Reliability Guideline on risk mitigation for emerging large loads. For hydropower and pumped-storage operators, this creates new dispatch obligations (faster frequency response to load swings) and qualification/ride-through requirements under PRC-029-1 Inverter-Based Resource guidance; it also signals potential scarcity value for synchronous hydro's inertial response and for PSH's second-scale ramping to absorb load shocks — contingent on PJM's eventual tariff implementation of NERC's standards revisions (currently in informal comment period through June 17, 2026).

documents.title: Item 01 – Interregional Planning Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Interregional transmission planning directly affects hydro and PSH deliverability, revenue realization from interregional trade, and strategic positioning in seams between PJM and neighboring RTOs (MISO, NYISO/New England).
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Transmission Expansion Advisory Committee reviewed an interregional planning update covering EIPC initiatives and scheduled IPSAC (Interregional Planning Stakeholder Advisory Committee) meetings with MISO (June 8 and August 17, 2026) and with New York & New England (TBD). Interregional transmission expansion and seams governance directly shape the economic value of hydro capacity at PJM boundaries — larger transfer capability into PJM increases price realization for exporting hydro (e.g., from New York) and improves market access; conversely, congestion on interregional paths limits arbitrage and reserve-sharing opportunities. Hydro owners with generation in MISO, NYISO, or adjacent regions should monitor these IPSAC meetings for proposals affecting interregional flow capability, cost allocation, and resource adequacy coordination.

documents.title: Item 02 – Generation Deactivation Notification Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: STRATEGIC OPTION: Generation deactivations and the RMR/DACC mechanism directly affect hydro fleet retirement decisions, resource adequacy, and transmission-driven constraints that can extend profitable plant life or trigger forced operations.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Q1 2026 deactivation status report tracks seven energy-storage and thermal units requesting exit by April 1, 2027, with reliability analyses completed and no impacts identified for any unit. The report illustrates PJM's deactivation-review process under its tariff: when a proposed exit could adversely affect transmission-system reliability due to upgrade-completion timelines, PJM may request the owner to extend operations under either a Cost of Service Recovery Rate (CSRR) filing at FERC or acceptance of a Deactivation Avoidable Cost Credit (DACC). While this cohort contains storage units (Beech Ridge, Grand Ridge, Mantua Creek, Green Mountain, Cape May County) rather than conventional hydro, the DACC and CSRR mechanisms are available to all dispatchable resources—including pumped-storage facilities and some run-of-river hydro—facing similar retirement pressure and transmission constraints. Hydro owners should monitor how PJM applies these mechanisms when transmission upgrades lag and scarcity conditions develop; forced-operation agreements can materially extend revenue life but require tariff-compliant cost recovery.

documents.title: Item 04 – Ugi Supplemental Projects
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Large customer load interconnection (500 MW in Newport Township) creates transmission expansion need that affects hydro's deliverability and strategic positioning in PJM; load growth directly increases scarcity value of existing hydro capacity.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's UGI transmission zone is evaluating a supplemental transmission project to serve a 500 MW customer load in Newport Township, with phased in-service dates of Q3 2029 (250 MW) and Q1 2030 (250 MW). This is a Need Meeting phase of the M-3 process, meaning stakeholder comments on the project scope are due within 10 days of the June 2, 2026 meeting to inform the Solutions phase. The project demonstrates demand-driven transmission expansion; for hydro owners in PJM, new firm load interconnections reduce transmission congestion risk to existing generation and increase energy scarcity value during peak periods, though the specific route and configuration are not yet determined in this early-stage need meeting.

documents.title: Item 07 – PPL Supplemental Projects

documents.ai_summary: PPL Transmission Zone has received a customer request to serve a 900 MW facility (by 2031) from a 230 kV source near Harrisburg, PA, with in-service date of March 2029. This is classified as a supplemental need under PJM's M-3 transmission-planning process and was presented at the June 2, 2026 TEAC meeting. The document is in the need-meeting stage; stakeholders have 10 days from the meeting to submit comments before the process advances. Large load interconnections like this one drive transmission buildout decisions that alter the network's capability to deliver hydro energy from the Northwest and other hydro-rich regions to load centers; the specifics of how PJM engineers the solution (new circuits, reinforcement, or non-wires alternatives) will shape long-term transmission scarcity rents and locational price signals that hydro operators depend on for revenue.

documents.title: Item 10 – COMED Supplemental Projects
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document concerns the PJM transmission planning process (M-3) for replacement of aging ComEd transmission equipment (transformers and circuit breakers) due to material condition and obsolescence—a pure transmission asset-management and infrastructure issue with no bearing on energy pricing, ancillary services, capacity accreditation, storage participation rules, interconnection queues, or any other hydro/PSH revenue stream, obligation, or strategic option.

documents.title: Item 11 – DOM Supplemental Projects
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Massive data-center load growth (3–6.8 GW by 2032) in Dominion's transmission zone directly affects hydro's strategic option to serve co-located or behind-the-meter load, and transmission constraints will shape scarcity pricing and deliverability of hydro exports from the region.
documents.ai_summary: Dominion Energy is presenting supplemental transmission projects to PJM's TEAC to accommodate extraordinary data-center load growth in northern Virginia (North Anna–Thornburg–Bristers corridor and Elmont–Fredericksburg corridor). The Bristers project will install 230 kV conductor to serve ~3 GW of requested data-center load with in-service Q4 2030 ($33.75 M, transmission-only cost). A larger multi-phase Ladysmith/Fredericksburg initiative ($450 M, phases 1 and 2 by 07/2029) targets 4,900–6,800 MW of new load by 2029–2032 via nine new substations in the Elmont–Fredericksburg corridor and ten in the Elmont–Chickahominy corridor. These transmission bottlenecks create windows of scarcity-driven pricing and make hydro's ability to reach and serve co-located or interruptible load behind data-center infrastructure increasingly valuable; owners should monitor whether Dominion's interconnection processes will allow direct-connect hydro or PSH to these load clusters.

documents.title: Informational Only – 2026 RTEP Assumption Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: RTEP transmission planning assumptions directly shape interconnection queues, generator deliverability criteria (including hydro and pumped-storage EEFORds), and transmission-constrained revenue for existing and new hydro/PSH resources.
documents.ai_summary: PJM posted its 2026 RTEP modeling assumptions for the Transmission Expansion Advisory Committee (June 2, 2026). Key hydro/PSH impacts: (1) Hydro generic EEFORd set at 8.15% and pumped storage at 5.57% — these forced-outage rates determine how much capacity PJM credits each technology in planning studies and interconnection queues. (2) Battery dispatch rules in base cases now specified (summer minimum charge at ENC × (1−EEFORd)/4, winter /8, light load charging mode), setting a precedent for how storage participates in transmission planning and potentially foreshadowing market participation models. (3) Offshore wind removals from 2031 and 2034 base cases and continued modeling of deactivating units create relief in transmission-constrained zones, directly affecting the scarcity-value of existing hydro and PSH deliverability. Timeline: competitive proposal window opens July 2026; Board approval expected October 2026–February 2027. Most exposed are PSH owners with interconnection in PJM's constrained MAAC/DOM zones, where EEFORd assumptions drive whether their resource clears in capacity/interconnection analysis.

documents.title: Item 03 – Market Efficiency Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Market Efficiency analysis drives transmission expansion planning that affects hydro deliverability and resource adequacy assumptions; reserve-requirement forecasting and generation-expansion modeling directly inform the capacity and energy price environment hydro faces.
documents.ai_summary: PJM posts its preliminary 2026/27 Market Efficiency base case in June 2026, with stakeholder feedback due August 31, 2026, and final case in January 2027; analysis will identify congestion drivers and proposed transmission solutions through September 2027, culminating in TEAC board approval by December 2027. The study models PJM's peak demand growing from 160.5 GW (2027) to 241.4 GW (2041) and uses preliminary ELCC class ratings to forecast generation adequacy; reserve requirements and topology are informed by the 2026 RTEP reliability window. Hydro operators should note that this cycle's load forecast, generation-expansion assumptions, and transmission topology directly shape which existing hydro assets are called to deliver energy and when locational scarcity pricing—and thus ancillary-service and energy margins—will emerge; the August 31, 2026 feedback window is the actionable juncture to flag hydro-specific accreditation or deliverability concerns in PJM's modeling.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a routine transmission-planning status update with no implications for hydropower revenue, compliance obligations, or strategic options.
documents.ai_summary: This RTEP update outlines PJM's transmission planning scope for 2026, including 5-year and 8-year analyses that will inform infrastructure needs and policy impacts on regional generation resources. The analysis may identify transmission constraints or upgrades relevant to hydropower and pumped-storage facility deliverability and resource adequacy within PJM's footprint.

documents.title: Item 14 – 2026 Capacity Expansion For RTEP Feedback Assumptions Results
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's capacity expansion modeling for the 2034 planning horizon determines generation-mix assumptions that directly affect the transmission needs, resource adequacy targets (ELCC-based), and economic viability of hydro and storage resources competing for capacity value in PJM's footprint.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is conducting capacity-expansion (CapEx) modeling for its 2026 RTEP 8-year case (2034 horizon) to supplement generation assumptions when the system lacks sufficient generation to meet forecast demand (218 GW summer, 205 GW winter peak). The model will select economically optimal resources—solar, wind, hybrid, battery (4–10 hour), CC/CT, and nuclear uprates—from interconnection queues (TC2 and Cycle 1), using ELCC-based resource-adequacy constraints that automatically adjust as resource mix changes. Scenario A.1 (base case) does not build gas in regulated states (NJ, MD, DE, IL, eastern PA) unless projects have ISA/GIA or state agreement backing; Scenario B models policy-driven deactivations and nuclear targets. Results will inform 5-year RTEP solution right-sizing and state commitments, with power flows to be developed for Scenario B. The CapEx approach aligns with FERC Order 1920's long-term regional transmission planning and will evolve over successive cycles. This exercise directly sets the competitive position of hydro (and especially pumped-storage) in PJM's capacity adequacy calculations—any resource sited and given ELCC credit in the 2034 baseline affects scarcity-driven revenue signals and accreditation assumptions that cascade into 5-year capacity-market and real-time dispatch outcomes.

documents.title: Informational Only – 2026 RTEP Assumptions Feedback
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's capacity expansion modeling methodology for RTEP transmission planning directly affects how hydro and storage resources are represented in future transmission needs assessments, which determines transmission investment priorities and interregional transfer capability available to hydro exporters.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is incorporating capacity expansion modeling into its 2026 RTEP cycle to project future generation buildout and inform backbone transmission investments through 2046. CEBA and FirstEnergy submitted feedback on April 20–22, 2026, regarding technical assumptions: CEBA supports the expansion modeling but urges PJM to model broader storage durations (6-, 8-, and 10-hour batteries and longer-duration systems), age-based retirement curves, and sensitivity analysis on natural gas prices and load growth. FirstEnergy requests clarification on whether the model will incorporate PJM's proposed "Connect and Manage" load flexibility framework, which would materially affect where new capacity is sited and thus transmission needs. The methodological choices—especially the treatment of storage technologies, retirement assumptions, and interconnection queue completeness—will shape which transmission corridors PJM prioritizes and whether interregional paths serving hydro exports are reinforced. PJM is scheduled to discuss 8-year modeling results at the May 8, 2026 TEAC meeting.

documents.title: Item 13 – 2026 Capacity Expansion For RTEP Feedback Assumptions Results
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Capacity expansion modeling for PJM's 2034 8-year planning horizon determines which resource types (solar, wind, battery, nuclear, gas, hydro uprates) are economically selected to meet resource adequacy, directly affecting the revenue competitiveness and dispatch value of existing hydro and potential uprate projects in PJM's footprint.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is using capacity expansion (CapEx) modeling to supplement generation assumptions for the 2034 planning horizon (8-year cases), selecting the most economic resources from interconnection queues (TC2, Cycle 1) to meet a 1-in-10 resource adequacy standard. The model employs ELCC curves that adjust dynamically based on resource-mix penetration—reflecting how hydro's capacity value changes with higher renewable penetration—and includes expansion candidates: solar, onshore/offshore wind, hybrid, combustion turbine, combined cycle, batteries (4–10 hour), and nuclear uprates. PJM will use Scenario B (policy-driven deactivations and Maryland nuclear commitments) as the baseline, with sensitivities testing gas siting restrictions, state storage targets, coal retirements, and gas cost impacts. Results will inform 5-year RTEP solution selection and may highlight development potential in northwestern PJM (ComEd zone). The approach aligns with FERC Order 1920 long-term regional transmission planning requirements and will evolve in successive RTEP cycles.

documents.title: Informational Only – PJM Statement On Public Policies Within The 2026 RTEP
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: PJM's 2026 RTEP capacity expansion modeling directly affects transmission investment and resource adequacy assumptions that touch hydro revenue (through load-growth scarcity signals and generation mix shifts), strategic options (resource adequacy constructs and storage targets), and planning obligations for all PJM-region generators including any hydro resources.
documents.ai_summary: PJM issued its statement on public policies incorporated into the 2026 RTEP 8-year case (2034 horizon), detailing which state and federal policies shaped two main scenarios (A.1 and B.1) plus five sensitivities. Scenario B.1 models state RPS targets and battery storage targets, including policy-driven generator deactivations; Scenario A.1 does economic resource-stack optimization without specific RPS modeling. The capacity expansion model assumes 218.3 GW summer and 204.5 GW winter peak load in 2034, forcing addition of solar, wind, gas, battery storage, and nuclear uprates. Storage targets (modeled in Sensitivity A.3 and Scenario B.1) and clean energy policies via generator retirements (Illinois CEJA, New Jersey CO₂ Rule, Virginia Clean Economy Act) reshape the assumed generation fleet PJM uses for transmission needs assessment, altering the scarcity and reserve conditions hydro will operate in and the competing resources for RA accreditation. This is the planning foundation for 2026–2034 transmission and resource-adequacy decisions across PJM.

documents.title: 1 Agenda
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO capacity market and manual updates touching accreditation, intermittency treatment, and modeling rules that directly affect hydro and storage revenue and dispatch obligations.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO Installed Capacity Working Group meeting (June 2, 2026) will address four rule changes: DER market-participation enhancements, ICAP Manual Attachment N updates, revised treatment of intermittency in capacity accreditation, and improved duct-firing modeling for fossil units. The intermittency revisions are most material — they modify how NYISO accredits variable and run-of-river hydro in the capacity market, and how storage is modeled in the reserve-margin calculation. Manual updates will be presented by Sushant Varghese and Vijay Kaki; Potomac Economics will discuss 2025 State of the Market findings, which often include capacity-accreditation trends and hydro-performance analysis. No comment deadline or vote date is stated in this agenda.

documents.title: 2 DER Market Enhancements
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: DER aggregation participation rules directly affect small hydropower owners' ability to participate in NYISO markets under Order 2222 frameworks; the DADSR concept and proposed telemetry/accreditation changes alter the strategic options and compliance burden for hydro resources enrolled in DER aggregations.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO proposes a Day-Ahead Demand-Side Resource (DADSR) variant within its existing DER & Aggregation participation model, designed to accommodate DSR with longer start-up times and limited real-time flexibility. DADSR would bid day-ahead, lock to a fixed real-time schedule, and be ineligible for operating reserves or regulation service; it would qualify for ICAP participation with 2–24-hour energy duration limitations and potentially reduced telemetry requirements (6-second with 30-second scan rates for sub-1 MW facilities versus current standards for 1+ MW). Small hydropower and run-of-river resources enrolled in DER aggregations face a strategic choice: participation under current DER rules (with full telemetry/ancillary-service obligations) or under DADSR (simpler telemetry, day-ahead certainty, but loss of regulation/reserve revenue and real-time flexibility upside). NYISO is also evaluating MNBT and ECBL enhancements and the 10 kW threshold; stakeholder discussion continues at the July ICAPWG/MIWG meeting.

documents.title: 3 ICAP Manual Attachment N Revisions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO is revising Attachment N methodology for calculating translation factors that convert nameplate capacity to unforced capacity (UCAP) for intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources, directly affecting hydro accreditation in resource adequacy and capacity-requirement studies.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO proposes revisions to ICAP Manual Attachment N to implement seasonal (summer and winter) translation factors for intermittent resources and limited-control run-of-river hydro, replacing the current summer-only methodology. The change responds to observed winter Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) beginning in the 2026–2027 IRM study and supports the Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements proposal (filed with FERC 2/18/2026, target implementation 5/1/2027 for the 2027–2028 Capability Year). The proposal also corrects the availability-factor formula to apply proper weighted averaging, aligning calculation with current implementation in IRM, LCR, and deliverability studies. For run-of-river hydro owners, this means winter availability will now drive a separate winter UCAP translation factor based on December–February production patterns weighted by winter LOLE distribution, potentially improving winter accreditation if winter production is high relative to winter scarcity hours, or reducing it if winter is a dry season; the revision will be presented to the Business Issue Committee on 6/10/2026.

documents.title: 4 Balancing Intermittency Manual Updates
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's Uncertainty Reserve Requirement (URR) design directly affects how intermittent resources and ramping/flexibility are procured and priced in both day-ahead and real-time markets, creating new reserve products that hydro will be called upon to supply and creating new demand curves that establish payment terms for flexibility hydro traditionally provides.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is finalizing manual revisions to implement its Uncertainty Reserve Requirement (URR), a new 24-hour reserve product designed to hedge forecast uncertainty for net load, land-based wind, offshore wind, and front-of-meter solar. The design was accepted by FERC in July 2025 (ER25-1998) and is scheduled for deployment in August 2026. The updated manuals clarify URR calculation methodology (incorporated in new Attachment D to the Ancillary Services Manual), specify that URR can be met using 30-Minute Reserve in the day-ahead market, introduce locational demand curves with a new Long Island requirement row (270 MW and 540 MW separately), and refine operating-capacity accounting for seasonal differences. Stakeholder votes are scheduled for June 10–11, 2026 (BIC and OC); comments can be submitted at the June 4 SOAS meeting. For hydro owners, URR creates both a revenue opportunity (new procurement of a flexibility product hydro naturally supplies) and a compliance obligation (must-offer rules in the 30-minute reserve product that backs URR); pumped-storage facilities are particularly positioned to capture value from locational reserve requirements, especially on Long Island where the demand curve now explicitly separates 270 MW and 540 MW tiers.

documents.title: 5 Improve Duct Firing Modeling Manual Updates
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Duct-firing modeling for combined-cycle gas turbines is a gas-generation-specific technical improvement with no direct application to hydropower revenue, obligations, or strategic options.

documents.title: 6 2025 State of the Market Report
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's 2025 State of the Market Report addresses capacity-market design, seasonal accreditation, resource-adequacy signals, and locational pricing — all mechanisms that directly shape revenue for hydro and pumped-storage resources in NYISO and influence investment and retention decisions.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Market Monitoring Unit (Potomac Economics) identifies structural gaps in the capacity market that undervalue reliability contributions and distort investment incentives. Key findings: (1) demand curves based on 2-hour battery cost understate Net CONE, risking premature retirements even as load grows; (2) seasonal incentives are misaligned—firm fuel/UDR elections locked 16 months in advance don't respond to winter market prices, and elected capacity for 2026/27 (15.2 GW) exceeds planning assumptions (12.3 GW), creating cost-allocation risk; (3) transmission security is undercompensated—downstate LCRs increasingly bind at floor, overpaying bottled generation (SCRs, large units) and underpaying non-firm gas units while creating $74M annual inefficiency post-CHPE. Recommendations filed or pending include raising the demand-curve floor (Rec. 2025-1a), moving seasonal-election deadlines closer to winter (Rec. 2022-2), introducing locational CAFs (Rec. 2025-2), and expanding capacity zones. These touch pumped-storage accreditation (2-hour battery CONE undermines longer-duration PSH value), seasonal energy adequacy (firm fuel elections timing), and locational value recognition (hydro deliverability and transmission-security contribution). No formal comment deadline is stated in this highlights presentation, but the full 2025 SOM Report (posted May 19, 2026) and tariff filing ER26-1431 are the operative documents.

documents.title: 4c Ancillary Services Manual Revisions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's Ancillary Services Manual revisions directly govern how hydropower and pumped storage are compensated and must perform for regulation, frequency response, voltage support, and operating reserves — the core revenue streams and operational obligations for dispatchable hydro and PSH.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO issued a draft Ancillary Services Manual (v10.0, effective May 1, 2026) with revisions to regulation and frequency response service, voltage support, operating reserves, and energy imbalance rules. The manual specifies qualification criteria, performance-tracking methods, settlement mechanics, and testing procedures for generators, synchronous condensers, HVDC with voltage-source converter (VSC) technology, and energy storage resources providing these services. Hydro and PSH operators must comply with reactive capability testing protocols (section 3.6), regulation service prequalification and performance audits (section 4.11), and operating-reserve supplier eligibility and day-ahead/real-time bidding rules (sections 6.2–6.4). This is a working draft for discussion at the Installed Capacity Working Group (committee acceptance 04/15/2026, OC acceptance 04/16/2026); the effective date is May 1, 2026, making this a compliance-critical update for all NYISO hydro and storage participants.

documents.title: 4b Day Ahead Scheduling Manual Revisions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Day-ahead scheduling manual revisions affect hydro's energy commitment rules, storage constraint evaluation processes, and ancillary service demand curve participation—core elements of revenue and operational flexibility in the day-ahead market.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO has issued revisions to its Day-Ahead Scheduling Manual (Manual 11, effective 10/29/2024, accepted by BIC 08/14/2024 and OC 08/15/2024). The manual governs SCUC initialization, unit commitment of dispatchable resources including hydropower, energy storage resource constraint evaluation, ancillary service demand curves, and bilateral transaction evaluations that directly shape day-ahead market dispatch and pricing. For hydro owners, key mechanics documented include scheduling of 'must run' resources, multiple response rates for resources, energy storage limits constraints, and limiting participation constraints—all of which define the operational envelope within which hydro's dispatch and AS offerings are optimized. Hydro and PSH operators rely on these rules to understand how their bids enter unit commitment and how their flexibility is valued in the scheduling process.

documents.title: 3b ICAP Appendix N clean
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Attachment N establishes the procedure to calculate translation factors for intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources in NYISO's capacity market accreditation, directly affecting how hydro capacity is credited and valued in NYISO's ICAP auctions.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Installed Capacity Manual Attachment N specifies the methodology for calculating translation factors that convert nameplate capacity into unforced capacity (UCAP) for intermittent power resources and limited-control run-of-river hydro. This accreditation procedure determines how much capacity credit variable hydro can claim in NYISO's ICAP auctions and capacity showings. The document also references Attachment M (Energy Limited Resource and Energy Duration Limitation procedures) and Attachment D (Dependable Maximum Net Capability test procedures including a hydro-generation form), indicating NYISO uses performance-data-driven methods to credential hydro's contribution to resource adequacy. For run-of-river and variable-output hydro operators in NYISO, the translation-factor calculation directly sets the capacity value realized in auctions and affects their RA contribution and revenue.

documents.title: 3a ICAP Appendix N redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Attachment N directly governs how NYISO calculates capacity accreditation for intermittent and run-of-river hydro resources—a core revenue determinant—and the redline signals revision to those translation-factor procedures.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO Capacity Market Products is revising Appendix N of the Installed Capacity Manual (draft for discussion, issued June 2, 2026). Attachment N contains the procedure to calculate translation factors for intermittent power resources and limited-control run-of-river hydro, which convert nameplate capacity into unforced capacity (the quantity offered into NYISO's ICAP auction). Changes to these translation factors directly alter the megawatt-accreditation—and thus auction revenue—that run-of-river and variable hydro operators can claim. The document is in draft-for-discussion phase with the Installed Capacity Working Group; the redline indicates active revision, though no comment deadline or vote date is stated in this excerpt.

documents.title: 4a T&D Manual Revisions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual revision affects hydro compliance obligations: reserve calculation, regulation requirements, outage scheduling, dispatch procedures, and real-time commitment processes that directly govern how hydropower units are monitored, operated, and settled in the market.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is issuing a revised Transmission and Dispatch Operations Manual (Manual 12) effective May 1, 2026, following committee acceptance on April 15–16, 2026. The manual covers operational monitoring (real-time data, security assessment, reserve and regulation requirements), transmission operations (operating limits, voltage control, phase angle regulators), real-time commitment and mitigation processes, and scheduling procedures. Hydro operators must comply with the manual's reserve calculation methodology, regulation-requirement definition, outage scheduling processes, and real-time dispatch instruction protocols—changes to any of these affect when hydro units are called on, how their output is metered and settled, and the operational flexibility they must maintain. The document's status as draft-for-discussion suggests revisions are still in circulation; the June 2, 2026 Installed Capacity Working Group meeting will likely discuss final language.

The report catalogs 56 GIP projects across transmission voltage levels (138 kV and 345 kV) with expected in-service dates through 2030 and estimated costs ranging from $0.1M to $50M per project; notably, eight battery energy-storage projects appear in the interconnection queue (Mulligan Storage, Phoebe Energy Storage, Moonshine Storage, San Jose BESS, River Ferry Battery Storage, Edwards 3 BESS, and BRP Rebel BESS), reflecting MISO's growing load-driven interconnection activity. The document serves as a public record of queue advancement and transmission-system expansion planning that establishes the backbone for all regional generation deliverability, including pumped-storage and conventional hydro seeking to serve or export within the MISO footprint. documents.ai_summary: MISO posted a status update on its Central region Definitive Planning Phase interconnection studies as of May 26, 2026, covering four active DPP cycles (2021–2025) with cost estimates for network upgrades (ERIS, NRIS, Interconnection Facility, LPC) required to integrate queued generation. DPP-2023-Central Phase 1 is final at $36.493B in network-upgrade costs; DPP-2022-Central Phase 1 is final at $17.097B; DPP-2025-Central Phase 1 (in progress) shows $4.174B to date. Phases 2 and 3 of multiple DPP cycles remain in progress with no cost estimates yet available. For hydro and storage developers with projects in MISO's Central queue, these cost and schedule milestones directly determine their interconnection-facility costs, network-upgrade obligation shares, and timeline to energization; the lack of Phase 2/3 estimates for recent DPP cycles creates scheduling uncertainty for later-queued projects. The document is post-only with no pending comment deadline or vote.

documents.filename: 20260602 CSPM Item 03 MISO26 Series Model Development Update.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MISO's MISO26 series model development—including powerflow, dynamics, and resource adequacy modeling—establishes the baseline simulation inputs for Generation Interconnection studies, SSR (System Support Resource) studies, LOLE (Loss of Load Expectation) studies, and PRA (Planning Resource Auction) studies that directly determine hydro plant accreditation, transmission deliverability, and seasonal capacity value.
documents.ai_summary: MISO reports completion of MISO26 powerflow models and ongoing development of MISO26 dynamic models (prefinal review in early June, final release mid-June) and initiation of ERAG MMWG 2026 powerflow modeling in June 2026. These model series are foundational inputs to Generation Interconnection queue studies, SSR retirement assessments, Loss of Load Expectation (LOLE) studies that set Planning Reserve Margin requirements, and the Planning Resource Auction (PRA) that determines resource adequacy obligations and seasonal accreditation. The models include wind and solar capacity-credit assumptions (18.2% wind, 50% solar in base cases) that drive the effective capacity value assigned to all dispatchable resources including hydro in capacity and adequacy determinations. Hydro owners relying on RA showings, interconnection queue progress, or SSR/retirement studies should monitor data requests: MISO issued dynamics data requests on March 28 with April 25 deadline (now passed); powerflow trial 1 information is due to Powertech in late June and MISO will email member requests for required updates.

documents.ai_summary: MISO's MTEP26 transmission planning cycle, entering subregional planning meetings in June 2026, targets 574 new Appendix A projects (190 in the Central region) with $4 billion in Expedited Project Review (EPR) investment as of May 2026. The planning process uses NERC reliability standards (TPL-001-5, FAC-013/014, PRC-023) across six analytical dimensions: steady-state, transient, voltage stability, transfer capability, nuclear plant, and deliverability assessment for 2/5/10-year and 20+ year horizons. For hydro owners, the scope is material: load-growth-driven transmission projects directly alter export pathways and scarcity signals that determine when hydro units clear energy and ancillary-services markets; interconnection of new generation (especially in Central region with a sharp uptick in Generator Interconnection requests) shifts locational marginal prices and ramping needs; and large-load additions (data centers, electrification) raise baseline energy scarcity and extend the value horizon for flexible dispatchable resources. The MTEP Portal is the single source of project data; stakeholder feedback windows close September 15 and October 27, with final Board approval by December 3, 2026.

documents.ai_summary: MISO reports completion of MISO26 powerflow models and progress on dynamics models (pre-final review in early June, final release mid-June), which feed MTEP, PRA (Planning Resource Auction / resource adequacy), generation-interconnection queue studies, and Eastern Interconnection reliability assessments (ERAG MMWG). These models incorporate wind and solar capacity credits (18.2% wind, 50% solar in base cases) and sensitivity scenarios across 2028–2036 with varying load and renewable-dispatch assumptions. The model series is foundational to MISO's seasonal adequacy studies and transmission-deliverability analysis—hydro owners rely on these inputs to understand seasonal scarcity conditions, interregional transfer constraints, and system needs driving future ancillary-service and energy prices. Member feedback on pre-final dynamics models is due mid-June; stakeholders should monitor for model assumptions (e.g., renewable dispatch profiles, hydro availability representation) that may misstate seasonal energy adequacy or undervalue dispatchable resources.

documents.title: 20260602 ESPM Item 01c Agenda.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MTEP26 reliability planning and generation interconnection studies directly shape transmission expansion and resource adequacy constructs that affect hydro deliverability, strategic siting of new PSH, and long-term portfolio value.
documents.ai_summary: MISO's East Subregional Planning Meeting on June 2, 2026 will review MTEP26 (Midcontinent Transmission Expansion Plan) model development, reliability planning study updates, and generation interconnection study plans. The reliability study will cover scope, schedule, and proposed projects, with stakeholder input solicited. MTEP26 is MISO's primary vehicle for identifying and prioritizing transmission reinforcements that determine where new generation—including hydro and PSH—can be economically sited and what locational energy and reserve value existing hydro plants can capture. Hydro owners depend on these studies to forecast transmission constraints that drive scarcity signals and to identify opportunities for storage-as-transmission projects or to upgrade interconnection at existing plants.

documents.title: 20260602 ESPM Minutes 20260210.pdf
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MTEP26 transmission planning process identifies large load growth and generator interconnection as drivers of $8.8B in proposed projects; load growth and interconnection queue developments directly affect hydro asset deliverability and scarcity value.
documents.ai_summary: MISO held its East Subregional Planning Meeting (SPM1) on February 10, 2026, to preview MTEP26 transmission planning scope. MTEP26 encompasses $8.8B in proposed projects across local, regional, and interregional needs, with the East Region alone proposing 102 projects totaling $1.123B—dominated by age/condition work ($426M, 70%) but with sharp growth in generator interconnection projects. Notably, Expedited Project Review (EPR) requests are driven primarily by load growth spanning the entire MISO footprint. The active generator interconnection queue consists of 754 projects (East Region ITC: 131 projects with 27.38k MW). Large load additions are listed as a core reliability study item alongside interregional planning with PJM and SPP. Next steps: MISO will perform independent reliability assessments of proposed projects and alternatives; stakeholders and TOs should continue using the MTEP Portal and provide input on proposed projects. SPM2 is scheduled for June 2, 2026. For hydro owners, the surge in load-growth-driven transmission expansion and interconnection activity signals increasing scarcity value and potential transmission constraints on hydro export capability; the portfolio of projects affecting Michigan (a key hydro-adjacent region) warrants tracking through completion.

documents.title: Item 02 – 1 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 13 Revision 98 establishes emergency operating procedures affecting how hydropower and other dispatchable resources participate in PJM's capacity and transmission emergency protocols, plus introduces a new Capacity Advisory mechanism that signals resource scarcity — both touch hydro operators' obligations to respond to emergency directives and may alter revenue opportunities during stress periods.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is revising Manual 13 (Emergency Operations) to clarify its roles as Reliability Coordinator, Balancing Authority, and Transmission Operator under NERC EOP-011. The revision introduces a new Capacity Advisory (Section 2.3) to provide advance notice when forecasted available capacity approaches the operating requirement — designed to address gaps in temperature-based emergency triggers and expected to increase in frequency as demand growth outpaces generation interconnection. Revision 98 also establishes Attachment O, a conditional procedure to mobilize backup generation at large data-center loads during extreme emergencies (triggered only if the DOE issues a Section 202(c) order); this procedure was activated during Winter Storm Fern (January 2026) but not implemented. The revision cleanly separates transmission-security emergency procedures from capacity-based procedures, removes capacity-related actions from transmission protocols, and updates language for Advanced Scheduled Resources in cold/hot weather alerts. Endorsement is sought before the summer operating period; the timeline shows second reads at the MRC on June 24, 2026. The Capacity Advisory may increase call frequency for emergency ramping from dispatchable hydro, while data-center load growth (now formally recognized in procedures) creates both scarcity signals that favor hydro dispatch and potential operational constraints if backup-gen mobilization competes for transmission or reserve capacity.

documents.title: Item 02 – 2 Manual 13 – Revision 98 – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 13 governs PJM emergency operations including load-shedding procedures, transmission security emergencies, and resource-limitation reporting that directly affect how dispatchable hydro and pumped-storage resources must respond to system stress and what operating obligations they face.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 13 Revision 98 (effective July 23, 2026) updates emergency-operations procedures across capacity emergencies, weather/environmental events, and transmission security. Key changes include: restructured transmission-security emergency procedures that no longer trigger PAIs or EEAs; split of Maximum Generation Emergency into separate Transmission Security–Local Maximum Generation Emergency Alert and Load Management Alert; addition of Capacity Advisory to Section 2.3; updated Advanced Schedule Resources language for cold and hot weather alerts; and new Attachment O on emergency back-up generator use. For hydro and pumped-storage operators, the procedure changes govern mandatory response protocols during transmission constraints (Section 5.2) and capacity shortages (Section 2), and clarify which emergency triggers obligate generation or discharge (e.g., load-shedding directives, transmission relief actions). The removal of PAI/EEA linkage to transmission-security procedures may reduce frequency of certain dispatch signals to storage resources during local constraints. Pumped-storage operators should review Section 2 (reserve and capacity procedures), Section 5.2 (transmission-security load and generation actions), and Attachments E–F (manual load-dump allocation) to confirm charging/discharging obligations under updated protocols.

documents.title: second revised straw proposal for the Storage Design and Modeling – Outage Management Topic Group
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO's proposal to model lithium-ion battery nonlinearity (foldback) and enhance outage-management systems directly affects how pumped-storage hydro and other dispatchable storage resources represent their operating constraints and state-of-charge limits in the market model, with interim workarounds and spring-2027 implementation affecting dispatch feasibility and outage reporting obligations.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO proposes two major changes to storage outage management: (1) developing new Master File fields and constraints to model nonlinearity (foldback) in lithium-ion batteries near state-of-charge limits, expected for implementation no earlier than spring 2027; until then, market participants must either avoid the nonlinear range in filings or submit plant-trouble outage cards to signal unavailability; and (2) four Outage Management System enhancements, prioritizing automatic acceptance of outage-change requests and power-minimum rerating for charging tests in 2026, with future improvements to out-of-service checkboxes and partial-outage submissions for ancillary services. The proposal applies specifically to non-generator resource participation model storage (excluding pumped storage and proxy demand resources), but the nonlinearity and outage-reporting logic will inform broader storage-design work. Comments are due June 1, 2026.

documents.title: 2026-05-29 IPSAC A02.2 ISO-NE Regional Planning Needs and Solutions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: transmission-planning decisions and constraint analyses affect hydro deliverability, resource adequacy valuation, and revenue-generating scarcity conditions; Loss of Source study baseline, LTTP RFP evaluation, and area studies (Connecticut 2034, daytime minimum load) directly shape transmission availability and locational scarcity signals that determine hydro's economic dispatch and reserve deployment value.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE's May 2026 regional planning update covers several transmission-planning initiatives affecting New England hydro deliverability and resource adequacy: (1) Loss of Source study with PJM and NYISO concluded without achieving full objectives but established new baseline for system constraints in neighboring RTOs; report anticipated Q2/Q3 2026. (2) 2025 Longer-Term Transmission Planning (LTTP) RFP evaluation ongoing, final determination by September 2026; stakeholder discussions on FERC Order 1920 compliance (interregional transmission coordination) paused pending RFP completion, resuming Q3 2026 with June 14, 2027 compliance-filing deadline. (3) Connecticut 2034 Solutions Study underway with preferred solution anticipated late 2026; 2035 Daytime Minimum Load Needs Assessment begins post-May 2026 CELT report release, base-case library Q3 2026. These constraint-relief and long-lead-time transmission projects directly affect hydro's ability to deliver reserve and energy products into neighboring markets and hydro's valuation in resource-adequacy showings; owners relying on New England hydro for interregional exports or RA accreditation should monitor LTTP RFP vendor evaluation (financial viability, construction feasibility) and Order 1920 stakeholder discussions resuming Q3 2026.

documents.title: 2026-05-29 IPSAC A02.1 PJM Regional Transmission Expansion Planning Process
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: TRANSMISSION PLANNING: PJM's 2025 RTEP window selected backbone transmission projects (765 kV lines, 500 kV HVDC) and load-growth driver analysis affecting interregional transfer capability and hydro deliverability in Mid-Atlantic, South/Dominion, and PJM West regions; hydro owners exporting or operating within these zones depend on these backbone routes for revenue realization.
documents.ai_summary: PJM completed evaluation of 134 proposals (57 greenfields, 77 upgrades) submitted in its 2025 Reliability Window 1 and selected regional backbone solutions totaling ~$11.7 billion: a 222-mile 765 kV line from Kammer–Buttermilk–Juniata (Mid-Atlantic), a 185-mile 500 kV HVDC line from Heritage–Mosby (South/Dominion), and three 765/345 kV lines in PJM West (Greentown–Teddy–Marysville, Guernsey–Conesville–West Millersport–Adkins, and 345 kV laterals). The primary drivers are load growth in PPL zone (~5–8.5 GW cumulative), data-center load external to Dominion, and southbound generation flowing to northern VA load centers, all creating 500 kV capacity violations on north–south corridors by 2030–2032. The next FERC 1000 window opens July 2026 with proposal close August/September 2026 and board approval targeted October 2026–February 2027. For hydro operators exporting from the South or West into load-heavy Mid-Atlantic, these backbone augmentations directly increase transmission import capacity and firm locational revenue signals; conversely, the selection of HVDC on the Heritage–Mosby corridor (rather than purely AC) creates a new constraint on traditional hydro exports via 500 kV AC paths.

documents.title: 2026-05-29 IPSAC A02.3 NYISO Updates on Comprehensive System Planning Process
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's system planning process and reliability assessments directly affect hydro and storage dispatch needs, transmission-constrained resource valuations, and generator deactivation/RMR decisions that reshape the market environment for peaking and flexible resources.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO presented its 2026–2027 Reliability Planning Process cycle, including the 2026 Reliability Needs Assessment (covering 2030–2036) and the 2027–2036 Comprehensive Reliability Plan. The 2025 Q3 and 2026 Q1 Short-Term Reliability Assessments (STARs) identified persistent deficiencies in New York City (Zone J) and Long Island (Zone K) that depend on continued operation of fossil peakers (Gowanus, Narrows, Far Rockaway, Danskammer) until transmission solutions energize or demand-side/storage alternatives materialize. Generator deactivations are being withdrawn or held in Interim Service Provider status pending solution entry. These assessments directly affect which generation assets receive RMR contracts or withdrawal approval, which in turn shapes the scarcity pricing, transmission congestion, and reserve-procurement environment in which hydro and storage must compete. Any expansion of transmission or storage-as-transmission alternatives in the planning scenarios could displace fossil peakers and alter the revenue drivers for hydro ramping and energy supply in the constrained zones.

documents.title: 2026-05-29 IPSAC A03 Queue Interconnection Studies Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Interconnection queue status and interregional coordination affects hydro deliverability, project timing, and transmission access for existing and prospective hydro and storage resources seeking to interconnect or upgrade in ISO-NE, NYISO, and PJM.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE reports on interconnection projects in ISO-NE, NYISO, and PJM queues with potential interregional effects, coordinating studies across RTO borders through the Joint ISO/RTO Planning Committee. The document catalogs active and pending generation and transmission projects (effective queue status as of April 1, 2026), including battery storage, HVDC imports, and solar resources in NYISO and PJM with impacts on neighboring systems. NYISO's Transition Cluster Study Phase 2 is underway with next cluster study anticipated July 31, 2026; this queue coordination directly affects timing and cost allocation for any hydro or pumped-storage project seeking interconnection in the three interconnected RTOs, particularly large-scale storage and transmission upgrades that require multi-RTO impact assessment.

documents.title: 2026-05-29 IPSAC A04 FERC Order 1920 Compliance Filing Dates
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document is a compliance-filing calendar for FERC Order 1920 across RTOs; it establishes procedural deadlines but does not address any hydro-specific or storage-specific market-design mechanism, revenue rule, operational requirement, or strategic option.
documents.ai_summary: This document presents expected filing dates for FERC Order 1920 compliance across multiple RTOs (ISO-NE, NYISO, PJM, MISO, SERTP). Order 1920 addresses interregional transmission coordination and planning, which directly affects hydropower resources' ability to access markets and transmit power across regional boundaries. Hydropower facilities depend on these coordination frameworks for reliable interconnection and market access.

documents.title: 2026-05-29 IPSAC A05 IPSAC NCSP25
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Interregional transmission planning and coordination directly affect hydro deliverability, resource adequacy needs assessment, and the strategic question of whether interregional transmission investments (vs. regional solutions) will shape hydro's revenue opportunities and grid export capacity in the Northeast.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE, NYISO, and PJM completed their 2025 coordinated interregional transmission planning cycle under FERC Order 1000, with draft results posted in early April 2026 and stakeholder comments due April 20–27, 2026. The planning process found no interregional transmission projects that are more efficient or cost-effective than separate regional solutions, meaning each ISO will pursue its own system needs independently rather than jointly. This outcome is significant for hydro owners because it confirms the near-term transmission topology will remain within existing regional footprints; interregional transmission constraints will not be relaxed by new joint projects, and the value of existing hydro resources for intraregional scarcity and export will depend on each ISO's separate needs assessments and queue development. The final NCSP25 report is pending posting.

documents.title: 2026-05-29 IPSAC A01 Agenda & Admin
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Interregional transmission coordination (Order 1000 compliance and IPSAC process) affects hydro deliverability across Northeast ISO/RTO seams; agenda includes interconnection queue coordination and regional planning needs that directly shape where hydro projects can interconnect and export power.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE's Planning Advisory Committee hosts the May 29, 2026 IPSAC (Interregional Planning Stakeholder Advisory Committee) meeting covering Order 1000 interregional coordination, interconnection queue status, and long-term firm transmission requests across PJM, ISO-NE, and NYISO. The meeting will present coordinated studies on regional transmission needs, discuss potential interregional solutions, and update stakeholder input on projects with cross-border impact. For hydro and PSH owners with assets in or exporting through the Northeast ISO/RTO footprint, this process determines which transmission projects get coordinated study and informs the collective regional planning that gates new interconnections and export capability — particularly relevant for PSH seeking to move energy across seams or conventional hydro seeking firm transmission service for seasonal exports.

documents.title: Resource Interconnection Standards Clarification Matrix - Interconnection Process Enhancements 5.0 - May 18, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Interconnection queue reform and deliverability rules directly affect hydro and PSH access to transmission and capacity markets; this tariff clarification may alter how existing and new hydro/storage facilities achieve commercial operation and interconnection cost allocation.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's Interconnection Process Enhancements 5.0 (IPE 5.0) presents a Resource Interconnection Standards Clarification Matrix to standardize technical and procedural requirements for queue projects, with draft tariff language in development as of May 18, 2026. The matrix appears to codify interconnection study methodology, equipment standards, and milestone-timing rules that apply to all resources seeking grid connection, including conventional hydro uprates and pumped-storage facility interconnection. Hydro and PSH owners will be affected by any changes to study timelines, cost allocation for network upgrades, and deliverability or surplus-interconnection-service pathways; the matrix's scope and specific tariff amendments require detailed review at the comment deadline.

documents.title: Draft Tariff Language Redline - Interconnection Process Enhancements 5.0 - May 18, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: CAISO's Interconnection Process Enhancements 5.0 tariff redline directly affects how new and uprated hydro and pumped-storage projects enter the queue, obtain surplus interconnection service, and secure deliverability — a core strategic option for NHA members.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO has issued draft tariff language for Interconnection Process Enhancements 5.0, a comprehensive queue-reform initiative addressing interconnection procedures, deliverability assignments, and surplus interconnection service rules. The redline reflects changes to study methodologies, queue management, and treatment of existing-plant uprates and new resources competing for transmission capacity. Comments are due as of the meeting date (May 28, 2026). Hydro and PSH owners must evaluate whether the revisions improve or constrain their ability to (1) uprate existing generating stations, (2) move projects through the queue faster, and (3) secure transmission deliverability for storage discharge and pumping cycles; the tariff language governs the mechanics of interconnection requests, feasibility studies, and system-impact assessments that directly determine whether a project becomes economically viable.

documents.title: Draft Transmission Planning Technical Guide Rev 8.5 (redline)
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Technical guide establishes modeling and study procedures for transmission planning that directly affect how hydropower and energy storage are represented in interconnection and system-adequacy studies—including generator models, maximum power ratings, reactive capability, and stability criteria that shape deliverability assessments and resource-adequacy accreditation for hydro and PSH.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE's Transmission Planning Technical Guide (Rev. 8.5, effective 9/30/2025) establishes baseline modeling procedures and criteria for all system planning studies in ISO-NE. The guide includes dedicated sections on conventional hydro generation (§2.3.7), energy storage systems (§2.3.8), and their representation in steady-state and transient stability analyses. Section 2.3.1–2.3.5 cover generator maximum power rating types, models, real power and reactive power ratings—parameters that directly determine how hydro and PSH capacity is credited in resource-adequacy and interconnection studies. The guide also specifies how dispersed energy resources (DER) are modeled and their treatment in transmission element loading calculations (§3.3.5), relevant to small and run-of-river hydro participation through aggregation. This is a technical reference document in active revision; further updates are noted as continuing.

documents.title: 2026-05-27 PAC A05.0 2025 LTTP RFP – RFP Objective Follow-up
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: TRANSMISSION PLANNING: ISO-NE's 2025 Longer-Term Transmission Planning (LTTP) RFP evaluation directly affects hydro export capability and interregional transfer limits (Maine–New Hampshire and Surowiec–South interfaces), which constrain the locational value and revenue of New England hydropower and determine whether transmission upgrades will be required to relieve scarcity.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE evaluated six Longer-Term Transmission Plan proposals submitted in response to NESCOE's RFP; two proposals (A1, A2) passed RFP Objectives testing (thermal, voltage, and transient stability at ≥3000 MW Maine–New Hampshire and ≥3200 MW Surowiec–South transfer limits) and proceed to No Adverse Impact Screening and Benefits Calculation, while four proposals (B1, C1, D1, D2) failed stability or voltage criteria and remain excluded. After follow-up reviews requested by proposal sponsors, ISO-NE confirmed that C1 failed transient stability testing due to insufficient dynamic reactive support, and D1/D2 sponsors acknowledged instability under single-fault scenarios; both determinations remain unchanged. ISO-NE expects to present its preliminary preferred Longer-Term Transmission Solution to PAC by September 2026. For hydro owners, the outcome determines which transmission projects advance to benefits evaluation — projects that increase Maine–New Hampshire or Surowiec–South transfer limits would reduce congestion and increase the marginal value of New England hydro's energy sales southward; rejection of four proposals may delay relief of these critical constraints.

documents.title: Draft Transmission Planning Technical Guide Rev 8.5 (clean)
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Transmission planning technical guide directly affects hydropower deliverability, interconnection modeling, and system planning study treatment of conventional hydro and energy storage resources — foundational to how hydro assets are rated, tested, and accommodated in the network.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE has issued Revision 8.5 of its Transmission Planning Technical Guide, effective September 30, 2025. The guide establishes modeling requirements and study protocols for the region's resource mix, including dedicated sections on conventional hydro generation (2.3.7) and energy storage systems (2.3.8), along with steady-state, transient stability, and short-circuit criteria used in transmission needs assessments. Changes to modeling conventions, reactive power requirements, stability test protocols, or resource-rating methodologies in this revision directly impact how existing hydro plants and new storage projects are characterized in interconnection studies, deliverability analyses, and system planning — affecting both operational flexibility valuations and accreditation inputs to New England's capacity market. The guide is now in effect; any comments on the revision history or requests for clarification should be directed to ISO-NE Planning.

documents.title: 2026-05-27 PAC A00 Agenda
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This is a meeting agenda with no substantive content on hydro/PSH revenue mechanisms, compliance obligations, or strategic options — it lists only transmission asset-management projects and study-update briefings with no market-design implications stated.
documents.ai_summary: The PAC meeting includes transmission planning study assumption updates and 2026 economic study benchmark scenarios that establish foundational parameters for evaluating generation resources including hydropower. Transmission owner asset management reviews may also affect transmission access for hydro facilities.
