documents.title: 2026-05-27 PAC A04.0 2026 Economic Study - Benchmark Scenario Assumptions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The 2026 Economic Study's benchmark scenario uses historical hydro operations data and models hydro capacity, efficiency, and dispatch; the study also models energy storage (including pumped storage at 74% efficiency) and ancillary services procurement, which affect revenue recognition and reserve accreditation for both resources.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is conducting its 2026 Economic Study to model New England's resource needs, transmission limitations, and emission profiles over a 10-year horizon. The benchmark scenario (preliminary results Q3 2026, final by December 2027) uses 2025 historical hydro operations data integrated into PLEXOS to determine monthly max/min capacity and energy availability; pumped storage is modeled at 74% efficiency with a $3/MWh VOM cost, and both hydro and storage are assigned reserve capabilities (10-minute spinning, 10-minute non-spinning, 30-minute operating reserves). The study will also examine potential System Efficiency Needs through competitive solicitation, which could affect transmission interconnection and deliverability. Stakeholders may submit requests for additional sensitivity analyses by June 2026.

documents.title: 2026-05-27 PAC A03.0 2026 TPTG Update Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: SATOA (Storage as a Transmission Only Asset) definition and planning-study treatment directly affects how pumped-storage and other dispatchable storage resources participate in transmission planning and potentially compete for transmission-backed revenue or non-wires-alternative roles.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE has updated its Transmission Planning Technical Guide (TPTG) to formally document Storage as a Transmission Only Asset (SATOA) — energy storage devices at 115 kV or higher used to address transmission reliability needs post-N-1-1 contingencies and to provide continuous reactive power support like synchronous condensers. SATOA treatment was accepted by FERC on October 19, 2023, and is now being incorporated into planning-study methodology in TPTG Section 2.3.8.1, with conforming changes to Section 2.3.8. This formalizes a pathway for storage (including pumped storage) to compete as a transmission solution in ISO-NE's competitive solutions process alongside traditional wires and other non-wires alternatives, potentially creating revenue streams for PSH owners who can locate facilities to address identified transmission needs. Comments on the draft TPTG are due by June 10, 2026.

documents.title: Joint Letter on LTTP Solicitation to the PAC
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: HVDC transmission expansion and black-start capability affect hydro's regional transmission access, cost recovery, and operational flexibility value; this RFP dispute touches the governance process through which major transmission investments that reshape hydro's market are selected.
documents.ai_summary: A coalition including National Grid Ventures, New England Power Company, and PowerBridge submitted two HVDC-based transmission proposals to ISO-NE's inaugural Longer-Term Transmission Planning (LTTP) solicitation but was excluded from further evaluation after failing stability analysis in March 2026. The coalition disputes ISO-NE's rejection of technical mitigation measures (adding an isolated circuit and transformer to the converter station), arguing the tariff allows non-material modifications and that iterative refinement is standard practice. The broader stakes: all expanded HVDC solutions were eliminated, leaving only HVAC alternatives—eliminating HVDC's claimed benefits of black-start capability, bi-directional power-flow control, and future cost reduction. For hydro owners, HVDC transmission expansion directly affects regional scarcity pricing, deliverability of remote hydro resources, and operational flexibility value; the coalition's exclusion narrows the solutions competitive set and may reduce the scope of future regional investments in transmission features that benefit dispatchable resources.

documents.title: Item 01 – 1 Rmr Resources – Tariff Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: Tariff revisions to PJM's RMR (Retained for Reliability) resource capacity-market treatment—specifically, how retained resources clear auctions, accredit UCAP, and receive capacity-market revenues—directly affect hydro and other dispatchable units that PJM may retain to address transmission reliability, and establish the revenue and settlement rules governing their participation in the capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: PJM staff have redlined tariff language (Attachment DD, section 5.3) governing Retained-for-Reliability (RMR) generation resources' participation in the capacity market (RPM auctions and accreditation). The proposal clarifies that RMR resources not clearing an auction on their own offer are deemed to submit a $0/MW-Day Sell Offer in the Base Residual Auction for their full Accredited UCAP (up to Capacity Interconnection Rights), provided they are deliverable, operationally available, and not subject to bilateral restrictions. Any accreditation increase post-auction is automatically offered in the Third Incremental Auction at $0/MW-Day; if accreditation decreases or the resource no longer meets RMR criteria, PJM procures replacement capacity. Capacity-market revenues from RMR units are credited to the load zones paying for the resource's continuing operations under its FERC-accepted rate schedule, rather than to the RMR unit operator; credits cease if the rate schedule expires, the unit deactivates, or catastrophic failure occurs. This provision extends through the 2029/2030 Delivery Year unless FERC accepts an extension. Retained dispatchable and storage resources relying on RMR rate schedules—including some legacy hydro units—will have their capacity revenues redirected to load-serving entities paying for reliability upgrades, fundamentally altering the revenue model for RMR-designated plants. The tariff change appears to be in draft form (redline status suggests it has not yet cleared stakeholder review or FERC).

documents.title: Item 01 – 2 Brandon Shores 12 And Wagner 34 Deactivation And Rmr – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: RMR (Reliability Must Run) arrangement extending beyond planned deactivation date directly affects hydro and thermal generator retirement decisions and the transmission-investment timeline that determines when generating resources become dispatchable rather than must-run — a core strategic option for fleet planning.
documents.ai_summary: PJM has assessed whether Brandon Shores Units 1&2 (635 MW + 638 MW, BGE-owned, Maryland) and Wagner Units 3&4 (305 MW + 397 MW, BGE-owned, Maryland) can safely deactivate on their current RMR end date of May 31, 2029. Planning studies found thermal and voltage violations would persist through 2030 even with baseline transmission projects in service, because several major 500 kV and 765 kV lines required for reliability (e.g., Morrisville–Vint Hill–Wishing Star, North Delta–High Ridge, Peach Bottom–Conastone rebuilds, Amos-Welton–Spring–Rocky Point, Joshua Falls–Yeat) do not reach in-service dates until mid-to-late 2030 or 2031. PJM's recommendation is to extend RMR beyond May 2029 through at least 2030 to maintain reliability until these transmission enhancements complete; next steps are RMR agreement negotiation and FERC filing. This determines whether 1,975 MW of generation will remain must-run (and thus unavailable for economic dispatch or retirement) well past currently assumed end dates — a precedent affecting all generators assessing retirement timing in constrained transmission zones.

title: Comments due - Extended Day-Ahead Market (EDAM) Market Update
meeting_hydro_reason: EDAM is a regional market expansion that affects hydropower participation in multi-state energy markets and market rules for energy scheduling, dispatch, and resource coordination that directly impact dispatchable hydro operations.

documents.title: Presentation - Market Update - May 14, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: EDAM market-performance reporting directly affects hydropower revenue through price formation, ancillary-services procurement, and interregional transfer mechanics that shape scarcity signals and flexibility product value across the expanded West.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO reports May 2026 EDAM operational performance across California, PacifiCorp East, and PacifiCorp West balancing areas, including price trends in energy, ancillary services (Imbalance Reserve Up/Down, Reliability Capacity), GHG, and real-time markets, plus early-stage observations on congestion and net-export constraints. The presentation documents stable RSE passage, GHG prices in the $10–20/MWh band, IRU/IRD price separation reflecting footprint economics, and Reliability Capacity procurement weighted toward morning/evening ramping periods—illustrating how hydro's flexibility is being priced and procured in the expanded footprint. CAISO also reports price corrections for May 1–5 intervals (invalid ELAPs, GHG shadow prices, Flex Ramp prices, and ITC congestion) and has extended the correction window to 10 business days through mid-June; participants can submit inquiries via the CIDI system or a linked comments template, with monthly performance reports and a public meeting planned for June discussion of May results.

title: Management Committee
meeting_hydro_reason: Management Committee meetings typically address administrative, organizational, and governance matters rather than specific market rules, resource participation requirements, or technical issues affecting hydropower operations.

documents.title: Final Motion
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO approves revisions to automatic ICAP surplus offers, which directly affects capacity accreditation and revenue for dispatchable resources including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities participating in NYISO's capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO Management Committee unanimously approved revisions to the Market Administration and Control Area Services Tariff governing Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers on May 27, 2026, with the Board of Directors now authorized to file the tariff revisions under FPA Section 205. The mechanism affects how ICAP (Installed Capacity) surplus—the cushion above actual resource adequacy requirement—is offered and priced in the capacity market. Hydropower and pumped-storage operators rely on ICAP accreditation for capacity revenue; changes to surplus-offer rules can shift the timing, volume, or price-formation signals that determine when and how much capacity-constrained resources are called upon, altering the effective capacity value and bidding strategy for hydro and PSH assets.

documents.title: 1 Agenda
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ICAP (capacity market) surplus-offer rules directly affect how hydro and pumped-storage resources bid and earn capacity revenue; this agenda item flags an active NYISO discussion and vote on capacity-market mechanics.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Management Committee will vote on changes to automatic ICAP surplus-offer rules on May 27, 2026. The mechanism affects how capacity is offered into NYISO's capacity market when resources have surplus capacity beyond their committed obligations — a setting that shapes net-revenue outcomes for hydro and PSH that participate in ICAP. The specific proposal details are not disclosed in the agenda, but this is a live vote item; no comment deadline is stated in the document.

documents.title: 2a Operations Report
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Hydro asset owners use monthly operational metrics to understand system conditions affecting their revenue (scarcity pricing opportunity during stress events, curtailment risk, interchange patterns) and obligations (reserve activations, load-following demand). This April 2026 report includes hydro outage data, net-load-ramp trends affecting flexibility value, wind/solar forecast performance and curtailment (competitive pressure on hydro's flexibility premium), and transmission changes (CHPE MTF, Smart Path Connect) affecting hydro deliverability and inter-regional pricing.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's April 2026 operations report tracks system reliability and market outcomes. Hydro-relevant findings: (1) Monthly generation mix shows 6,324 MW hydro nameplate (including pumped storage); (2) Hydro and other resources showed measurable day-ahead capacity unavailability in real time (charted but values not extracted due to daily volatility); (3) Net load ramps driven by wind and solar variability remained significant (15–35 MW hourly average), sustaining demand for flexible ramping; (4) Wind and FTM solar curtailment totaled 1.5% system-wide in April (wind 8,453 MWh, FTM solar 2,486 MWh), indicating transmission constraints that compress energy value for all resources in congested zones; (5) Champlain Hudson Power Express (CHPE MTF) merchant transmission completed integration testing in late April and began commissioning, with Smart Path Connect incrementally entering service throughout the month—these transmission additions will improve hydro export paths from Quebec and reduce NY congestion costs. No member action required; document is reference material for understanding April revenue drivers and seasonal resource-adequacy conditions.

documents.title: 3b Motion
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO proposes automatic ICAP (capacity) surplus offer rules, which directly affects how capacity accreditation and surplus management operate—a core revenue mechanism for hydro and PSH capacity participation in NYISO.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO Management Committee approves revisions to the Market Administration and Control Area Services Tariff governing automatic ICAP surplus offers, with the Board of Directors to authorize filing under FPA Section 205. The proposal changes the rules by which capacity surplus is offered into the market automatically, affecting how hydro and pumped-storage units manage their capacity accreditation obligations and surplus positioning—particularly relevant for PSH operators managing state-of-charge and capacity ratings. The full tariff language and detailed mechanism are presented in the accompanying slide deck 'Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers' (May 27, 2026); the proposal moves to Board filing stage pending Board authorization.

documents.title: 3 Automatic ICAP Surplus Offers
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: MECHANISM: NYISO is revising the ICAP market's surplus-offer rules to automatically resell capacity that load-serving entities and ICAP suppliers procure but do not need, at zero-dollar bids. For hydropower and storage owners selling capacity into NYISO's ICAP market—whether as primary capacity suppliers or via bilateral trades—this rule removes their option to withhold surplus capacity and directly affects the monetization path and price realization for unsold UCAP.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO proposes a new tariff section (5.14.4) that automatically submits zero-dollar offers for any Unforced Capacity (UCAP) surplus held by load-serving entities, ICAP suppliers, and ICAP marketers after the monthly certification deadline, effective May 1, 2027 (Board approval and FERC filing required). Currently, surplus UCAP often goes unoffered, potentially raising market-clearing prices; the automatic zero-dollar offer guarantees resale at the auction clearing price. Hydropower and pumped-storage owners—particularly those acquiring capacity via bilateral trades or selling excess UCAP—will face mandatory resale of any certified surplus, eliminating the ability to withhold capacity or negotiate off-market prices for unexpectedly available MW; however, capacity associated with a physical transmission interface (PTID) and 'unoffered MW' remain subject to physical-withholding rules and are excluded from automatic resale.

documents.title: 3a MST 5.14 redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: ICAP capacity-market accreditation and demand-curve methodology directly affect hydro and PSH revenue from capacity markets, particularly the periodic review process that sets accreditation reference points and the demand-curve adjustments that drive cleared capacity prices.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's tariff Section 5.14 establishes the ICAP Spot Market Auction mechanics and demand-curve parameters that clear monthly unforced-capacity obligations. The document redlines the periodic review methodology (every four years, next review for 2025/2026 capability year), which assesses the levelized cost of a peaking plant, net energy and ancillary-services revenue offsets, and seasonal differences in capacity availability. Beginning with 2025/2026, demand curves will account for seasonal variations in capacity availability—summer and winter curves now reflect different excess-capacity assumptions. The 2025/2026 NYCA summer curve peaks at $21.69/kW-month; winter at $16.39/kW-month, with locational adders (NYC summer peak $41.30). Hydro and PSH capacity accreditation is measured against these ICAP demand curves translated to unforced-capacity terms; seasonal demand-curve splits will alter how seasonal hydro availability and storage round-trip efficiency factor into capacity showings, potentially reducing winter accreditation if available supply diverges seasonally from the reference peaking-plant assumption.

documents.title: 4 2025 State of the Market Report
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: NYISO's 2025 State of the Market Report analyzes energy and ancillary services pricing, capacity market performance and locational incentives, demand response, transmission planning, and external transactions—all material to hydro and PSH revenue signals, obligations, and competitive position in the Northeast market.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO's Market Monitoring Unit releases its 2025 annual State of the Market Report, covering wholesale and retail costs, energy and ancillary services market performance, capacity market results and locational pricing assessment, transmission congestion management, demand response, and competitive dynamics. The report includes analysis of fossil and non-fossil generator performance during peak conditions, assessment of transmission security limits' impact on capacity payments, evaluation of excessive qualification of conventional generators, and critique of the large load interconnection process. For hydropower and PSH owners, this report provides the baseline market-monitor assessment of (1) whether ancillary services pricing is adequate to support reservoir and storage operations, (2) locational capacity-market signals and whether transmission constraints are suppressing payments in hydro-rich zones, (3) performance metrics that may inform cost-of-service or mitigation rules, and (4) findings on demand-response and load-growth trends that shift the value of flexibility and scarcity-price exposure. The document is posted to NYISO's Management Committee agenda; no specific vote or comment deadline is stated in the excerpt.

documents.title: 1 Agenda
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses ICAP (Installed Capacity) market rules and winter reliability enhancements that directly affect hydropower's capacity accreditation and resource adequacy participation in NYISO.
documents.ai_summary: This agenda covers ICAP Manual revisions related to winter reliability capacity enhancements, which are directly relevant to how hydropower and pumped-storage facilities are accredited for capacity value and contribute to resource adequacy in NYISO. Changes to ICAP rules materially affect hydro participation and compensation.

documents.title: 3a 2026 Winter Reliability Capacity Enhancements: ICAP Manual Revisions - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document addresses ICAP capacity market rules, seasonal capacity requirements, and deliverability rights (UDRs/EDRs) that directly affect how hydropower and energy storage resources participate in NYISO's capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is proposing seasonal ICAP minimum requirements, seasonal elections for Unforced Capacity Deliverability Rights (UDRs) and External-to-Rest of State Deliverability Rights (EDRs), and must-offer requirements effective May 1, 2027. These changes establish distinct Summer and Winter capability periods and impose penalties for failure to offer or certify capacity, which affect how dispatchable resources like hydropower and pumped-storage can deliver capacity across seasonal variations and transmission constraints.

documents.title: 3b Attachment Q - Draft
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Document establishes capacity accreditation and penalty calculation methodologies for external unforced capacity deliverability rights, which directly affects how hydropower and other resources are valued and incentivized in NYISO's capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: This attachment details UCAP calculations for External UDRs/EDRs using capacity accreditation factors, forced outage rates, and derating factors—mechanisms that apply to hydropower resources delivering capacity over external transmission interfaces. documents.title: 2026-05-21 RC A05.a Capacity Auction Reforms - Seasonal/Accreditation - DECR Modeling & Accreditation - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Document addresses capacity accreditation framework and seasonal auction reforms that directly affect how distributed energy resources—including storage facilities (CSF, BSF)—are modeled and accredited for resource adequacy, which impacts market participation rules for hydropower and pumped-storage resources.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing accreditation reforms for Distributed Energy Capacity Resources (DECRs) under FERC Order 2222 compliance, including Continuous Storage Facilities (CSF) and Binary Storage Facilities (BSF). The framework uses performance-based profiling and Marginal Reliability Impact (MRI) Hours modeling to determine capacity accreditation; storage resources aggregated as DECRs must use DERA-level profiles and may be subject to T&D loss adjustments. These rules affect how pumped-storage and battery storage participate in ISO-NE's seasonal capacity auctions effective Q2 2027.

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing a new MRI (performance-based) accreditation framework for Distributed Energy Capacity Resources (DECRs) that replaces the previous sum-of-individual-facilities approach. This framework will impact how storage resources and other DERs are credited for capacity contributions, affecting their market revenues and participation incentives—relevant to PSH and battery storage operators.

documents.ai_summary: PJM has established an expedited interconnection process (RGIS) outside the standard interconnection queue that allows hydropower and other generation owners to transfer Capacity Interconnection Rights from deactivating units to replacement resources at the same POI more quickly, with faster RTEP model inclusion and GIA execution. This affects how hydro operators can pursue repowering, relicensing, or facility replacement projects in PJM.

Section 6 on Generation and Section 5 on Reserves during Restoration are particularly relevant to hydro's role in grid recovery, and Attachment A defines critical black-start criteria that affect hydropower resource qualification.

documents.title: Item 01 – 1 Schedule 2 Penalties – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Changes to cost-based offer penalty formulae in PJM's Operating Agreement could affect how hydropower and pumped-storage facilities are penalized for non-compliant offers, particularly in scenarios involving negative LMPs.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is revising penalty formulas under Operating Agreement Schedule 2, section 6.1 to address cases where negative LMPs result in negative penalties (credits) to market sellers. This directly impacts hydropower and storage operators' exposure to penalty mechanisms when submitting cost-based offers, especially during oversupply periods when LMPs turn negative.

documents.title: Item 02a – 1 Manual 11 Revisions Advance Scheduled Resources Phase 1 – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document addresses advance scheduling of generation resources with long start-up times (>24 hours) and cost-based offer rules that directly affect how hydropower and other dispatchable resources participate in PJM's Day-ahead and Real-time Energy Markets.
documents.ai_summary: PJM has implemented advance pre-scheduling of resources with notification/start-up times exceeding 24 hours based on binding cost-based offers, with restrictions on offer updates during the Day-ahead Market bid period. These rules affect how hydroelectric facilities with long lead times manage their bids, offer flexibility, and participation in energy and reserve markets, particularly in response to system emergencies.

issues.canonical_name: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market

documents.title: Item 02a – 2 Manual 11 Revisions – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses cost-capping and dispatch rules for resources with extended start-up or notification times (>24 hours) called in advance of the day-ahead market, which directly affects how hydroelectric facilities with longer commitment lead times are economically dispatched and compensated in PJM markets.
documents.ai_summary: Manual 11 changes clarify cost-based offer placement and cost-capping rules for resources requiring advance notice prior to day-ahead market scheduling, with specific application of 'switch to cost' flags in both day-ahead and real-time markets. These provisions materially affect how hydroelectric units with extended start-up times—including some run-of-river and storage facilities—are called and compensated when scheduled ahead of the DA market.

issues.canonical_name: Resource Scheduling Prior to the Day Ahead Energy Market

documents.title: Item 02a – 3 Manual 11 Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Manual 11 governs PJM's energy and ancillary services market operations, including regulation and reserve markets that are critical to hydropower and pumped-storage participation and pricing.
documents.ai_summary: This manual establishes business rules for PJM's day-ahead and real-time energy markets, regulation market (Section 3), and reserve markets (Section 4)—all mechanisms through which hydropower and pumped-storage resources bid, are cleared, and receive compensation. The manual covers locational marginal pricing, ancillary service requirements, and settlement procedures that directly affect hydro dispatch and revenue.

documents.title: Item 02b – 1 Manuals 20a 21b And 22 Periodic Reviews – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Document addresses ELCC (Effective Load Carrying Capability) accreditation, capacity ratings, and resource performance metrics—all directly relevant to hydropower and storage capacity market participation in PJM.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 20A revisions clarify ELCC Class Rating and Resource Specific ELCC Rating methodologies used to determine capacity accreditation. Manual 21B updates capacity determination procedures including data gathering for ELCC analysis. Manual 22 revisions update performance indices and accredited UCAP factors pool-wide. These manuals govern how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are credited for capacity obligations.

documents.title: Item 02b – 2 Manuals M20a M21b And M22 Revisions – Executive Summary
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Document describes manual updates to PJM's Resource Adequacy Analysis (M20A), Generation Capacity Determination (M21B), and Generator Resource Performance Indices (M22)—all directly governing capacity accreditation and resource adequacy rules affecting hydropower and storage participation.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is updating three critical manuals governing resource adequacy, capacity determination, and generator performance metrics. These changes will affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are evaluated for capacity accreditation and contribution to resource adequacy in the PJM market.

documents.title: Item 02b – 3 Manual 20a Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This manual defines ELCC (Effective Load Carrying Capability) accreditation methodology and resource adequacy criteria that directly apply to hydropower and pumped-storage capacity ratings in PJM's capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 20A establishes the Reserve Requirement Study (RRS) and ELCC calculation procedures that determine how much capacity credit hydropower and pumped-storage resources receive. Section 2 details ELCC Class Ratings and resource-specific ELCC ratings, which are critical for hydro participation in PJM's capacity market and affect revenue adequacy for these resources.

documents.title: Item 02b – 4 Manual 21b Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This manual directly addresses ELCC (Effective Load Carrying Capability) accreditation rules and capacity determination procedures that apply to hydropower and pumped-storage resources, including classification of limited duration and combination resources relevant to PSH.
documents.ai_summary: PJM Manual 21B establishes the framework for determining generating capability and ELCC accreditation across resource classes, including Limited Duration Resources and Combination Resources classes that encompass pumped-storage hydropower. The manual specifies ELCC calculation methodologies, accredited UCAP determination, testing requirements, and capacity interconnection rights critical to how hydro/PSH participate in PJM's capacity market.

documents.title: Item 02b – 5 Manual 22 Revisions – Redline
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This manual establishes generator resource performance indices and accredited capacity factors (UCAP) used in PJM's capacity market and resource adequacy calculations, which directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are accredited and compensated.
documents.ai_summary: Manual 22 defines methodologies for calculating forced outage rates, f-factors, and accredited UCAP factors that determine how much capacity hydro/PSH units receive credit for in PJM's capacity market. Changes to these performance indices and the pool-wide average Accredited UCAP Factor directly impact hydro resource adequacy and capacity market participation.

documents.title: Item 03 – 2026 PJM Strategy Development Update – Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses PJM's strategic priorities including resource adequacy, capacity market design, energy storage integration, transmission planning, and flexible interconnection—all of which directly affect hydropower and pumped-storage participation in PJM markets.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's 2026 strategy development includes specific topics relevant to hydro: capacity construct redesign, resource performance and flexibility/flexible interconnection, Order 2222 DER integration, advanced grid technology and storage, demand flexibility, and proactive transmission planning. The strategy's four objectives—reliable grid operation, market incentives alignment for resource adequacy, forward-looking planning, and decision-making efficiency—will shape the regulatory environment in which hydropower and pumped-storage resources operate.

documents.title: Powering Reliability Through Market Design
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document addresses fundamental market design questions affecting resource adequacy, capacity markets, and long-term contracting mechanisms that directly impact hydropower and pumped-storage participation and investment signals in PJM.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is examining whether its foundational market design assumptions remain valid amid structural supply-demand imbalances. The analysis discusses capacity market mechanisms (RPM), long-term hedging requirements, and the tension between price signals and investment certainty—all critical to hydropower/PSH economics and revenue adequacy in a tight resource environment.

documents.title: Considerations TO Better Align Default Service Programs With PJM Capacity Market
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses PJM's capacity market design and default service procurement strategies, which directly affect how hydropower and energy storage resources compete and are compensated in PJM's resource adequacy framework.
documents.ai_summary: PJM recommends that states diversify default service procurement strategies, including longer-term contracting (5+ years) and de-harmonization from the Base Residual Auction schedule. These recommendations affect pricing signals and market participation opportunities for hydropower and pumped-storage resources that provide flexible capacity and energy services in PJM.

documents.ai_summary: The meeting addresses the 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook and 2026 RNA (Resource Needs Assessment) scenario assumptions, which inform capacity accreditation and resource adequacy rules affecting hydropower. Order 1920 updates and interconnection agreement enhancements are also discussed, both relevant to hydro facility interconnection and transmission deliverability.

documents.title: 02a 2025-2044 System and Resource Outlook - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document analyzes transmission congestion, resource adequacy, and generation mix across multiple policy scenarios that directly affect hydropower deliverability and market participation in NYCA.
documents.ai_summary: The 2025-2044 System & Resource Outlook modeling shows significant transmission congestion (particularly Central East constraints) emerging under high renewable penetration scenarios, which affects hydropower's ability to deliver power and its locational value. The analysis includes net import/export trends and zonal LBMPs that are critical for understanding hydropower's competitive position and the need for flexible ramping resources in New York's evolving resource portfolio.

documents.title: 02b 2025-2044 System and Resource Outlook - Preliminary Draft Report
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This 20-year system planning outlook directly addresses resource adequacy, capacity mix evolution, transmission constraints, and how policy-driven renewable and storage deployment will reshape New York's grid—all critical factors for hydropower and pumped-storage market participation and competitive positioning.
documents.ai_summary: The report models multiple policy scenarios (2025–2044) that will determine hydropower's role in meeting clean energy targets, capacity needs, and transmission-constrained zones. It assesses fossil phase-out timelines, large load growth, offshore wind and nuclear deployment impacts, and regional transmission bottlenecks—all factors that shape market value and operational opportunities for existing and new hydropower/PSH resources in New York.

documents.title: 03 2026 RNA: Scenario Assumptions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document describes scenario assumptions for NYISO's 2026 Reliability Needs Assessment (RNA), including generation availability, resource retirement modeling, and transmission security assumptions that directly affect how hydropower and storage resources are modeled in resource adequacy planning.
documents.ai_summary: The RNA scenario development process will model aging generation retirements, planned generator project delays, wind/solar resource availability, and reactive support capabilities. These assumptions are foundational to how hydropower's role in reliability, capacity accreditation, and ancillary service provision are evaluated in NYISO's resource adequacy analysis. Hydropower stakeholders should engage during the scenario feedback process to ensure hydro-specific assumptions (seasonal availability, operational flexibility, reactive capability) are appropriately represented.

documents.title: 04a Order 1920 Update - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: FERC Order 1920 on long-term regional transmission planning directly affects how hydropower projects are evaluated, prioritized, and integrated into NYISO's transmission expansion plans.
documents.ai_summary: NYISO is implementing FERC Order 1920 compliance tariff revisions governing long-term regional transmission planning (OATT Sections 31.1, 31.2, 31.7, and others). These rules establish processes for identifying transmission needs, evaluating solutions including storage and flexible resources, and cost allocation—all of which determine whether hydropower and pumped-storage projects receive planning support and transmission access. Hydro stakeholders should engage by the May 22 feedback deadline.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Document covers CAISO market policy priorities including resource adequacy, energy storage design and modeling, ancillary services, and EDAM enhancements—all directly affecting hydropower and pumped-storage participation and valuation.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO's 2026-2028 Market Policy Roadmap includes multiple tracks relevant to hydro: Resource Adequacy modeling and design (affecting hydro accreditation), Storage Design and Modeling (outage management, state-of-charge, uplift bids), EDAM enhancements affecting dispatch and market seams, and ancillary services optimization. The roadmap prioritizes storage-specific policy work and resource visibility improvements critical to pumped-storage and flexible hydro operations.

documents.title: BOSR Comments on 2026 Market Policy Initiative and Catalog and Roadmap Process - May 11, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses energy storage design and modeling, bilateral market liquidity affecting hydro operations, EDAM performance, and congestion revenue allocation—all directly relevant to hydropower and pumped-storage market participation.
documents.ai_summary: The BOSR supports ongoing work in the Storage Design and Modeling policy initiative, emphasizing that storage resources must accurately participate in markets and that uplift payments should not insulate them from real-time prices. The document also addresses bilateral market hub indices (Mid-Columbia, Palo Verde) used for price caps and default energy bids, and EDAM enhancements affecting storage discharge optimization during peak demand periods.

stakeholders.name: Western Energy Markets Body of State Regulators
stakeholders.entity: BOSR (representing state utility regulatory commissions from AZ, CA, ID, MT, NV, NM, OR, SD, TX, UT, WA, WY, plus consumer-owned utilities and federal power marketing administrations)

documents.title: Appian Way Presentation - Western Market Seams Coordination - Apr 08, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses western market seams coordination, transmission congestion allocation, and parallel flow impacts on CAISO constraints—issues directly affecting hydropower deliverability, transmission access rights, and market participation across interconnected balancing authorities.
documents.ai_summary: The presentation highlights how rapid solar and battery deployment in the desert southwest is driving increased south-to-north flows and congestion on CAISO internal paths, and argues for fair allocation mechanisms between CAISO and external entities (EDAM and Markets+ participants) to prevent transmission rate-payer subsidization of non-CAISO market flows. Hydropower operators and pumped-storage facilities relying on transmission access for energy delivery and ancillary service provision are materially affected by seams coordination rules and congestion allocation methodologies.

documents.title: California Department of Water Resources Presentation - Adjustment to LSE Flexible RA - Apr 08, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses flexible resource adequacy (RA) allocations and Commercial Operation Date (COD) delays affecting variable energy resources, which directly impacts how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are treated in CAISO's capacity procurement and compliance frameworks.
documents.ai_summary: California Department of Water Resources presents a proposal to adjust LSE flexible RA obligations when solar/wind projects experience COD delays, arguing the current tariff creates 'phantom' obligations that impose unnecessary costs without reliability benefit. While focused on solar/wind, this establishes precedent for how RTO tariffs handle delayed resource availability and cost causation—principles directly applicable to hydropower and pumped-storage resources, which also face COD uncertainties and should have comparable flexibility in RA obligation adjustments.

documents.title: California Department of Water Resources Presentation - Adjustment to LSEs with RTCO Changes - Apr 08, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: RTCO (Real-Time Congestion Offset) rate increases directly affect California's hydropower operators, particularly the California Department of Water Resources, by increasing their real-time congestion costs and potentially impacting the economics of hydropower dispatch and participation in CAISO markets.
documents.ai_summary: CDWR presents analysis of steady RTCO rate increases since 2011, attributing some to convergence bidding and transmission constraints in Southern California. The presentation requests CAISO investigation into root causes and stakeholder process to lower RTCO charges, which are material operational and financial concerns for hydropower operators managing large water resources portfolios in CAISO.

documents.title: California Public Utilities Commission Presentation - Energy Division Staff Annual Policy Catalog Proposals - Apr 08, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses CAISO market design changes (EDAM/DAME) and ancillary service products (Imbalance Reserves, Reliability Capacity) that directly affect how flexible resources like hydropower and pumped-storage can participate and be compensated in the market.
documents.ai_summary: The CPUC Energy Division Staff proposal reviews new EDAM and DAME market products launching May 1, 2026, including imbalance reserves and reliability capacity products that are critical for dispatchable hydropower. The staff recommends assessing qualification requirements (15-minute start-up, 30-minute dispatchability), adequacy of reserve levels, and market design parameters—all of which directly impact hydro resource participation, valuation, and dispatch.

documents.title: PacifiCorp Presentation - Imbalance Reserve Enhancements - Apr 08, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Imbalance reserves are a key ancillary service product that affects hydropower and storage resource dispatch, pricing, and market participation, particularly as EDAM expands reserve procurement across multiple balancing authorities.
documents.ai_summary: PacifiCorp proposes evaluating imbalance reserve performance under EDAM operations to assess alignment between day-ahead procurement and real-time system needs. Results could affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are awarded, priced, and dispatched for reserve products, with potential implications for cost allocation and operational flexibility.

documents.title: Pacific Gas and Electric Presentation - 2028 EDAM Review - Ancillary Services Real-Time Re-Optimization - Apr 08, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document proposes real-time ancillary service re-optimization and discusses flexibility value for storage and hybrid resources, which directly affects hydropower and pumped-storage market participation and dispatch efficiency.
documents.ai_summary: PG&E proposes a stakeholder initiative to re-optimize ancillary services in CAISO real-time markets, including re-bidding, elimination of dispatch constraints (ASSOC), and simplified service definitions. This initiative explicitly targets unlocking flexibility value for storage, hybrids, and variable renewable resources, with direct implications for how pumped-storage hydropower and run-of-river hydro can participate in and benefit from western market integration.

documents.title: The Energy Authority Presentation - EDAM BAA Transactional Seams - Apr 08, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses EDAM market design changes affecting inter-regional energy trading, transmission scheduling, and resource access that could impact hydropower generators' and storage operators' ability to participate in day-ahead and hour-ahead markets across multiple BAAs.
documents.ai_summary: This proposal to enhance EDAM BAA transactional seams would enable hourly-block energy trading at external interties for both firm contracts and economy energy, potentially expanding market access for hydropower and pumped-storage resources. The changes affect transmission service provision, congestion revenue allocation, and scheduling models (SP-Tie vs. GAP-Tie) that influence how dispatchable hydro resources compete and deliver across regional boundaries.

documents.title: The Joint Parties Presentation - Maximum Import Capability Enhancements - Apr 08, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Maximum Import Capability enhancements directly affect how out-of-state hydropower and other resources can be counted toward resource adequacy requirements and long-term contracting, which is critical for hydropower imports and deliverability.
documents.ai_summary: The document addresses MIC allocation challenges that constrain long-term power purchase agreements for out-of-state resources, including hydropower. Hydropower operators and LSEs relying on OOS hydro imports need improved MIC methodologies and multi-year certainty to support contracting with future commercial operation dates, particularly relevant to the 19 GW of OOS resources planned by 2045.

documents.title: Western Power Trading Forum Presentation - Resource Adequacy Reliability - Seams - EDAM Enhancements - Apr 08, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses resource adequacy reliability, EDAM market enhancements, and market seams that directly affect hydropower and storage participation in CAISO markets, including discussion of RA products, dispatch rules, and market integration.
documents.ai_summary: WPTF proposes eliminating the Flexible RA product and updating CPM backstop mechanisms to align with 1-in-10 LOLE standards, which affect how hydropower resources qualify and are compensated for flexibility services. The document also advocates for formal policy processes to address EDAM implementation issues including bidding rules and intertie participation that are critical to hydropower and storage deliverability.

documents.title: Straw Proposal - Resource Adequacy Modeling and Program Design - Track 2 - May 11, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This proposal directly addresses resource adequacy availability mechanisms, UCAP accreditation, must-offer obligations, and outage substitution rules that significantly affect hydropower and pumped-storage participation in CAISO markets.
documents.ai_summary: CAISO proposes major reforms to RAAIM (Resource Adequacy Availability Incentive Mechanism) and must-offer obligations, shifting from static assessment hours to dynamic triggers based on system conditions and scarcity. These changes affect how hydro resources are valued in capacity accreditation, when they must bid into markets, and how outage substitution works—all critical for PSH and seasonal hydro participation in California's RA framework.

documents.title: Straw Proposal - Resource Adequacy Modeling and Program Design - Track 2 - May 12, 2026
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document directly addresses resource adequacy rules, RAAIM incentive mechanisms, outage substitution, and must-offer obligations that significantly affect how hydropower and storage resources participate in CAISO markets and are credited for capacity.
documents.ai_summary: The proposal reforms California's Resource Adequacy Availability Incentive Mechanism (RAAIM) to better target critical periods and improve generator availability, while also discussing UCAP (unforced capacity) accreditation methodologies that reflect resource-specific outage rates—both of which directly impact how hydro and pumped-storage resources are valued, incented, and must be offered in the market. Outage substitution enhancements and clarified must-offer obligations also affect operational flexibility that hydro resources typically provide.

documents.ai_summary: CAISO is reforming RAAIM to improve incentives for RA resource availability during critical conditions, proposing tiered pricing and per-event assessments rather than monthly netted charges. documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC A04b - Pay-For-Performance Revisions: Performance Payment Rate - FirstLight Power Amendment
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document addresses the Pay-for-Performance (PFP) capacity market mechanism and Performance Payment Rate (PPR) in ISO-NE's Forward Capacity Market, which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are compensated for capacity performance during reserve deficiency events.
documents.ai_summary: FirstLight Power, a major hydropower operator in New England, proposes amending the effective date of ISO-NE's proposed PPR reduction (from $9,337/MWh to $3,500/MWh) from September 2026 to June 2028 to protect resources that have already cleared auctions at the higher rate. The PPR is a critical two-settlement mechanism that determines performance payment/penalty rates for hydropower and other capacity resources, and FirstLight argues that retroactive changes to this rate after auctions clear violate the principle of fixed financial positions and could undermine the market's incentive structure.

documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC A03 - Pay-For-Performance Revisions: External Transactions - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses Pay-for-Performance capacity market revisions that directly affect how external transactions (exports/imports) are treated in ISO-NE's Forward Capacity Market, which impacts resource adequacy and capacity accreditation rules that affect hydropower and pumped-storage participation.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes to charge system-backed exports the Performance Payment Rate (PPR) and remove them from the Capacity Balancing Ratio numerator to improve Forward Capacity Market efficiency. This tariff change affects how hydropower and storage resources can participate in external transactions while maintaining capacity obligations, potentially constraining arbitrage opportunities and affecting economic viability of flexible generation during capacity scarcity conditions.

documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC A05 - GISWG - MA 83D Memo

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing changes to allow separate designation and tracking of renewable energy certificates from hydroelectric generation imported under Massachusetts Section 83D agreements. The modifications enable HQ Energy Services (representing Canadian hydropower) and Massachusetts utilities to designate specific certificates from a pool of 62 generating units, affecting how hydropower-sourced certificates are recorded and traded in the GIS system.

documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC A06 - Day-Ahead Ancillary Services Post-Implementation Adjustments - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Day-ahead ancillary services design changes directly affect how hydropower and other dispatchable resources compete in ISO-NE's A/S market, particularly through modifications to Forecast Energy Requirement, Non-Performance Factor, and strike price mechanisms.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes post-implementation adjustments to its day-ahead ancillary services market, including reducing FER demand quantity by expected uncleared front-of-meter wind/solar, setting NPF at 115%, and implementing a strike price floor based on combustion turbine economics. These changes affect the cost and competitiveness of hydropower in ancillary services procurement and may reduce overall A/S costs by ~$269M, potentially impacting how hydro resources are dispatched and compensated.

documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC A02 - Pay-For-Performance Revisions: Balancing Ratio - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Pay-for-Performance capacity market revisions directly affect how dispatchable hydropower and pumped-storage resources are compensated and charged under ISO-NE's capacity mechanism.
documents.ai_summary: FERC Order EL25-106 requires ISO-NE to cap the Capacity Balancing Ratio at 1.0 to prevent capacity resources (including hydro and PSH) from being charged obligations exceeding their Capacity Supply Obligation. The proposal reallocates deficient Capacity Performance Payments and discontinues Capacity Performance Bilaterals, materially affecting how hydro/storage resources settle charges during scarcity conditions.

documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC A02 - Pay-For-Performance Revisions: Balancing Ratio - Voting Memo
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses capacity market rules, specifically Capacity Balancing Ratio capping and Capacity Performance Payments, which directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources are charged and compensated in ISO-NE's capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing FERC-ordered revisions to cap the Capacity Balancing Ratio at 1.0, preventing capacity resources (including hydropower and PSH) from being charged for obligations exceeding their Capacity Supply Obligation. The proposal also reallocates Capacity Performance Payments and removes underutilized Capacity Performance Bilaterals, both of which affect how hydro resources participate in ISO-NE's pay-for-performance framework.

documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC/RC A07a - CAR-SA - Resource Qualification - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document describes capacity accreditation and resource qualification rules under CAR-SA that directly affect how hydropower and variable resources participate in ISO-NE's capacity market, including MRIC calculations, seasonal auction mechanics, and forced outage provisions.
documents.ai_summary: CAR-SA eliminates minimum size exceptions for intermittent power resources (IPRs, which include variable hydropower), discontinues the Estimated Qualified Capacity process for newly-commercial resources, and introduces an Unexpected Full Forced Outage provision allowing resources with season-long outages to withdraw from seasonal auctions. These changes materially affect hydropower resource qualification, accreditation methodology, and capacity market participation in New England.

documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC/RC A07e - CAR-SA - FlatIron Energy - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The presentation directly addresses resource accreditation modeling assumptions that affect how energy storage and hydropower resources are valued in ISO-NE's capacity market, with specific focus on Type 2 and Type 3 MRI hours that heavily impact storage resource accreditation.
documents.ai_summary: FlatIron Energy challenges ISO-NE's proposed accreditation base case for determining resource adequacy, arguing that modeling assumptions—particularly the use of 'at-criteria' conditions with artificially high load additions (+5-6.6 GW)—misalign with expected system conditions and systematically under-accredit energy-limited resources like pumped-storage hydropower. This misaccreditation artificially increases capacity prices and creates poor investment signals for flexible, dispatchable resources that provide multi-hour energy support during stressed system conditions.

documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC/RC A07g - CAR-SA - Impact Analysis: Market Clearing - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document directly addresses capacity market accreditation reforms (CAR-SA) including seasonal capacity payments, demand curves, and reliability criteria that fundamentally affect how hydropower and storage resources participate in ISO-NE's capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: CAR-SA proposes seasonal capacity payment rates ($7.26/kW-month summer, $15.65/kW-month winter) derived from seasonal reliability metrics and Net CONE, which will significantly impact hydropower and pumped-storage revenues and participation incentives. The analysis includes gas constraint demand curves that may provide differential accreditation for non-firm resources, directly relevant to hydro dispatchability valuation.

documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC/RC A07g - CAR-SA - Seasonal Payment Rate Calculation - Memo
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses seasonal capacity market design and payment rate methodology under CAR-SA, which directly affects how hydropower and storage resources participate in ISO-NE's capacity market and recover costs.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes a new methodology for calculating seasonal payment rates to replace Net CONE in the Capacity Auction Redesign with Seasonal Auctions (CAR-SA) framework. The seasonal payment rates will anchor demand curves for summer and winter capacity markets, with rates derived from assumptions about annual cost equivalence and uniform cost of unserved energy across seasons. This impacts how dispatchable hydropower and pumped-storage resources receive seasonal capacity payments and investment signals in New England's capacity market.

documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing seasonal capacity accreditation and cost allocation (CAR-SA) with a proposed effective date of Q2 2027. The presentation details how non-firm gas capacity credits will reduce winter capacity auction costs, and importantly, lists seven capacity market charges including 'Hydro-Quebec interconnection capability credits (HQICC) capacity charge,' indicating that interconnected hydropower resources face specific treatment in the revised capacity market structure.

documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC/RC A07k - CAR-SA - Cost Allocation Design Detail for Non-firm Gas Capacity Costs Memo
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document details Capacity Auction Reforms (CAR) affecting capacity market cost allocation in ISO-NE, including explicit provisions for hydro resources (Hydro-Quebec Interconnection Capability Credits) and their treatment under the new seasonal capacity auction structure.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE's CAR redesigns capacity cost allocation from forward-annual to prompt-seasonal auctions. The memo explicitly addresses how Hydro-Quebec Interconnection Capability Credits (HQICC) will be calculated under the new Seasonal Capacity Auction (SCA) charge rate framework, directly affecting hydropower resources' cost recovery and capacity market participation in New England.

documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC/RC A07f - CAR-SA - FirstLight - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: FirstLight's presentation directly addresses winter weather year selection and weighting in ISO-NE's Capacity Accreditation Resource (CAR) methodology, which materially affects how energy storage resources like pumped-storage hydropower are accredited in the capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: FirstLight argues that ISO-NE's current winter weather sampling methodology overweights extreme cold events (e.g., January 2004), causing miscalibration that understates the value of energy storage resources in typical capacity shortage conditions. FirstLight proposes an alternative weighting approach using 5-day weather blocks and synthetic winter sampling to better reflect tail-event probabilities, potentially improving accreditation for storage resources and reducing system costs.

documents.ai_summary: The CAR-SA (Capacity Auction Reforms: Seasonal/Accreditation) proposal modifies how resources—including energy-limited and storage resources—are accredited for capacity. documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC A04 - PFP PPR - MA AGO Memo
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document discusses Pay-for-Performance (PfP) capacity market rules, Performance Payment Rate (PPR) revisions, and capacity supply offers—all of which directly affect how dispatchable resources including hydropower and pumped-storage compete in ISO-NE's capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes reducing the Performance Payment Rate from $9,337/MWh to $3,500/MWh, which affects the Common Value Component of capacity supply offers and capacity clearing prices. The Massachusetts Attorney General's Office supports this reduction as it should lower capacity costs while maintaining adequate performance incentives during Capacity Scarcity Conditions—a policy outcome that impacts all dispatchable resources including hydro competitors.

documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Document directly addresses capacity accreditation rules for imports under CAR-SA, including exclusions of energy storage technologies and intermittent generation—critical to hydro and pumped-storage resource qualification and market participation in ISO-NE.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE proposes to exclude energy storage technologies requiring grid-charging and hybrid facilities with storage components from resource-backed import accreditation under CAR-SA, while establishing MRIC (Minimum Rated Installed Capacity) calculation methods for eligible external resources. This affects how pumped-storage hydropower and other storage-backed imports qualify for seasonal capacity auctions and their accreditation values relative to internal resources.

documents.title: 2026-05-12-14 MC A06 - Day-Ahead Ancillary Services Post-Implementation Adjustments - FirstLight Memo
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: FirstLight's memo directly addresses ancillary services market design changes affecting fast-start hydropower resources' compensation and incentive structure in ISO-NE's Day-Ahead Ancillary Services market.
documents.ai_summary: FirstLight Power Management challenges ISO-NE's proposed strike price floor for DAAS, arguing it would materially reduce performance incentives for first-in-dispatch fast-start resources (including hydropower) by 41-47% in lower-load hours while only reducing costs by 2%, thereby undermining the cost-incentive balance critical to resource adequacy. The proposal would set strike prices well above actual Real-Time LMPs in 94% of hours annually, potentially removing DAAS compensation for the very hydro resources ISO-NE Operations relies on first to address contingencies.

documents.ai_summary: PJM has established an expedited RGIS process allowing hydropower and other generators to transfer CIRs from deactivating facilities to replacement resources at the same point of interconnection, operating in parallel to the standard interconnection cycle. This expedited process enables faster Generation Interconnection Agreement execution and earlier inclusion in transmission reliability studies, benefiting replacement hydro and PSH projects.

Hydropower projects seeking to interconnect, repower, or transfer capacity rights in PJM will need to navigate this new process and may benefit from understanding CIR transfer mechanisms.

documents.ai_summary: The Critical Issue Fast Path meeting is developing alternative proposals for PJM's reliability backstop procurement mechanism, which will establish new procurement rules and requirements for resources needed to meet resource adequacy standards. These rules will affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources can participate in or be compensated through PJM's capacity and reliability markets, with particular relevance to their role in meeting PJM's forward resource adequacy requirements.

documents.title: Agenda Doc
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: Reliability backstop procurement rules directly affect capacity accreditation, resource adequacy, and market participation for dispatchable resources including hydropower and pumped-storage hydropower.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's Critical Issue Fast Path process on reliability backstop procurement likely addresses capacity market mechanisms, resource adequacy rules, or emergency procurement that determine how hydro and PSH resources qualify for and compete in capacity markets or are called upon for reliability support.

documents.title: Item 02a – Joint Stakeholders Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses capacity market rules, resource eligibility criteria, and long-term resource adequacy mechanisms that directly affect hydropower and pumped-storage participation in PJM's Reliability Backstop Auction, including specific provisions for existing generation and license extension support.
documents.ai_summary: This proposal establishes eligibility criteria for a new Reliability Backstop Auction in PJM that includes hydropower and pumped-storage resources. Key provisions affecting hydro include: eligibility for uprated facilities with ≥$300/kW investment, reactivated generation, and existing resources with above-curve offer caps; a long-term resource adequacy mechanism to prevent economically-challenged hydro from retiring; and bilateral contracting incentives for resources with license extensions that have demonstrated financial challenge (including hydro with FERC relicense needs).

documents.title: Item 02b – Constellaton And Vistra Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document proposes expansion of PJM's Reliability Backstop Auction (RBA) eligibility to include demand response and backup generators with discounted ELCC, directly affecting how storage and flexible resources compete in capacity markets against hydropower.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is considering new product definitions and eligibility rules for the RBA that would introduce discounted ELCC for demand response and backup generators. This affects the competitive landscape and capacity accreditation for hydropower and pumped-storage resources, which typically provide firm or highly available capacity and may be disadvantaged by new lower-cost alternatives.

documents.title: Item 02d – Smeco Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses PJM's Reliability Backstop Procurement (RBP) Phase II, which establishes new capacity market rules, credit requirements, and cost allocation mechanisms that directly affect how all dispatchable resources—including hydropower and pumped-storage hydropower—participate in capacity markets and manage collateral obligations.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's RBP Phase II proposes capacity credit rates (0.2x vs. 0.5x price multipliers), collateral/credit posting requirements, and deficiency charge mechanisms that will apply to planned generation resources including hydro. The document also discusses discount rates for resource credit calculations and default allocation rules that affect all capacity market participants' financial exposure and ability to compete in future auctions.

documents.title: Item 02e – EKPC And Exelon Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document addresses resource adequacy procurement (Reliability Backstop Procurement) and capacity market settlement mechanisms that directly affect how dispatchable resources including hydropower are obligated and compensated in PJM's capacity markets.
documents.ai_summary: The EKPC & Exelon proposal reframes PJM's Reliability Backstop Procurement to assign capacity obligations and financial settlements to Load Serving Entities (LSEs) rather than Electric Distribution Companies (EDCs), with implications for how large loads and their associated generation (including hydro) are credited and settled across multiple delivery years in PJM's capacity framework.

documents.title: Item 02e – EKPC Paper
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses capacity market procurement, financial settlement mechanisms, and LSE responsibilities within PJM's market structure, which directly affects how all generation resources including hydropower participate in capacity markets and reliability backstop procurement.
documents.ai_summary: EKPC proposes a framework for Reliability Backstop Procurement (RBP) that assigns procurement targets and financial responsibility to Load Serving Entities rather than EDCs. This procurement mechanism and the capacity market rules it builds upon are relevant to hydropower and pumped-storage operators participating in PJM's capacity markets, as they affect how capacity needs are determined and how resources are compensated for reliability.

documents.title: Item 02f – Imm Paper
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses capacity market design, ELCC treatment, reserve margin requirements, and resource adequacy mechanisms that directly affect how hydropower and pumped-storage resources participate in PJM's capacity markets.
documents.ai_summary: This IMM proposal for a data-center-focused backstop auction establishes new capacity procurement mechanics including ELCC-based accreditation for new resources, 15-year bilateral contracting, and explicit treatment of energy/ancillary service net revenues in capacity pricing. The auction structure, locational pricing (CETO/CETL), and reserve margin calculations have implications for how existing hydropower competes and how new storage resources might participate in PJM's resource adequacy framework.

documents.title: Item 02g – Glatz And Silverman Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses PJM's Reliability Backstop Procurement and Connect & Manage rules affecting large load procurement, which directly impacts capacity market design and resource adequacy—key regulatory mechanisms affecting hydropower and storage participation and competitiveness.

These market design changes affect the competitive landscape and pricing signals for all capacity resources including hydropower and pumped-storage, particularly regarding capacity accreditation, non-firm vs. firm service valuations, and long-term contracting incentives.

documents.title: Item 02g – Silverman Securitization Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document discusses PJM's reliability backstop procurement mechanism for meeting resource adequacy needs, which directly affects how new generation capacity—including hydropower and pumped-storage—will be financed, contracted, and integrated into the market.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is proposing a securitized funding pool to finance 10–15 GW of new generation capacity needed to serve data center load growth. This mechanism is technology-neutral and will support competitively-priced generation of all types, including hydropower and pumped-storage. The securitization structure and capacity contracting approach will influence how hydro projects secure long-term offtake agreements and participate in resource adequacy obligations.

documents.title: Item 02h – Odec Presentation – Updated

documents.ai_summary: The LDES Council proposes modifications to PJM's RBP to broaden resource eligibility, enhance evaluation metrics beyond UCAP to credit extended-duration reliability, introduce COD timing flexibility (2031–2033 window), and address interconnection queue barriers—all of which are relevant to how pumped-storage hydropower can compete and demonstrate value in reliability procurement.

documents.title: Item 02j – Base Power Presentation

documents.title: Item 02l – Amp Presentation

documents.ai_summary: PJM is managing Transition Cycle 1 and Cycle 2 generator interconnection phases with 48 GI applications totaling 9,031 MW in the current cycle. Hydro and PSH developers must track Phase 2 study completion (reports due June 5, 2026), agreement negotiation timelines, and Cycle 1 application deadlines, as interconnection queue status and facility study results directly impact project viability and in-service dates.

documents.title: Item 04 – Replacement Gen Nextgen Update
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document describes PJM's Replacement Generation interconnection process, which directly affects how hydropower and pumped-storage resources can interconnect by transferring Capacity Interconnection Rights (CIRs) at existing Points of Interconnection, a mechanism highly relevant to the hydro industry.
documents.ai_summary: PJM has established an expedited Replacement Generation interconnection process that allows deactivating generation resources to transfer their CIRs to replacement resources at the same POI, operating outside the standard Cycle Process. This is particularly relevant for hydropower and pumped-storage facilities seeking to upgrade or repurpose existing interconnection points, with expedited GIA execution and RTEP inclusion for reliability studies.

documents.title: Item 05 – Transition Cycle 2 Decision Point Ii Requirements
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document outlines interconnection queue requirements and decision points that directly affect all generation technologies, including hydropower and pumped-storage projects seeking network interconnection in PJM.
documents.ai_summary: This document establishes Decision Point II requirements for Transition Cycle 2 interconnection projects in PJM, including site control, permits, readiness deposits, and allowable modifications. Hydropower and PSH projects in PJM's interconnection queue must meet these requirements to advance, including fuel/water agreements, state-level interconnection process evidence, and network upgrade cost allocations.

documents.title: Item 06 – Icm Ibr Verification
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses IBR verification processes and NERC model validation requirements that directly affect how inverter-based resources (including battery storage and potentially some hydro technologies) interconnect and operate in PJM, with implications for resource adequacy and market participation.
documents.ai_summary: PJM's IBR Verification Program establishes mandatory model validation and as-built verification requirements for inverter-based resources under NERC MOD-026-2 (effective April 1, 2026), with costs charged to project developers. While primarily focused on solar/wind inverters, the verification framework and FERC Order 901 compliance apply broadly to all BES resources and non-BES resources ≥20 MVA at voltages >60 kV, potentially affecting pumped-storage and battery-hybrid hydro projects seeking interconnection.

documents.title: Item 07a – Problem Statement
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document directly addresses fuel-switching procedures for interconnected projects with Capacity Injection Rights, which is highly relevant to pumped-storage hydropower developers seeking to convert previously-studied solar or other projects into storage facilities.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is proposing a process to allow projects with existing interconnection studies and CIRs to change fuel types (e.g., solar to storage) without forfeiting their interconnection position, provided material transmission impacts are ruled out. This is directly applicable to PSH and battery-storage co-location opportunities, and references NYISO and ISO-NE precedents that permit fuel changes through material-modification review.

documents.title: 2026-04-22 RC A05.b Capacity Auction Reforms - Seasonal/Accreditation - Summary of DCap and MCap Values - Presentation
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses capacity accreditation reforms and seasonal capacity auction design that directly affect how hydropower and other resources are valued and accredited in ISO-NE's capacity market.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing Capacity Auction Reforms with Seasonal/Accreditation (CAR-SA) that introduce Maximum Capability (MCap) and Dependable Capability (DCap) metrics to replace existing accreditation methodology. MCap represents physical supply capability irrespective of seasonality (including a Global MCap for intermittent resources), while DCap represents expected availability during seasonal peak load hours. These changes will materially affect how hydroelectric and pumped-storage resources are accredited and compensated in ISO-NE's capacity market starting Q2 2027.

issues.canonical_name: Capacity Auction Reforms

documents.title: 2026-04-22 RC A05.b Capacity Auction Reforms - Seasonal/Accreditation - Summary of DCap and MCap Values - Memo - Rev1
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This memo addresses capacity accreditation reforms including seasonal maximum and dependable capability calculations that directly affect how hydropower and other dispatchable resources are valued in ISO-NE's resource adequacy and capacity auction processes.
documents.ai_summary: ISO-NE is implementing new seasonal and global capability (MCap/DCap) metrics for capacity accreditation under CAR-SA reforms. The framework establishes temperature-normalized seasonal performance standards and removes certain audit requirements, which will impact how hydropower resources demonstrate their seasonal capabilities and are accredited in the capacity market. Hydropower operators will need to ensure compliance with summer (≥80°F) and winter (≤32°F) audit requirements.

documents.title: Item 02 – 1 Connect And Manage Problem – Statement – Il Cub Proposed Revisions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: The document addresses capacity market design changes that will affect how large flexible loads participate in PJM's RPM auction, which directly impacts resource adequacy rules and market participation opportunities for all generation resources including hydropower.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is implementing a 'Connect and Manage' framework for large loads (primarily data centers) with curtailment obligations during system stress. The document discusses whether and how these loads should be reflected in the RPM demand curve, which affects capacity market clearing prices and revenue opportunities for all generators, including hydropower facilities that provide flexible ramping and reserve services.

documents.title: Item 02 – 2 Connect And Manage Issue – Charge – Il Cub Proposed Revisions
documents.hydro_relevance_reason: This document addresses capacity market treatment of large load additions and demand response, with no specific discussion of hydropower, pumped-storage, energy storage operations, or market rules directly affecting hydro participation.
documents.ai_summary: PJM is implementing a 'Connect and Manage' framework for large load additions that will modify how demand is reflected in the capacity market (RPM). The framework requires large loads to either bring their own new generation (BYONG) or face potential curtailment, which affects the total capacity requirements PJM must procure and thus the demand curve that determines capacity prices and accreditation levels for all resources including hydropower and pumped-storage facilities.

documents.ai_summary: PJM is implementing a 'Connect and Manage' framework for new large load additions that lack their own generation, which affects how demand is included in the Reliability Pricing Model (RPM) capacity market. This framework may influence capacity market clearing prices and reserve margin requirements that hydropower and pumped-storage facilities depend on for adequate compensation, and could create opportunities for flexible resources (including storage) to provide demand response and curtailment services.
